# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: Suspension Galvanometer Sensitivity**

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2023]**

> *With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a suspension-type moving coil galvanometer. Define its current sensitivity and voltage sensitivity. Why is the voltage sensitivity dependent on the external circuit resistance while the current sensitivity is a constant of the instrument?*

**Model Answer (Concise ‚ÄĒ 3-mark format):**

**Construction (1 mark):** A suspension galvanometer consists of a rectangular coil of fine enameled copper wire suspended between the poles of a permanent magnet by a thin phosphor-bronze strip. The same strip provides the controlling torque and acts as one current lead. A small mirror attached to the coil reflects a light beam onto a scale, providing pointer-less, friction-free indication. The coil moves in a radial magnetic field created by pole pieces and a soft-iron core.

**Current Sensitivity (1 mark):** $S_I = \dfrac{NBA}{K}$ (deflection per unit current; depends only on the mechanism ‚ÄĒ $N$, $B$, $A$, and suspension stiffness $K$).

**Voltage Sensitivity (1 mark):** $S_V = \dfrac{S_I}{R_m + R_{ext}}$ ‚ÄĒ depends on the *total* circuit resistance because the *voltage* that drives the current is divided across $R_m$ *and* any external resistance $R_{ext}$ the galvanometer is connected to. Hence, $S_V$ varies with $R_{ext}$ while $S_I$ does not.

---

### **Question 2: PMMC Scale Linearity**

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2022]**

> *Why is the scale of a PMMC instrument linear, while that of a moving-iron instrument is non-linear? How is damping achieved in a PMMC instrument?*

**Model Answer (3-mark format):**

**Linearity (1.5 marks):** In a PMMC, the soft-iron cylindrical core makes the magnetic field **radial** ‚ÄĒ the field lines are always perpendicular to the coil sides at every angular position. Hence the force on each coil side is always tangential, and the deflecting torque $T_d = BINA$ is **independent of deflection angle $\theta$**. With $T_c = K\theta$, equilibrium gives $\theta \propto I$ ‚Üí **linear scale**.

In a moving-iron instrument, the force depends on the *square* of the current (or the change in inductance of the coil), and the geometry causes $T_d \propto I^2 f(\theta)$ ‚Üí **non-linear (square-law) scale**.

**Damping (1.5 marks):** Damping in PMMC is **eddy-current damping** (electromagnetic damping). The aluminum former, moving in the radial magnetic field, cuts field lines and has **eddy currents** induced in it. By Lenz's law, these currents oppose the motion, producing a torque $T_{damp} = D\, d\theta/dt$ proportional to the angular velocity ‚ÄĒ this brings the pointer to rest quickly without extra moving parts.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Module 1 Choice Pattern:** Question A OR Question B (student attempts one).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Design of Shunt & Multiplier for a PMMC Movement**

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024, Modified Past Paper]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the multiplying power of a shunt used to extend the range of a PMMC ammeter. A PMMC movement has $R_m = 50\ \Omega$ and $I_m = 5$ mA. Design a shunt to extend its range to 0‚Äď5 A. Calculate the value of the shunt and verify. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A DC voltmeter uses a PMMC movement with $R_m = 100\ \Omega$, $I_m = 1$ mA. Design multiplier resistors to provide ranges of (i) 0‚Äď10 V, (ii) 0‚Äď50 V, and (iii) 0‚Äď250 V. State the sensitivity of the voltmeter in ő©/V. Comment on the loading effect. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Model Solution [7 marks]**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

For a shunt $R_{sh}$ in parallel with the meter ($R_m$, $I_m$):

Voltage across both is equal:
$$I_m R_m = I_{sh} R_{sh}$$

KCL: $I = I_m + I_{sh} \Rightarrow I_{sh} = I - I_m$

Substituting:
$$I_m R_m = (I - I_m) R_{sh}$$

$$R_{sh} = \frac{I_m R_m}{I - I_m}$$

Define **multiplying power** $m = I / I_m$:
$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1}$$

**Numerical Solution (3 marks):**

Given: $R_m = 50\ \Omega$, $I_m = 5$ mA $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ A, $I = 5$ A.

$$m = \frac{5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000$$

$$R_{sh} = \frac{50}{1000 - 1} = \frac{50}{999} \approx 0.05005\ \Omega$$

**Verification (1 mark):**
Shunt current $I_{sh} = I - I_m = 5 - 0.005 = 4.995$ A.
Voltage across shunt = $4.995 \times 0.05005 = 0.250$ V.
Voltage across meter = $0.005 \times 50 = 0.250$ V ‚úď
Both voltages match ‚ÄĒ design correct.

---

#### **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Model Solution [7 marks]**

**Formula (1 mark):** $R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_m} - R_m$

**Range (i) 0‚Äď10 V (2 marks):**
$$R_{s,1} = \frac{10}{1 \times 10^{-3}} - 100 = 10000 - 100 = 9900\ \Omega$$

**Range (ii) 0‚Äď50 V (2 marks):**
$$R_{s,2} = \frac{50}{1 \times 10^{-3}} - 100 = 50000 - 100 = 49900\ \Omega$$

**Range (iii) 0‚Äď250 V (2 marks):**
$$R_{s,3} = \frac{250}{1 \times 10^{-3}} - 100 = 250000 - 100 = 249900\ \Omega$$

**Sensitivity (1 mark, included above):**
$$\Omega/V = \frac{1}{I_m} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000\ \Omega/V = 1\ \text{k}\Omega/V$$

**Loading Effect Comment:**
This voltmeter has a sensitivity of only 1 kő©/V. When measuring a source of comparable impedance (e.g., a 5 kő© source), the voltmeter significantly loads the circuit. A 20 kő©/V voltmeter (using $I_m = 50\ \mu$A) would be far superior for low-power circuits.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Ohmmeter Design & Multimeter Architecture**

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Draw and explain the working of a **series-type ohmmeter**. Derive the expression for the half-scale resistance and justify why the scale is non-linear. A series ohmmeter has internal battery EMF 3 V, meter resistance $R_m = 100\ \Omega$, and full-scale current $I_m = 1$ mA. Find the value of the series (current-limiting) resistor. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a functional block diagram, describe the construction and working of an analog **multimeter (VOM)**. Explain the role of the **Ayrton shunt** in providing multiple current ranges. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Model Solution [7 marks]**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):** In a series ohmmeter, the unknown resistance $R_x$ is connected in series with an internal battery $E$, a current-limiting resistor $R_1$ (also called *zero-adjust* resistor), and the PMMC movement ($R_m$). The circuit is closed only when $R_x$ is connected across the *ohms* terminals of the meter.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Total circuit resistance $= R_m + R_1 + R_x$. Define $R_{int} = R_m + R_1$.

$$I = \frac{E}{R_{int} + R_x}$$

- When $R_x = 0$ (short circuit across terminals): $I = E / R_{int} = I_{fs}$ ‚Üí pointer at full scale ‚Üí marked **0 ő©** (right).
- When $R_x = \infty$ (open terminals): $I = 0$ ‚Üí pointer at zero ‚Üí marked **‚ąě ő©** (left).
- When $I = I_{fs}/2$: $\frac{E}{R_{int} + R_x} = \frac{E}{2 R_{int}}$ ‚Üí $R_{int} + R_x = 2 R_{int}$ ‚Üí $\boxed{R_x = R_{int}}$.

This is the **half-scale resistance** ‚ÄĒ the value of $R_x$ that gives mid-scale deflection.

**Non-linearity Justification (1 mark):** $\dfrac{I}{I_{fs}} = \dfrac{R_{int}}{R_{int} + R_x} = \dfrac{1}{1 + R_x/R_{int}}$ ‚ÄĒ a **hyperbolic** function. The scale is compressed near 0 ő© (small changes in $R_x$ cause large changes in $I$) and expanded near ‚ąě (large $R_x$ changes give small $I$ changes).

**Numerical Calculation (1 mark):**
At full scale, $I_{fs} = E / R_{int}$:
$$R_{int} = \frac{E}{I_{fs}} = \frac{3}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 3000\ \Omega$$
$$R_1 = R_{int} - R_m = 3000 - 100 = 2900\ \Omega$$

---

#### **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Model Solution [7 marks]**

**Multimeter Description (3 marks):** An analog multimeter (Volt-Ohm-Milliammeter or VOM) is an integrated instrument that uses a **single PMMC movement** to measure DC voltage, DC current, and resistance, by switching the movement into different external circuits via a **multi-position selector switch**. The front panel has binding posts for common (‚Äď) and positive (+) inputs, a function switch, and a range switch.

**Block Diagram (drawn in words, 2 marks):**
- **Function Switch** directs the input to one of: Ammeter section, Voltmeter section, or Ohmmeter section.
- **Ammeter Section:** Ayrton shunt (a string of series resistors with a moving tap).
- **Voltmeter Section:** Multiplier resistors (selected by range switch).
- **Ohmmeter Section:** Internal 1.5 V/9 V battery + series resistor.
- All sections feed the **common PMMC movement** with pointer and scale.

**Ayrton Shunt Role (2 marks):** An Ayrton (universal) shunt is a chain of precision resistors connected in series across the meter terminals, with intermediate taps. A selector switch determines which tap the current enters at. The advantage is:
1. **One shunt for many ranges** ‚ÄĒ no need for separate shunt resistors.
2. **No need for a make-before-break switch** ‚ÄĒ the meter is never disconnected from the shunt (always protected).
3. **Simpler calibration** ‚ÄĒ each range shares the total chain resistance.

The current at terminal $k$ is $I_k = I_m\left(1 + \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{R_m + \sum_{i=k+1}^{n} R_i}\right)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Mistakes That Cost KTU Students Their Marks (Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the minus-one:** In the shunt formula, $R_{sh} = R_m / (m-1)$ ‚ÄĒ many students write $R_m / m$ and lose 1‚Äď2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $S_I$ and $S_V$:** Voltage sensitivity is **not** a constant of the instrument ‚ÄĒ it depends on $R_{ext}$. Quote the value of $R_{ext}$ always.
> 3. **Mixing series vs. shunt ohmmeter:** Series ohmmeter for *high* $R_x$, shunt for *low* $R_x$. Wrong choice = wrong answer.
> 4. **Wrong ohmmeter scale direction:** 0 ő© on the *right* (full scale), ‚ąě ő© on the *left* (zero). Reversing the scale direction = -2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting units in $\Omega/V$:** Always state the unit explicitly when computing voltmeter sensitivity.
> 6. **PMMC on AC question:** Many students answer *yes* because they confuse PMMC with moving-iron. **PMMC = DC only.** Full stop.
> 7. **Zero-adjust in ohmmeter:** When the battery is fresh, $E = 1.55$ V; when old, $E = 1.25$ V. The operator adjusts $R_1$ to maintain $I_{fs}$ at the short-circuit terminals. If you skip this explanation, lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this for last-day exam prep!)**

### Suspension Galvanometer
- Coil **suspended** by thin phosphor-bronze strip ‚Üí zero pivot friction ‚Üí highest sensitivity.
- Same strip provides **controlling torque** *and* current lead.
- Used as **null detector** in bridges and potentiometers, *not* as a portable meter.
- $S_I = NBA / K$ (constant of instrument), $S_V = S_I / (R_m + R_{ext})$ (depends on circuit).
- Strip torsional constant: $K = G w t^3 / (3l)$.

### PMMC Mechanism (D'Arsonval Movement)
- **DC only**, linear scale (radial field), moving coil in permanent magnetic field.
- Deflecting torque: $T_d = BINA$ ‚ÄĒ **independent of $\theta$** ‚Üí linearity.
- Controlling torque: $T_c = K\theta$ from spiral hairsprings.
- Damping: **eddy-current** damping in the aluminum former (no separate dashpot).
- Equation of motion: $J \ddot\theta + D\dot\theta + K\theta = BINA$.
- Errors: aging of magnet, temperature (use **swamping resistor** of manganin), external fields (use **magnetic shunt**).

### DC Ammeter
- Range extended using **shunt** $R_{sh}$ **in parallel** with meter.
- $R_{sh} = R_m / (m-1)$ where $m = I / I_m$ is the multiplying power.
- **Ayrton shunt** = single series string with multiple taps for multi-range ammeters.

### DC Voltmeter
- Range extended using **multiplier** $R_s$ **in series** with meter.
- $R_s = V / I_m - R_m$.
- Sensitivity = **ő©/V = 1 / $I_{fs}$** (in amperes). Higher = better (less loading).

### Ohmmeter
- **Series type:** for medium/high $R_x$. Half-scale $R_x = R_{int} = R_m + R_1$. Scale: 0 ő© right, ‚ąě left. Non-linear (hyperbolic).
- **Shunt type:** for low $R_x$. Scale reversed.
- Battery $E$ varies with age ‚Üí operator adjusts $R_1$ (zero-adjust) to keep $I_{fs}$ correct.

### Multimeter (VOM)
- Single PMMC movement + selector switch + range switch.
- Combines ammeter (Ayrton shunt), voltmeter (multipliers), ohmmeter (battery circuit).
- The **Ayrton shunt** is the key innovation enabling multi-range current measurement safely.

### Master Equation of All PMMC Applications

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{BNA}{K} \cdot I_{\text{through coil}}}$$

Every other instrument (ammeter, voltmeter, ohmmeter) is built around this equation by **controlling $I$** that flows through the coil.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AC Indicating Instruments - Dynamometer, Watthour Meter, Power Factor Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** AC Indicating Instruments - Dynamometer, Watthour Meter, Power Factor Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: AC Indicating Instruments

This module covers instruments that can measure AC quantities effectively. The three primary AC indicating instruments discussed in this section are the **Dynamometer**, the **Watt-Hour Meter (Energy Meter)**, and the **Power Factor Meter**. Each of these instruments is engineered to overcome the limitations of permanent magnet moving coil (PMMC) and moving iron (MI) instruments when operating with alternating current.

---

## 1. The Dynamometer Type Instrument

### 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Dynamometer type instrument** is an electrodynamic indicating instrument in which the operating (deflecting) torque is produced by the electromagnetic interaction between **two coils** ‚ÄĒ a *fixed coil* carrying the load current and a *moving coil* carrying a current proportional to the load voltage (or a fraction of the load current). The instantaneous interaction force is proportional to the product of the two coil currents.

This is essentially an **air-core (or iron-core) electromagnetic instrument** whose torque is a function of the **product of two currents**, making it the foundational principle for AC wattmeters, transfer instruments, and laboratory-grade voltmeters/ammeters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Tug of War Between Two Magnets":**
> Imagine two horses pulling a sled. The harder the load (one horse pulls harder), and the harder the operator pulls on the rope (the other horse pulls harder), the faster the sled moves. A dynamometer is exactly like that ‚ÄĒ it has **two "magnetic horses"**: one fixed coil and one moving coil. The moving coil is dragged by the magnetic field created by the fixed coil, and the resulting twist (torque) on a spring indicates the measurement. The strength of interaction is proportional to **how much current flows in each coil**, making it ideal for measuring **power (V √ó I √ó cos ŌÜ)**, where both voltage and current quantities are involved.

### 1.3 Classification of Dynamometer Instruments

| Variant | Quantity Measured | Coil Connection |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| Dynamometer **Wattmeter** | Active Power (W) | Fixed coil = current coil (series), Moving coil = potential coil (parallel) |
| Dynamometer **Voltmeter** | Voltage (V) | Fixed & moving coils connected in series across load |
| Dynamometer **Ammeter** | Current (A) | Fixed & moving coils connected in series with load |
| **Transfer Instrument** | Reference transfer standard | Both coils highly accurate & interchangeable |

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Frequency range used:** 0 Hz to **1000 Hz** (DC + AC capability)
- **Power consumption:** Approximately **3‚Äď5 W** in current coil and **1 W** in potential coil
- **Torque-to-weight ratio:** Very low compared to MI instruments ‚ÄĒ this is its principal drawback.

---

## 2. The Watt-Hour Meter (Single-Phase Induction Type Energy Meter)

### 2.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Single-Phase Induction Type Watt-Hour Meter** is an integrating instrument that measures the total **electrical energy (in kWh)** consumed by a load over a period of time. It operates on the **Ferraris principle** (induction motor principle) where the driving torque is generated by the interaction of two alternating fluxes produced by the voltage coil and the current coil on a thin aluminum disc, and the braking torque is produced by a permanent magnet acting on the same disc.

### 2.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Odometer of a Car Powered by Electricity":**
> Think of your car's odometer. It keeps *adding up* the distance as the wheels keep turning, even when you don't know the exact speed. A watt-hour meter is the **electrical odometer of a building**. Instead of counting wheel rotations, it counts how many times an aluminum disc turns. The disc turns **faster** when power is high, and **slower** when power is low. The number of disc turns over time is directly proportional to the energy used. The permanent magnet acts like the "speed governor" ‚ÄĒ it provides friction to ensure the disc speed is *exactly* proportional to power.

### 2.3 Constructional Block Overview

- **Driving System:** Voltage coil (highly inductive), current coil (low resistance), aluminum rotor disc.
- **Braking System:** Permanent magnet (Alnico) providing eddy-current braking.
- **Registering System:** Gear train, recording dials, pointer.
- **Auxiliary:** Lag plate, friction compensator, creep adjustment holes.

### 2.4 Standard Specifications

- **Meter constant (K):** Number of revolutions per kWh ‚ÄĒ typically **600 to 1000 rev/kWh** (residential meters).
- **Operating voltage:** **230 V, 50 Hz** (India standard) or **110 V / 220 V / 240 V** depending on country.
- **Current rating:** **5 A, 10 A, 20 A, 30 A** (and up to 100 A) for residential applications.
- **Accuracy class:** Class 1.0 or Class 2.0 (typical).

---

## 3. The Power Factor Meter

### 3.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Single-Phase Dynamometer Type Power Factor Meter** is a special-purpose indicating instrument whose **deflection angle (őł) is directly equal to the phase angle (ŌÜ) of the load**, hence indicating the **power factor (cos ŌÜ)** of the AC circuit. It is unique in that it has **NO controlling torque** (no spring) ‚ÄĒ instead, the **ratio of two deflecting torques**, produced by two moving coils mounted at right angles to each other, causes the pointer to settle at an angle corresponding to the load's power factor.

### 3.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Seesaw in a Gym":**
> Imagine a seesaw with two kids sitting at right angles (perpendicular), one on the "pure-resistance" side and one on the "pure-inductance" side. The resistance kid pulls horizontally, the inductance kid pulls vertically. The seesaw's tilt angle depends on *how much each one is pulling*. The horizontal pull represents the **in-phase component (cos ŌÜ)** and the vertical pull represents the **quadrature component (sin ŌÜ)**. The pointer sits where the seesaw balances, and the angle it makes with horizontal **directly gives the power factor angle ŌÜ**. When both pulls are equal in magnitude, ŌÜ = 45¬į, cos ŌÜ = 0.707.

### 3.3 Two Main Variants

| Type | Principle | Application |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Dynamometer type (1-ŌÜ)** | Two moving coils at 90¬į on the same spindle | Single-phase laboratory use |
| **Dynamometer type (3-ŌÜ)** | Two crossed-coil systems driven from two phases | Three-phase balanced loads |
| **Moving Iron Type** | Two vanes ‚ÄĒ one in-phase, one quadrature | Switchboard use |

### 3.4 Key Standard Specs

- **Scale range:** 0.5 lagging ‚Üí 1 ‚Üí 0.5 leading
- **Operating frequency:** **50 Hz / 60 Hz**
- **Voltage ratings:** **110 V, 220 V, 440 V**
- **Current ratings:** **5 A, 10 A** (extendable via CTs)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

---

## 1. Dynamometer Type Instrument ‚ÄĒ Detailed Theory

### 1.1 Construction

A dynamometer instrument consists of:

1. **Two Fixed Coils (FF'):** Hollow cylindrical coils wound with heavy-gauge wire. They carry the *load current* and produce a *uniform magnetic field* in the central region. They are typically connected in **series** with the load (current coil configuration).

2. **Moving Coil (M):** A small, lightweight coil mounted on a spindle. It carries a *current proportional to the load voltage* (in a wattmeter) and is positioned in the air gap between the fixed coils.

3. **Spiral Control Springs:** Two springs (used for current lead) provide the **controlling torque** $T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$.

4. **Pointer and Scale:** Attached to the spindle, indicating the deflection angle őł.

5. **Damping System:** **Air-friction damping** (vanes in a chamber) ‚ÄĒ eddy current damping is avoided because eddy currents would distort the operating magnetic field.

6. **Shielding:** A cast-iron outer case provides **magnetic shielding** from external stray fields.

### 1.2 Principle of Operation

When the two coils are energized, the magnetic field produced by the **fixed coil** is proportional to $I_1$, and the current through the **moving coil** is $I_2$. The instantaneous deflecting torque is proportional to the product of these currents and the rate of change of mutual inductance.

$$T_d \propto I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

Where:
- $I_1$ = current in fixed coil
- $I_2$ = current in moving coil
- $M$ = mutual inductance between the two coils
- $\theta$ = angle of deflection

For a properly designed instrument, $\frac{dM}{d\theta}$ is made a **linear function of $\theta$** (achieved by special coil geometry), so that:

$$T_d = K_1 \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2$$

In steady-state equilibrium: $T_d = T_c$

$$K_1 \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2 = K_s \cdot \theta$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{K_1}{K_s} \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2 = K \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2}$$

### 1.3 AC Behavior ‚ÄĒ The Average Torque

In DC, the deflection is steady. In AC, however, the instantaneous torque varies with time. The **pointer, being a mechanical low-pass filter, responds to the average torque**, which is:

$$T_{d,\text{avg}} = K_1 \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi$$

Where $\phi$ is the **phase angle between $I_1$ and $I_2$**.

For a **wattmeter** (where $I_1 = I_{load}$ and $I_2 \propto V_{load}$):

$$T_{d,\text{avg}} \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = P_{\text{active}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight ‚ÄĒ Why This is Revolutionary:**
> The dynamometer wattmeter is one of the few instruments whose deflection is **directly proportional to active power**. PMMC instruments can only measure DC; MI instruments measure RMS values; but only the **dynamometer wattmeter** truly multiplies $V \times I \times \cos\phi$ ‚ÄĒ making it the gold standard for AC power measurement.

### 1.4 Sources of Error in Dynamometer Instruments

| Error Type | Cause | Effect on Reading |
|------------|-------|-------------------|
| **Temperature Error** | Heating changes coil resistance | Up to ¬Ī0.1% per ¬įC |
| **Frequency Error** | Change in coil impedance with frequency | ¬Ī0.2% at 50‚Äď500 Hz |
| **Eddy Current Error** | Induced currents in metal parts | Reduced torque at high frequency |
| **Stray Magnetic Field Error** | External DC or AC fields | Deflection offset |
| **Power Consumption Error** | Coils draw power from circuit | Error in low-power circuits |

### 1.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Used as a **transfer instrument** in calibration labs (as accurate on AC as on DC).
- Laboratory-grade **wattmeters**, **voltmeters**, and **ammeters**.
- **Reference standards** in metrology and standards laboratories.
- **Power quality analyzers** (modern digital form uses Hall-effect sensors and multipliers).

---

## 2. Watt-Hour Meter ‚ÄĒ Detailed Theory

### 2.1 The Induction Motor Principle (Ferraris Principle)

When two AC fluxes, $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$, displaced in space by 90¬į and in time by an angle ő≤, interact with a conducting disc, an **EMF is induced** in the disc. The resulting eddy currents, interacting with the fluxes, produce a **driving torque**:

$$\boxed{T_d = K_1 \cdot \phi_1 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot f \cdot \sin\beta}$$

Where:
- $\phi_1$ = flux from current coil (proportional to $I$)
- $\phi_2$ = flux from voltage coil (proportional to $V$)
- $f$ = supply frequency
- $\beta$ = angle between the two fluxes (‚Čą 90¬į in correctly lagged meter, so $\sin\beta = 1$)

In a properly adjusted watt-hour meter, the voltage coil is highly inductive, and the lag plate ensures that $\phi_2$ is **lagging by exactly 90¬į behind the voltage**. The flux $\phi_2 \propto V$, and the flux $\phi_1 \propto I$ is in phase with the load current. Thus the angle between $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ is $(90¬į - \phi)$, where $\phi$ is the load power factor angle.

Therefore:

$$T_d = K_1 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = K_1 \cdot P_{\text{load}}$$

> The driving torque is **directly proportional to active power** in the load.

### 2.2 Braking Torque ‚ÄĒ The Permanent Magnet

The aluminum disc rotates in the magnetic field of a permanent magnet. The induced eddy currents interact with the magnet's flux to produce a **braking torque** proportional to the disc speed $N$:

$$\boxed{T_b = K_2 \cdot N}$$

Where $K_2$ is a constant depending on the magnet's strength and disc geometry.

### 2.3 Steady-State Speed Equation

At equilibrium, $T_d = T_b$:

$$K_1 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = K_2 \cdot N$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{K_1}{K_2} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = K \cdot P}$$

The disc speed is **directly proportional to active power**.

### 2.4 Energy and Meter Constant

The total number of revolutions $N_{total}$ over a time interval $t$:

$$N_{total} = K \cdot \int_0^t P \, dt = K \cdot E$$

Where $E$ is the energy in kWh.

The **Meter Constant (K_h)** is defined as:

$$\boxed{K_h = \frac{\text{Number of revolutions}}{\text{Energy in kWh}}}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Energy (kWh)} = \frac{N_{total}}{K_h}$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electricity billing** in every household, commercial complex, and industrial unit.
- **Load management** in distribution networks.
- **Sub-metering** for cost allocation in factories and offices.
- **Modern smart meters** (digital + IOT) are based on the same fundamental principles.

### 2.6 Friction Compensation and Creep Adjustment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction Compensation:** A small compensating coil is wound on the central limb of the voltage-coil magnetic structure, creating a small flux that produces a tiny extra torque to overcome the bearing and gear-train friction. Adjustable via a permanent magnet shunt.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Creep:** When the voltage is applied with **no load current**, the disc may still rotate slowly due to vibration, voltage coil acting as current coil, and over-compensation. This is prevented by:
> 1. Drilling two small holes in the disc (saturated, low-flux regions).
> 2. Adjusting the lag plate to provide minimum torque.
> 3. A small permanent magnet (creep adjuster) that induces a counter-torque.

---

## 3. Power Factor Meter ‚ÄĒ Detailed Theory

### 3.1 Dynamometer Type Single-Phase Power Factor Meter

This meter has **no control springs** ‚ÄĒ the only restoring torque is produced by the **interaction between the two moving coils themselves**.

**Two Moving Coils (A and B) are mounted at right angles (90¬į) on the same spindle:**

- **Coil A:** Connected in series with a **non-inductive resistance R** (purely resistive path) ‚ÄĒ carries current $I_A \propto V \cos\phi$.
- **Coil B:** Connected in series with a **highly inductive reactance ŌČL** ‚ÄĒ carries current $I_B \propto V \sin\phi$.

The two coils share the same fixed field coil that carries the load current $I$.

### 3.2 Torque Analysis

Let the load current be $I \angle 0¬į$ (reference) and load voltage be $V \angle \phi$.

- **Current in Coil A:** $I_A = \frac{V \angle \phi}{R} \angle -\phi$ (since voltage leads current by 0 in R)... Wait ‚ÄĒ careful: the current through R is in **phase with V**, so $I_A = \frac{V}{R} \angle \phi$.

  Phase angle between $I$ and $I_A = \phi$ (since $I$ is reference).

  The torque from coil A is: $T_A = K_A \cdot I \cdot I_A \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \sin\theta_A$

  Where $\theta_A$ is the angle between the magnetic axes. If coil A's plane is at angle $\theta$ from a reference:
  
  $$T_A = K_A \cdot I \cdot I_A \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\theta$$
  
  (where $\theta$ is the deflection from the fixed-coil axis)

- **Current in Coil B:** $I_B = \frac{V \angle \phi}{j\omega L} = \frac{V}{\omega L} \angle (\phi - 90¬į)$

  Phase angle between $I$ and $I_B = (\phi - 90¬į)$.

  The torque from coil B is: $T_B = K_B \cdot I \cdot I_B \cdot \cos(\phi - 90¬į) \cdot \cos(\theta - 90¬į)$
  $$T_B = K_B \cdot I \cdot I_B \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \sin\theta$$

### 3.3 Equilibrium Condition (No Spring!)

In equilibrium, the **net torque = 0** (no controlling spring means the pointer moves to where torques balance):

$$T_A = T_B$$

$$K_A \cdot I \cdot \frac{V}{R} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\theta = K_B \cdot I \cdot \frac{V}{\omega L} \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \sin\theta$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\tan\theta = \frac{\omega L}{R} \cdot \tan\phi}$$

For a properly designed instrument, $\omega L = R$ (by choosing $L$ such that $\omega L = R$):

$$\tan\theta = \tan\phi$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \phi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The pointer's deflection angle őł is DIRECTLY EQUAL to the load phase angle ŌÜ.** The power factor can be read directly from a $\cos\phi$ calibrated scale.

### 3.4 Why No Spring is Needed

If a control spring were present, the deflection would depend on **current magnitude** (giving misleading readings). By having **two opposing torques** that depend only on phase angle, the deflection becomes **independent of load magnitude** ‚ÄĒ only the **angle ŌÜ** matters. This is the genius of the power factor meter.

### 3.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Synchronizing alternators** in power stations to a grid.
- **Power factor correction** capacitor banks control.
- **Phase sequence monitoring** in three-phase systems.
- **Substation switchboards** for operator monitoring.

|----------------|-----------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Dynamometer (general) | $T_d = K_1 \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$ | $I_1$, $I_2$ = coil currents; $M$ = mutual inductance | DC wattmeter derivation |
| Dynamometer (AC average torque) | $T_{d,\text{avg}} = K \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi$ | $\phi$ = phase angle between currents | AC wattmeter principle |

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 1. Derivation: Deflection Equation of a Dynamometer Wattmeter

We need to derive that the **deflection őł of a dynamometer wattmeter is proportional to the active power $P = VI\cos\phi$** consumed by the load.

### Step 1: Define Coil Currents

The **fixed (current) coil** carries the **load current** $I$:

$$I_1 = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The **moving (potential) coil** is connected across the load voltage, but with a series multiplier resistance $R_s$ to make it nearly non-inductive. So:

$$I_2 = \frac{V_m}{R_s} \sin(\omega t - \phi) \approx \frac{V_m}{R_s} \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Where $\phi$ is the **load phase angle** (angle by which $V$ leads $I$).

### Step 2: Write the Instantaneous Deflecting Torque

The instantaneous deflecting torque is proportional to the product of the two currents and the spatial derivative of mutual inductance:

$$T_d(t) = K_1 \cdot I_1(t) \cdot I_2(t) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

Assuming $\frac{dM}{d\theta}$ is constant (linear coil design):

$$T_d(t) = K \cdot I_1(t) \cdot I_2(t)$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Instantaneous Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d(t) &= K \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot \frac{V_m}{R_s} \sin(\omega t - \phi) \\
&= \frac{K \cdot I_m \cdot V_m}{R_s} \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\omega t - \phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Trigonometric Product Identity

Using the identity:

$$\sin(A) \cdot \sin(B) = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d(t) &= \frac{K \cdot I_m \cdot V_m}{2 R_s} \cdot [\cos(\omega t - (\omega t - \phi)) - \cos(\omega t + \omega t - \phi)] \\
&= \frac{K \cdot I_m \cdot V_m}{2 R_s} \cdot [\cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Take the Average (Pointer Reads the Mean)

The pointer, due to mechanical inertia, cannot follow the **$2\omega$ oscillation**, so it reads the **average value** over a complete cycle. The average of $\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$ over a period is **zero**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle T_d \rangle &= \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T T_d(t) \, dt \\
&= \frac{K \cdot I_m \cdot V_m}{2 R_s} \cdot \cos\phi - 0 \\
&= \frac{K \cdot I_m \cdot V_m}{2 R_s} \cdot \cos\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Convert to RMS Values

Recall $I_m = I\sqrt{2}$ and $V_m = V\sqrt{2}$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle T_d \rangle &= \frac{K \cdot \sqrt{2} I \cdot \sqrt{2} V}{2 R_s} \cdot \cos\phi \\
&= \frac{K \cdot I \cdot V}{R_s} \cdot \cos\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Equilibrium with Control Spring Torque

The control spring torque is $T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$. At equilibrium $T_d = T_c$:

$$\frac{K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}{R_s} = K_s \cdot \theta$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{K}{K_s \cdot R_s} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = K_m \cdot P_{\text{active}}}$$

Where $K_m$ is the meter constant of the wattmeter and $P_{\text{active}} = VI\cos\phi$ is the **active power in watts**.

> ‚úÖ **Conclusion:** The deflection őł is **directly proportional to active power** ‚ÄĒ the wattmeter reads true power regardless of waveform or power factor.

---

## 2. Derivation: Speed-Energy Relation of Watt-Hour Meter

We need to derive the relation between **disc revolutions** and **energy consumed**.

### Step 1: Driving Torque in Steady State

From Ferraris principle, the driving torque is:

$$T_d = K_1 \cdot \phi_1 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot f \cdot \sin\beta$$

For a properly designed meter, the voltage coil is highly inductive so that $\phi_2$ lags the voltage by 90¬į, and the lag plate ensures that the two fluxes are in **time quadrature** (ő≤ = 90¬į), so $\sin\beta = 1$.

Also, $\phi_1 \propto I$ and $\phi_2 \propto V$. Therefore:

$$T_d = K_1 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot f = K_2 \cdot V \cdot I$$

If the voltage-coil circuit is further arranged so that the angle between $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ is $(90¬į - \phi)$ rather than 90¬į, then:

$$T_d = K_2 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = K_2 \cdot P$$

### Step 2: Braking Torque from the Permanent Magnet

The aluminum disc moves in the magnetic field $B_m$ of the permanent magnet. By Faraday's law, eddy currents are induced that oppose the motion (Lenz's law). The braking torque is proportional to disc angular velocity $N$ (rev/s):

$$T_b = K_3 \cdot N$$

### Step 3: Steady-State Equilibrium

At constant load, $T_d = T_b$:

$$K_2 \cdot P = K_3 \cdot N$$

Solving for N:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{K_2}{K_3} \cdot P = K \cdot P \quad \text{(rev/s)}}$$

### Step 4: Integrate Over Time to Get Total Energy

If the load varies with time, $P(t) = V(t) \cdot I(t) \cdot \cos\phi(t)$, the total number of revolutions in time $T$ is:

$$N_{total} = \int_0^T N(t) \, dt = K \int_0^T P(t) \, dt = K \cdot E$$

Where $E$ is the total energy in joules. Converting to kWh ($1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J):

$$N_{total} = K \cdot E_{\text{kWh}} \cdot 3.6 \times 10^6$$

### Step 5: Define the Meter Constant

The **meter constant $K_h$** is defined as the number of revolutions per kWh:

$$\boxed{K_h = \frac{N_{total}}{E_{\text{kWh}}} \quad \text{(rev/kWh)}}$$

Therefore, **energy consumed**:

$$\boxed{E_{\text{kWh}} = \frac{N_{total}}{K_h}}$$

This is the fundamental equation used by electricity boards to compute bills.

---

## 3. Derivation: Deflection Equation of the Power Factor Meter

We need to prove that the **deflection őł equals the load phase angle ŌÜ**.

### Step 1: Coil Currents

Let the load current be $I \angle 0¬į$ (reference), and load voltage be $V \angle \phi$.

- **Coil A** (in series with R): current is in **phase with V**:
$$I_A = \frac{V}{R} \angle \phi = I_A \angle \phi$$

  Phase angle between $I$ (reference) and $I_A$ is **ŌÜ**.

- **Coil B** (in series with jŌČL): current **lags V by 90¬į**:
$$I_B = \frac{V}{j\omega L} \angle \phi = \frac{V}{\omega L} \angle (\phi - 90¬į) = I_B \angle (\phi - 90¬į)$$

  Phase angle between $I$ and $I_B$ is **$(\phi - 90¬į)$**.

### Step 2: Torque Produced by Each Coil

**Torque from Coil A:** Deflecting torque is proportional to the product of currents and the cosine of the phase angle between them:

$$T_A = K_A \cdot I \cdot I_A \cdot \cos\phi$$

But this torque acts on a coil whose magnetic axis makes an angle őł with the fixed coil's axis (where őł is the deflection from some reference). The torque's effective component is:

$$T_A = K_A \cdot I \cdot I_A \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Torque from Coil B:** Phase angle is $(\phi - 90¬į)$, so $\cos(\phi - 90¬į) = \sin\phi$. The coil B is mounted at 90¬į to coil A, so its effective angular contribution is $\cos(\theta - 90¬į) = \sin\theta$:

$$T_B = K_B \cdot I \cdot I_B \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \sin\theta$$

### Step 3: Equilibrium Condition

In a power factor meter, **there is NO control spring**. The pointer rotates until the two torques balance ($T_A = T_B$):

$$K_A \cdot I \cdot \frac{V}{R} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\theta = K_B \cdot I \cdot \frac{V}{\omega L} \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \sin\theta$$

The $I \cdot V$ terms cancel (which is **why the reading is independent of magnitude**):

$$K_A \cdot \frac{\cos\phi \cdot \cos\theta}{R} = K_B \cdot \frac{\sin\phi \cdot \sin\theta}{\omega L}$$

### Step 4: Solve for tan őł

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \frac{K_A \cdot \omega L}{K_B \cdot R} \cdot \frac{\cos\phi}{\sin\phi}$$

$$\tan\theta = \frac{K_A \cdot \omega L}{K_B \cdot R} \cdot \tan\phi$$

If we design $K_A = K_B$ and choose $L$ such that $\omega L = R$:

$$\boxed{\tan\theta = \tan\phi \implies \theta = \phi}$$

### Step 5: Calibration

The scale is marked in **degrees of ŌÜ** or **cos ŌÜ values** (0.5 lag ‚Üí 1 ‚Üí 0.5 lead). The pointer therefore indicates:

- **őł = 0¬į:** Unity power factor (cos ŌÜ = 1, resistive load)
- **őł = 60¬į:** Power factor = 0.5 (highly inductive or capacitive)
- **őł = 90¬į:** Zero power factor (purely reactive)

---

## 4. Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Example:** A dynamometer wattmeter is used to measure the power in a single-phase circuit. The load voltage is $V = 230\text{ V}$ at 50 Hz, the load current is $I = 10\text{ A}$, and the power factor is 0.8 lagging. Find the active power.

### Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi \\
&= 230 \times 10 \times 0.8 \\
&= 1840 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example (Energy Meter):** A household consumes 1840 W for 4 hours. If the meter constant is 625 rev/kWh, find the number of disc revolutions.

### Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= P \times t = 1840 \text{ W} \times 4 \text{ hr} = 7.36 \text{ kWh} \\
N &= K_h \times E = 625 \times 7.36 = 4600 \text{ revolutions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example (Power Factor Meter):** For a load with phase angle ŌÜ = 36.87¬į, find the power factor and the deflection of a properly designed PFM.

### Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi &= \cos(36.87¬į) \approx 0.8 \text{ (lagging)} \\
\text{Deflection } \theta &= \phi = 36.87¬į \text{ (PFM with } \omega L = R)
\end{aligned}
$$

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a dynamometer type wattmeter. Why is it suitable only for AC?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

A dynamometer wattmeter consists of **two fixed coils** (current coil) connected in series with the load, and a **moving coil** (potential coil) connected across the load through a high multiplier resistance. The fixed coils produce a magnetic field proportional to the **load current I**, while the moving coil carries a current proportional to the **load voltage V**. The interaction between these two magnetic fields produces a deflecting torque proportional to $VI\cos\phi$, i.e., the **active power**. A spiral spring provides the controlling torque, and air-friction damping is used.

It is suitable for AC because its torque is proportional to the **product of two currents**, and the time-averaged product naturally yields $\cos\phi$ ‚ÄĒ making it a true power-measuring instrument. **(1.5 marks for construction, 1.5 marks for working and AC suitability)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: What is "creep" in a single-phase induction type energy meter? How is it prevented?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Creep** is the slow but continuous rotation of the aluminum disc of a watt-hour meter even when **no load current flows** but the voltage coil is energized. It is caused by:
- Stray magnetic fields
- Voltage coil acting as a small current coil
- Over-compensation of friction compensator
- Mechanical vibrations

**Prevention methods:**
1. **Drilling two small holes** in the aluminum disc ‚ÄĒ these holes break the eddy-current path under the voltage-coil pole, reducing driving torque to near zero when no load current is present.
2. **Adjusting the lag plate** to minimize the residual phase error.
3. A small **permanent magnet** (creep adjuster) creating a small counter-torque.

**(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for prevention methods)**

---

## Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 ‚ÄĒ Model Question Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat diagram, the construction and working principle of a single-phase dynamometer type wattmeter. Derive the expression for deflecting torque and show that the deflection is proportional to the true power in AC circuit. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A dynamometer wattmeter is used to measure the power consumed by a single-phase load. The load voltage is $V = 220 \text{ V}$ at 50 Hz, the load current is $I = 12 \text{ A}$, and the power factor angle is $\phi = 30¬į$ lagging. Calculate:
  (i) The active power consumed.
  (ii) The reactive power consumed.
  (iii) The apparent power.
  (iv) The deflection of the wattmeter (in percentage) if its full-scale reading is 5 kW. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Construction and Working (7 marks):**

A single-phase dynamometer wattmeter consists of:

- **Two Fixed Coils (FF'):** Wound with heavy-gauge wire, connected in series with the load. They carry the **load current I** and produce a uniform magnetic field in the central region.
- **Moving Coil (M):** A lightweight coil mounted on a spindle, connected across the load through a **multiplier resistor Rs** (to make the coil circuit non-inductive and limit current).
- **Two Control Springs:** Provide the controlling torque $T_c = K_s \theta$.
- **Pointer and Scale:** Indicate the deflection angle őł.
- **Air Friction Damper:** Two aluminum vanes moving in air chambers.
- **Magnetic Shielding:** Cast-iron outer case to prevent stray-field errors.

**Working Principle:**

The deflecting torque is:

$$T_d = K \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi$$

Where $I_1 = I$ (load current in fixed coil), $I_2 = V/R_s$ (current in moving coil proportional to voltage), and $\phi$ is the load phase angle.

**Derivation:**

In AC, the instantaneous currents are:
- $i_1 = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ (in fixed coil)
- $i_2 = \frac{V_m}{R_s} \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ (in moving coil)

Instantaneous torque:
$$T_d(t) = K \cdot i_1 \cdot i_2 = \frac{K \cdot I_m \cdot V_m}{R_s} \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Using the identity:
$$\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$$

$$
T_d(t) = \frac{K \cdot I_m \cdot V_m}{2 R_s} \cdot [\cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)]
$$

Average torque:
$$\langle T_d \rangle = \frac{K \cdot I_m \cdot V_m}{2 R_s} \cdot \cos\phi = \frac{K \cdot V \cdot I}{R_s} \cdot \cos\phi$$

At equilibrium, $T_d = T_c$:
$$\frac{K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}{R_s} = K_s \cdot \theta$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{K}{K_s R_s} \cdot V I \cos\phi = K_m \cdot P_{\text{active}}}$$

Hence the deflection is **directly proportional to active power** in the AC circuit. **(3 marks construction, 2 marks working, 2 marks derivation)**

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $V = 220$ V, $I = 12$ A, $\phi = 30¬į$, Full-scale = 5 kW = 5000 W

**(i) Active Power:**
$$
P = V I \cos\phi = 220 \times 12 \times \cos(30¬į) = 220 \times 12 \times 0.866 = 2286.4 \text{ W}
$$

**(ii) Reactive Power:**
$$
Q = V I \sin\phi = 220 \times 12 \times \sin(30¬į) = 220 \times 12 \times 0.5 = 1320 \text{ VAR}
$$

**(iii) Apparent Power:**
$$
S = V I = 220 \times 12 = 2640 \text{ VA}
$$

**(iv) Percentage Deflection:**
$$
\% \text{ Deflection} = \frac{P}{\text{Full scale}} \times 100 = \frac{2286.4}{5000} \times 100 = 45.73\%
$$

**(2 marks each for first three, 1 mark for the last)**

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024 ‚ÄĒ Alternative Internal Choice]

**Q: (a)** Describe with a neat sketch, the construction and working of a single-phase induction type watt-hour meter. Derive the expression for the driving torque, braking torque, and the meter constant. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A single-phase energy meter has a meter constant of 750 rev/kWh. The disc makes 300 revolutions in 24 minutes. Calculate:
  (i) The energy consumed in kWh.
  (ii) The average load in kW.
  (iii) If the meter makes 150 revolutions in 12 minutes with no apparent load, what is the percentage error due to creep? **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Construction, Working, and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A single-phase induction-type watt-hour meter has four main parts:

1. **Driving System:** Voltage coil (highly inductive, on center limb), current coil (low turns, in series with load), aluminum disc mounted on spindle.
2. **Braking System:** Permanent magnet (Alnico) with adjustable position.
3. **Registering System:** Gear train, recording dials.
4. **Auxiliary Devices:** Lag plate, friction compensator, creep adjuster holes in disc.

**Working:** The voltage coil produces a flux $\phi_2 \propto V$ lagging by ~90¬į behind V. The current coil produces a flux $\phi_1 \propto I$ in phase with I. The interaction of these two alternating fluxes with the aluminum disc induces **eddy currents** that produce a **driving torque** (Ferraris principle).

**Derivation of Driving Torque:**
$$T_d = K_1 \cdot \phi_1 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot f \cdot \sin\beta$$

For proper lag adjustment, $\beta = 90¬į$ and $\sin\beta = 1$:
$$T_d = K_1 \cdot \phi_1 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot f$$

With $\phi_1 \propto I$ and $\phi_2 \propto V$:
$$T_d \propto V \cdot I = P$$

When the lag plate is adjusted to make the angle between fluxes $(90¬į - \phi)$:
$$T_d = K_2 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = K_2 \cdot P$$

**Braking Torque:**
The aluminum disc rotates in the field of the permanent magnet. By Faraday's law, an EMF is induced, producing eddy currents that oppose the motion:
$$T_b = K_3 \cdot N$$

**Equilibrium and Meter Constant:**
At steady state, $T_d = T_b$:
$$K_2 \cdot P = K_3 \cdot N \implies N = K \cdot P \quad (\text{where } K = K_2/K_3)$$

Total revolutions:
$$N_{total} = \int_0^t N \, d\tau = K \int_0^t P \, d\tau = K \cdot E$$

Meter constant:
$$K_h = \frac{N_{total}}{E_{\text{kWh}}}$$

**(2 marks construction, 2 marks working, 3 marks derivation)**

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $K_h = 750$ rev/kWh, $N_{total} = 300$ rev, $t = 24$ min = 0.4 hr

**(i) Energy consumed:**
$$
E = \frac{N_{total}}{K_h} = \frac{300}{750} = 0.4 \text{ kWh}
$$

**(ii) Average Load:**
$$
P_{avg} = \frac{E}{t} = \frac{0.4}{0.4} = 1 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Creep Error:**

Without load, in 12 minutes, disc makes 150 revolutions. True energy should be 0, but meter shows:
$$
E_{creep} = \frac{150}{750} = 0.2 \text{ kWh} \text{ in 12 min}
$$

In terms of equivalent power:
$$
P_{creep} = \frac{0.2}{12/60} = \frac{0.2}{0.2} = 1 \text{ kW (apparent)}
$$

**Percentage error due to creep** = (Apparent energy)/(True energy) √ó 100, but in a more relevant KTU context, it is the **percentage of creep revolution** compared to rated speed:

At full load (say 1 kW at 0.4 hr), revolutions = 300. So creep in 12 min = 150 rev is **50% of full-load revolutions in that time**, which is unacceptably high. This is the **creep problem**.

**(2 marks each for first two, 3 marks for creep analysis)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Examiner's Advisory:**
>
> 1. **Confusing meter constant with revolutions:** Students often invert the relation. **Remember:** $K_h$ = revolutions per kWh, so $E = N / K_h$ (not $E = N \times K_h$).
> 2. **Forgetting the cos ŌÜ factor in watt-hour meter derivation:** Many students write $T_d \propto VI$ ‚ÄĒ but for a lag-adjusted meter, the **$\cos\phi$ factor appears because the angle between fluxes is $(90¬į - \phi)$**, not $90¬į$. This is a 1-mark loss in long answers.
> 3. **Drawing the wattmeter without labeling the multiplier resistor:** The moving coil must show a series resistance Rs; this is part of construction marks.
> 4. **Confusing the spring in PFM:** The dynamometer type power factor meter has **NO control spring**. Writing about springs will lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing deflection őł with power factor cos ŌÜ:** The deflection őł equals the **phase angle ŌÜ**, and the scale is marked in **cos ŌÜ**. Be clear about the difference.
> 6. **In PF meter derivation, forgetting the 90¬į spatial offset of Coil B:** The torque from coil B is $\propto \sin\theta$, not $\cos\theta$. This leads to wrong equation in 2 marks.
> 7. **Skipping the air-friction damping explanation:** KTU explicitly asks about damping systems. **Air damping is used in dynamometer wattmeters** (not eddy current damping ‚ÄĒ that would distort the operating field).
> 8. **In the energy meter numericals, unit conversion errors:** Time must be in **hours**, energy in **kWh**, and load in **kW**. Watch for minute-to-hour conversions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 ‚ÄĒ AC Indicating Instruments):**

### Dynamometer Type Instrument
- ‚≠ź Two coils: **Fixed (current)** and **Moving (potential)**.
- ‚≠ź Deflecting torque: $T_d = K I_1 I_2 \cos\phi$ (AC average).
- ‚≠ź Used as **wattmeter** (true power), voltmeter, ammeter, and **transfer instrument**.
- ‚≠ź **Air friction damping** is used (not eddy current).
- ‚≠ź Errors: temperature, frequency, eddy current, stray field.
- ‚≠ź **Low torque/weight ratio** is the main disadvantage.

### Watt-Hour Meter
- ‚≠ź Operates on **Ferraris principle** (induction motor).
- ‚≠ź Driving torque: $T_d \propto VI\cos\phi = P$ (active power).
- ‚≠ź Braking torque: $T_b \propto N$ (from permanent magnet).
- ‚≠ź Speed: $N = K \cdot P$; Energy: $E = N_{total} / K_h$.
- ‚≠ź **Creep prevention:** two holes in disc + lag plate adjustment.
- ‚≠ź **Friction compensation:** small compensating coil on voltage coil.
- ‚≠ź **Lag plate** makes the angle between $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ equal to $(90¬į - \phi)$.

### Power Factor Meter (Dynamometer Type)
- ‚≠ź **NO control spring** ‚ÄĒ unique feature.
- ‚≠ź Two moving coils at **90¬į** to each other.
- ‚≠ź Coil A in series with **R** (resistive) ‚Üí torque $\propto \cos\phi \cos\theta$.
- ‚≠ź Coil B in series with **ŌČL** (inductive) ‚Üí torque $\propto \sin\phi \sin\theta$.
- ‚≠ź Deflection: $\theta = \phi$ (when $\omega L = R$).
- ‚≠ź Scale calibrated in **cos ŌÜ** (0.5 lag to 1 to 0.5 lead).
- ‚≠ź **Independent of voltage and current magnitudes** (only depends on phase angle).

### Key Numerical Facts to Memorize
- ‚≠ź $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.
- ‚≠ź Typical meter constant range: **500‚Äď1000 rev/kWh**.
- ‚≠ź Standard residential voltage (India): **230 V, 50 Hz**.
- ‚≠ź For cos ŌÜ = 0.8 lag, ŌÜ = 36.87¬į; For cos ŌÜ = 0.5 lag, ŌÜ = 60¬į.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheatstone, Kelvin Bridges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wheatstone, Kelvin Bridges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wheatstone Bridge ‚ÄĒ Formal Definition

The **Wheatstone Bridge** is a fundamental **null-type D.C. bridge** network invented by Sir Charles Wheatstone (1843). It consists of four resistive arms arranged in a diamond (quadrilateral) configuration, with a **galvanometer** connected between two opposite nodes (the detector branch) and a **DC excitation source** connected across the other two opposite nodes (the source branch). It is the standard, classical instrument used for the precise measurement of **medium-range resistances (typically 1 ő© to 1 Mő©)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A bridge circuit in which the unknown resistance is determined by balancing the bridge so that the detector current is zero. The condition of balance is achieved by varying calibrated known resistances until the galvanometer indicates null deflection.

### 1.2 Kelvin Bridge ‚ÄĒ Formal Definition

The **Kelvin Double Bridge** (Lord Kelvin, 1861) is a modified, sophisticated version of the Wheatstone Bridge specifically engineered to overcome the lead and contact-resistance errors that plague low-resistance measurement. It is the standard instrument for measuring resistances **below 1 ő©** (typically 1 őľő© to 1 ő©), such as shunt resistances, armature windings, cable resistances, and switch contact resistances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** At very low resistance values (milliohms), the resistance of the connecting leads and contact terminals becomes comparable to (or even larger than) the unknown resistance. The Wheatstone bridge cannot eliminate these parasitic resistances, whereas the Kelvin bridge's unique **double-ratio-arm topology** mathematically cancels them out.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Seesaw of Currents"

Imagine a perfectly balanced **seesaw** (the galvanometer) at the center of a four-way junction:

- **Wheatstone Bridge:** Four people of different weights sit on the four arms. By adjusting one person (the **variable standard resistor**), you make the seesaw perfectly level. The weight of the unknown person can then be **deduced** from the ratios of the other three. *No current flows through the seesaw at balance ‚ÄĒ hence "null-deflection method."*
- **Kelvin Bridge:** A more sophisticated "double seesaw" with extra balancing linkages to neutralize the **weight of the connecting ropes** (lead resistances). Without this compensation, the rope weight would distort the measurement of the lightest person (the small unknown resistance).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Excitation voltage (Wheatstone) | 1.5 V ‚Äď 10 V DC | $V_{ex}$ |
| Measurement range (Wheatstone) | **1 ő© to 1 Mő©** | ‚ÄĒ |
| Measurement range (Kelvin) | **1 őľő© to 1 ő©** | ‚ÄĒ |
| Galvanometer sensitivity | 1 mm/őľA (typical) | $S_g$ |
| Standard laboratory accuracy | **¬Ī 0.02 % to ¬Ī 0.2 %** | ‚ÄĒ |
| Bridge balance indicator | D'Arsonval Galvanometer / Null Detector | ‚ÄĒ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wheatstone Bridge ‚ÄĒ Operational Principle

The Wheatstone bridge operates on the **Null-Deflection Principle**: the unknown resistance $R_x$ is calculated from the known values of three other arms when the bridge is balanced (galvanometer current = 0).

**The Four Arms:**
- Arm **AB** = $P$ (Known ratio arm 1)
- Arm **BC** = $Q$ (Known ratio arm 2)
- Arm **AD** = $R$ (Known variable standard resistance)
- Arm **DC** = $R_x$ (Unknown resistance)

**Nodes:**
- **A‚ÄďC**: Connected to the DC excitation source $V_{ex}$
- **B‚ÄďD**: Connected to the galvanometer (detector)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Null Deflection:** When the bridge is balanced, **no current flows through the galvanometer**. This eliminates the galvanometer's own internal resistance and calibration errors from the measurement equation. The measurement depends ONLY on the precision of the known resistors, which is why null methods are inherently more accurate than deflection methods.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Balance Equation (Conceptual Walkthrough)

At balance, $I_g = 0$, therefore:
- The current through $P$ equals the current through $R$ ‚Üí call it $I_1$
- The current through $Q$ equals the current through $R_x$ ‚Üí call it $I_2$

The potential at node B equals the potential at node D (since $I_g = 0$ means no IR drop across galvanometer). The voltage drop from A to B must equal the drop from A to D:

$$V_{AB} = V_{AD}$$

$$I_1 \cdot P = I_2 \cdot R_x$$

Similarly, the drop from B to C equals the drop from D to C:

$$I_1 \cdot Q = I_2 \cdot R$$

Dividing these two equations eliminates the currents $I_1$ and $I_2$, yielding the **canonical balance equation**:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{P}{Q} = \dfrac{R}{R_x} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R_x = R \cdot \dfrac{Q}{P}}$$

### 2.3 Sensitivity of the Wheatstone Bridge

The **bridge sensitivity** $S_b$ is defined as the deflection of the galvanometer per unit change in the unknown resistance, evaluated at the balance point. A common expression for a balanced bridge (all four arms equal to $R$, galvanometer resistance $R_g$, source resistance negligible):

$$S_b = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4R} \cdot \dfrac{R_g}{R + R_g}$$

**Maximum sensitivity** occurs when the galvanometer resistance matches the Thevenin resistance of the bridge looking out from the galvanometer terminals:

$$R_g = R_{th} = R \quad (\text{when } P = Q = R = R_x = R)$$

### 2.4 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheatstone Balance | $R_x = R \cdot \dfrac{Q}{P}$ | All in ő© | Core measurement |
| 2 | Galvanometer current (unbalanced) | $I_g = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot (QR_x - PR)}{R_g(P+Q)(R_x+R) + PQ(R_x+R) + R_xR(P+Q)}$ | Amperes | Sensitivity analysis |
| 3 | Bridge Sensitivity | $S_b = \dfrac{d\theta}{dR_x} \bigg\vert_{balance}$ | rad/ő© | Design optimization |
| 4 | Kelvin Balance | $R_x = R_s \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q} + \dfrac{r \cdot q}{p+q+Q} \cdot \left( \dfrac{P}{Q} - \dfrac{p}{q} \right)$ | All in ő© | Low-resistance measurement |
| 5 | Kelvin (Double-Ratio, p/q = P/Q) | $R_x = R_s \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | All in ő© | Ideal Kelvin case |
| 6 | Thevenin voltage (Wheatstone, unbalanced) | $E_{th} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{QR_x - PR}{(P+Q)(R_x+R)}$ | Volts | Detector drive |
| 7 | Thevenin resistance (looking into galvanometer) | $R_{th} = \dfrac{PQ}{P+Q} + \dfrac{R \cdot R_x}{R + R_x}$ | ő© | Matching for max sensitivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule for Tables:** In the table above, the absolute value bars are written as `\dfrac` (fraction). When using absolute values in a markdown table, ALWAYS use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` ‚ÄĒ never the raw pipe character `|`, as it breaks the table column delimiter!

### 2.5 Limitations of the Wheatstone Bridge

1. **Lead & Contact Resistance Errors:** Significant when measuring low resistances ($< 1\ \Omega$), since lead resistance ($0.1$ to $1\ \Omega$ per lead) becomes a sizeable fraction of $R_x$.
2. **Thermal EMF Errors:** Thermoelectric EMFs generated at dissimilar metal junctions produce spurious galvanometer deflection. Mitigated by reversing source polarity and averaging.
3. **Insulation Leakage:** At very high resistances ($> 10\ \text{M}\Omega$), leakage paths across the bridge arms introduce parallel-resistance errors.
4. **Component Aging:** Precision of measurement is bounded by the long-term stability of $P$, $Q$, and $R$.

### 2.6 Why the Kelvin Bridge Solves the Low-Resistance Problem

In the Wheatstone bridge, the **connecting lead from $R_x$ to node A** carries the main current and introduces a parasitic resistance $r$ in series with $R_x$. This lead resistance is indistinguishable from $R_x$ itself, causing gross error.

The **Kelvin bridge's elegant solution** is a second pair of ratio arms ($p$ and $q$) connected to a "yoke" point that taps the unknown resistance at a point physically *between* the unknown resistor and the troublesome lead. The mathematical cancellation that results is the heart of the Kelvin bridge (full derivation in Section 3).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Power Systems** | Measurement of shunt resistance, bus-bar resistance, switch contact resistance |
| **Electric Machines** | Armature winding resistance, field coil resistance |
| **Cable Industry** | Conductor DC resistance per km |
| **Battery Manufacturing** | Internal resistance of cells |
| **Aerospace & Automotive** | Bonding resistance verification, ground strap testing |
| **Process Instrumentation** | RTD calibration, precision resistor trimming |
| **Electronics Manufacturing** | PCB trace resistance, via resistance testing |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Wheatstone Bridge Balance Equation

We start with the circuit of the Wheatstone bridge. Let the four arms be denoted as $P$, $Q$, $R$, and $R_x$ as shown in Section 4. The DC excitation source $V_{ex}$ is connected across nodes A and C. The galvanometer of resistance $R_g$ is connected between nodes B and D.

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around loop **ABDA** (Source ‚Üí A ‚Üí B ‚Üí D ‚Üí A) at balance. The current in arm AB is $I_1$ and the current in arm AD is $I_2$.

$$I_1 \cdot P - I_g \cdot R_g - I_2 \cdot R = 0$$

**Step 2:** At balance condition, the galvanometer current is zero:

$$I_g = 0$$

This simplifies the equation drastically:

$$I_1 \cdot P = I_2 \cdot R \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply KVL around loop **BCDB** (B ‚Üí C ‚Üí D ‚Üí B):

$$I_1 \cdot Q - I_g \cdot R_g - I_2 \cdot R_x = 0$$

Substituting $I_g = 0$:

$$I_1 \cdot Q = I_2 \cdot R_x \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

**Step 4:** Divide Equation 2 by Equation 1 to eliminate the unknown currents $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$\dfrac{I_1 \cdot Q}{I_1 \cdot P} = \dfrac{I_2 \cdot R_x}{I_2 \cdot R}$$

**Step 5:** The currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ cancel out, leaving the **canonical balance equation**:

$$\dfrac{Q}{P} = \dfrac{R_x}{R}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to solve for the unknown resistance $R_x$:

$$\boxed{R_x = R \cdot \dfrac{Q}{P}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The unknown resistance is the product of the known variable standard $R$ and the ratio $Q/P$. By choosing different ratio values for $P$ and $Q$, the range of measurable resistances can be extended across many decades.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Kelvin Bridge Balance Equation

The Kelvin bridge has **six resistive arms**:
- $R_x$ ‚ÄĒ unknown low resistance
- $R_s$ ‚ÄĒ known low standard resistance
- $P, Q$ ‚ÄĒ outer ratio arms (first pair)
- $p, q$ ‚ÄĒ inner ratio arms (second pair)
- $r$ ‚ÄĒ yoke resistance (lead resistance between $R_x$ terminal and the $p$‚Äď$q$ junction)

Refer to the Mermaid circuit diagram in Section 4 for topology.

**Step 1:** Define the bridge balance condition. At balance, no current flows through the galvanometer ($I_g = 0$). This implies that:
- The current through the galvanometer is zero
- The current through $p$ equals the current through $R_x$ (call it $I_1$)
- The current through $q$ equals the current through $R_s$ (call it $I_2$)

**Step 2:** Apply KVL around the loop containing $R_x$, $p$, and the galvanometer. Since $I_g = 0$, the voltage drop across $R_x$ plus the drop across the lead segment from $R_x$ to the $p$‚Äď$q$ junction (which is part of $r$) equals the drop across $p$:

$$I_1 \cdot \left( R_x + r_1 \right) = I_g \cdot R_g + I_1 \cdot p$$

With $I_g = 0$:

$$I_1 \cdot (R_x + r_1) = I_1 \cdot p \cdot \dfrac{P}{P+Q} \cdot \text{adjustment factor}$$

Let us instead follow the canonical derivation rigorously.

**Step 3:** Define $r$ as the total resistance of the yoke connecting the junction of $p$‚Äď$q$ to the junction of $R_x$ and $R_s$. The current through $r$ is $I_1 - I_2$ (since the galvanometer takes no current at balance).

**Step 4:** Apply KVL to the lower loop (galvanometer loop):

$$I_1 \cdot p + (I_1 - I_2) \cdot r = I_2 \cdot q$$

Expand:

$$I_1 \cdot p + I_1 \cdot r - I_2 \cdot r = I_2 \cdot q$$

Rearrange:

$$I_1 \cdot (p + r) = I_2 \cdot (q + r)$$

**Step 5:** Apply KVL to the upper loop (source loop):

$$I_1 \cdot (R_x + p) = I_2 \cdot (R_s + q)$$

(Note: this is because the source voltage divides equally between the two branches at balance.)

**Step 6:** Take the ratio of the two KVL equations. Dividing the upper loop equation by the lower loop equation:

$$\dfrac{I_1 \cdot (R_x + p)}{I_1 \cdot (p + r)} = \dfrac{I_2 \cdot (R_s + q)}{I_2 \cdot (q + r)}$$

The currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ cancel:

$$\dfrac{R_x + p}{p + r} = \dfrac{R_s + q}{q + r}$$

**Step 7:** Cross-multiply:

$$(R_x + p)(q + r) = (R_s + q)(p + r)$$

Expand both sides:

$$R_x \cdot q + R_x \cdot r + p \cdot q + p \cdot r = R_s \cdot p + R_s \cdot r + q \cdot p + q \cdot r$$

**Step 8:** The term $p \cdot q$ appears on both sides and cancels:

$$R_x \cdot q + R_x \cdot r + p \cdot r = R_s \cdot p + R_s \cdot r + q \cdot r$$

**Step 9:** Expand $p \cdot r$ and $q \cdot r$, and use the fact that the bridge ratio condition is set such that the main ratio arms maintain $\dfrac{P}{Q} = \dfrac{p}{q}$. Substitute $p = \dfrac{P \cdot q}{Q}$ into the equation to obtain:

$$R_x = R_s \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q} + \dfrac{r \cdot q}{p+q+Q} \cdot \left( \dfrac{P}{Q} - \dfrac{p}{q} \right)$$

This is the **exact balance equation** of the Kelvin bridge.

**Step 10:** If the second ratio arm is adjusted such that $\dfrac{p}{q} = \dfrac{P}{Q}$ (this is the **double-ratio condition**), then the second term vanishes entirely:

$$\boxed{R_x = R_s \cdot \dfrac{P}{Q}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance of Step 10:** When the double-ratio condition is met, the lead resistance $r$ has **zero effect** on the measurement. This is the elegant mathematical cancellation that makes the Kelvin bridge the definitive instrument for low-resistance measurement. The yoke resistance $r$ can be made arbitrarily large (long leads, dirty contacts) and it will not affect accuracy ‚ÄĒ provided the ratio arm pairs are perfectly matched.

---

### 3.3 Sensitivity Analysis ‚ÄĒ Galvanometer Current in an Unbalanced Wheatstone Bridge

This derivation is often a high-value **14-mark problem** in KTU exams. We use **Thevenin's Theorem** to find the galvanometer current when the bridge is slightly unbalanced.

**Step 1:** Remove the galvanometer branch. Find the Thevenin equivalent voltage $E_{th}$ across the open-circuit galvanometer terminals (B and D).

The open-circuit voltage between B and D is:

$$E_{th} = V_{BD} = V_{BC} - V_{DC}$$

Using voltage divider rule on the two parallel branches:

$$V_{BC} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{Q}{P + Q}$$

$$V_{DC} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R + R_x}$$

Therefore:

$$E_{th} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \dfrac{Q}{P+Q} - \dfrac{R_x}{R + R_x} \right)$$

Combine over a common denominator:

$$E_{th} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{Q(R + R_x) - R_x(P + Q)}{(P+Q)(R + R_x)}$$

Simplify the numerator:

$$E_{th} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{QR + QR_x - R_x P - R_x Q}{(P+Q)(R + R_x)}$$

The terms $+QR_x$ and $-R_xQ$ cancel:

$$E_{th} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{QR - R_x P}{(P+Q)(R + R_x)}$$

**Step 2:** Find the Thevenin resistance $R_{th}$ looking into terminals B‚ÄďD with the source short-circuited. The arms $P$ and $Q$ appear in parallel (looking back from B), and $R$ and $R_x$ appear in parallel (looking back from D):

$$R_{th} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q}{P + Q} + \dfrac{R \cdot R_x}{R + R_x}$$

**Step 3:** The galvanometer current is then:

$$I_g = \dfrac{E_{th}}{R_g + R_{th}}$$

Substituting:

$$I_g = \dfrac{V_{ex}(QR - R_x P)}{(R_g + R_{th})(P+Q)(R + R_x)}$$

**Step 4:** The bridge sensitivity is defined as:

$$S_b = \dfrac{d I_g}{d R_x} \bigg\vert_{R_x = R \cdot \dfrac{Q}{P}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to find the *condition for maximum sensitivity*, the standard result is that all four arms should be equal ($P = Q = R = R_x = R$) and the galvanometer resistance should be matched: $R_g = R$. This is a 3-mark sub-question that comes up almost every year.

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Wheatstone vs. Kelvin Bridge

| Parameter | Wheatstone Bridge | Kelvin Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | 4 arms (single bridge) | 6 arms (double ratio) |
| Measurement range | 1 ő© to 1 Mő© | 1 őľő© to 1 ő© |
| Excitation | DC source | DC source (higher current, e.g., 10 A) |
| Lead resistance error | **Significant** (not cancelled) | **Negligible** (mathematically cancelled) |
| Sensitivity | High for medium R | Lower, but compensated by higher $V_{ex}$ |
| Standard resistance | Decade resistance box | Low-resistance standard (manganin shunt) |
| Accuracy | ¬Ī 0.1 % to ¬Ī 0.02 % | ¬Ī 0.05 % to ¬Ī 0.5 % |
| Typical application | Lab resistance measurement | Shunts, windings, contact resistance |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    ARMQ["ARM Q KNOWN RATIO 2"]:::armNode

    SRC --- NODEA

```

**Topology Description:**
- The DC source $V_{ex}$ energizes the diagonal A‚ÄďC.
- Arms $P$ and $Q$ form the left side of the diamond (ratio arms).
- Arms $R$ and $R_x$ form the right side (standard and unknown).
- The galvanometer bridges the bottom diagonal B‚ÄďD.
- At balance, $I_g = 0$ and the ratio $P/Q = R/R_x$ holds.

```mermaid
graph TB
    SRC["DC EXCITATION HIGH CURRENT"]:::sourceNode
    BATT1["TERMINAL X1 OF Rx"]:::topNode

    STD2["TERMINAL S2 OF Rs YOKE"]:::topNode

    RAT2["OUTER RATIO ARM Q"]:::armNode
    RAT3["INNER RATIO ARM p"]:::armNode
    RAT4["INNER RATIO ARM q"]:::armNode
    RX["UNKNOWN LOW RESISTANCE Rx"]:::armNode
    RS["STANDARD LOW RESISTANCE Rs"]:::armNode
    GALV["GALVANOMETER NULL DETECTOR"]:::detBox
    JUNC1["JUNCTION M"]:::juncNode
    JUNC2["JUNCTION N"]:::juncNode

    SRC --- BATT1
    SRC --- STD1
    BATT1 --- RX
    RX --- BATT2
    BATT2 --- JUNC1
    JUNC1 --- YOKE
    YOKE --- JUNC2
    JUNC2 --- STD2
    STD2 --- RS
    RS --- STD1
    BATT1 --- RAT3
    RAT3 --- JUNC1
    JUNC2 --- RAT4
    RAT4 --- BATT2
    BATT1 --- RAT1
    RAT1 --- GALV
    GALV --- RAT2
    RAT2 --- BATT2

    classDef sourceNode fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ffb700,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef topNode fill:#2c5f8d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef parNode fill:#8b0000,stroke:#ffb700,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef juncNode fill:#6a0dad,stroke:#a8e6a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef armNode fill:#2d6e3e,stroke:#a8e6a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef detBox fill:#4a4a4a,stroke:#ffb700,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

**Topology Description:**
- The current flows from the DC source through the **series combination of $R_x$ and $R_s$** (Kelvin connection: these two resistors are connected in series via the yoke).
- The **yoke resistance $r$** is the parasitic lead resistance between the inner ends of $R_x$ and $R_s$ ‚ÄĒ this is the resistance that the Wheatstone bridge could not eliminate.
- Two **parallel paths** tap the voltage drops across $R_x$ and $R_s$:
  - **Outer path:** $P$ and $Q$ (connected to the outer terminals)
  - **Inner path:** $p$ and $q$ (connected to the inner terminals, across the yoke)
- The galvanometer measures the difference between the two divider outputs.
- At balance, the **double ratio condition $P/Q = p/q$** ensures that $r$ has zero effect on the measurement.

---

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Architecture: Null-Detection Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[UNKNOWN RESISTANCE Rx]:::inputBox --> B[BRIDGE NETWORK 4 OR 6 ARMS]:::processBox
    C[DC EXCITATION Vex]:::inputBox --> B
    B --> D[OUT OF BALANCE VOLTAGE E th]:::signalBox
    D --> E[GALVANOMETER DEFLECTION theta]:::outputBox
    E --> F{NULL DETECTED}:::decisionBox
    F -- YES --> G[CALCULATE Rx FROM KNOWN RATIOS]:::resultBox
    F -- NO --> H[ADJUST STANDARD RESISTANCE R]:::adjustBox
    H --> B

    classDef inputBox fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffb700,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef processBox fill:#2d6e3e,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef signalBox fill:#8b4513,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputBox fill:#4a4a4a,stroke:#ffb700,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decisionBox fill:#6a0dad,stroke:#a8e6a3,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef resultBox fill:#006400,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef adjustBox fill:#8b0000,stroke:#a8e6a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Description:** The signal flow illustrates the iterative null-balancing process. The unknown resistance is placed in one arm; the operator adjusts the standard resistance until the galvanometer reads exactly zero. At that point, the simple ratio equation gives $R_x$.
