Questions on motor components in KTU exams are often framed from this **actuator perspective**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | $N_s$ in **RPM**, $f$ in **Hz**, $P$ = number of poles | AC Induction & Synchronous Motors | | 2 | Rotor (Actual) Speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | $N_r$ in **RPM**, $s$ = slip (fraction) | Induction Motor | | 3 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Dimensionless (per-unit) | Induction Motor | | 4 | Mechanical Power | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ | $P$ in **W**, $T$ in **N¬∑m**, $\omega$ in **rad/s** | All Rotating Machines | | 5 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | $\omega$ in **rad/s**, $N$ in **RPM** | All Rotating Machines | | 6 | Torque from Power | $T = \dfrac{P}{\omega} = \dfrac{60 \cdot P}{2 \pi N}$ | $T$ in **N¬∑m** | Motor Selection | | 7 | Back EMF (DC Motor) | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N}{60 A}$ | $E_b$ in **V**, $\Phi$ in **Wb**, $Z$ = conductors, $A$ = parallel paths | DC Machine Analysis | | 8 | Motor Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Performance Evaluation | > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU's "Basic Concepts" scope, the formulas above are **reference formulas**. Students are expected to know the **synchronous speed** and **slip** relations for induction motors and the **power-torque-speed** relationship universally. Detailed derivations of $E_b$ or torque expressions are **not** required at the introductory level. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In instrumentation and process control, motors serve as the **muscle** of the system: - **Valve Actuators:** Motors drive valve stems to regulate flow. - **Robotic Arms:** Servo/stepper motors provide precise angular positioning. - **Conveyor Belts & Pumps:** Induction motors provide bulk mechanical power. - **CNC Machines:** Stepper/servo motors move tool heads with sub-mm accuracy. - **Hard Disk Drives:** BLDC motors spin platters with extreme speed stability. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Since the KTU 2024 syllabus for PCBRT403 specifies **"Basic Concepts only required"** for this sub-topic, the derivations below are limited to **fundamental, board-exam-style** relationships that students are expected to be able to write down. Complex electromagnetic derivations are intentionally excluded. ## 3.1 Derivation: Synchronous Speed of a Rotating Magnetic Field **Given:** - A 3-phase induction motor has $P$ magnetic poles and is supplied by a balanced 3-phase AC source of frequency $f$ Hz. - The three stator windings are spatially displaced by **120¬į electrical** from each other. - The time-phase displacement between the three phase currents is also **120¬į**. **To Derive:** The expression for synchronous speed $N_s$. ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Define Electrical and Mechanical Degrees One complete cycle of AC (360¬į electrical) corresponds to **$360¬į/P$ mechanical** for a $P$-pole machine. For a 2-pole machine, electrical and mechanical degrees are equal. ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Time Period of Supply The supply has frequency $f$ Hz, so one full cycle takes $T = 1/f$ seconds. ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Magnetic Field Rotation per Electrical Cycle The three-phase currents, displaced in time by $T/3$, produce a resultant magnetic flux vector that rotates through **one pair of poles (360¬į electrical)** in **one full cycle $T$** seconds. ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Convert to Revolutions per Second In one cycle, the field rotates by **$1/P$** of a full mechanical revolution (because $P$ poles = $P/2$ pole-pairs, and one electrical cycle covers one pole-pair span). Therefore, revolutions per second $= f / (P/2) = 2f/P$. ### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Convert to Revolutions per Minute (RPM) Multiply by 60: $$\boxed{N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} \text{ RPM}}$$ **Numerical Example (KTU Board Style):** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has synchronous speed: $$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$ If the rotor runs at 1440 RPM, slip: $$s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = 0.04 = 4\%$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between Power, Torque, and Speed **Starting from Newton's second law for rotation:** $$P_{mech} = \text{Work done per unit time} = T \cdot \omega$$ where $T$ is the torque in N¬∑m and $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad/s. **Convert $\omega$ to RPM ($N$):** Since one revolution = $2\pi$ radians, and one minute = 60 seconds: $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{\pi N}{30}$$ **Substitute back:** $$P_{mech} = T \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$ **Solve for Torque:** $$T = \frac{60 \cdot P_{mech}}{2 \pi N} \text{ (N¬∑m)}$$ This relation is used universally for **motor selection** in instrumentation ‚ÄĒ for example, sizing a servo motor to drive a control valve against a known fluid pressure. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Component-to-Failure Mapping | Component | Primary Function | Common Failure Mode | Symptom | |---|---|---|---| | Stator Winding | Produce magnetic field | Insulation breakdown | Overheating, short-circuit, blown fuse | | Rotor / Armature | Carry induced / supplied current | Bar cracking (cage rotor) | Vibration, reduced torque, crawling | | Bearings | Support shaft, enable rotation | Lubricant degradation, wear | Noise, vibration, seizure | | Shaft | Transmit torque | Fatigue, bending | Misalignment, coupling failure | | Commutator | Mechanical rectification | Pitting, mica protrusion | Sparking at brushes, poor commutation | | Brushes | Transfer current to rotating part | Wear, chipping | Sparking, intermittent operation | | Cooling Fan | Dissipate heat | Broken blades, dust clogging | Thermal overload tripping | | Terminal Box | Electrical connection | Loose terminals, corrosion | Voltage drop, phase imbalance | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Cross-Sectional View of a Squirrel-Cage Induction Motor ```mermaid flowchart TB A["STATOR CORE
(Laminated Si-Steel)"] --> B["STATOR WINDING
(3-Phase Copper)"] A --> C["STATOR FRAME / YOKE
(Cast Iron / Steel)"] A --> D["END SHIELD - DE
(Bearing Housing)"] A --> E["END SHIELD - NDE
(Bearing + Fan Housing)"] F["ROTOR - SQUIRREL CAGE
(Aluminium Bars + End Rings)"] --> G["SHAFT
(Hardened Steel)"] G --> H["BEARINGS
(DE and NDE)"] G --> I["COOLING FAN
(Mounted on NDE)"] J["TERMINAL BOX
(Y / Delta Links)"] --> B K["NAMEPLATE
(Ratings & Specs)"] -.-> A L["AIR GAP
(0.5 to 3 mm)"] --> F B --> L ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **stator** (stationary parts) surrounds the **rotor** with a small mechanical **air gap**. - The **shaft** runs through the rotor and is supported by **two bearings** ‚ÄĒ one at the **Drive End (DE)** and one at the **Non-Drive End (NDE)**. - The **fan** is mounted on the **NDE** of the shaft, drawing air over the **frame ribs** for cooling. - The **terminal box** on top routes external 3-phase supply to the **stator windings**. ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Functional Architecture of a DC Motor ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FIELD["FIELD SYSTEM (Stator)"] F1["Field Pole Core"] F2["Field Winding / PM"] F3["Yoke (Return Path)"] end subgraph ARMATURE["ARMATURE (Rotor)"] A1["Armature Core
(Laminated)"] A2["Armature Winding
(In Slots)"] end subgraph COM["COMMUTATION SYSTEM"] C1["Commutator
(Copper Segments)"] C2["Brushes
(Carbon/Graphite)"] end subgraph MECH["MECHANICAL / AUXILIARY"] M1["Shaft + Keyway"] M2["Bearings + End Shields"] M3["Cooling Fan"] M4["Terminal Box"] end F2 -- "Main Flux" --> A2 A2 -- "Induced / Carried Current" --> C1 C2 -- "External DC Supply" --> C1 M1 -- "Torque Output to Load" --> Load["External Load"] M3 -- "Cools" --> ARMATURE ``` **How the blocks interact:** - The **field system** generates a stationary magnetic flux. - The **armature** carries current, and the interaction (Lorentz force) between armature current and field flux produces torque on the **shaft**. - The **commutator + brushes** ensure that the current direction in each armature conductor always produces force in the **same rotational direction** (unidirectional torque). - **Auxiliary blocks** (bearings, fan, terminals) provide structural and operational support. ## 4.3 Mermaid Component Matrix ‚ÄĒ Motor Family √ó Component Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DCM["DC MOTOR"] DCM1["Stator = Field Poles"] DCM2["Rotor = Armature + Commutator"] DCM3["Brushes Mandatory"] end subgraph IM["INDUCTION MOTOR (3-Phase AC)"] IM1["Stator = 3-Phase Winding"] IM2["Rotor = Squirrel Cage OR Wound"] IM3["No Brushes (Cage) / Slip-Rings (Wound)"] end subgraph SYN["SYNCHRONOUS MOTOR"] SYN1["Stator = 3-Phase Armature Winding"] SYN2["Rotor = DC Excited / PM Field"] SYN3["Slip-Rings + Brushes (DC Excited)"] end subgraph BLDC["BRUSHLESS DC (BLDC)"] B1["Stator = Multi-Phase Winding"] B2["Rotor = Permanent Magnets"] B3["Electronic Commutation (No Brushes)"] end subgraph ST["STEPPER MOTOR"] S1["Stator = Toothed Poles + Coils"] S2["Rotor = PM OR Variable Reluctance"] S3["Discrete Step Rotation"] end Universal["SHAFT + BEARINGS + FRAME + FAN + TERMINAL BOX"] -.-> DCM Universal -.-> IM Universal -.-> SYN Universal -.-> BLDC Universal -.-> ST ``` **Reading this matrix:** - All motors share the **universal components** (shaft, bearings, frame, fan, terminal box). - The **distinguishing elements** are the stator-rotor construction and the **commutation method** (mechanical via brushes vs. electronic via inverter). - In KTU exam questions, students are often asked: *"List the components of a DC motor that are absent in a squirrel-cage induction motor."* The answer: **Commutator and Brushes** (replaced by electronic switching in inverters or by induction in cage rotors). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024, Model] **List the major components of a DC motor and state the function of each.** **Model Answer:** The major components of a DC motor are: 1. **Yoke (Frame):** Provides mechanical support and acts as the magnetic flux return path. 2. **Field Poles & Field Windings:** Produce the main magnetic flux in which the armature rotates. 3. **Armature Core:** A laminated iron core that houses the armature winding and reduces eddy current losses. 4. **Armature Winding:** Carries current; interaction of this current with the field flux produces torque. 5. **Commutator:** A segmented copper cylinder that mechanically rectifies armature current to ensure unidirectional torque. 6. **Brushes & Brush Holders:** Sliding contacts that transfer current from the external circuit to the rotating commutator. 7. **Shaft:** Transmits the developed mechanical torque to the load. 8. **Bearings & End Shields:** Support the shaft and allow smooth rotation. 9. **Cooling Fan:** Dissipates heat generated in the windings and core. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model] **Differentiate between a squirrel-cage rotor and a wound (slip-ring) rotor of a 3-phase induction motor.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Squirrel-Cage Rotor | Wound (Slip-Ring) Rotor | |---|---|---| | Construction | Cast aluminium bars in slots, shorted by end rings | 3-phase insulated winding, star-connected | | External resistance | Cannot be added | Can be added via slip rings | | Brushes | Not required | Required (with slip rings) | | Starting torque | Moderate (~1.5√ó full load) | High (controllable via external resistance) | | Maintenance | Very low (rugged) | Higher (brushes & slip rings need upkeep) | | Cost | Low | High | | Application | Pumps, fans, conveyors | Cranes, lifts, high-inertia loads | --- ## PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Option Set 1 **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper, May 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **constructional features and functions of the major components of a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **working principle of a 3-phase induction motor** with reference to the **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** and derive the expression for **synchronous speed**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Constructional Features and Components of a 3-Phase Squirrel-Cage Induction Motor:** 1. **Stator Core:** Built from **thin laminated silicon steel sheets** (0.35‚Äď0.5 mm thickness) stacked and clamped together. The laminations are insulated from each other to reduce **eddy current losses**. Slots are punched on the inner periphery to hold the stator winding. 2. **Stator Winding:** A **3-phase winding** made of insulated copper conductors distributed in the stator slots. The three phases are spatially displaced by **120¬į electrical**. When connected to a balanced 3-phase AC supply, currents displaced in time by 120¬į flow through them. 3. **Stator Frame (Yoke):** A robust cast-iron or fabricated steel structure that: - Holds the stator core tightly. - Provides a low-reluctance magnetic flux return path. - Carries the mechanical mounting feet. 4. **Rotor (Squirrel Cage):** A laminated cylindrical iron core with **conducting bars** (aluminium or copper) placed in semi-closed slots. The bars are short-circuited at both ends by **end rings**, forming a closed electrical cage. There is **no insulation** between the bars and the core (unlike stator). 5. **Shaft:** A high-grade steel rod passing through the rotor, transmitting mechanical torque to the load. The shaft is **keyed** to the rotor core. 6. **Bearings:** Two **ball bearings** (or roller for large motors) ‚ÄĒ one at the **Drive End (DE)** and one at the **Non-Drive End (NDE)** ‚ÄĒ mounted in the **end shields**. 7. **End Shields:** Cast covers supporting the bearings and providing enclosure. 8. **Cooling Fan:** Mounted on the NDE of the shaft, blows air over the ribbed outer surface of the frame (TEFC construction). 9. **Terminal Box:** On the top of the frame, contains **six terminal studs** (U1, V1, W1, U2, V2, W2) with link plates to configure the winding in **Star (Y)** or **Delta (őĒ)**. 10. **Air Gap:** A uniform mechanical gap of **0.5 to 3 mm** between stator and rotor ‚ÄĒ critical for magnetic coupling and motor performance. *(Refer to the Mermaid cross-sectional diagram in SECTION 4 for visual support.)* **(b) Working Principle and Synchronous Speed Derivation:** When a **3-phase balanced AC supply** is given to the stator windings (displaced 120¬į in space), three alternating currents displaced 120¬į in time flow. Each phase current produces its own **pulsating magnetic flux**. The **vector sum** of these three pulsating fluxes is a **single resultant flux of constant magnitude** that **rotates** at a constant speed. This is the **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)**. The RMF rotates at the **synchronous speed** $N_s$, given by: $$\boxed{N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \text{ RPM}}$$ **Derivation (Step-by-Step):** - The RMF covers **360¬į electrical** (one pole-pair) in **one supply cycle** $T = 1/f$ seconds. - For a $P$-pole machine, the number of pole-pairs is $P/2$. - Hence, one mechanical revolution requires $P/2$ electrical cycles, i.e., time $= (P/2) \cdot (1/f)$ seconds. - Revolutions per second $= 2f/P$. - Converting to RPM: multiply by 60 ‚Üí $N_s = 120f/P$. The RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing an EMF (Faraday's law). Since the rotor bars form a closed circuit, a current flows. By **Lenz's law**, the rotor current opposes the relative motion of the RMF, forcing the rotor to **chase the RMF** in the same direction. The rotor can never reach $N_s$ (else no relative motion = no induced EMF = no torque), so it always runs at a slightly lower speed ‚ÄĒ the slip is defined as: $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ This is the **induction motor principle**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Option Set 2 (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper, Dec 2023]** **(a)** Draw the **block diagram of a DC motor** and explain the function of the **field system, armature, commutator, and brushes**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare the **constructional features of a DC motor, a 3-phase induction motor, and a stepper motor** in a tabular form. Also state **two instrumentation applications** of each. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) DC Motor ‚ÄĒ Block Diagram and Function of Key Components:** *(Refer to the Functional Architecture Mermaid block in SECTION 4.2 for the visual.)* **1. Field System (Stator):** The field system consists of **pole cores** and **field windings** mounted on the inner periphery of the **yoke**. When DC excitation is supplied, it produces a **stationary magnetic flux** $\Phi$ that passes through the armature. In PMDC motors, **permanent magnets** replace field windings. **2. Armature (Rotor):** The armature is the rotating part consisting of a **laminated cylindrical iron core** with slots. The **armature winding** (closed, multi-coil) is placed in these slots. Current is fed into the winding through the brushes and commutator. The force on each current-carrying conductor in the field is $F = BIL$ (**Lorentz force**), producing torque $T = F \times r$ on the shaft. **3. Commutator:** A **cylindrical assembly of copper segments** (insulated from each other by mica) mounted on the shaft. It acts as a **mechanical reversing switch** ‚ÄĒ it reverses the current direction in each armature coil as it passes from one pole to the other, ensuring that the torque on every conductor is always in the **same rotational sense**, producing **unidirectional rotation**. **4. Brushes:** **Carbon/graphite blocks** held in brush holders against the commutator by spring tension. They provide a **sliding electrical contact** between the stationary external DC supply circuit and the rotating armature winding. Brushes gradually wear out and are a **scheduled maintenance item**. **(b) Comparative Table and Applications:** | Feature | DC Motor | 3-Phase Induction Motor | Stepper Motor | |---|---|---|---| | Supply | DC | 3-Phase AC | DC pulses | | Rotor Type | Armature with commutator | Squirrel-cage or wound | PM or Variable Reluctance (VR) | | Commutation | Mechanical (brush-commutator) | Not required (induction) | Electronic (driver circuit) | | Speed Control | Easy (V or flux control) | Complex (V/f inverter) | Discrete steps, open-loop | | Position Control | Poor (without feedback) | Not suitable | Excellent (open-loop) | | Cost | Moderate | Low | Moderate-High | | Maintenance | High (brushes) | Very low | Low | **Instrumentation Applications (Two Each):** - **DC Motor:** Small DC motors in chart recorders, flow-meter displays, portable instruments. - **3-Phase Induction Motor:** Driving large pumps, blowers, and conveyors in process plants. - **Stepper Motor:** Precision valve positioning, X-Y plotters, robotic joint control, laboratory automation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Mark-Loss Areas:** > 1. **Conflating "armature" and "rotor":** In a DC motor, the armature IS the rotor. In an AC induction motor, the rotor is NOT called an armature (it has no commutator). Use the correct term for the machine type. > 2. **Forgetting the air gap:** Many students describe the components but fail to mention the **air gap** between stator and rotor, which is a critical constructional feature. > 3. **Commutator function confusion:** The commutator does NOT "convert AC to DC" ‚ÄĒ it **reverses current in the armature coils** to maintain unidirectional torque. Stating "it rectifies" is vague and loses marks. > 4. **Brushes in induction motors:** Students wrongly state that "all motors have brushes." Squirrel-cage induction motors and BLDC motors do **not** have brushes. Read the question carefully. > 5. **Unit mistakes in $N_s$ formula:** Always write the units explicitly ‚ÄĒ $N_s$ in **RPM**, $f$ in **Hz**, $P$ in number of poles (not pole-pairs). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ‚ÄĒ Motor Components:** - **Electric motor** = electromechanical converter (electrical ‚Üí mechanical energy). - **Two main families:** DC motors and AC motors. - **Universal components in every motor:** Stator, Rotor, Shaft, Bearings, Frame/Yoke, End shields, Cooling fan, Terminal box. - **DC motor ‚ÄĒ distinguishing components:** Commutator and Brushes (mechanical commutation). - **3-Phase Induction motor ‚ÄĒ distinguishing components:** Either **squirrel-cage** (no brushes) or **wound rotor with slip rings**. - **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ RPM ‚ÄĒ the speed of the rotating magnetic field. - **Slip:** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ ‚ÄĒ always present in induction motors; zero in synchronous motors. - **Mechanical power relation:** $P = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$. - **Stator frame / Yoke** = mechanical support + magnetic flux return path. - **Laminated cores** (stator and rotor) are used to **reduce eddy current losses**. - **Air gap** between stator and rotor is small (0.5‚Äď3 mm) ‚ÄĒ critical for magnetic coupling and torque production. - **Bearings** support the shaft and handle radial/axial loads; **DE** and **NDE** denote drive-end and non-drive-end bearings. - **Cooling fan** is mounted on the **NDE** of the shaft in TEFC (Totally Enclosed Fan Cooled) motors. - **Nameplate** carries the **ratings**: voltage, current, power, speed, frequency, IP class, insulation class, duty. - **Stepper motor** = open-loop digital positioning device ‚ÄĒ key actuator in instrumentation and lab automation. - **Servo motor** = closed-loop high-performance motor used in CNC, robotics, and precision actuators. - **Remember:** Slip-ring rotor ‚Üí has brushes; Squirrel-cage rotor ‚Üí no brushes. - **KTU focus:** Understand **what each component does**, not how to design it. The course outcome is *identification and functional understanding*, not electromagnetic design.

topics.title: DC Motors - DC Shunt Motor, Separately Excited and Self Excited, DC Series Motor, DC Compound Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DC Motors - DC Shunt Motor, Separately Excited and Self Excited, DC Series Motor, DC Compound Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **DC Motor** is a rotary electromechanical energy conversion device that converts direct-current electrical energy into mechanical energy (rotational) based on the principle of **Lorentz Force** interaction between current-carrying conductors placed in a magnetic field. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme, DC motors are broadly classified into two excitation categories:

**A. Separately Excited DC Motor** ‚ÄĒ A motor in which the field winding (producing the main working flux) is energized from an **independent external DC source**, fully isolated from the armature supply circuit.

**B. Self-Excited DC Motor** ‚ÄĒ A motor in which the field winding receives its excitation current either directly from the armature terminals or through a configuration tied to the same supply. Self-excited motors are further subdivided into:
- **DC Shunt Motor** (field winding connected in **parallel** with the armature)
- **DC Series Motor** (field winding connected in **series** with the armature)
- **DC Compound Motor** (combination of both series and shunt field windings on the same pole core)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** "A DC motor is a self-contained electromechanical actuator in which the back EMF $E_b$ is generated in opposition to the applied voltage, governed by the equation $E_b = k\Phi\omega$, where $k$ is the machine construction constant."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚ÄĒ The Bicycle Wheel Generator Concept**
> Imagine pushing a child on a swing. The harder you push (more current into the armature), the faster the swing moves (higher speed). Now, imagine placing strong magnets around the swing's path ‚ÄĒ the swing will move slower because the magnetic "braking" effect (back EMF) opposes the push. This **opposition** is exactly what **back EMF ($E_b$)** does inside a DC motor.
> 
> In a **shunt motor**, the magnets (field) are independently strong and constant, so speed is steady ‚ÄĒ like a fan that runs at the same RPM regardless of breeze. In a **series motor**, the magnets get stronger the harder you push, making it incredibly powerful at startup but variable in speed ‚ÄĒ like a vehicle's traction motor. In a **compound motor**, you get the best of both ‚ÄĒ a controlled blend of constant and variable field behavior.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Magnetic flux ($\Phi$):** measured in **Weber (Wb)**
- **Back EMF ($E_b$):** measured in **Volts (V)**
- **Angular velocity ($\omega$):** measured in **rad/s** (or N in RPM)
- **Machine constant ($k$):** dependent on construction ‚ÄĒ units of **V¬∑s/rad**
- **Armature resistance ($R_a$):** measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**, typically **0.5 ő© to 1.5 ő©** for industrial DC motors
- **Field winding resistance ($R_{sh}$ or $R_{se}$):** measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)** ‚ÄĒ shunt field: **50‚Äď300 ő©**, series field: **very low (< 1 ő©)**
- **Number of poles ($P$):** typically **2, 4, or 6** in standard DC machines
- **Number of conductors ($Z$):** a function of machine rating
- **Number of parallel paths ($A$):** equals **$P$** for wave winding, **$2P$** for lap winding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Emphasis:** The back EMF $E_b$ is *always* less than the applied voltage $V$ in motoring mode, and the difference $V - E_b$ drops across the armature resistance, giving rise to the armature current $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

A DC motor operates on the principle that when a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a force given by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**.

### 2.2 Generic Equations Applicable to ALL DC Motors

The behavior of every DC motor ‚ÄĒ whether shunt, series, or compound ‚ÄĒ is governed by **three foundational equations** that students must memorize for KTU examinations.

#### 2.2.1 Back EMF Equation (EMF Induced in the Rotating Armature)

When the armature rotates at speed $N$ (in RPM) in a magnetic field of flux $\Phi$ per pole:

$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$$

In a more compact form, where $k = \frac{PZ}{60A}$:

$$E_b = k \, \Phi \, N$$

Using angular velocity $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z \omega}{2\pi A} = K_a \, \Phi \, \omega$$

where $K_a$ is the **armature constant**.

#### 2.2.2 Voltage (KVL) Equation of the Armature Circuit

By applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** to the armature loop:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a + V_{brushes}$$

For most KTU problems, brush drop is neglected:

$$\boxed{V = E_b + I_a R_a}$$

#### 2.2.3 Torque Equation

The torque developed by the armature is:

$$T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = K_a \, \Phi \, I_a$$

The shaft (useful) torque accounts for mechanical losses (friction, windage):

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{mech\,losses} = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\omega}$$

#### 2.2.4 Power Equation

Mechanical power developed in the armature:

$$P_{dev} = E_b \, I_a = T_a \, \omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These four equations form the analytical backbone of every DC motor problem in KTU ESE papers. From here onward, we only modify them to suit the field excitation type.

### 2.3 Type-Wise Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Separately Excited DC Motor

- Field current $I_f$ is independent of armature current.
- $I_L = I_a$ (line current equals armature current).
- Flux $\Phi$ is a function of $I_f$ only, controlled by an external **field rheostat**.
- Excellent **speed control** over a wide range using **armature voltage control** or **field weakening**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinguishing Feature:** In a separately excited motor, the field winding is *not* electrically connected to the armature circuit. This allows independent control of torque (via $I_a$) and flux (via $I_f$).

#### 2.3.2 DC Shunt Motor (Self-Excited)

- Shunt field winding is connected in **parallel** with the armature.
- Field current is small: $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ (approximately constant if supply voltage is constant).
- Line current: $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$
- Flux $\Phi$ is **practically constant** (since $I_{sh}$ is constant).
- **Speed regulation** is **good (3‚Äď5%)** ‚ÄĒ speed drops only slightly with load.
- Used in: **lathes, fans, pumps, machine tools** requiring constant speed.

#### 2.3.3 DC Series Motor (Self-Excited)

- Series field winding is connected in **series** with the armature.
- Line current: $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$ (the same current flows through both).
- Before magnetic saturation: $\Phi \propto I_a$ ‚áí $\Phi = k_1 I_a$
- Torque: $T \propto \Phi \, I_a \propto I_a^2$ (high starting torque)
- Speed: $N \propto 1/I_a$ approximately (varies inversely with load)
- **Cannot be started at no-load** ‚ÄĒ speed becomes dangerously high.
- Used in: **traction systems, electric locomotives, cranes, hoists, elevators**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** Series motors must NEVER be started without mechanical load. The absence of load current causes flux to drop drastically, leading to infinite theoretical speed and mechanical destruction of the armature.

#### 2.3.4 DC Compound Motor (Self-Excited)

- Contains **both** series and shunt field windings.
- **Cumulative compound:** Series flux *aids* shunt flux ‚ÄĒ $\Phi_{net} = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$
- **Differential compound:** Series flux *opposes* shunt flux ‚ÄĒ $\Phi_{net} = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$
- Combines high starting torque (from series field) with good speed regulation (from shunt field).
- Used in: **presses, shears, elevators, rolling mills**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Shunt Motor** | **Series Motor** | **Compound Motor** | **Separately Excited** |
|:-----:|:--------------|:----------------|:------------------|:--------------------|:------------------------|
| 1 | Field connection | Parallel to armature | Series with armature | Both | Independent source |
| 2 | Line current | $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$ | $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$ | $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$ | $I_L = I_a$ |
| 3 | Field current | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | $I_{se} = I_a$ | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | $I_f = V_f / R_f$ |
| 4 | Back EMF | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ |
| 5 | Speed (general) | $N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$ | $N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k\Phi}$ | $N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k\Phi_{net}}$ | $N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$ |
| 6 | Torque | $T = k\Phi I_a$ | $T = k\Phi I_a \propto I_a^2$ | $T = k\Phi_{net} I_a$ | $T = k\Phi I_a$ |
| 7 | Flux nature | $\Phi \approx \text{constant}$ | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (linear region) | $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} \pm \Phi_{se}$ | $\Phi$ controlled externally |
| 8 | Speed regulation | Good (3‚Äď5%) | Poor (very high) | Moderate (5‚Äď10%) | Excellent |
| 9 | Starting torque | Moderate | Very high | High (cumulative) | Moderate‚ÄďHigh |
| 10 | No-load safety | Safe | Dangerous | Safe | Safe |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC shunt motors** drive **DC analog panel meters, chart recorders, X-Y plotters, and tape drives** in instrumentation systems where constant RPM is critical.
- **DC series motors** are used in **portable electric tools, hair dryers, vacuum cleaners, and toy trains** where high starting torque is essential.
- **DC compound motors** are used in **industrial actuators and printing press drives** where both good regulation and high torque are needed.
- **Separately excited motors** are the workhorses of **servo systems, robotics, CNC machines, and electric vehicle drives** where precise decoupled control of torque and flux is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industrial Context:** In modern instrumentation labs, separately excited DC motors are interfaced with **PID controllers** and **DACs** to study **closed-loop speed control**, a direct application of these foundational equations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Back EMF Equation $E_b = \frac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$

This is the **most important derivation** asked in KTU 2024 ESE for DC machines.

**Step 1:** Consider a single conductor rotating in a magnetic field.

The average EMF induced in one conductor when it moves through flux $\Phi$ (Webers) is:

$$e_{avg} = \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \frac{\Phi}{T_c}$$

where $T_c$ is the **time period** (in seconds) for one conductor to traverse one magnetic pole.

**Step 2:** Relate time period $T_c$ to rotational speed.

When the armature completes **one full revolution**, every conductor cuts flux under **$P$ poles**. Hence, the time to cut flux under one pole is:

$$T_c = \frac{60}{N \cdot P}$$

where $N$ is the speed in revolutions per minute (RPM).

**Step 3:** Substitute $T_c$ into the EMF expression.

$$e_{avg} = \frac{\Phi}{T_c} = \frac{\Phi}{\left(\frac{60}{N \cdot P}\right)} = \frac{\Phi \cdot N \cdot P}{60}$$

**Step 4:** Account for all conductors.

There are $Z$ total conductors. The number of conductors **in series per parallel path** is $Z/A$. Therefore, the total induced EMF is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \text{EMF per parallel path} \times \text{Number of conductors in series} \\
&= e_{avg} \times \frac{Z}{A} \\
&= \frac{\Phi \cdot N \cdot P}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Final simplified form.

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The factor $P/60$ converts the rotational motion (P poles per revolution, 60 seconds per minute) into a per-second quantity, $Z/A$ is the number of conductors in series in any one parallel path, and $\Phi$ is the flux cut per pole.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation $T = \frac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$

**Step 1:** Force on a single current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field:

$$F = B \, l \, I$$

where $B$ is the flux density, $l$ is the length of the conductor, and $I$ is the current.

**Step 2:** Express flux density in terms of flux and pole area:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{A_{pole}}$$

Hence the force per conductor becomes:

$$F = \frac{\Phi \, l \, I}{A_{pole}}$$

**Step 3:** Torque per conductor (force √ó radius):

$$\tau = F \times r = \frac{\Phi \, l \, I \, r}{A_{pole}}$$

For a cylindrical armature: $A_{pole} \times \text{effective radius factor}$ simplifies such that:

$$F = \frac{\Phi \, I}{\text{(machine geometry factor)}}$$

A more rigorous approach uses the **magnetic field energy method**:

The total torque is the rate of change of co-energy with respect to angular position. For a linear magnetic system:

$$T = \frac{dW_{mech}}{d\theta}$$

For a DC motor with $Z$ conductors carrying current $I_a$ divided into $A$ parallel paths:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi \, A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The factor $2\pi$ in the denominator arises from the conversion of angular displacement (radians) to one full revolution. The factor $P$ accounts for the number of poles producing torque simultaneously.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Speed Equation for DC Shunt Motor

**Step 1:** Start with the back EMF equation:

$$E_b = \frac{P\Phi Z N}{60A}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for speed $N$:

$$N = \frac{60 A \cdot E_b}{P \Phi Z}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $E_b = V - I_a R_a$:

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{60 A}{P \Phi Z} \cdot (V - I_a R_a) \\
N &= \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi} \quad \text{where} \quad k = \frac{PZ}{60A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Since $\Phi$ is approximately constant in a shunt motor, the no-load speed is:

$$N_0 = \frac{V}{k\Phi}$$

**Step 5:** Speed regulation is defined as:

$$\text{Speed Regulation (\%)} = \frac{N_0 - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100$$

where $N_{FL}$ is the full-load speed.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Speed Equation for DC Series Motor (Below Saturation)

**Step 1:** In the linear (unsaturated) region:

$$\Phi = K_1 I_a \quad \text{(proportional to armature current)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the speed equation:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a - I_a R_{se}}{k \Phi} = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k K_1 I_a}$$

**Step 3:** Approximating $I_a R_a$ as small compared to $V$ for moderate loads:

$$N \approx \frac{V}{k K_1 I_a} \propto \frac{1}{I_a}$$

**Step 4:** Since torque $T \propto I_a^2$, we have $I_a \propto \sqrt{T}$, therefore:

$$\boxed{N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Result:** This inverse-square-root relationship is the foundation of all series motor problems involving speed estimation under varying mechanical loads.

---

### 3.5 Solved Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 250 V, 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt motor has 800 conductors. The armature and field resistances are 0.2 ő© and 250 ő© respectively. The flux per pole is 25 mWb. Calculate:
1. The no-load speed (assuming $I_a$ at no-load = 2 A).
2. The full-load speed if the full-load armature current is 50 A.
3. The developed torque at full load.

**Given:**
- $V = 250$ V
- $P = 4$
- $Z = 800$
- $A = 2$ (wave wound, $A = 2$)
- $R_a = 0.2$ ő©
- $R_{sh} = 250$ ő©
- $\Phi = 25$ mWb $= 25 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb
- $I_{a,NL} = 2$ A
- $I_{a,FL} = 50$ A

**Step 1: Calculate machine constant $k$:**

$$k = \frac{PZ}{60A} = \frac{4 \times 800}{60 \times 2} = \frac{3200}{120} = 26.67$$

**Step 2: No-load speed:**

$$E_{b,NL} = V - I_{a,NL} R_a = 250 - (2)(0.2) = 249.6 \text{ V}$$

$$N_{NL} = \frac{E_{b,NL}}{k \Phi} = \frac{249.6}{26.67 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{249.6}{0.6667} \approx 374.4 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3: Full-load speed:**

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 250 - (50)(0.2) = 250 - 10 = 240 \text{ V}$$

$$N_{FL} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{k \Phi} = \frac{240}{0.6667} \approx 360 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4: Developed torque at full load:**

$$T_{dev} = k \Phi I_a = 26.67 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 = 33.33 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

**Step 5: Speed regulation:**

$$\text{Reg. (\%)} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{374.4 - 360}{360} \times 100 = 4\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The typical speed regulation of a DC shunt motor is **3‚Äď5%**, which confirms our calculated result of 4% as a realistic KTU answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Classification of DC Motors

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DCMotorsMain]:::main --> B[SeparatelyExcited]
    A --> C[SelfExcited]
    C --> D[DCShuntMotor]
    C --> E[DCSeriesMotor]
    C --> F[DCCompoundMotor]
    F --> G[CumulativeCompound]
    F --> H[DifferentialCompound]
    B --> I[IndependentFieldSource]
    D --> J[FieldParallelToArmature]
    E --> K[FieldSeriesWithArmature]
    G --> L[SeriesFluxAidsShuntFlux]
    H --> M[SeriesFluxOpposesShuntFlux]
    classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Functional Architecture of a Separately Excited DC Motor

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1[DCPowerSupplyV]:::supply --> FA1[FieldRheostat]
    FA1 --> FW1[FieldWinding]
    V2[DCPowerSupplyV]:::supply --> AR1[ArmatureCircuit]
    AR1 --> AC1[ArmatureWinding]
    AC1 --> BR1[BrushCommutatorAssembly]
    BR1 --> SH1[MechanicalShaftOutput]
    SH1 --> LD1[MechanicalLoad]
    FW1 -.MagneticCoupling.-> AC1
    AC1 --> BE1[BackEMFGenerator]
    BE1 --> ARM1[ArmatureReaction]
    ARM1 --> SP1[SpeedSensor]
    SP1 --> CT1[Tachogenerator]
    classDef supply fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Torque‚ÄďSpeed Characteristics of DC Motors

```mermaid
graph TD
    CHAR[CharacteristicsComparison]:::header --> CS1[ShuntMotor]
    CHAR --> CS2[SeriesMotor]
    CHAR --> CS3[CompoundMotor]
    CS1 --> CS1A[TConstantModerateTorque]
    CS1 --> CS1B[NConstantVeryGoodRegulation]
    CS2 --> CS2A[THighStartingTorqueSquaresI]
    CS2 --> CS2B[NDecreasesWithLoadInversely]
    CS2 --> CS2C[CannotStartNoLoad]
    CS3 --> CS3A[CumulativeHighTorqueGoodRegulation]
    CS3 --> CS3B[DifferentialSteadySpeedLowTorque]
    classDef header fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Circuit Topology of Each Motor

```mermaid
graph TD
    SCM[ShuntMotorCircuit]:::topo --> SA1[Supply+]
    SA1 --> SB1[ShuntFieldRsh]
    SA1 --> SC1[ArmatureRa]
    SB1 --> SD1[CommonReturn]
    SC1 --> SD1
    SERC[SeriesMotorCircuit]:::topo --> SE1[Supply+]
    SE1 --> SF1[SeriesFieldRse]
    SF1 --> SG1[ArmatureRa]
    SG1 --> SH1[CommonReturn]
    COMPC[CompoundMotorCircuit]:::topo --> SI1[Supply+]
    SI1 --> SJ1[ShuntFieldRsh]
    SI1 --> SK1[SeriesFieldRse]
    SK1 --> SL1[ArmatureRa]
    SJ1 --> SM1[CommonReturn]
    SL1 --> SM1
    classDef topo fill:#4a148c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.5 ASCII Schematic ‚ÄĒ Power Flow in a DC Shunt Motor

```
        +-------------+         +-------------+
        |  DC Supply  |         |  Mechanical |
   V --> |   V = 250V  |  ---->  |   Shaft     |
        |  (P, N)     |  P_mech |  (T, ŌČ)     |
        +-------------+         +-------------+
              |                        ^
              | I_L                    | Output
              v                        | Power
        +-----------+                  |
        |   I_sh =  |                  |
        |  V/R_sh   |                  |
        |  (~1 A)   |                  |
        +-----------+                  |
              |                        |
        +-----+-----+                  |
        |   Shunt   |                  |
        |   Field   |                  |
        |  Winding  |                  |
        +-----------+                  |
              ||                       |
              ||  Magnetic Coupling    |
              ||  (Flux Phi)           |
              vv                       |
        +-----------+  I_a    +-------+-------+
        | Armature  | ------> |  Back EMF     |
        | Winding   |         |  E_b (V)      |
        | (R_a)     |         +---------------+
        +-----------+                  ^
              |                        |
              +------------------------+
              |    KVL: V = E_b + I_a R_a
              +---------> Load
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram above shows the energy flow: electrical input (armature + field) is converted to magnetic field, which produces torque that rotates the shaft. Back EMF is the inherent opposition created by Lenz's law.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024]**
**Define back EMF in a DC motor. Why is it considered a "self-regulating" quantity?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The EMF induced in the armature conductors of a DC motor, due to its rotation in the magnetic field, is called **back EMF** (counter EMF or generated EMF). Mathematically:

$$E_b = \frac{P\Phi NZ}{60A} = k\Phi N$$

**Self-Regulating Nature:** Back EMF acts as a built-in feedback that automatically regulates the armature current and hence the speed.

**Working Principle:**
1. When supply voltage $V$ is applied, the armature starts rotating.
2. As $N$ increases, $E_b$ increases (since $E_b \propto N$).
3. From $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$, the armature current decreases.
4. Torque $T \propto I_a$ decreases.
5. Equilibrium is reached when motor torque equals load torque.

> **Self-regulation:** If the load increases, the motor slows down, $E_b$ drops, $I_a$ rises, torque rises ‚ÄĒ restoring equilibrium. This is **Lenz's Law in action**.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]**
**Compare the starting torque of DC shunt, series, and compound motors. Which is highest and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Motor Type** | **Starting Torque** | **Reason** |
|:----------------|:--------------------|:-----------|
| DC Shunt | Low to Moderate (1.5√ó to 2√ó FL torque) | Flux $\Phi$ is constant; $I_a$ is limited by starting resistor |
| DC Series | **Very High (5√ó to 10√ó FL torque)** | $\Phi \propto I_a$ AND $T \propto I_a$, so $T \propto I_a^2$ at low loads |
| DC Compound (Cumulative) | High (3√ó to 4√ó FL torque) | Combined effect of series and shunt fields |

**Highest Starting Torque:** DC **Series Motor**, because both flux and armature current increase together during startup, giving a **quadratic relationship** $T_{start} \propto I_{a,start}^2$.

---

### 5.2 PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation and torque equation of a DC motor. State the relationship between them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V, 4-pole DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.3 ő© and shunt field resistance of 110 ő©. The armature has 480 conductors wave-wound. The flux per pole is 30 mWb. If the motor takes a line current of 40 A at full load, calculate: **(7 Marks)**
- (i) The speed at full load
- (ii) The developed torque
- (iii) The armature current at the instant of starting with full voltage (no starter)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**EMF Equation Derivation:**

Let $P$ = number of poles, $\Phi$ = flux per pole in Wb, $Z$ = total armature conductors, $N$ = speed in RPM, $A$ = parallel paths.

For a conductor cutting flux $\Phi$ per pole at speed $N$ RPM, the flux linkages per second equal:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Flux cut per conductor per sec} &= \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N}{60} \\
\text{EMF per conductor} &= \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \\
\text{EMF per parallel path (E_b)} &= \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}}$$

**Torque Equation Derivation:**

Consider total mechanical power developed in the armature, $P_{dev} = T \cdot \omega$ (where $\omega$ in rad/s).

Equating electrical power converted to mechanical power:

$$E_b \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

Substituting $E_b$:

$$\frac{P\Phi NZ}{60A} \cdot I_a = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

Solving for $T$:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}}$$

**Relationship between EMF and Torque:**

$$\frac{E_b}{T} = \frac{P\Phi NZ}{60A} \cdot \frac{2\pi A}{P\Phi Z I_a} = \frac{2\pi N}{60 I_a} = \frac{\omega}{I_a}$$

This shows that the ratio $E_b / T = \omega / I_a$ is a fundamental machine relationship.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $V = 220$ V, $P = 4$, $Z = 480$, $A = 2$ (wave), $R_a = 0.3$ ő©, $R_{sh} = 110$ ő©, $\Phi = 30$ mWb $= 30 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb, $I_L = 40$ A.

**Step 1: Field current**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Armature current at full load**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 40 - 2 = 38 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Back EMF at full load**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (38)(0.3) = 220 - 11.4 = 208.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Speed at full load**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P\Phi Z} = \frac{208.6 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 480} = \frac{25032}{57.6} \approx 434.6 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5: Developed torque**

$$T = \frac{P\Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{4 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 480 \times 38}{2\pi \times 2}$$

$$T = \frac{4 \times 0.03 \times 480 \times 38}{4\pi} = \frac{2188.8}{12.566} \approx 174.2 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

**Step 6: Starting armature current (no starter)**

At starting, $N = 0$, so $E_b = 0$:

$$I_{a,start} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{220}{0.3} = 733.3 \text{ A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is 19 times the full-load current!** This justifies the mandatory use of a **starter** in DC shunt motors. The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to comment on this dangerous condition and recommend a 3-point or 4-point starter.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023] ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With neat circuit diagrams, explain the construction and working of a **DC series motor** and a **DC compound motor**. Discuss the torque-speed characteristics of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 250 V DC series motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 ő© and a series field resistance of 0.3 ő©. When running at 1000 RPM, the motor draws 50 A from the supply. Assuming the magnetic circuit is unsaturated, calculate: **(7 Marks)**
- (i) The flux at this load
- (ii) The speed when the armature current is 25 A
- (iii) The new speed if the load torque is doubled from its initial value

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**DC Series Motor:**

The series field winding is connected in **series** with the armature. Since the same current flows through both:

$$I_L = I_a = I_{se}$$

**Construction:** The series field winding consists of a few turns of **thick wire** (low resistance) wound on each pole.

**Working:** When current flows, both armature and series field are magnetized. The interaction produces torque.

**Characteristics (Below Saturation, Linear Region):**
- $\Phi \propto I_a$
- $T \propto \Phi I_a \propto I_a^2$ (parabolic)
- $N \propto 1/I_a$ (inverse)

**DC Compound Motor:**

Has **two** field windings on each pole:
1. **Shunt field** (many turns, thin wire) connected across supply
2. **Series field** (few turns, thick wire) in series with armature

**Two Types:**
- **Cumulative:** Series flux *aids* shunt flux ‚Üí high starting torque, good regulation
- **Differential:** Series flux *opposes* shunt flux ‚Üí nearly constant speed, low starting torque

**Torque-Speed Characteristics:**
- **Cumulative compound:** Speed decreases as load increases (similar to shunt, but steeper).
- **Differential compound:** Speed remains nearly constant regardless of load.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $V = 250$ V, $R_a = 0.5$ ő©, $R_{se} = 0.3$ ő©, $N_1 = 1000$ RPM at $I_{a1} = 50$ A.

**Step 1: Back EMF at initial condition**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_{a1}(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 50(0.5 + 0.3) = 250 - 40 = 210 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Flux at initial load (in terms of unknown $k$)**

$$E_{b1} = k\Phi_1 N_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k\Phi_1 = \frac{E_{b1}}{N_1} = \frac{210}{1000} = 0.21$$

**Step 3: Speed at $I_{a2} = 25$ A**

Assuming unsaturated region: $\Phi_2 / \Phi_1 = I_{a2} / I_{a1} = 25/50 = 0.5$

$$E_{b2} = V - I_{a2}(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 25(0.8) = 250 - 20 = 230 \text{ V}$$

$$k\Phi_2 = 0.5 \times k\Phi_1 = 0.5 \times 0.21 = 0.105$$

$$N_2 = \frac{E_{b2}}{k\Phi_2} = \frac{230}{0.105} \approx 2190 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4: New speed when load torque is doubled**

Initial torque: $T_1 \propto \Phi_1 I_{a1} = (1)(50) = 50$ (relative units)
Doubled torque: $T_2 = 100$, and since $T \propto I_a^2$ in linear region:

$$I_{a3} = I_{a1} \sqrt{2} = 50\sqrt{2} \approx 70.71 \text{ A}$$

$$\Phi_3 = \Phi_1 \sqrt{2} \propto 70.71$$

$$E_{b3} = V - I_{a3}(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 70.71(0.8) = 250 - 56.57 = 193.43 \text{ V}$$

$$k\Phi_3 = 0.21 \times \sqrt{2} = 0.297$$

$$N_3 = \frac{E_{b3}}{k\Phi_3} = \frac{193.43}{0.297} \approx 651.3 \text{ RPM}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to include **series field resistance** $R_{se}$ when computing back EMF in series motors. This loses 2‚Äď3 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Assuming flux is constant in a **series motor** below saturation ‚ÄĒ flux is **directly proportional to armature current**, not constant.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** For **compound motors**, students often forget to specify whether the question refers to **cumulative or differential** compounding. Always clarify.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Wrong parallel path $A$ ‚ÄĒ remember $A = 2$ for wave winding, $A = P$ for lap winding. A common source of marks deduction.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Not mentioning that series motors cannot start on no-load. A **definitive must-state** in any KTU answer on series motors.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ DC Motors (KTU 2024 Module 2)**

- ‚úÖ A DC motor converts electrical energy to mechanical energy using **Lorentz Force** and **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**.
- ‚úÖ The three master equations are: **$V = E_b + I_a R_a$**, **$E_b = k\Phi N$**, **$T = k\Phi I_a$**.
- ‚úÖ **Back EMF $E_b$** is the self-induced voltage opposing the applied voltage; it is **directly proportional to speed and flux**.
- ‚úÖ **Separately excited motors** have an independent field source; flux is **externally controlled** ‚ÄĒ ideal for servo applications.
- ‚úÖ **DC Shunt Motor**: Field in parallel; flux **constant**; speed **fairly constant**; used in **lathes, fans, pumps**.
- ‚úÖ **DC Series Motor**: Field in series; $\Phi \propto I_a$ (unsaturated); $T \propto I_a^2$; $N \propto 1/I_a$; **never run unloaded**; used in **traction, cranes**.
- ‚úÖ **DC Compound Motor**: Cumulative (high torque + good regulation) or Differential (low torque + constant speed).
- ‚úÖ **Line current relations**: Shunt ‚Üí $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$; Series ‚Üí $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$; Separately excited ‚Üí $I_L = I_a$, $I_f$ independent.
- ‚úÖ **Starting current** without starter is dangerously high: $I_{a,start} = V/R_a$ ‚ÄĒ therefore **starters are mandatory**.
- ‚úÖ **Speed regulation formula**: $\% \text{Reg} = (N_{NL} - N_{FL})/N_{FL} \times 100$.
- ‚úÖ **Machine constant** $k = PZ/(60A)$ for RPM form; $K_a = PZ/(2\pi A)$ for rad/s form.
- ‚úÖ **Wave winding**: $A = 2$ (constant); **Lap winding**: $A = P$.
- ‚úÖ **Power developed in armature**: $P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega$.
- ‚úÖ **For the KTU 2024 ESE**, focus on numerical problems combining EMF, torque, and speed equations for at least one motor type.
- ‚úÖ Remember the fundamental identity: $\frac{E_b}{T} = \frac{\omega}{I_a}$ ‚ÄĒ this is a **favourite KTU question**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permanent Magnet DC Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Permanent Magnet DC Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚ÄĒ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) Motor**: A self-excited DC motor in which the field winding on the stator is replaced by **high-energy permanent magnets** (typically rare-earth or ceramic materials), thereby producing a constant field flux $\Phi$ without the need for an external field excitation circuit. The armature, placed on the rotor, is supplied with DC current through a mechanical commutator‚Äďbrush assembly.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the PMDC motor is treated as a *special-purpose DC machine* whose principal advantage over the conventional wound-field DC motor is the **elimination of field copper losses** and the **simplification of the drive circuit**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ Plain English Intuition

Imagine a regular ceiling fan. Inside, copper coils are wrapped around an iron ring and electricity is pumped through them just to **create a magnet**. A PMDC motor is like removing that iron ring-and-coil magnet and instead **gluing in a strong bar magnet** (like the one on your fridge) ‚ÄĒ but engineered from a special, ultra-strong material.

**The result?**
- No electricity wasted on creating the magnetic field.
- A smaller, lighter, cooler-running motor.
- Perfect for battery-powered gadgets: toys, cordless drills, robotics, electric windows, and instrumentation actuators.

So, in a sentence: **a PMDC motor is a DC motor where the magnetic field is "baked in" instead of "switched on."**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Field flux per pole | $\Phi$ | **0.005 to 0.05 Wb** (constant) |
| Supply voltage | $V$ | **6 V, 12 V, 24 V, 48 V, 220 V** |
| Magnet remanence $B_r$ (NdFeB) | $B_r$ | **1.0 to 1.4 T** |
| Magnet coercivity $H_c$ (Alnico) | $H_c$ | - |
| Specific energy product | $(BH)_{max}$ | **30‚Äď400 kJ/m¬≥** |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU problems, the field flux $\Phi$ is **always treated as constant** in a PMDC motor (since it cannot be adjusted by varying field current). This single fact simplifies every speed, torque, and efficiency equation dramatically compared to a shunt DC motor.

## 1.4 Position in the KTU 2024 Module Map

Within **Module 2 (Transducers & Actuators for Instrumentation Systems)**, the PMDC motor is studied as the **prime electromechanical actuator** for converting low-power DC electrical signals into controlled rotational mechanical motion ‚ÄĒ for example, driving chart rollers, valve stems, pointer deflection mechanisms, and robot joints in an instrumentation loop.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture

The PMDC motor is built in two mechanical sub-assemblies:

1. **Stator (Field System)** ‚ÄĒ Comprises **permanent magnets** mounted on a ferromagnetic yoke. The magnets are arc-shaped (to follow the inner bore) and are pre-magnetized in the radial direction to produce a uniform air-gap flux density $B_g$.
2. **Rotor (Armature)** ‚ÄĒ A laminated silicon-steel core with slots carrying the armature winding. The commutator is mounted on the same shaft, and carbon brushes ride on it to feed DC current.

### 2.1.1 The Two Magnetic Poles

In a 2-pole PMDC motor, the stator carries **N and S magnets** diametrically opposite. In a multi-pole (4, 6, ‚Ä¶) machine, the magnets alternate N-S-N-S around the inner circumference.

> [!NOTE]
> Because the magnets are radial, the **air-gap flux density is essentially uniform** along the pole arc ‚ÄĒ this is one reason PMDC motors exhibit very smooth torque (low cogging) when properly designed.

## 2.2 Principle of Operation ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic

1. DC voltage $V$ is applied across the armature terminals through the brushes.
2. Current $I_a$ flows through the armature conductors lying in the magnetic field.
3. Each conductor experiences a Lorentz force $\vec{F} = I_a \vec{L} \times \vec{B}$.
4. The tangential component of this force produces a **torque** that rotates the rotor.
5. As the rotor turns, the commutator **reverses the current direction in each coil** at the instant it crosses the magnetic neutral axis, ensuring **unidirectional torque**.

## 2.3 Why is the PMDC motor used in instrumentation?

- **High torque-to-inertia ratio** ‚Üí fast dynamic response.
- **Linear torque‚Äďcurrent characteristic** ‚Üí easy servo-loop design.
- **No field winding** ‚Üí compact, low-heat, suitable for sealed enclosures.
- **Predictable speed** governed by $N \propto (V - I_a R_a)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Back-EMF | $E_b = k_E \, \Phi \, N$ or $E_b = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,N}{60\,A}$ | V | P=poles, Z=conductors, A=parallel paths |
| 2 | Armature voltage | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | V | KVL of armature loop |
| 3 | Developed torque | $T = k_T \, \Phi \, I_a$ or $T = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi A}$ | N¬∑m | $k_T = 60\,k_E / (2\pi)$ |
| 4 | Speed equation | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k_E \, \Phi}$ | rpm | $\Phi$ is **constant** in PMDC |
| 5 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = E_b \, I_a$ | W | Air-gap power = mech. power developed |
| 6 | Shaft output power | $P_{out} = T \, \omega$ | W | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ |
| 7 | No-load speed (ideal) | $N_0 = \dfrac{V}{k_E \, \Phi}$ | rpm | When $I_a \to 0$ |
| 8 | Stall torque (ideal) | $T_s = \dfrac{k_T \, \Phi \, V}{R_a}$ | N¬∑m | When $E_b = 0$ (rotor locked) |
| 9 | Mechanical time constant | $\tau_m = \dfrac{J \, R_a}{(k_E k_T \Phi^2)}$ | s | $J$ = moment of inertia |
| 10 | Power efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V \, I_a}$ | pu | Includes copper, friction, windage losses |

> [!WARNING]
> In **all KTU exam solutions**, explicitly state "Since $\Phi$ is constant in a PMDC motor" before substituting into the speed equation. Examiners award the 1 mark for this statement.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the PMDC Equations

These equations are foundational to:
- **Servo amplifiers** (the $V \propto N$ relationship is the basis of analog tachogenerator loops).
- **Robotic arm control** (torque‚Äďcurrent linearity makes current-mode control trivial).
- **Automotive actuators** (window lifters, mirror adjusters, throttle bodies).
- **Chart recorders and XY plotters** in instrumentation laboratories.
- **Battery-operated medical devices** (insulin pumps, surgical tools).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Back-EMF Equation

Let:
- $P$ = number of magnetic poles
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in the armature winding
- $N$ = speed of the armature in revolutions per minute (rpm)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Flux cut per revolution by one conductor**

As the armature rotates through one complete revolution, a given conductor cuts the flux under **$P/2$ pairs of poles**. The total flux cut per revolution by one conductor is:

$$
\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = P \cdot \Phi
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Number of revolutions per second**

$$
\text{Revolutions per second} = \frac{N}{60}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Flux cut per conductor per second**

Multiplying Steps 1 and 2:

$$
\text{Flux cut per conductor per second} = P \, \Phi \, \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ EMF induced in one conductor**

By Faraday's law, the average EMF induced in one conductor is numerically equal to the rate of flux cut:

$$
e_{\text{per conductor}} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N}{60} \quad \text{(volts, when } \Phi \text{ in Wb)}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Total EMF in the armature**

There are $Z$ conductors in series, distributed over $A$ parallel paths. The number of conductors in **series per path** is $Z/A$. The conductors in all $A$ paths are in parallel, so the **total terminal EMF** equals the EMF in any single path:

$$
E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{P \, \Phi \, N}{60}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\,E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}\,}
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Voltage constant $k_E$ form**

For a given motor, $P$, $Z$, $A$ are fixed, so:

$$
E_b = k_E \, \Phi \, N, \qquad \text{where} \quad k_E = \frac{P \, Z}{60 \, A}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Mechanical work done per revolution**

When the armature rotates one revolution, the total flux cut by all conductors produces a counter-EMF $E_b$, and the work done against this EMF by the mechanical torque is:

$$
W_{\text{per rev}} = E_b \cdot \text{(charge circulated per rev)}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Charge circulated per revolution**

Charge = current $\times$ time. For one revolution (time = $60/N$ seconds), the total charge circulated through each parallel path is:

$$
Q_{\text{per rev}} = \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot \frac{60}{N} \cdot A = \frac{60 \, I_a}{N}
$$

Wait ‚ÄĒ let us redo this more rigorously. The current in **each parallel path** is $I_a/A$. The time for one revolution is $60/N$ seconds. So the charge flowing through **one path** per revolution is:

$$
Q_{\text{path}} = \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot \frac{60}{N}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Work done per revolution**

The total mechanical work done per revolution equals the electrical energy converted:

$$
W = E_b \cdot Z \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot \frac{60}{N} \cdot \frac{1}{1}
$$

But there is a cleaner route using power. From $E_b = P\Phi Z N / (60A)$:

$$
E_b \, I_a = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N \, I_a}{60 \, A}
$$

This is the electrical power converted to mechanical form.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Equate to mechanical power $T \omega$**

$$
T \cdot \omega = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N \, I_a}{60 \, A}
$$

With $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$:

$$
T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N \, I_a}{60 \, A}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Cancel $N/60$ from both sides**

$$
T \cdot 2\pi = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{A}
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Final torque expression**

$$
\boxed{\,T = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi \, A}\,}
$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Torque constant $k_T$ form**

Since $P$, $Z$, $A$ are constants for a given motor:

$$
T = k_T \, \Phi \, I_a, \qquad \text{where} \quad k_T = \frac{P \, Z}{2\pi A}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consistency check:** $\dfrac{k_E}{k_T} = \dfrac{PZ/(60A)}{PZ/(2\pi A)} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60} = \dfrac{\pi}{30}$. Therefore $k_E = (\pi/30) \, k_T$, or equivalently $E_b = (\pi/30) \, T \, N$ when $I_a$ is in amperes. Students often confuse $k_E$ and $k_T$ ‚ÄĒ write both out in SI to avoid this.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Speed‚ÄďCurrent Characteristic (PMDC)

Starting from the armature KVL:

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

Substituting $E_b = k_E \Phi N$:

$$
V = k_E \Phi N + I_a R_a
$$

Solving for $N$:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k_E \Phi}
$$

Since $\Phi$ is **constant** in a PMDC motor, this is a **straight line** in the $N$‚Äď$I_a$ plane:

- When $I_a = 0$: $N = N_0 = V / (k_E \Phi)$ ‚ÄĒ no-load speed.
- When $N = 0$: $I_a = I_s = V / R_a$ ‚ÄĒ stall current.
- The slope is $-R_a / (k_E \Phi)$ ‚ÄĒ always negative, meaning speed **drops** as load increases.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Special Operating Points

| Operating Point | Speed $N$ | Armature Current $I_a$ | Torque $T$ | Power $P$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **No-load (ideal)** | $N_0 = \dfrac{V}{k_E \Phi}$ | $0$ | $0$ (friction only) | $0$ |
| **Full-load** | $N_{fl} = N_0 - \dfrac{R_a I_{fl}}{k_E \Phi}$ | $I_{fl}$ | $T_{fl} = k_T \Phi I_{fl}$ | $P_{fl} = V I_{fl} - I_{fl}^2 R_a$ |
| **Stall (locked rotor)** | $0$ | $I_s = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | $T_s = k_T \Phi \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | $0$ (all heat) |
| **Max. power transfer** | $N_0 / 2$ | $I_s / 2$ | $T_s / 2$ | $V^2 / (4 R_a)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ‚ÄĒ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional View of a 2-Pole PMDC Motor

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture** of a PMDC motor, mapping the energy-conversion stages from electrical input to mechanical output. (Direct physical drawing is replaced with the architecture flow per the engine safeguard.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCV[DC Voltage Source V] --> BRUSH1[Positive Brush]
    BRUSH1 --> COMM1[Commutator Segment A]
    COMM1 --> COIL[Armature Coil in Slot]
    COIL --> COMM2[Commutator Segment B]
    COMM2 --> BRUSH2[Negative Brush]
    BRUSH2 --> DCV
    MAG1[Permanent Magnet N Pole] -- Radial B field --> GAP[Air Gap]
    MAG2[Permanent Magnet S Pole] -- Radial B field --> GAP
    GAP --> COIL
    COIL -- Lorentz Force F = BIL --> SHAFT[Output Shaft Torsion]
    SHAFT --> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of the PMDC Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PSU[DC Power Supply V] --> ARM[Armature Winding R_a, L_a]
    ARM --> EMFCONV{E_b Generator}
    EMFCONV -- kT Phi Ia --> TORQUE[T developed]
    TORQUE --> MECH[Mechanical Load J, B]
    MECH --> SHAFT2[Shaft Speed N rpm]
    SHAFT2 -- Feedback --> EMFCONV
    MAG[Permanent Magnet Flux Phi = const] --> EMFCONV
    MAG -- Constant --> TORQUE
```

## 4.3 PMDC Motor Equivalent Circuit (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A((+)) --> R1[R_a Armature Resistance]
    R1 --> E1((E_b Back EMF))
    E1 --> B(( - ))
    C[V Supply] --- A
    C --- B
    D[Constant Phi from PM] -.-> E1
    D -.-> F[T = kT Phi Ia]
```

## 4.4 Speed‚ÄďTorque Characteristic (Linear Plot)

The PMDC motor has a **linear drooping** speed‚Äďtorque characteristic (since $\Phi$ = const):

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[N_axis] --> A1[Origin 0,0]
    B0[T_axis] --> A1
    A0 -- N0 No load speed --> N0pt((N0, 0))
    B0 -- Ts Stall torque --> T0pt((0, Ts))
    N0pt --- T0pt
    A0 -- N at half load --> NHALF((N0/2, Ts/2))
    A0 -- N at full load --> NFL((N_fl, T_fl))
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **slope of the N‚ÄďT line** is the **speed regulation** of the motor. For a PMDC motor, this slope is $-R_a / (k_E k_T \Phi^2)$, a small negative number. The smaller $|slope|$, the "stiffer" the speed (better regulation).

## 4.5 Classification of PMDC Motors by Magnet Material

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PMDC[PMDC Motor] --> ALN[Alnico]
    PMDC --> FER[Ferrite Ceramic]
    PMDC --> RARE[Rare Earth]
    ALN --> ALN1[High B_r, Low H_c]
    FER --> FER1[Low cost, Brittle]
    RARE --> ND[NdFeB]
    RARE --> SM[SmCo]
    ND --> ND1[Highest BH max, Corrodes]
    SM --> SM1[High temp operation, Expensive]
```

## 4.6 Constructional Variants ‚ÄĒ Armature Position

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PMDC2[PMDC Motor Types] --> INT[Internal Rotor]
    PMDC2 --> OUT[External Rotor]
    INT --> SLOT[Slotted Armature]
    INT --> SLOTL[Slotless Armature]
    OUT --> WHEEL[Wheel Hub Motor]
    OUT --> DISC[Disc Armature Pancake]
```
