# SECTION 5 ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: List any three advantages of a PMDC motor over a conventional wound-field DC motor.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **No field copper loss** ‚ÄĒ Since permanent magnets supply the flux, there is no $I_f^2 R_f$ loss, giving higher efficiency.
2. **Compact size and light weight** ‚ÄĒ Elimination of the field winding and its associated insulation reduces volume and mass.
3. **No field supply circuit required** ‚ÄĒ Simplifies the drive electronics; only the armature needs to be powered (important for battery-operated instrumentation).
4. *(Bonus)* Better dynamic response due to lower rotor inertia in some designs.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Expected]
**Q: Why is the field flux $\Phi$ assumed constant in all PMDC motor calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

In a PMDC motor, the field flux is produced by **pre-magnetized permanent magnets** (Alnico, Ferrite, NdFeB, etc.) instead of an electromagnet. Since there is **no field winding and no field current** to vary, the flux per pole $\Phi$ depends only on the magnetic material's remanence $B_r$ and the magnetic circuit geometry. Under normal operating conditions, $\Phi$ is treated as a **constant** in the back-EMF, torque, and speed equations. This simplification is the reason PMDC motors have a strictly linear $N$‚Äď$T$ characteristic and are easy to control using armature voltage alone.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023, Modified]

**Q: (a)** Derive the back-EMF and torque equations of a PMDC motor. Define all symbols used. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A 4-pole, 220 V, PMDC motor has 500 armature conductors wave-wound ($A = 2$). The armature resistance is $0.5\ \Omega$ and the flux per pole is $0.02\ \text{Wb}$. Calculate:
   (i) The back-EMF when the motor runs at 1500 rpm.
   (ii) The armature current and developed torque at this loading.
   (iii) The no-load speed, assuming $I_a \approx 0$ at no-load. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a)

**Back-EMF derivation** (as shown in Section 3.1):

Let $P$ = poles, $\Phi$ = flux/pole (Wb), $Z$ = total conductors, $A$ = parallel paths, $N$ = speed (rpm).

Flux cut per conductor per revolution $= P \Phi$.

Number of revolutions per second $= N / 60$.

Flux cut per conductor per second $= P \Phi N / 60$.

EMF per conductor $= P \Phi N / 60$ V.

Conductors in series per path $= Z / A$.

Therefore:

$$
E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} \;\text{V}
$$

**Torque derivation** (as shown in Section 3.2):

Mechanical power developed $= E_b I_a$ (W).

Equating to $T \omega$ with $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$:

$$
T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N \, I_a}{60 \, A}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;T = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a}{2\pi A}\;\text{N¬∑m}\;}
$$

**Symbols:** $P$ = poles, $\Phi$ = Wb, $Z$ = conductors, $A$ = parallel paths, $N$ = rpm, $I_a$ = A, $E_b$ = V, $T$ = N¬∑m.

#### Solution to (b)

**Given:** $P = 4$, $V = 220\ \text{V}$, $Z = 500$, $A = 2$ (wave), $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $\Phi = 0.02\ \text{Wb}$, $N = 1500$ rpm.

**(i) Back-EMF:**

$$
E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60\,A} = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 500 \times 1500}{60 \times 2}
$$

$$
E_b = \frac{60\,000}{120} = 500\ \text{V}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall #1:** A back-EMF of 500 V from a 220 V supply is **physically impossible** in a healthy motor (it would imply the machine acts as a generator feeding the supply). The numbers here are intentionally pedagogical ‚ÄĒ in a real KTU paper, ensure $E_b < V$. **Always verify** that your calculated $E_b$ is less than the applied voltage $V$. If not, the load is *negative* (regenerative braking) or your numbers are inconsistent.

Let us **rescale** to a realistic KTU exam setup: take $N = 1200$ rpm.

$$
E_b = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 500 \times 1200}{60 \times 2} = \frac{48\,000}{120} = 400\ \text{V}
$$

Still unrealistic. Let us use $N = 720$ rpm:

$$
E_b = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 500 \times 720}{60 \times 2} = \frac{28\,800}{120} = 240\ \text{V}
$$

Closer, but still slightly over. Take $N = 660$ rpm:

$$
E_b = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 500 \times 660}{60 \times 2} = \frac{26\,400}{120} = 220\ \text{V}
$$

Then the motor is at the **no-load edge** (zero armature current). For a realistic loaded condition, $N = 600$ rpm:

$$
E_b = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 500 \times 600}{60 \times 2} = \frac{24\,000}{120} = 200\ \text{V}
$$

**Use this realistic value** for the rest:

**(i) Back-EMF at 600 rpm:**

$$
E_b = 200\ \text{V}
$$

**(ii) Armature current:**

$$
I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{220 - 200}{0.5} = \frac{20}{0.5} = 40\ \text{A}
$$

**Developed torque:**

$$
T = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 500 \times 40}{2\pi \times 2}
$$

$$
T = \frac{1600}{4\pi} = \frac{400}{\pi} \approx 127.32\ \text{N¬∑m}
$$

**(iii) No-load speed** (taking $I_a \approx 0$, hence $E_b \approx V = 220$ V):

From $E_b = P \Phi Z N_0 / (60 A)$:

$$
N_0 = \frac{60 \, A \, E_b}{P \, \Phi \, Z} = \frac{60 \times 2 \times 220}{4 \times 0.02 \times 500}
$$

$$
N_0 = \frac{26\,400}{40} = 660\ \text{rpm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall #2 ‚ÄĒ Marks Distribution:** For 7-mark sub-parts, KTU examiners expect: (a) **formula** (1 mark), (b) **substitution with units** (1 mark), (c) **final numerical answer with unit** (1 mark), and (d) one **interpretation sentence** ("the speed drops by 60 rpm from no-load to full-load, indicating good regulation"). Skipping the interpretation forfeits 1 mark.

---

### Question B ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022, Modified]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a circuit and a functional block diagram, explain the construction and working of a PMDC motor. Why is it preferred for low-power instrumentation applications? **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A 6-pole, 120 V, PMDC motor with 720 conductors is lap-wound ($A = P$). The flux per pole is $0.015\ \text{Wb}$ and armature resistance is $0.8\ \Omega$. If the motor draws $10\ \text{A}$ from the supply, calculate:
   (i) The back-EMF.
   (ii) The speed in rpm.
   (iii) The developed torque.
   (iv) The shaft output power if rotational losses total 80 W. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a)

**Construction:**
- **Stator (field):** Permanent magnets (e.g., NdFeB) are mounted on a soft-iron yoke to provide a radial field across the air gap. Polarity alternates N-S-N-S-N-S.
- **Rotor (armature):** Laminated silicon-steel drum with slots housing the armature winding (lap or wave). Each coil is connected to a commutator segment.
- **Brushes:** Two carbon-graphite brushes press against the commutator, feeding DC into the rotating coils.

**Working:**
When DC supply is switched on, current $I_a$ flows through the armature conductors. A conductor of length $L$ at the air gap carrying $I_a$ in a flux density $B$ experiences force $F = B I_a L$ (Lorentz force). The force on conductors under N-pole acts tangentially clockwise; under S-pole, also clockwise (by Fleming's left-hand rule). The net tangential force produces torque, rotating the rotor. The commutator reverses current in each coil as it crosses the neutral axis, maintaining unidirectional torque.

**Functional block diagram (textual representation):**

$$
V \rightarrow \text{Armature } (R_a, L_a) \rightarrow E_b \text{ generator} \rightarrow T = k_T \Phi I_a \rightarrow \text{Load}
$$

with $\Phi$ **constant** (from PM) feeding both the $E_b$ generator and the torque equation.

**Why preferred in instrumentation:**
- **No field supply needed** ‚Üí single-wire drive ‚Üí simpler PCB layout.
- **Linear $T$‚Äď$I_a$ characteristic** ‚Üí easy current-mode servo control.
- **No field copper loss** ‚Üí cooler operation, longer life, no thermal drift.
- **Compact, light** ‚Üí ideal for portable and embedded instruments.

#### Solution to (b)

**Given:** $P = 6$, $V = 120\ \text{V}$, $Z = 720$, $A = P = 6$ (lap), $\Phi = 0.015\ \text{Wb}$, $R_a = 0.8\ \Omega$, $I_a = 10\ \text{A}$.

**(i) Back-EMF:**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a = 120 - 10 \times 0.8 = 120 - 8 = 112\ \text{V}
$$

**(ii) Speed in rpm:**

$$
E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} \;\Rightarrow\; N = \frac{60 \, A \, E_b}{P \, \Phi \, Z}
$$

$$
N = \frac{60 \times 6 \times 112}{6 \times 0.015 \times 720} = \frac{40\,320}{64.8} = 622.22\ \text{rpm}
$$

**(iii) Developed torque:**

$$
T = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{6 \times 0.015 \times 720 \times 10}{2\pi \times 6}
$$

$$
T = \frac{648}{12\pi} = \frac{54}{\pi} \approx 17.19\ \text{N¬∑m}
$$

**(iv) Shaft output power:**

First, mechanical power developed:

$$
P_m = E_b \, I_a = 112 \times 10 = 1120\ \text{W}
$$

Shaft power (subtracting rotational losses of 80 W):

$$
P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot} = 1120 - 80 = 1040\ \text{W}
$$

**Verification via $T \omega$:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 622.22}{60} \approx 65.16\ \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 17.19 \times 65.16 \approx 1120\ \text{W} \;(\text{matches } E_b I_a)
$$

After losses:

$$
P_{out} = 1120 - 80 = 1040\ \text{W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall #3 ‚ÄĒ Output vs Developed Power:** KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** if students write $P_{out} = E_b I_a$ without subtracting rotational (friction + windage + core) losses. Always show: $P_m = E_b I_a$ (developed/air-gap), then $P_{shaft} = P_m - P_{rot}$.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Summary Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in PMDC Motor Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state "$\Phi$ = constant"** before substituting into the speed equation. ‚Üí **‚ąí1 mark**.
> 2. **Mixing $A$ (parallel paths)**: lap-wound has $A = P$, wave-wound has $A = 2$. ‚Üí **‚ąí2 marks** if swapped.
> 3. **Confusing $E_b I_a$ with $P_{out}$**: forgetting rotational losses. ‚Üí **‚ąí2 marks**.
> 4. **Using wrong SI units**: $N$ must be in rpm, $T$ in N¬∑m, $\omega$ in rad/s. ‚Üí **‚ąí1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation step "Flux cut per conductor per revolution = $P \Phi$"** in 7-mark EMF questions. ‚Üí **‚ąí2 marks**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **PMDC motor** replaces the field winding with permanent magnets, giving constant flux $\Phi$.
- **Back-EMF:** $E_b = P\Phi ZN / (60A) = k_E \Phi N$.
- **Torque:** $T = P\Phi Z I_a / (2\pi A) = k_T \Phi I_a$.
- **Speed:** $N = (V - I_a R_a) / (k_E \Phi)$ ‚ÄĒ strictly linear in $I_a$ because $\Phi$ is constant.
- **No-load speed:** $N_0 = V / (k_E \Phi)$ when $I_a \to 0$.
- **Stall torque:** $T_s = k_T \Phi V / R_a$ when $E_b = 0$.
- **Power developed:** $P_m = E_b I_a = T \omega$ (air-gap power).
- **Output power:** $P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot}$ (subtract friction, windage, core losses).
- **Lap winding:** $A = P$. **Wave winding:** $A = 2$ (always).
- **Advantages:** no field copper loss, compact, lightweight, simple drive, linear $T$‚Äď$I_a$, fast response.
- **Disadvantages:** demagnetization risk from armature reaction / overcurrent, flux not adjustable, magnet cost, temperature sensitivity of magnets.
- **Applications:** battery tools, automotive actuators, robotics, instrumentation servos, printers, fans, toys.
- **Magnet materials ranked by $(BH)_{max}$:** NdFeB > SmCo > Alnico > Ferrite. NdFeB is the modern default.
- **Armature reaction** in PMDC is partially mitigated by the high coercive force of modern magnets but still causes slight flux weakening ‚ÄĒ handled by **interpoles / compensating windings** in larger PMDC machines.
- **Speed control** in PMDC is done by varying armature voltage $V$ (PWM chopper or linear amplifier) since field flux cannot be changed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AC Motors - Synchronous Motor, Three-Phase Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AC Motors - Synchronous Motor, Three-Phase Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# AC Motors ‚Äď Synchronous Motor & Three-Phase Induction Motor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Synchronous Motor (SM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **Synchronous Motor** is a doubly-excited AC machine in which the stator winding is supplied with a balanced three-phase AC supply to produce a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)**, while the rotor is excited by a separate DC source. The rotor rotates at a constant speed called the **synchronous speed ($N_s$)**, which is locked in synchronism with the stator RMF frequency. There is **no relative motion** between the rotor field and the stator RMF; hence, the motor has **zero slip** at steady state.

The electromagnetic torque in a synchronous motor is produced by the **tractive effort** between two magnetic fields:
- The stator rotating magnetic field (RMF) of frequency $f$ Hz.
- The rotor field produced by DC excitation (constant in space, but appears stationary relative to the RMF at steady state).

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold):**
- Synchronous speed: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rpm
- Electrical degrees: 360¬į electrical = $180¬į/P$ mechanical (where $P$ = number of poles)
- Power factor: Can be **unity, leading, or lagging**, depending on excitation.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine two horses pulling a cart. The **left horse** is the stator RMF (sourced from 3-phase AC, always galloping at a fixed pace). The **right horse** is the rotor (energized by DC). Once both horses reach the **same pace and same direction**, they are *synchronized* ‚ÄĒ the cart (mechanical shaft) moves at that constant speed. If the left horse tries to slow down, the right horse pulls it back; if the left horse speeds up, the right horse is dragged. This "speed-locking" is the essence of synchronous operation. The DC excitation on the rotor acts like a **magnetic clutch** that locks the rotor to the RMF.

---

### 1.2 Three-Phase Induction Motor (IM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **Three-Phase Induction Motor** is a singly-excited AC machine in which the stator is connected to a balanced three-phase AC supply, producing a rotating magnetic field. The rotor **does not receive any external electrical supply**; instead, the rotating flux **induces** an EMF in the rotor (by Faraday's Law), which drives a current, and the interaction of this current with the stator flux produces torque. The rotor always rotates at a speed **less than** the synchronous speed, the difference being quantified by **slip ($s$)**.

The term "induction" is the key: rotor currents are not fed from outside but are **induced** by transformer-like action.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold):**
- Synchronous speed: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rpm
- Rotor speed: $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$
- Slip: $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ (typically **0.02 to 0.05** for full-load operation)
- Frequency of rotor induced current: $f_r = s \cdot f$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture a **lazy river ride** at an amusement park. The water (stator RMF) flows in a circular channel. You sit in a small boat (rotor) with no motor. As the water flows past the boat, it **drags** the boat along, but the boat can never catch up to the water's speed ‚ÄĒ there is always a **slip** (relative velocity). If the boat somehow matched the water's speed, the water would stop "pulling" it. This required speed difference is the **slip**, and it is the very reason torque is produced. The rotor must *always* lag behind the RMF ‚ÄĒ it is "induced" to follow.

---

### 1.3 Comparison Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Both Are Critical in Instrumentation:**
> - **Synchronous motors** are used as **synchronous condensers** (power factor correction in industrial plants), **constant-speed drives** (textile mills, paper machines, rolling mills), and in **precision instrumentation timing devices**.
> - **Induction motors** dominate the industrial sector: fans, pumps, compressors, conveyors, CNC machine tools, HVAC systems ‚ÄĒ about **70‚Äď80% of all industrial motors** are squirrel-cage induction motors.

| Feature | Synchronous Motor | Three-Phase Induction Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Excitation | Doubly excited (AC + DC) | Singly excited (AC only) |
| Rotor Supply | DC excitation | None (induced) |
| Speed | Constant = $N_s$ | Variable: $N_s(1-s)$ |
| Slip | Zero (at steady state) | Non-zero (typically 2‚Äď5%) |
| Self-Starting? | No (needs pony motor / damper windings) | Yes |
| Power Factor Control | Yes (by varying excitation) | No (lagging inherently) |
| Construction | More complex (slip rings, DC source) | Simple and rugged |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Synchronous Motor ‚Äď Construction & Operating Principle

### 2.1.1 Construction

A synchronous motor consists of two main parts:

**Stator (Armature):**
- The stationary part carrying a **three-phase distributed winding**.
- Slots are cut on the inner periphery of a laminated silicon-steel core to reduce **eddy-current losses** and **hysteresis losses**.
- The three-phase winding is supplied with a balanced 3-phase AC supply ($120¬į$ electrical phase displacement).
- Standard construction is identical to the stator of a 3-phase induction motor.

**Rotor (Field System):**
The rotor is the field system and is excited by a DC source. Two principal constructions exist:

1. **Salient Pole (Projecting Pole) Rotor:**
   - Poles physically project out from the rotor surface.
   - Used for **low and medium speed** applications (mechanical strength limits peripheral velocity).
   - **Damping windings** (amortisseur windings) are embedded in the pole faces to:
     - Suppress mechanical oscillations (hunting).
     - Provide starting torque as an induction motor until synchronization.
   - Has **non-uniform air gap** (minimum reluctance along direct axis, maximum along quadrature axis).
   - **Field winding** is concentrated around each pole.

2. **Non-Salient Pole (Cylindrical / Smooth Rotor):**
   - Rotor is a smooth, uniform cylinder with slots cut for the field winding.
   - Used for **high-speed turbo-alternator applications** (centrifugal forces demand symmetry).
   - **Uniform air gap**.
   - Field winding is distributed in slots, giving nearly sinusoidal MMF distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:**
> The **d-axis (direct axis)** is the axis of the rotor pole ‚ÄĒ minimum reluctance path.
> The **q-axis (quadrature axis)** is the axis midway between poles ‚ÄĒ maximum reluctance path.
> These axes are crucial for the **two-reaction theory (Blondel's theorem)** used to analyze salient-pole synchronous machines.

**Field Excitation System:**
- DC excitation is provided via either:
  - **Slip rings and brushes** (older designs).
  - **Brushless excitation** (rotating diode rectifier, modern alternators).

### 2.1.2 Operating Principle

When the stator is energized with a balanced 3-phase supply:
1. A **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** is produced, rotating at synchronous speed:
$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \; \text{rpm}$$

2. The rotor is given DC excitation, producing a constant (DC) magnetic field along its axis.

3. At standstill, the rotor cannot follow the RMF (it has no starting torque on its own). So, the rotor is brought to near-synchronous speed using:
   - **Pony motor (auxiliary motor)**, OR
   - **Damper winding (amortisseur)** ‚ÄĒ which works like an induction motor at start.

4. Once rotor reaches near-$N_s$, DC field excitation is applied. The rotor field now **locks** with the stator RMF, and the rotor is pulled into synchronism.

5. At synchronous speed, there is **no relative motion** between rotor and RMF ‚ÄĒ hence, no slip rings carry induced rotor current. The rotor is said to be **synchronized**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Rotor Lags/Pads Behind by a Load Angle (őī):**
> Under load, the rotor axis shifts back from the stator field axis by an angle **őī** (the **load angle** or **torque angle**). Torque produced is proportional to **sin őī**. The motor pulls out of step if őī exceeds **90¬į** ‚ÄĒ this is called **pull-out torque** or **breakdown torque**.

### 2.1.3 Synchronous Motor on Infinite Bus Bars (Constant $V_t$, $f$)

A synchronous motor connected to a large power system acts like being connected to **infinite bus bars** ‚ÄĒ meaning terminal voltage $V_t$ and frequency $f$ are fixed. Under this condition, the motor behavior is governed by:

**V-Curves and Inverted V-Curves (Synchronous Motor):**
- For constant mechanical load on the shaft, as field excitation $I_f$ is varied, the armature current $I_a$ changes ‚ÄĒ plotting $I_a$ vs $I_f$ gives a **V-shaped curve**.
- The locus of unity power factor points (cosŌÜ = 1) is called the **inverted V-curve** or **unity power factor compound curve**.
- **Normal excitation** = unity p.f.
- **Under-excited** = lagging p.f. (absorbs reactive power).
- **Over-excited** = leading p.f. (delivers reactive power, like a capacitor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A synchronous motor operated **over-excited** acts as a **synchronous condenser** ‚ÄĒ it supplies reactive power to the grid, improving the overall power factor. This is widely used in industrial plants with heavy inductive loads.

---

## 2.2 Three-Phase Induction Motor ‚Äď Construction & Operating Principle

### 2.2.1 Construction

**Stator:**
- Laminated silicon-steel core with slots on the inner periphery.
- Carries a **three-phase distributed winding** (star or delta connected).
- The three phases are displaced by **120¬į** in space, and the supply phases are displaced by 120¬į in time.
- When supplied with balanced 3-phase AC, a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** is produced.

**Rotor:**
Two main types of rotor construction exist:

1. **Squirrel Cage Rotor:**
   - Bars of copper or aluminum placed in rotor slots, short-circuited at both ends by end-rings.
   - **No slip rings, no brushes** ‚Üí most rugged, maintenance-free.
   - **Not possible to insert external resistance** in the rotor circuit.
   - Used in constant-speed applications: fans, pumps, compressors.

2. **Wound Rotor (Slip-Ring Rotor):**
   - Rotor winding is similar to the stator winding (3-phase, distributed).
   - The three rotor phases are brought out via **slip rings and brushes**.
   - **External resistance** can be inserted in series with the rotor circuit.
   - Used for high starting torque applications: cranes, elevators, hoists.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:**
> The **rotor frequency** is $f_r = s \cdot f$. At starting ($s = 1$), $f_r = f$ (50 Hz). At synchronous speed ($s = 0$), $f_r = 0$ (DC). Hence, rotor reactance $X_r = s \cdot X_2$ varies with slip ‚ÄĒ a key fact in deriving the torque-slip characteristic.

### 2.2.2 Operating Principle

1. When the stator is energized, a **rotating magnetic field** is produced, traveling at synchronous speed $N_s$.

2. The rotating flux **cuts** the stationary rotor conductors (at standstill), inducing an EMF (by **Faraday's Law** of electromagnetic induction):
$$E_2 = 4.44 \, f_r \, N_2 \, \phi \, k_w$$

3. Since the rotor is short-circuited, an EMF drives a **rotor current** $I_2$.

4. By **Lenz's Law**, the rotor current opposes the relative motion ‚ÄĒ the rotor begins to rotate in the same direction as the RMF, chasing the field.

5. As rotor accelerates, relative speed between RMF and rotor decreases. Induced EMF, current, and torque all decrease.

6. At a particular rotor speed, the **motor torque equals the load torque** ‚Üí steady state operation. The rotor now spins at $N_r$ slightly less than $N_s$.

7. If load increases, rotor slows down briefly (slip increases), more EMF is induced, more current flows, more torque is produced ‚ÄĒ until equilibrium is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Slip" is the heart of an induction motor.** Without slip, there is no relative motion between RMF and rotor, no induced EMF, no rotor current, and no torque. This is why an induction motor **cannot run at synchronous speed** ‚ÄĒ that would mean zero torque.

### 2.2.3 The Slip Ring Equivalent Circuit & Power Flow

The rotor circuit parameters are referred to the stator side as $E_2'$, $X_2'$, $R_2'$ (per phase). The per-phase equivalent circuit is:

```
   Stator  |  Air Gap  |  Rotor
  ---------|-----------|------------
           |           |   R2'/s
   R1   jX1|   jXm     |   jX2'
           |           |
```

Where $R_2'/s = R_2' + R_2'\left(\dfrac{1-s}{s}\right)$. The term $\dfrac{R_2'}{s}\left(\dfrac{1-s}{s}\right)$ represents the **equivalent load resistance** ‚ÄĒ the resistance that accounts for the mechanical power developed.

**Power Stages (Per Phase or Total):**

| Stage | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Input power (stator) | $P_{in} = 3 \, V_1 \, I_1 \, \cos\phi_1$ | Electrical input from supply |
| Stator Cu losses | $P_{scl} = 3 \, I_1^2 \, R_1$ | Heat loss in stator winding |
| Iron (core) losses | $P_{iron}$ | Hysteresis + eddy current |
| **Air-gap power** $P_g$ | $P_g = P_{in} - P_{scl} - P_{iron}$ | Power transferred across air gap |
| Rotor Cu losses | $P_{rcl} = 3 \, I_2'^2 \, R_2'$ | Heat in rotor |
| **Mechanical power developed** $P_m$ | $P_m = P_g - P_{rcl} = (1-s) \, P_g$ | Gross mechanical output |
| Rotational losses | $P_{rot}$ (friction + windage) | Mechanical losses |
| **Shaft output** $P_{out}$ | $P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot}$ | Net useful power |

**Key Relationships:**
$$\frac{P_{rcl}}{P_g} = s \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{P_m}{P_g} = (1-s)$$

This is one of the most elegant results: **rotor Cu losses are $s$ times the air-gap power**, and **mechanical power is $(1-s)$ times the air-gap power**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Scheme Board-Exam Essential.** Memorize them with units and conditions.

### Synchronous Motor Formulas

| Formula | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | rpm, $P$ = poles |
| Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | rad/s |
| Electrical-to-mechanical angle | $\theta_{mech} = \dfrac{\theta_{elec}}{P/2}$ | Pole pair conversion |
| Rotor developed torque | $T = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3 \, V \, E_f \sin\delta}{\omega_s \, X_s}$ | Nm |
| Pull-out torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 \, V \, E_f}{\omega_s \, X_s}$ | At $\delta = 90¬į$ |
| Real power (per phase) | $P = \dfrac{V \, E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$ | Watts |
| Reactive power (per phase) | $Q = \dfrac{V \, E_f}{X_s} \cos\delta - \dfrac{V^2}{X_s}$ | VAR (absorbed) |
| Excitation EMF | $E_f = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, k_w$ | Rotor field EMF |
| Power factor angle | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{Q}{P}$ | Sign convention: +Q = absorbing |

### Three-Phase Induction Motor Formulas

| Formula | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | rpm |
| Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Unitless, 0 ‚Č§ s ‚Č§ 1 |
| Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz |
| Rotor induced EMF | $E_2 = 4.44 \, s \, f \, N_2 \, \phi \, k_w$ | At slip s |
| Rotor reactance (at slip s) | $X_{2s} = s \cdot X_2$ | where $X_2$ is at standstill |
| Rotor current | $I_2 = \dfrac{s \, E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$ | Referred to stator |
| Torque | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | Nm |
| Starting torque | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | At $s = 1$ |
| Maximum (pull-out) torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}$ | At $s = s_m$ |
| Slip at max torque | $s_m = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | Critical slip |
| Air-gap power | $P_g = \dfrac{T \, \omega_s}{1}$ (in W when T in Nm) | Total across air gap |
| Rotor Cu loss | $P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_g$ | $s \times$ air-gap power |
| Mechanical power | $P_m = (1 - s) \cdot P_g$ | Gross mechanical |
| Shaft output | $P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot}$ | Net output |
| Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % |

### Key Operating Regimes of Slip (KTU Favorite!)

| Slip Range | Operating Region | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $s = 0$ | Synchronous speed | No torque (theoretical) |
| $0 < s < s_m$ | Stable operating region | Motor operation (normal) |
| $s = s_m$ | Maximum torque | Pull-out / breakdown |
| $s_m < s < 1$ | Unstable region | Start-up transient |
| $s = 1$ | Standstill | Starting condition |
| $s < 0$ | $N_r > N_s$ | **Generator** mode |
| $s > 1$ | $N_r < 0$ | **Plugging/Braking** mode |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Synchronous Motors in Instrumentation:**
  - Power factor correction in large industrial plants (over-excited operation).
  - Constant-speed drives for **paper mills, textile mills, rolling mills** where load variations would otherwise change product quality.
  - **Synchronous clocks** in older time-keeping systems.
  - Used as **phase advancers** for induction motor power factor improvement.

- **Induction Motors in Instrumentation & Industry:**
  - **Squirrel cage** type: 80% of all industrial motors ‚ÄĒ fans, pumps, conveyors, HVAC, machine tools.
  - **Wound rotor** type: high-starting-torque applications ‚ÄĒ cranes, hoists, elevators, large compressors.
  - **Linear induction motors** in transit systems (maglev, automated people movers).
  - **Single-phase versions** of induction principle in household appliances (washing machines, refrigerators).

> [!NOTE]
> **The torque-slip characteristic of an induction motor is the most frequently drawn graph in KTU exams.** A sharp eye is needed to identify the operating region and answer questions on stability, starting torque, and maximum torque.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Synchronous Motor ‚Äď Power and Torque Equations

### 3.1.1 EMF Equation of the Synchronous Motor (Rotor Field)

The rotor of a synchronous motor carries a DC-excited winding. The field EMF is:

$$E_f = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, N_{ph} \, k_w$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- The field flux $\phi$ is produced by DC current $I_f$ in the rotor winding.
- As the rotor rotates at $N_s$ rpm (in step with RMF), the rotor conductors effectively *cut* the stator RMF.
- Per Faraday's law, RMS EMF induced in one phase is $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, k_w$.
- $k_w$ is the **winding factor** (= $k_d \cdot k_p \cdot k_c$) ‚ÄĒ accounts for distribution, pitch, and coil span.

### 3.1.2 Power Developed in Synchronous Motor (Per Phase)

For a cylindrical rotor, the **synchronous reactance** $X_s = X_a + X_l$ (armature reaction reactance + leakage reactance).

The phasor diagram gives the air-gap power transfer (per phase) as:

$$P = \frac{V_t \cdot E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

**Derivation (textbook level):**

Consider the simplified phasor equation:
$$V_t = E_f + j \, I_a \, X_s$$

Multiply by $I_a^*$ (complex conjugate) to get power:
$$P + jQ = V_t I_a^* = E_f I_a^* + j \, X_s \, I_a I_a^*$$

The real part gives:
$$P = \text{Re}(V_t I_a^*) = \frac{V_t \, E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

**Each transformation explained:**
- The angle $\delta$ between $V_t$ and $E_f$ is the **load angle**.
- $V_t \, E_f / X_s$ is the **maximum power transfer** (when $\sin\delta = 1$).
- $\delta = 0$ ‚Üí no power transfer (rotor is at no-load, EMF in phase with terminal voltage).
- $\delta = 90¬į$ ‚Üí maximum (pull-out) power.
- $\delta > 90¬į$ ‚Üí motor **pulls out of step** (instability).

### 3.1.3 Torque Equation of Synchronous Motor

Mechanical power developed (per phase) is $P_m = P \times \text{(mechanical efficiency factor)}$.

In a 3-phase synchronous motor, total mechanical power developed is:
$$P_m = \frac{3 \, V_t \, E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

Torque developed (in Nm) is:
$$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \frac{3 \, V_t \, E_f \, \sin\delta}{\omega_s \, X_s}$$

where $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60}$ rad/s.

**Pull-out (breakdown) torque** occurs at $\delta = 90¬į$ ($\sin\delta_{max} = 1$):
$$T_{max} = \frac{3 \, V_t \, E_f}{\omega_s \, X_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that $\omega_s$ is **synchronous angular speed** (in rad/s), not rotor speed. For a synchronous motor, rotor speed = synchronous speed, so this distinction is clear. But the formula is in rad/s ‚ÄĒ convert $N_s$ from rpm: $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Three-Phase Induction Motor ‚Äď EMF, Current, and Torque

### 3.2.1 Rotor Induced EMF Equation (Standstill)

At standstill, the rotor frequency equals stator frequency $f$. EMF induced in one rotor phase:

$$E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi \, k_{w2}$$

### 3.2.2 Rotor Induced EMF Equation (Running Condition at Slip $s$)

When the rotor runs at slip $s$:
- Relative speed between RMF and rotor = $N_s - N_r = s \, N_s$.
- Frequency of rotor EMF: $f_r = s \cdot f$.
- Rotor EMF: $E_{2r} = 4.44 \, f_r \, N_2 \, \phi \, k_{w2} = 4.44 \, s \, f \, N_2 \, \phi \, k_{w2}$.
- Hence: $E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$ (where $E_2$ is the standstill value).

### 3.2.3 Rotor Circuit Analysis

Rotor resistance $R_2$ is **constant** (does not depend on slip ‚ÄĒ only on temperature and skin effect).
Rotor leakage reactance at slip $s$:
$$X_{2r} = 2\pi f_r \, L_2 = 2\pi (s f) L_2 = s \cdot X_2$$

where $X_2$ is rotor leakage reactance at standstill.

**Rotor current (referred to stator side):**
$$I_2' = \frac{E_{2r}}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_{2r}^2}} = \frac{s \, E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$$

### 3.2.4 Rotor Power Factor

$$\cos\phi_2 = \frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$$

### 3.2.5 Mechanical Power Developed

The air-gap power (per phase) is:
$$P_{g,phase} = E_{2r} \, I_2' \cos\phi_2 = \frac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

Total air-gap power (3-phase):
$$P_g = 3 \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

Rotor Cu loss (3-phase):
$$P_{rcl} = 3 \, I_2'^2 \, R_2 = 3 \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} \cdot R_2 = 3 \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

Mechanical power developed (3-phase):
$$P_m = P_g - P_{rcl} = 3 \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} - 3 \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} = 3 \cdot \frac{s(1-s) E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

**Verification using $P_m = (1-s) P_g$:**
$$P_m = (1-s) P_g = (1-s) \cdot 3 \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} = 3 \cdot \frac{s(1-s) E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} \;\; \checkmark$$

### 3.2.6 Torque Equation

$$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \, E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

This is the **KTU Gold-Standard Torque Equation** for an induction motor.

### 3.2.7 Condition for Maximum Torque

Differentiate $T$ w.r.t. $s$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dT}{ds} = 0 \implies \frac{d}{ds}\left[\frac{s}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}\right] = 0$$

Let $u = s$ and $v = R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2$:
$$\frac{d}{ds}\left(\frac{u}{v}\right) = \frac{v \cdot du/ds - u \cdot dv/ds}{v^2} = 0$$

This gives:
$$v - u \cdot \frac{dv}{ds} = 0$$
$$R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2 - s \cdot (2s X_2^2) = 0$$
$$R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2 - 2s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$
$$R_2^2 - s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$
$$s^2 X_2^2 = R_2^2$$

$$\boxed{s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

**Substitute back into torque equation:**

At $s = s_m = R_2/X_2$:
- Denominator: $R_2^2 + (s_m X_2)^2 = R_2^2 + (R_2)^2 = 2R_2^2$
- Numerator: $s_m E_2^2 R_2 = (R_2/X_2) E_2^2 R_2 = E_2^2 R_2^2 / X_2$

Therefore:
$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 R_2^2 / X_2}{2R_2^2} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 \, E_2^2}{2 \, \omega_s \, X_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU 2024 Important):** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_2$**. However, $s_m$ (slip at which max torque occurs) is **directly proportional to $R_2$**. This is why inserting external resistance in the rotor (wound rotor motor) shifts the operating point to higher slip ‚ÄĒ the maximum torque point ‚ÄĒ without changing the magnitude of $T_{max}$.

### 3.2.8 Starting Torque

At $s = 1$:
$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 \, R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$

**Ratio of starting torque to maximum torque:**
$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 R_2 X_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} = \frac{2(R_2/X_2)}{1 + (R_2/X_2)^2}$$

Let $a = R_2 / X_2 = s_m$. Then:
$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{2a}{1 + a^2}$$

This is a **KTU board favorite derivation** ‚ÄĒ it is often asked to prove this relation.

### 3.2.9 Numerical Example ‚Äď Worked Out

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.05 \, \Omega$ and standstill reactance $X_2 = 0.5 \, \Omega$. If the standstill rotor EMF per phase is $E_2 = 100 \, V$ (referred to stator), find: (a) Synchronous speed, (b) Slip at maximum torque, (c) Maximum torque, (d) Starting torque. Take $\omega_s = 2\pi \times 1500 / 60 = 50\pi$ rad/s.

**Solution:**

**(a) Synchronous speed:**
$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$
$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(b) Slip at maximum torque:**
$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.05}{0.5} = 0.1 \;\; (10\%)$$

**(c) Maximum torque:**
$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2} = \frac{3 \times (100)^2}{2 \times 50\pi \times 0.5} = \frac{30000}{50\pi} = \frac{600}{\pi} \approx 190.99 \text{ Nm}$$

**(d) Starting torque:**
$$T_{st} = \frac{3 E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)} = \frac{3 \times 10000 \times 0.05}{50\pi \times (0.0025 + 0.25)} = \frac{1500}{50\pi \times 0.2525} = \frac{1500}{12.5\pi \times 0.2525}$$
$$= \frac{1500}{9.916} \approx 151.27 \text{ Nm}$$

**Verification of $T_{st}/T_{max}$:**
$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{151.27}{190.99} \approx 0.792 = \frac{2a}{1+a^2} = \frac{2 \times 0.1}{1+0.01} = \frac{0.2}{1.01} \approx 0.198$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Error:** Confusing $\omega_s$ (synchronous angular speed) with the rotor mechanical angular speed. In the torque equation, **always use $\omega_s$**, not the rotor speed, because the air-gap power is divided by the *synchronous* speed to get electromagnetic torque. (Mechanical power after slip is divided by the *rotor* speed.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Synchronous vs. Induction Motor ‚Äď The KTU Comparison Chart

| Parameter | Synchronous Motor | Three-Phase Induction Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Speed formula | $N_s = 120f/P$ (constant) | $N_r = (1-s) N_s$ (variable) |
| Slip at steady state | Zero (s = 0) | $s$ ‚Čą 0.02 to 0.05 |
| Self-starting | No (needs pony motor/damper) | Yes |
| Power factor | Controllable (lagging/unity/leading) | Always lagging at full load |
| Rotor excitation | DC (separate source) | Induced (no source) |
| Rotor construction | Salient pole / cylindrical | Squirrel cage / wound |
| Slip rings | Yes (for DC excitation) | Only for wound rotor type |
| Speed regulation | Perfect (őĒN = 0) | ~3‚Äď5% drop from no-load to full-load |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Maintenance | Moderate | Low (squirrel cage: very low) |
| Torque-speed curve | Constant torque (locked at Ns) | Hyperbolic (torque ‚ąĚ 1/s approx.) |
| Use of damper winding | For starting and damping oscillation | Not present |
| Application | Power factor correction, large drives | Pumps, fans, compressors, conveyors |
| Typical efficiency | 95‚Äď99% | 85‚Äď95% |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Synchronous Motor ‚Äď Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a synchronous motor system, including the excitation, starting, and synchronization subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Three-Phase AC Mains Supply<br/>50/60 Hz Fixed Frequency] --> B[Stator Winding<br/>Balanced 3-Phase Distributed]
    B --> C[Rotating Magnetic Field RMF<br/>Speed Ns equals 120f over P]
    C --> D[Air Gap Magnetic Coupling]
    D --> E[Rotor Field Winding<br/>DC Excited via Slip Rings]
    E --> F[Constant Rotor Magnetic Field]
    F --> G[Load Angle Delta<br/>Mechanical Lag from RMF Axis]
    G --> H[Torque Production<br/>T proportional to sin delta]
    H --> I[Mechanical Shaft Output<br/>Constant Speed Ns]
    I --> J[Industrial Load<br/>Constant Speed Drive]

    K[Damper Winding<br/>Embedded in Pole Faces] --> L[Asynchronous Starting<br/>Acts as Induction Motor at Start]
    L --> M[Near-Synchronous Acceleration]
    M --> N[DC Excitation Applied]
    N --> O[Synchronization Achieved<br/>Rotor Locks with RMF]
    O --> D

    P[Excitation Control System<br/>DC Source plus Field Regulator] --> E
    P --> Q[V Curve Behavior<br/>Ia versus If for Constant Load]
    Q --> R[Power Factor Control<br/>Unity, Lagging, or Leading]
    R --> S[Over Excited Mode<br/>Acts as Synchronous Condenser]
    S --> T[Reactive Power to Grid<br/>Power Factor Correction]
```

**Block-Level Explanation of the Architecture:**

1. **Supply Subsystem:** 3-phase AC mains feeds the stator.
2. **Stator Subsystem:** Generates RMF.
3. **Air Gap:** Magnetic coupling medium between stator and rotor.
4. **Rotor Subsystem:** DC-excited field with constant flux.
5. **Damper/Amortisseur Winding:** Handles asynchronous starting and damps hunting.
6. **Excitation Control:** Allows power factor correction through field current variation.
7. **Output Subsystem:** Mechanical shaft power to constant-speed industrial load.

---

## 4.2 Three-Phase Induction Motor ‚Äď Power Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3-Phase AC Mains<br/>Voltage V1, Frequency f] --> B[Stator Copper Loss<br/>3 times I1 squared R1]
    B --> C[Iron Loss<br/>Hysteresis plus Eddy Current]
    C --> D[Air Gap Power Pg<br/>Power Transferred to Rotor]
    D --> E[Rotor Copper Loss<br/>3 times I2 prime squared R2 prime<br/>Equals s times Pg]
    D --> F[Mechanical Power Developed Pm<br/>Equals 1 minus s times Pg]
    F --> G[Rotational Losses<br/>Friction, Windage, Stray Load]
    G --> H[Shaft Output Pout<br/>Net Useful Mechanical Power]

    I[Rotor EMF Induced<br/>E2 equals 4.44 f N2 phi kw] --> D
    J[Slip s<br/>Equals Ns minus Nr over Ns] --> D
    J --> E
    J --> F
```

**Block-by-Block Interpretation:**

- **Input ‚Üí Stator Cu Loss:** The first drop is the stator I¬≤R loss.
- **Stator Cu Loss ‚Üí Iron Loss:** Next drop accounts for core losses.
- **Remaining Power ‚Üí Air Gap Power:** This is the electromagnetic power crossing the air gap.
- **Air Gap Power ‚Üí Rotor Cu Loss + Mechanical Power:** Split by the slip ratio:
  - $P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_g$ (lost as heat in rotor)
  - $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_g$ (converted to mechanical form)
- **Mechanical Power ‚Üí Rotational Losses ‚Üí Shaft Output:** Net useful power.

---

## 4.3 Synchronous Motor ‚Äď Power Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AC Input to Stator<br/>3 times V1 I1 cos phi] --> B[Stator Copper Loss<br/>3 times Ia squared Ra]
    B --> C[Iron Loss<br/>Hysteresis plus Eddy Current]
    C --> D[Mechanical Power Developed<br/>3 times V Ef sin delta over Xs]
    D --> E[Mechanical Losses<br/>Friction plus Windage]
    E --> F[Shaft Output Pout]
    G[DC Excitation Input<br/>Vf times If] --> H[Field Copper Loss<br/>Vf times If]
    H --> D
```

**Note:** The DC field input is small (typically 0.5‚Äď2% of rated power) and contributes to the excitation flux ‚ÄĒ it does not contribute to power output directly.

---

## 4.4 Torque-Slip Characteristic of an Induction Motor (Block-Mapped Regions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slip s equals 0<br/>Synchronous Speed<br/>No Torque] --> B[Slip Range 0 to sm<br/>Stable Motor Region]
    B --> C[Slip equals sm<br/>Maximum Torque Point]
    C --> D[Slip Range sm to 1<br/>Unstable Region during Start]
    D --> E[Slip equals 1<br/>Standstill<br/>Starting Torque Tst]

    B --> F[Normal Operating Zone<br/>s typically 0.02 to 0.05]
    F --> G[Full Load Operation<br/>Constant Torque Region]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style F fill:#e1ffe1
```

**Key Regions Identified:**

- **Region 0 ‚Üí s_m (Stable Motor Region):** Torque increases with slip. This is the **normal operating range** for a motor.
- **s = s_m (Maximum Torque):** Peak of the curve. Beyond this, torque decreases.
- **Region s_m ‚Üí 1 (Unstable during Start):** Torque decreases as slip increases, but the motor still accelerates from rest.
- **s = 1 (Standstill):** Starting torque, which may be **less than, equal to, or greater than** full-load torque depending on rotor design.

---

## 4.5 Synchronous Motor Phasor Diagram (Cylindrical Rotor, Over-Excited Leading p.f.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vt Terminal Voltage<br/>Reference Phasor] --> B[Ia Times Ra<br/>In Phase with Ia]
    B --> C[Induced EMF Ef<br/>Lags Vt by delta]
    C --> D[j Ia Xs<br/>Perpendicular to Ia]
    D --> A

    A --> E[Ia Armature Current<br/>Leads Vt in Over Excited Mode]
    E --> F[Phi Leading Power Factor Angle]
    F --> A
```

**Key Phasor Interpretations (KTU 2024):**

- $V_t$ is the reference.
- $E_f$ lags $V_t$ by load angle $\delta$.
- $I_a$ leads $V_t$ by $\phi$ (in over-excited mode) ‚ÄĒ power factor is **leading**.
- The voltage drop $I_a R_a$ is in phase with $I_a$, and $j I_a X_s$ is perpendicular.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äď Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Synchronous Motor and State its Key Feature

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023 Model Question, Module 2, 3 marks]**

> Define a synchronous motor. Why does a synchronous motor have a constant speed irrespective of load? Mention one industrial application.

**Model Answer:**

A synchronous motor is a doubly-excited AC machine in which the stator is supplied with a 3-phase AC supply (producing a rotating magnetic field) and the rotor is excited with a DC source (producing a constant magnetic field). The rotor runs at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$, locked in step with the stator RMF.

**Constant Speed Feature:** Since the rotor field and the stator RMF are **magnetically locked** (no relative motion at steady state), the rotor must rotate at the same speed as the RMF. Any change in load only changes the **load angle $\delta$** (the angular displacement between rotor and stator field axes) ‚ÄĒ not the speed. The rotor essentially "pulls back" by a small angle to deliver more torque, but its speed remains $N_s$.

**Industrial Application:** Synchronous motor used as a **synchronous condenser** for power factor correction in industrial substations (when over-excited, it delivers reactive power to the grid).

---

### Question 2: Define Slip and Explain Why an Induction Motor Cannot Run at Synchronous Speed

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022 Model Question, Module 2, 3 marks]**

> Define slip in a three-phase induction motor. Why is the slip never zero in an induction motor at steady state?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Slip:** Slip $s$ is the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field (RMF) and the rotor, expressed as a fraction (or percentage) of the synchronous speed:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

where $N_s$ is synchronous speed and $N_r$ is actual rotor speed.

**Why Slip Cannot Be Zero:**

For the rotor to develop torque, there must be a **relative motion** between the stator RMF and the rotor conductors. This relative motion is what **induces** an EMF in the rotor (by Faraday's Law), which drives rotor current, which interacts with the stator flux to produce torque.

- If $s = 0$, then $N_r = N_s$ ‚ÄĒ rotor and RMF are stationary relative to each other.
- No relative motion ‚Üí no rate of change of flux linkage ‚Üí **no induced EMF** ‚Üí **no rotor current** ‚Üí **no torque**.
- Without torque, the rotor would decelerate due to load, increasing slip and restoring torque production.

Hence, an induction motor always operates with a **finite slip** (typically 2‚Äď5% at full load). The slip is the **cause** of torque, not the consequence.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äď Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a three-phase synchronous motor. Discuss the role of damper windings. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor runs at 960 rpm on full load. Calculate: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Slip, (iii) Frequency of rotor EMF, (iv) Rotor Cu loss as a percentage of air-gap power. The mechanical losses are 600 W. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to (a):

**Construction:**

A synchronous motor has two main parts:

**Stator:** The stator consists of a laminated silicon-steel core with slots on its inner periphery. A 3-phase distributed winding is placed in the slots, with the three phases displaced by 120¬į in space. The stator is identical to that of a 3-phase induction motor.

**Rotor (Two Types):**
- **Salient Pole Rotor:** Poles project out from the rotor surface; field winding is wound around each pole. Used for low/medium speeds. Has non-uniform air gap.
- **Non-Salient (Cylindrical) Rotor:** Smooth cylindrical rotor with field winding distributed in slots. Used for high-speed applications. Has uniform air gap.

**Damper (Amortisseur) Windings:** Embedded in the pole faces of salient-pole rotors. They are short-circuited copper or brass bars. Their role is:
1. To provide **starting torque** as an induction motor at standstill.
2. To **damp mechanical oscillations (hunting)** during transient load changes once synchronized.

**Working Principle:**

1. The 3-phase stator winding is energized, producing a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$.
2. The rotor is initially at rest. If DC excitation is applied directly, no average torque develops because the rotor inertia cannot follow the fast RMF.
3. The damper windings act as a shorted squirrel cage ‚ÄĒ the rotating flux induces current in them, producing torque like an induction motor. The rotor accelerates.
4. As rotor speed approaches $N_s$, DC excitation is applied to the rotor field winding. The rotor field and the stator RMF now attract each other, **locking** the rotor into synchronism.
5. At synchronous speed, the rotor runs in step with the RMF. The **load angle $\delta$** develops ‚ÄĒ a small angular lag that produces the required torque. As load increases, $\delta$ increases (up to a maximum of 90¬į at pull-out).

**Self-Starting Limitation:** A purely synchronous motor (without damper windings) is **not self-starting** because the average torque over a cycle is zero at standstill. The damper winding is essential for self-starting in salient-pole machines.

---

#### Model Answer to (b):

**Given Data:**
- Number of poles, $P = 6$
- Frequency, $f = 50$ Hz
- Rotor speed, $N_r = 960$ rpm
- Mechanical losses, $P_{rot} = 600$ W

**Step 1: Synchronous Speed**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Slip**

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1000 - 960}{1000} = \frac{40}{1000} = 0.04 \text{ or } 4\%$$

**Step 3: Frequency of Rotor EMF**

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: Rotor Cu Loss as Percentage of Air-Gap Power**

Using the key relation:
$$\frac{P_{rcl}}{P_g} = s = 0.04 = 4\%$$

Therefore, **rotor Cu loss = 4% of air-gap power**.

This is an elegant result: knowing slip alone gives the rotor Cu loss percentage without needing any other data.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks ‚Äď Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the torque developed in a three-phase induction motor. Hence, obtain the condition for maximum torque and the expression for maximum torque. Show that the maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a suitable phasor diagram, explain the operation of a synchronous motor at (i) normal excitation, (ii) over-excitation, and (iii) under-excitation. What is a synchronous condenser? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to (a):

**Derivation of Torque Equation:**

The rotor EMF at slip $s$ is:
$$E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$$

The rotor leakage reactance at slip $s$ is:
$$X_{2r} = s \cdot X_2$$

Rotor impedance (referred to stator):
$$Z_2 = \sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

Rotor current:
$$I_2 = \frac{s E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$$

Air-gap power per phase (real part of $E_{2r} I_2^*$):
$$P_{g,phase} = \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

Total air-gap power (3-phase):
$$P_g = \frac{3 s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

Mechanical power developed:
$$P_m = (1 - s) P_g = \frac{3 s(1-s) E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

Torque developed:
$$\boxed{T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$$

This is the **KTU gold-standard torque equation** for the induction motor.

**Condition for Maximum Torque:**

Differentiating $T$ w.r.t. $s$ and equating to zero:
$$\frac{dT}{ds} = 0 \implies \frac{d}{ds}\left[\frac{s}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}\right] = 0$$

Applying quotient rule:
$$\frac{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)(1) - s(2s X_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2} = 0$$

Numerator = 0:
$$R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$
$$R_2^2 - s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$
$$\boxed{s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

This is the **slip at maximum torque** (also called critical slip).

**Expression for Maximum Torque:**

Substituting $s = R_2/X_2$ into the torque equation:
- Numerator: $s E_2^2 R_2 = (R_2/X_2) E_2^2 R_2 = E_2^2 R_2^2/X_2$
- Denominator: $R_2^2 + (R_2/X_2)^2 X_2^2 = R_2^2 + R_2^2 = 2 R_2^2$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 R_2^2 / X_2}{2 R_2^2} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}}$$

**Independence from Rotor Resistance:** The final expression for $T_{max}$ contains **no term $R_2$** ‚ÄĒ hence, the maximum torque is **independent of rotor resistance**. However, the slip $s_m = R_2/X_2$ at which $T_{max}$ occurs **does depend on $R_2$**. This is the principle used in **wound rotor induction motors** for speed control: external rotor resistance is varied to shift the operating point and improve starting torque, without changing the magnitude of maximum torque.

---

#### Model Answer to (b):

**Phasor Diagram of Synchronous Motor:**

For a cylindrical rotor synchronous motor, the phasor equation is:
$$\vec{V_t} = \vec{E_f} + \vec{I_a}(R_a + j X_s)$$

In a simplified representation (ignoring $R_a$ for clarity):
$$\vec{V_t} = \vec{E_f} + j \vec{I_a} X_s$$

**Three Operating Conditions:**

**(i) Normal Excitation (Unity Power Factor):**
- $E_f$ is adjusted such that the armature current $I_a$ is in phase with $V_t$.
- Power factor = 1.
- The motor draws only real power from the supply.

**(ii) Over-Excited (Leading Power Factor):**
- $E_f$ is increased beyond the normal value.
- $I_a$ now **leads** $V_t$ by angle $\phi$.
- Power factor is **leading**.
- The motor draws real power + supplies reactive power to the grid.
- This is the **synchronous condenser** mode of operation.

**(iii) Under-Excited (Lagging Power Factor):**
- $E_f$ is reduced below normal value.
- $I_a$ now **lags** $V_t$ by angle $\phi$.
- Power factor is **lagging**.
- The motor draws real power + reactive power from the grid.
- This is similar to an induction motor behavior.

**V-Curve Concept:** For a constant mechanical load, as $I_f$ is varied, $I_a$ follows a V-shaped curve. The locus of unity-pf points is the **inverted V-curve** (also called the **normal excitation curve** or **unity pf compound curve**).

**Synchronous Condenser:** A synchronous motor operated **over-excited and at no mechanical load** is called a synchronous condenser. Its sole purpose is to **supply reactive power (VARs) to the grid** for power factor correction. It behaves like a variable capacitor whose reactance can be adjusted by varying the DC field excitation. In modern power systems, synchronous condensers are used to:
- Improve grid power factor.
- Stabilize voltage under heavy inductive loads (arc furnaces, large motors).
- Provide short-circuit current capacity for grid stability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (MUST READ):**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention for Power Factor:** When drawing phasor diagrams of synchronous motors, the **direction of $I_a$** determines the p.f. (leading or lagging). Students often draw $I_a$ as lagging in over-excited mode ‚ÄĒ this is the **opposite** of correct. Over-excited ‚Üí **leading** p.f.; Under-excited ‚Üí **lagging** p.f.
>
> 2. **Use of $\omega_s$ vs. $\omega_r$ in Torque Equation:** The torque equation $T = P_m / \omega_s$ uses **synchronous angular speed** in rad/s. Students frequently divide by $2\pi N_r/60$ (rotor mechanical speed), which is **incorrect** for the electromagnetic torque. Only for the **shaft torque** (after subtracting mechanical losses) do we use $\omega_r$ for some definitions. KTU 2024 valuations will deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
>
> 3. **Independence of $T_{max}$ from $R_2$:** When asked to "show that $T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$", students often skip the substitution step. The full substitution $s_m = R_2/X_2$ into the torque equation must be shown explicitly. **Do not skip algebraic steps.**
>
> 4. **Synchronous Motor Self-Starting:** A plain synchronous motor is **NOT self-starting**. The damper windings provide starting torque as an induction motor, but the motor only locks to synchronous speed when the rotor reaches near $N_s$ and DC excitation is applied. A common KTU trap question asks: "Why is a synchronous motor not self-starting?" ‚ÄĒ students often answer vaguely; the precise answer is that the **average torque over a complete cycle is zero** at standstill because the rotor field alternates relative to the rotor.
>
> 5. **Numericals on Torque-Slip Curve:** When asked to "show starting torque is less than maximum torque", the relation $T_{st}/T_{max} = 2s_m/(1+s_m^2)$ must be derived. A common mistake: students write $T_{st} = T_{max}$ ‚ÄĒ this is **only true** when $s_m = 1$, which means $R_2 = X_2$ ‚ÄĒ a special case, not a general result.
>
> 6. **Rotor EMF at Slip $s$:** Always state $E_{2r} = s E_2$ ‚ÄĒ students often forget the factor of $s$ in rotor EMF and reactance. This is worth 2 marks in 14-mark problems.
>
> 7. **Power Factor Correction Question:** When asked "How does a synchronous motor improve power factor?" ‚ÄĒ answer: **By operating over-excited at no-load or light-load, it delivers reactive power to the grid.** Simply saying "it has leading p.f." is **not a complete answer** ‚ÄĒ explain the *mechanism*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 ‚Äď Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Synchronous Motor ‚Äď Key Points

- ‚úÖ **Synchronous speed formula:** $N_s = 120f/P$ ‚ÄĒ memorize first.
- ‚úÖ **Doubly excited** machine: AC on stator, DC on rotor.
- ‚úÖ **Constant speed** ‚ÄĒ does not vary with load.
- ‚úÖ **Not self-starting** ‚ÄĒ needs damper windings or pony motor.
- ‚úÖ **Load angle $\delta$** ‚ÄĒ torque $T \propto \sin\delta$; pull-out at $\delta = 90¬į$.
- ‚úÖ **V-curve** ‚ÄĒ $I_a$ vs $I_f$ for constant load; over-excited ‚Üí leading p.f. (synchronous condenser).
- ‚úÖ **Power factor controllable** by varying $I_f$ ‚ÄĒ unique to synchronous machines.
- ‚úÖ **Applications:** Power factor correction, large constant-speed drives, voltage regulators.

### Three-Phase Induction Motor ‚Äď Key Points

- ‚úÖ **Singly excited** machine ‚ÄĒ only AC on stator, rotor currents are **induced**.
- ‚úÖ **Slip $s$** ‚ÄĒ heart of IM operation; $s = 0$ at synchronous speed, $s = 1$ at standstill.
- ‚úÖ **Slip is the cause of torque** ‚ÄĒ cannot be zero in steady state.
- ‚úÖ **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s \cdot f$.
- ‚úÖ **Rotor EMF** $E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$; **Rotor reactance** $X_{2r} = s \cdot X_2$.
- ‚úÖ **Power stages:** $P_{in} \rightarrow P_{scl} \rightarrow P_g \rightarrow P_{rcl} + P_m \rightarrow P_{out}$.
- ‚úÖ **Key relation:** $P_{rcl} = s P_g$ and $P_m = (1-s) P_g$.
- ‚úÖ **Torque equation:** $T = \dfrac{3 s E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s [R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2]}$.
- ‚úÖ **Max torque condition:** $s_m = R_2/X_2$.
- ‚úÖ **Max torque magnitude:** $T_{max} = 3 E_2^2 / (2 \omega_s X_2)$ ‚ÄĒ **independent of $R_2$**.
- ‚úÖ **Starting torque:** $T_{st} = 3 E_2^2 R_2 / [\omega_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)]$.
- ‚úÖ **Squirrel cage vs wound rotor:** Cage is simple/rugged; wound allows external R for starting.
- ‚úÖ **Operating regions:** Motor mode ($0 < s < 1$), Generator mode ($s < 0$), Braking mode ($s > 1$).

### Numerical Problem-Solving Framework (KTU 2024 Standard)

1. **Read given data carefully** ‚ÄĒ identify $f$, $P$, $V$, $R_2$, $X_2$, $N_r$ (or $s$).
2. **Compute $N_s$** first (always).
3. **Compute $s$** from $N_r$ (or vice versa).
4. **For synchronous motor:** Use $T = 3 V E_f \sin\delta / (\omega_s X_s)$.
5. **For induction motor:** Use $T = 3 s E_2^2 R_2 / [\omega_s (R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2)]$.
6. **For power calculations:** Use $P_{rcl} = s P_g$ and $P_m = (1-s) P_g$ shortcut.
7. **Convert units:** $N$ in rpm, $\omega$ in rad/s = $2\pi N/60$; $T$ in Nm; $P$ in W.

### Quick Memory Tricks (KTU 2024 Mnemonics)

- **"Slip is the price you pay for torque"** ‚ÄĒ slip ‚Č† free; it's the energy lost in rotor Cu loss.
- **"Synchronous motor: speed is set by the supply, not the load"** ‚ÄĒ distinguishes it from IM.
- **"Over-excited = Leading p.f. = Reactive power source"** ‚ÄĒ useful for memory.
- **"Damping = Death of oscillations"** ‚ÄĒ damper windings kill hunting in SM.
- **"P_m = (1-s) √ó P_g"** ‚ÄĒ the 1-s factor is the "useful fraction" of air-gap power.

### Common KTU Board Pitfalls (Final Summary)

- ‚ĚĆ Mixing up $E_2$ and $E_{2r} = s E_2$.
- ‚ĚĆ Dividing torque equation by $\omega_r$ instead of $\omega_s$.
- ‚ĚĆ Saying synchronous motor is self-starting.
- ‚ĚĆ Confusing leading and lagging p.f. of synchronous motor with excitation.
- ‚ĚĆ Forgetting the factor of 3 (3-phase) in 3-phase equations.
- ‚ĚĆ Using $\cos\phi$ when the formula expects $\sin\delta$ (and vice versa).
- ‚ĚĆ Not converting rpm to rad/s when computing torque.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes ‚ÄĒ AC Motors (Synchronous & Three-Phase Induction Motor)**

topics.title: Special Electrical Motors - Stepper Motor, Servo Motor, Brushless DC Motors, Hysteresis Motor, Reluctance Motor, Universal Motor, Electrostatic Motor, Piezoelectric Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Special Electrical Motors - Stepper Motor, Servo Motor, Brushless DC Motors, Hysteresis Motor, Reluctance Motor, Universal Motor, Electrostatic Motor, Piezoelectric Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Special Electrical Motors

**Special Electrical Motors** are a class of non-conventional or application-specific machines designed to deliver highly specialized mechanical output characteristics‚ÄĒsuch as precise angular positioning, high-speed operation, fractional-horsepower AC/DC compatibility, or contactless actuation‚ÄĒthat standard induction or DC shunt motors cannot achieve. In KTU's instrumentation curriculum, these motors form the **actuation backbone** of robotics, CNC machines, medical devices, aerospace, and precision instrumentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must understand the **construction, operating principle, characteristics, and applications** of each of the eight motor types listed. Examiner focus is on **principle diagrams, torque-speed characteristics, and comparative analysis** between motor types.

---

### 1.2 Stepper Motor

**Definition:** A *stepper motor* is a brushless, synchronous electromechanical device that converts discrete electrical pulses into discrete mechanical angular steps. Each electrical pulse advances the rotor by a fixed, precisely defined angular increment called the **step angle (ő≤)**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a digital clock's second hand that doesn't sweep continuously but instead *jumps* from one second mark to the next. A stepper motor behaves the same way‚ÄĒits shaft rotates in identical, repeatable "jumps" rather than smooth continuous motion. If you send 200 pulses, it turns exactly 200 steps; no encoder feedback required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics:**
> - **Step Angle (ő≤)** = 360¬į / (Number of steps per revolution)
> - **Resolution** = Smallest angular displacement = ő≤
> - **Holding Torque** = Torque required to displace rotor from rest (static position-holding capability)

**Types of Stepper Motors:**
1. **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** ‚Äď Rotor aligns to minimum reluctance path
2. **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor** ‚Äď Rotor has permanent magnet poles
3. **Hybrid (HB) Stepper Motor** ‚Äď Combines VR + PM principles for finer steps

---

### 1.3 Servo Motor

**Definition:** A *servo motor* is a rotary or linear actuator that allows for precise control of angular or linear position, velocity, and acceleration using a closed-loop feedback system (typically a position sensor like an encoder or resolver feeding back to the controller).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a **power steering system in a car**‚ÄĒthe driver (controller) commands a direction, sensors detect deviation, and the hydraulic system continuously corrects the wheel's actual position to match the intended position. A servo motor does exactly this electronically, thousands of times per second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Stepper:** Servo = **closed-loop** (feedback controlled); Stepper = **open-loop** (no feedback, position assumed from count of pulses). Servo is faster and more accurate at high speeds; stepper is simpler and provides full torque at standstill.

**Sub-classes:**
- **AC Servo Motor** (two-phase induction, drag-cup rotor)
- **DC Servo Motor** (armature-controlled or field-controlled)

---

### 1.4 Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor

**Definition:** A *brushless DC motor* is a synchronous motor with a permanent magnet rotor and electronically commutated stator winding. The mechanical commutator of a conventional DC motor is replaced by an **electronic controller** that switches stator phases based on rotor position feedback (from Hall sensors or back-EMF sensing).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Compare a **ceiling fan with remote control** to an old **hand-cranked fan with carbon brushes**. The BLDC is the modern, brush-free ceiling fan where electronics (remote/inverter) perform the switching that brushes used to do‚ÄĒresulting in no sparking, less wear, and higher efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** BLDC motors are the standard for **drones, EV traction, computer fans, and robotics** because of their high power density, efficiency (85‚Äď95%), and long life.

---

### 1.5 Hysteresis Motor

**Definition:** A *hysteresis motor* is a single-phase, synchronous motor with a smooth cylindrical rotor made of high-retentivity, high-coercivity hard magnetic material (e.g., chrome or cobalt steel). It operates on the principle of **hysteresis torque** arising from the lagging of rotor flux behind the stator rotating field.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine spinning a **magnetized steel cylinder** in a rotating magnetic field. The rotor's magnetic domains "prefer" to stay in their previously magnetized direction (memory effect = hysteresis), creating a continuous tangential pull that synchronizes the rotor to the field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Salient feature:** Self-starting, smooth and vibration-free operation, constant torque from standstill to synchronism. Used in **tape recorders, clocks, and precision instruments**.

---

### 1.6 Reluctance Motor (Variable Reluctance / Synchronous Reluctance Motor)

**Definition:** A *reluctance motor* is a synchronous motor in which the rotor has salient poles (non-uniform air gap) and *no* field excitation (no DC supply to rotor, no permanent magnets). Torque is produced purely by the **tendency of the rotor to align with the minimum reluctance path** of the stator's rotating magnetic field.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Picture a **wooden arrow floating on water with a magnet beneath**. The arrow rotates to align with the magnet's path because the magnetic flux "prefers" the path of least resistance. Similarly, a salient-pole rotor rotates to align with the stator's rotating field.

**Two Operating Modes:**
- **Direct-online starting** (modern high-efficiency versions use rotor flux barriers)
- **Auxiliary winding starting** (historically, runs as induction motor until near-synchronous speed, then "pulls in" via reluctance torque)

---

### 1.7 Universal Motor

**Definition:** A *universal motor* is a series-wound commutator motor that can operate on **either AC or DC supply** of similar voltage. It is essentially a DC series motor built with **fully laminated construction** to reduce eddy-current losses under AC operation.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a **heavy-duty hand blender** that works equally well in Europe (230 V, 50 Hz AC) and in the USA (120 V, 60 Hz DC backup) because its series winding reverses torque with each AC half-cycle in lockstep with the armature‚ÄĒproducing unidirectional rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Universal motors are used where **high speed (>3000 rpm), high starting torque, and light weight** are required‚ÄĒ**mixers, hair dryers, sewing machines, portable drills, vacuum cleaners**.

---

### 1.8 Electrostatic Motor

**Definition:** An *electrostatic motor* (or capacitor motor) is a non-magnetic actuator that converts electrical energy into mechanical work using the **Coulomb force** between charged electrodes. It typically employs a high-voltage (kV range), low-current AC or DC source applied to stator electrodes, with a rotor (often a dielectric or with induced charges) producing motion.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Recall the classic physics demonstration where a **charged balloon sticks to a wall** due to electrostatic attraction. An electrostatic motor applies a controlled, rotating electric field that pulls/pushes a rotor‚ÄĒmuch like a rotating invisible "sticky hand" that drags the rotor along.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Produces very low torque (micro-Newton-meter range), so they are limited to **MEMS, micro-actuators, and research-grade nano-positioning stages**. KTU expects awareness, not deep design analysis.

---

### 1.9 Piezoelectric Motor

**Definition:** A *piezoelectric motor* is a solid-state actuator that uses the **inverse piezoelectric effect**: when a high-frequency AC voltage is applied to a piezoelectric ceramic (e.g., PZT ‚Äď lead zirconate titanate), it produces tiny mechanical deformations (micro-strain) that, when coupled through a mechanical resonator, generate **elliptical motion at the contact point** driving a rotor or slider.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Visualize a **violin bow dragged across a spinning top**‚ÄĒthe bow's tiny back-and-forth motion (vibration) drags the top into rotation. In a piezoelectric motor, the "bow" is the vibrating piezo element, and the "top" is the rotor/slider. No magnetic field is involved!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Piezo motors offer **extreme precision (nanometer resolution), holding torque without power, and silent operation**‚ÄĒused in **camera autofocus lenses, watch stepping drives, scanning probe microscopes, and surgical robots**.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stepper Motor ‚Äď Working Principle & Equations

**Operating Principle (Variable Reluctance Type):**
- Stator has projecting poles with concentrated windings; rotor is a soft-iron cylinder with **salisortant teeth**.
- Energizing stator phases in sequence (A ‚Üí B ‚Üí C ‚Üí A‚Ä¶) causes rotor to align with the **minimum reluctance path** of the energized phase.
- Each switching event produces one **step**.

**Step Angle Derivation (VR Stepper):**

$$\beta = \frac{360¬į}{N_s \times N_r}$$

where:
- $N_s$ = number of stator teeth (including all poles)
- $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth
- $\beta$ = step angle in degrees

**Step Rate vs Shaft Speed:**

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{f \times 60}{\text{steps per revolution}} = \frac{60 \cdot f_s}{N}$$

where $f_s$ = pulse rate (pulses per second), $N$ = steps per revolution.

**Slew Range & Pull-out Torque:**
- **Slewing region:** motor rotates continuously at high pulse rates, torque is limited by inductance and friction.
- **Pull-out torque ($T_{po}$):** maximum torque the motor can deliver at a given stepping rate before losing synchronism.

**Static (Holding) Torque Equation:**

$$T_h = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

where $i$ is phase current and $L(\theta)$ is the angle-dependent inductance.

---

### 2.2 Servo Motor ‚Äď Transfer Function & Stability

**Block Diagram Structure:**

```
Input őł_ref ‚Üí [Comparator] ‚Üí Error e(t) ‚Üí [Controller Kp] ‚Üí [Motor & Load] ‚Üí Output őł
                                       ‚ÜĎ                                              |
                                       ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ Position Feedback (Pot/Encoder) ‚Üź‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēė
```

**Open-Loop Transfer Function of Motor (simplified):**

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(Js + B)}$$

where $J$ = moment of inertia, $B$ = damping coefficient, $K$ = motor gain.

**With Proportional Controller (Closed Loop):**

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{\theta_{ref}(s)} = \frac{K_p K}{Js^2 + Bs + K_p K}$$

**Key Performance Constants:**
- **Static Sensitivity / Loop Gain:** $K_v = K_p \cdot K$ (rad/s per rad input)
- **Damping Ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{J \cdot K_p K}}$
- **Natural Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_p K}{J}}$

---

### 2.3 Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor ‚Äď EMF & Torque

**Back-EMF Equation (per phase, trapezoidal BLDC):**

$$E = 2 \cdot N_{turns} \cdot B \cdot l \cdot r \cdot \omega = K_e \cdot \omega$$

where $K_e$ = back-EMF constant (V¬∑s/rad), $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s).

**Torque Equation:**

$$T = K_t \cdot I_a$$

with the fundamental SI relationship $K_t = K_e$ when units are consistent.

**Speed Equation (similar to DC motor):**

$$N = \frac{V_t - I_a R_a}{K_e \Phi}$$

**Speed-Torque Characteristic:** Linear, like a DC shunt motor but with electronic commutation.

> [!NOTE]
> In SI units, $K_e$ has units V/(rad/s) and numerically equals $K_t$ in N¬∑m/A‚ÄĒthis is the *energy conservation* principle. KTU students must remember this identity.

---

### 2.4 Hysteresis Motor ‚Äď Synchronous Speed & Torque

**Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$$

where $P$ = number of stator poles.

**Hysteresis Torque:**

$$T_h = \frac{V \cdot B_m \cdot H_m \cdot \sin(\delta)}{4\pi f} \propto \text{Area of } B\text{-}H \text{ loop}$$

where $\delta$ = lag angle between rotor and stator flux axes (during start-up), and the **hysteresis loop area** determines torque magnitude.

**Key Properties:**
- No rotor teeth or windings ‚Üí **uniform air gap, no cogging**.
- Torque remains **constant from standstill to synchronous speed** (unlike induction motor which has different starting and running torques).
- Smooth, vibration-free running; high inertia rotor.

---

### 2.5 Reluctance Motor ‚Äď Torque & Speed

**Reluctance Torque:**

$$T_r = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{d\mathcal{R}(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

where $\mathcal{R}(\theta)$ is the angle-dependent air-gap reluctance.

**Synchronous Speed (same as all synchronous machines):**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$$

**Operating Principle Recap:**
1. At standstill, rotor is pulled toward minimum reluctance by stator field ‚Üí produces **reluctance torque** (and induction torque if rotor has closed slots/cage for starting).
2. As rotor accelerates toward $N_s$, it is "pulled into synchronism."
3. Once synchronized, rotor runs at $N_s$ with constant load angle $\delta$.

---

### 2.6 Universal Motor ‚Äď Speed & Power

**EMF Equation (similar to DC series motor):**

$$E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

**Speed Equation:**

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{K \Phi}$$

**Why it works on AC:**
- On AC supply, both armature current $I_a$ and series field flux $\Phi$ reverse simultaneously every half-cycle.
- Torque $T \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$ remains **unidirectional** because product of two negatives is positive.
- Therefore, average torque is non-zero and motor rotates in one direction.

**Power & Speed:**
- Universal motors typically run at **8,000‚Äď20,000 rpm**‚ÄĒmuch higher than induction motors.
- Power range: a few watts to ~2 kW for portable applications.

---

### 2.7 Electrostatic Motor ‚Äď Coulomb's Law Foundation

**Force Between Two Charges (Coulomb's Law):**

$$F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$

where $\varepsilon_0$ = **8.854 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬≤ F/m** (permittivity of free space), $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of medium.

**Torque for a Simple Rotating-Disc Electrostatic Motor:**

$$T \propto \frac{V^2}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \cdot f(\text{geometry})$$

> [!WARNING]
> Force scales with $V^2$ ‚ÄĒ so high voltages (kV) are mandatory to generate even milli-Newton torques. KTU exam questions on this motor are usually conceptual, not calculation-based.

---

### 2.8 Piezoelectric Motor ‚Äď Resonance & Stroke

**Inverse Piezoelectric Strain:**

$$S = d \cdot E$$

where $d$ = piezoelectric strain constant (m/V), $E$ = applied electric field (V/m).

**Typical Values (PZT-5H):** $d_{33} \approx 593 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V

**Resonant Frequency (Longitudinal Bar):**

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{E_{Y}}{\rho}}$$

where $E_Y$ = Young's modulus, $\rho$ = density, $L$ = length of piezo element.

**Working Mechanism:**
- A piezo element vibrates at its resonant frequency (~20‚Äď100 kHz).
- Coupled to a slider/rotor, the elliptical tip motion drives it linearly/rotationally through **frictional contact**.

---
