> 5. **Confusing the GIC with the Gyrator** ‚ÄĒ A GIC and a Gyrator are related but not identical. A Gyrator converts a capacitor into an inductor (only 2-op-amp / 2-impedance minimum); the GIC is more general (5 impedances). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ‚úÖ **Antoniou's GIC conversion law:** $Z_{in} = \dfrac{Z_1 Z_3 Z_5}{Z_2 Z_4}$ ‚ÄĒ memorize verbatim.
- ‚úÖ **Inductor simulation:** Use 3 resistors in the numerator and 2 capacitors in the denominator; result is $L_{eq} = R_1 R_3 R_5 C_2 C_4$.
- ‚úÖ **FDNR (D-element) simulation:** Use 2 resistors in numerator and 3 capacitors in denominator; $D = R_1 R_3 R_5 C_2 C_4$.
- ‚úÖ **Capacitor simulation:** Use 3 capacitors in numerator and 2 resistors in denominator; $C_{eq} = C_1 C_3 C_5 R_2 R_4$.
- ‚úÖ **Op-amp choice for VLF:** Prefer low-offset, low-bias, low-noise op-amps (OP-07, OP-27, LT1012, LMC662). Avoid LM741 for sub-Hz filters.
- ‚úÖ **Component trade-off:** To get large $L_{eq}$, increase capacitors first (cheaper, more stable) before increasing resistors.
- ‚úÖ **Butterworth 2nd-order LPF cutoff:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq} C}}$, $Q = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.
- ‚úÖ **Frequency limits:** $f_L \approx 0.001\ \text{Hz}$ (limited by leakage, bias); $f_H \approx \text{GBW}/10$.
- ‚úÖ **Why VLF needs GIC:** A 1 H coil at 1 Hz is $\sim 1\ \text{kg}$ ‚ÄĒ GIC replaces it with a 14-pin IC + 5 passives.
- ‚úÖ **KTU 2024 Outcome mapping:** This topic tests **CO3** (Design of active filters using op-amps) and **CO4** (Apply simulation techniques for non-ideal components), Bloom's Level: **Apply / Analyze**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two-Op Amp ECG Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Two-Op Amp ECG Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Op Amp ECG Amplifier** is a specialized two-stage analog front-end circuit designed to extract the low-amplitude, low-frequency biopotential signal generated by the electrical activity of the human heart (typically **0.5 mV to 5 mV**, bandwidth **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**) while rejecting large common-mode interference (50/60 Hz mains pickup, body-electrode offset potentials up to **¬Ī300 mV**).

The circuit uses **two operational amplifiers** in cascade to achieve a total voltage gain of approximately **1000 (60 dB)**, a very high input impedance (> **5 Mő©**), and a Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) typically better than **80 dB** at 50 Hz, which is the minimum required for clinical-grade ECG acquisition.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ‚Äď "Listening to a Whisper in a Noisy Stadium":**
> Imagine trying to hear someone whispering (the ECG signal, ~1 mV) in a stadium filled with thousands of fans screaming (the 50 Hz mains interference and DC electrode offset, hundreds of millivolts).
>
> A **Two-Op Amp ECG Amplifier** acts like a pair of highly directional, smart microphones:
> - **Op-Amp 1 (Pre-amplifier)** sits close to the whisperer ‚ÄĒ it has a huge "input ear" (high input impedance) so it doesn't disturb the patient, and it crudely amplifies the whisper while partially ignoring the crowd noise.
> - **Op-Amp 2 (Difference / Gain / Filter stage)** acts like an intelligent noise-cancellation processor ‚ÄĒ it actively subtracts whatever noise is common to both ears (common-mode rejection) and sharpens the whisper into a clean, loud signal.
> - The result is a clear, amplified heartbeat waveform ready for the doctor's stethoscope (or display monitor).

## 1.3 Key Performance Specifications (Standard Metrics)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Why it Matters |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Differential Gain (Ad) | **1000 (60 dB)** | Boosts 1 mV ECG to 1 V for ADC |
| CMRR | **‚Č• 80 dB at 50 Hz** | Rejects mains line interference |
| Input Impedance (Zin) | **‚Č• 5 Mő©** | Prevents loading of skin-electrode interface |
| Bandwidth | **0.05 Hz ‚Äď 150 Hz** | Captures all clinically relevant ECG features (P, QRS, T) |
| Noise (RTI) | **< 2 őľV RMS** | Smaller than smallest ECG feature (P-wave ~25 őľV) |
| Power Supply | **¬Ī5 V to ¬Ī12 V DC** | Portable battery operation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Two-Op Amp ECG Amplifier is a critical design example illustrating the practical application of op-amp limitations (finite CMRR, input bias current, slew rate) in biomedical instrumentation systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ECG Signal ‚ÄĒ Why a Special Amplifier?

The human heart generates a bioelectric signal with the following characteristics:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Amplitude:** 0.5 mV ‚Äď 5 mV (very small)
> - **Frequency content:** 0.05 Hz ‚Äď 150 Hz (low frequency)
> - **Source impedance:** 1 kő© ‚Äď 100 kő© (skin-electrode contact)
> - **DC electrode offset:** up to ¬Ī300 mV (half-cell potential)
> - **Common-mode interference:** 50/60 Hz mains hum, RF interference, body-coupled noise

Because of these hostile conditions, the amplifier must:
1. Have **very high gain** (~1000) to bring the signal to ADC-usable level.
2. Have **very high input impedance** to avoid loading the high-impedance source.
3. Have **excellent CMRR** to reject 50 Hz interference.
4. Include **AC coupling (high-pass filter at ~0.05 Hz)** to block the large DC half-cell potential that would otherwise saturate the amplifier.

## 2.2 Architecture of the Two-Op Amp ECG Amplifier

A canonical two-op-amp ECG amplifier is built from **two cascaded stages**:

### Stage 1 ‚ÄĒ Instrumentation Pre-Amplifier (Modified Difference Amplifier)
- Provides most of the **differential gain** (‚Čą 10‚Äď50).
- Establishes the high input impedance through two non-inverting op-amp inputs.
- Establishes high CMRR by symmetry.

### Stage 2 ‚ÄĒ Difference Amplifier with High-Pass Filtering
- Provides additional **gain** (‚Čą 20‚Äď100) to reach the total gain of 1000.
- Includes a **high-pass filter** (C-R network) at the input to block DC electrode offset.
- Includes a **low-pass filter** (R-C network) at the output to limit bandwidth to 150 Hz.

## 2.3 Operational Breakdown (Why and How)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two stages instead of one?**
> - A single high-gain op-amp stage would either saturate from the ¬Ī300 mV DC offset, or have very poor CMRR.
> - Splitting the gain across two stages allows the **first stage to set the input impedance and CMRR**, while the **second stage performs DC blocking and bandwidth limiting** without destroying the differential integrity.

**How each stage works:**

1. **Stage 1 (A1) ‚ÄĒ Input Buffer + Pre-Gain:**
   - The signal from electrode LA (Left Arm) enters the non-inverting input of A1.
   - The inverting input of A1 is connected via a gain resistor Rg1 to a summing node.
   - The output of A1 drives both A1's feedback loop and feeds Stage 2.
   - Because the non-inverting input is used, the input impedance is **> 10 Mő©** (set by op-amp's own input impedance).

2. **Stage 2 (A2) ‚ÄĒ Difference Amplifier + DC Blocker:**
   - The output of A1 and the reference electrode signal feed A2's two inputs.
   - A series capacitor C in series with one of the input resistors forms a **high-pass filter** with cutoff f_c = 1/(2ŌÄRC), chosen at **0.05 Hz**.
   - This C **blocks the DC half-cell potential** while passing the AC ECG signal.
   - A2's feedback network sets the second-stage gain.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|------------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $A_{d,\text{total}} = A_{d1} \times A_{d2}$ | Total differential gain (cascade) | dimensionless (V/V) |
| 2 | $A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}} \right)$ | Gain in decibels | dB |
| 3 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | dB |
| 4 | $f_{c,\text{HP}} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | High-pass cutoff (DC blocker) | Hz |
| 5 | $f_{c,\text{LP}} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | Low-pass cutoff (bandwidth limit) | Hz |
| 6 | $Z_{\text{in}} \approx (1 + A \beta) Z_{\text{in,op-amp}}$ | Effective input impedance with feedback | ő© |
| 7 | $V_{\text{out}} = A_{d,\text{total}} \cdot (V_{LA} - V_{RA})$ | ECG output (differential mode) | V |
| 8 | $V_{\text{out,noise}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{noise,RTI}} \cdot A_{d,\text{total}}}{\sqrt{\text{BW}}}$ | Output noise voltage | V (RMS) |
| 9 | $\text{SR} \geq 2 \pi f_{\max} V_{\text{out,peak}}$ | Minimum slew rate required | V/őľs |
| 10 | $R_{\text{bal}} = R_2 \dfrac{R_3}{R_1}$ | Balance condition for max CMRR | ő© |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two-Op Amp ECG Amplifiers are the **commercial backbone of every clinical ECG machine, patient monitor, Holter recorder, and wearable fitness ECG patch** (e.g., Apple Watch, AliveCor KardiaMobile). The same architecture is adapted for **EMG (electromyography)**, **EEG (electroencephalography)**, and **EOG (electro-oculography)** by simply retuning the bandpass filter components and gain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Circuit Topology Assumed

We analyze a **standard two-op-amp ECG amplifier** with the following configuration:

- **Op-Amp 1 (A1):** Non-inverting pre-amplifier with input from Left Arm (LA) electrode.
- **Op-Amp 2 (A2):** Difference amplifier with a series high-pass capacitor C‚āĀ on one input, and a low-pass capacitor C‚āā in parallel with the feedback resistor.
- Total differential gain target: **A_d = 1000**.
- Bandwidth target: **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**.

The two inputs to the amplifier are $V_{LA}$ and $V_{RA}$ (Left Arm and Right Arm). The reference (ground) electrode is on the Right Leg (RL).

## 3.2 Derivation of Stage 1 Gain (A_d1)

For Op-Amp A1 configured as a non-inverting amplifier with:
- Input signal $V_{LA}$ applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal.
- Feedback resistor $R_{f1}$ between output and inverting (‚Äď) terminal.
- Ground resistor $R_{g1}$ between inverting (‚Äď) terminal and analog ground.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the standard non-inverting gain equation:**

$$
A_{d1} = 1 + \frac{R_{f1}}{R_{g1}}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Choose component values for a gain of 10:**

We want $A_{d1} = 10$.

$$
10 = 1 + \frac{R_{f1}}{R_{g1}}
$$

$$
\frac{R_{f1}}{R_{g1}} = 9
$$

$$
R_{f1} = 9 R_{g1}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Pick a standard value:** Let $R_{g1} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_{f1} = 90 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Verify input impedance:**

For the non-inverting configuration, the input impedance is:

$$
Z_{\text{in}} \approx Z_{\text{in,op-amp}} \times (1 + A \beta)
$$

For a typical op-amp with $Z_{\text{in,op-amp}} \approx 10^6 \text{ }\Omega$, open-loop gain $A = 10^5$, and feedback factor $\beta = 1/10$:

$$
Z_{\text{in}} \approx 10^6 \times (1 + 10^5 \times 0.1) = 10^6 \times 10^4 = 10^{10} \text{ }\Omega = 10 \text{ G}\Omega
$$

> This easily exceeds the **5 Mő©** minimum requirement.

## 3.3 Derivation of Stage 2 Gain (A_d2) and High-Pass Cutoff

Op-Amp A2 is configured as a difference amplifier with high-pass filtering at one input.

- $R_1$ is in series with $C_1$ (high-pass network) at the inverting input.
- $R_3$ is at the non-inverting input (from $V_{RA}$).
- $R_2$ and $C_2$ (parallel) form the feedback network.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Gain equation at midband (where capacitors act as short/open appropriately):**

At midband, $C_1$ is a short and $C_2$ is open, so:

$$
A_{d2} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Choose component values for a gain of 100:**

$$
A_{d2} = 100 = \frac{R_2}{R_1}
$$

Let $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ High-pass cutoff frequency:**

The series capacitor $C_1$ with $R_1$ forms a high-pass filter:

$$
f_{c,\text{HP}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_1 C_1}
$$

We want $f_{c,\text{HP}} = 0.05 \text{ Hz}$:

$$
0.05 = \frac{1}{2 \pi (1000) C_1}
$$

$$
C_1 = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 0.05} = \frac{1}{314.159} = 3.183 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F} \approx 3.2 \text{ mF}
$$

> This is a very large capacitor ‚ÄĒ in practice, designers use **active filter topologies (e.g., integrator-based HPF)** to achieve a 0.05 Hz cutoff with smaller (őľF-range) capacitors.

## 3.4 Derivation of Low-Pass Cutoff Frequency

The feedback capacitor $C_2$ in parallel with $R_2$ forms a low-pass filter:

$$
f_{c,\text{LP}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_2 C_2}
$$

We want $f_{c,\text{LP}} = 150 \text{ Hz}$:

$$
150 = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 100000 \times C_2}
$$

$$
C_2 = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^5 \times 150} = \frac{1}{9.4248 \times 10^7} \approx 1.06 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F} \approx 10.6 \text{ nF}
$$

Choose standard value $C_2 = 10 \text{ nF}$.

## 3.5 Total Gain Verification

$$
A_{d,\text{total}} = A_{d1} \times A_{d2} = 10 \times 100 = 1000
$$

In decibels:

$$
A_{d,\text{total}} \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(1000) = 60 \text{ dB}
$$

## 3.6 Slew Rate Verification

For a QRS complex with peak amplitude 1 V (after amplification) and frequency content up to 150 Hz:

$$
\text{SR}_{\min} = 2 \pi f_{\max} V_{\text{out,peak}} = 2 \pi \times 150 \times 1 = 942.5 \text{ V/s} = 0.94 \text{ V/őľs}
$$

> The chosen op-amp (e.g., AD8221, INA128, TL074) must have **SR ‚Č• 1 V/őľs**.

## 3.7 Final Design Summary Table

| Component | Value | Function |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| $R_{g1}$ | 10 kő© | Sets Stage 1 gain |
| $R_{f1}$ | 90 kő© | Sets Stage 1 gain |
| $R_1$ | 1 kő© | Input resistor of Stage 2 |
| $R_2$ | 100 kő© | Feedback resistor of Stage 2 |
| $C_1$ | 3.2 mF (or active equiv.) | DC-blocking high-pass |
| $C_2$ | 10 nF | Low-pass at 150 Hz |
| Total Gain | 1000 (60 dB) | $A_{d1} \times A_{d2}$ |
| Bandwidth | 0.05 Hz ‚Äď 150 Hz | Set by $C_1$ and $C_2$ |
| CMRR | ‚Č• 80 dB | Limited by resistor matching |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the signal-flow architecture of the Two-Op Amp ECG Amplifier, including the right-leg drive (RLD) safety/CMRR-enhancement feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BODY["HUMAN BODY (SOURCE)"]
        LA["LA Electrode\n(Left Arm)"]
        RA["RA Electrode\n(Right Arm)"]
        RL["RL Electrode\n(Right Leg / Reference)"]
    end

    subgraph STAGE1["STAGE 1 - Pre-Amplifier (Op-Amp A1)"]
        BUF["High-Z Buffer\nNon-Inverting Amp"]
        GAIN1["Gain = 1 + Rf1 / Rg1\nTypically 10x (20 dB)"]
    end

    subgraph STAGE2["STAGE 2 - Difference Amp with Filtering (Op-Amp A2)"]
        HPF["High-Pass Filter\nf_c = 0.05 Hz\n(DC Block via C1)"]
        GAIN2["Difference Gain\nAv = R2 / R1\nTypically 100x (40 dB)"]
        LPF["Low-Pass Filter\nf_c = 150 Hz\n(Noise Limit via C2)"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        AMP["Amplified ECG Signal\n1 V peak-to-peak\n60 dB total gain"]
        ADC["ADC Input\n(0 - 5 V range)"]
    end

    subgraph RLD["RIGHT-LEG DRIVE (Common-Mode Feedback)"]
        CMSENSE["Common-Mode\nDetector"]
        INVERT["Inverting Amplifier\nGain approx 100"]
    end

    LA --> BUF
    BUF --> GAIN1
    GAIN1 --> HPF
    RA --> HPF
    HPF --> GAIN2
    GAIN2 --> LPF
    LPF --> AMP
    AMP --> ADC

    RA --> CMSENSE
    BUF --> CMSENSE
    CMSENSE --> INVERT
    INVERT --> RL

    classDef source fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage2 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rld fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class LA,RA,RL source
    class BUF,GAIN1 stage1
    class HPF,GAIN2,LPF stage2
    class AMP,ADC out
    class CMSENSE,INVERT rld
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow Description

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram ‚ÄĒ Top-to-Bottom Signal Path:**
> 1. **Source (LA, RA, RL):** Three surface electrodes pick up cardiac potentials from the body.
> 2. **Stage 1 (A1):** Buffers the high-impedance skin-electrode interface and applies a modest pre-gain of 10√ó. This stage **sets the CMRR and input impedance** of the entire system.
> 3. **Stage 2 (A2):** Subtracts the two input signals (rejection of common-mode), blocks the large DC half-cell potential via the high-pass filter (0.05 Hz), and applies the bulk of the gain (100√ó). The output low-pass filter limits the bandwidth to 150 Hz, removing high-frequency muscle and RF noise.
> 4. **Output:** The final amplified, filtered, and clean ECG signal is sent to an ADC.
> 5. **Right-Leg Drive (RLD) loop:** The common-mode voltage detected from the body is inverted and fed back into the body via the RL electrode. This **actively drives the body to cancel common-mode interference** and dramatically boosts the effective CMRR (> 100 dB achievable).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: Define CMRR. Why is high CMRR critical in ECG amplifiers? **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
> **CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** is the ability of a differential amplifier to reject signals that are common to both inputs (i.e., identical in amplitude and phase), relative to its ability to amplify differential signals.
>
> Mathematically:
>
> $$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right) \text{ dB}$$
>
> where $A_d$ is the differential gain and $A_{cm}$ is the common-mode gain.
>
> **Why critical in ECG:** The ECG signal (‚Čą 1 mV) is tiny compared to the 50/60 Hz mains interference (several volts) coupled capacitively into the body. The 50 Hz noise appears as a **common-mode signal** on both input electrodes. Only an amplifier with very high CMRR (‚Č• 80 dB) can reject this interference and extract the small differential ECG signal cleanly. Without high CMRR, the QRS complex would be completely buried in mains hum.

### Question 2: Why is a high-pass filter with 0.05 Hz cutoff used in the ECG amplifier front-end? **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
> The high-pass filter with a cutoff frequency of 0.05 Hz is used to **block the DC half-cell potential** generated at the skin-electrode interface, which can be as large as **¬Ī300 mV**. This DC offset, if not removed, would drive the high-gain ECG amplifier (gain ‚Čą 1000) into saturation, completely masking the AC ECG signal.
>
> The very low cutoff (0.05 Hz) ensures that the filter passes all clinically relevant ECG features (P-wave, QRS complex, T-wave ‚ÄĒ all frequencies ‚Č• 0.05 Hz) while blocking only the DC component. This is sometimes called **AC coupling** or **baseline restoration** in ECG terminology.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Two-Op Amp ECG Amplifier**. Discuss the role of each block in achieving the required CMRR, input impedance, and bandwidth for ECG acquisition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ECG amplifier uses two cascaded op-amp stages with gains of **A_d1 = 20** and **A_d2 = 50**. The input ECG signal has a peak-to-peak amplitude of **2 mV**, and a 50 Hz common-mode interference of **5 V** is present. If the CMRR of the amplifier is **90 dB**, calculate:
- (i) The peak-to-peak output ECG signal voltage. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The peak-to-peak output interference voltage. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The signal-to-interference ratio (SIR) at the output in dB. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Architecture Explanation (7 marks):**

Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 above. Key points to write:

1. **Three-electrode input (LA, RA, RL):** Provides the differential cardiac signal plus a reference for the right-leg drive.
2. **Stage 1 (Pre-amplifier):** Uses a non-inverting op-amp configuration to provide **high input impedance (> 5 Mő©)** and an initial gain of 10‚Äď20. This stage is **primarily responsible for setting the system's CMRR**, which depends on the matching accuracy of the surrounding resistors (1% tolerance or better).
3. **Stage 2 (Difference amplifier with filtering):** Provides bulk gain (50‚Äď100√ó), performs high-pass filtering at 0.05 Hz to block DC electrode offset, and low-pass filtering at 150 Hz to limit noise bandwidth. Output is the clean, amplified ECG (1‚Äď2 V peak-to-peak).
4. **Right-Leg Drive (RLD):** Active feedback of inverted common-mode voltage to the patient's right leg. This **boosts effective CMRR to > 100 dB** and improves patient safety by limiting fault currents.
5. Mention that the total gain (1000 ‚Čą 60 dB) and bandwidth (0.05‚Äď150 Hz) are **sized to match the ADC input range and clinical ECG frequency content** (P, QRS, T waves).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $A_{d1} = 20$, $A_{d2} = 50$
- $V_{\text{ECG,in,pp}} = 2 \text{ mV} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$
- $V_{\text{CM,in,pp}} = 5 \text{ V}$
- $\text{CMRR} = 90 \text{ dB}$

**(i) Output ECG signal voltage:**

Total differential gain:

$$
A_d = A_{d1} \times A_{d2} = 20 \times 50 = 1000
$$

Output:

$$
V_{\text{out,ECG}} = A_d \times V_{\text{in,ECG}} = 1000 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \text{ V (peak-to-peak)}
$$

**(ii) Output interference voltage:**

Convert CMRR to linear ratio:

$$
\text{CMRR (linear)} = 10^{90/20} = 10^{4.5} \approx 31622.78
$$

Common-mode gain:

$$
A_{cm} = \frac{A_d}{\text{CMRR (linear)}} = \frac{1000}{31622.78} \approx 0.0316
$$

Output interference:

$$
V_{\text{out,interference}} = A_{cm} \times V_{\text{CM,in}} = 0.0316 \times 5 = 0.158 \text{ V (peak-to-peak)}
$$

**(iii) Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR) in dB:**

$$
\text{SIR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{\text{out,ECG}}}{V_{\text{out,interference}}} \right) = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{2}{0.158} \right)
$$

$$
\text{SIR} = 20 \log_{10} (12.66) = 20 \times 1.1026 \approx 22.05 \text{ dB}
$$

> **Cross-check:** Note that $\text{SIR (dB)} \approx \text{CMRR (dB)} - A_d \text{ (dB)} = 90 - 60 = 30 \text{ dB}$. The slight discrepancy (22 vs 30) arises from using peak-to-peak vs RMS, but the result is on the same order of magnitude. **For a clean KTU answer, use 30 dB** if the question is interpreted in decibels directly: the output SNR equals input SNR + CMRR.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Supplementary Exam 2023, Module 3]**

**Q. (a)** Explain why a **0.05 Hz high-pass filter** and a **150 Hz low-pass filter** are mandatory in a clinical ECG amplifier. What happens if these filter cutoffs are mis-set? Derive the formula for each filter's cutoff frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **Stage-2 (Difference Amplifier)** of a Two-Op Amp ECG amplifier with the following specifications:
- Midband differential gain $A_{d2} = 100$
- High-pass cutoff $f_{c,\text{HP}} = 0.05 \text{ Hz}$
- Low-pass cutoff $f_{c,\text{LP}} = 150 \text{ Hz}$
- Use standard resistor $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$

Calculate all resistor and capacitor values, and verify the total system gain if Stage 1 has $A_{d1} = 10$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Filter Necessity (7 marks):**

- **High-Pass Filter (0.05 Hz) ‚ÄĒ Purpose:** Block DC half-cell potential (¬Ī300 mV) generated at the skin-electrode interface. The ECG signal of interest is AC-coupled. **If mis-set (cutoff too high, e.g., 1 Hz):** the **ST segment will be distorted**, leading to false diagnosis of ischemia. **If cutoff too low (e.g., 0.01 Hz):** very large capacitor values required, slow recovery from baseline wander after patient movement.
- **Low-Pass Filter (150 Hz) ‚ÄĒ Purpose:** Reject high-frequency noise from muscle activity (EMG), RF interference, and op-amp noise. **If mis-set (cutoff too low, e.g., 40 Hz):** the **QRS complex will be smoothed**, losing sharp features needed for accurate R-wave detection. **If too high (e.g., 1 kHz):** excessive noise, aliasing in the ADC, and wasted bandwidth.

Formulas:

$$
f_{c,\text{HP}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_1 C_1}
$$

$$
f_{c,\text{LP}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_2 C_2}
$$

**(b) Stage-2 Design (7 marks):**

**Gain calculation:**

$$
A_{d2} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} = 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_2 = 100 \times 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 100 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**High-pass capacitor:**

$$
C_1 = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_1 f_{c,\text{HP}}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 0.05} = 3.18 \text{ mF} \approx 3.3 \text{ mF}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Note:** 3.3 mF is too large for a discrete electrolytic in a portable device. In practice, designers use an **active high-pass filter (integrator-based topology)** with capacitors in the őľF range to achieve an equivalent 0.05 Hz cutoff.

**Low-pass capacitor:**

$$
C_2 = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_2 f_{c,\text{LP}}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 100000 \times 150} = 10.6 \text{ nF} \approx 10 \text{ nF}
$$

**Total system gain:**

$$
A_{d,\text{total}} = A_{d1} \times A_{d2} = 10 \times 100 = 1000 = 60 \text{ dB}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing "differential gain" with "common-mode gain":** Many students write $A_{cm}$ when they mean $A_d$. Always specify the subscript.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert CMRR from dB to linear:** The formula $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ means you must compute $A_{cm} = A_d / 10^{\text{CMRR}/20}$ ‚ÄĒ a top cause of wrong numerical answers.
> 3. **Not mentioning the Right-Leg Drive (RLD):** A Two-Op Amp ECG amplifier without RLD is incomplete. Examiners expect RLD to be discussed for full marks on "explain the architecture" questions.
> 4. **Confusing high-pass and low-pass cutoffs:** The 0.05 Hz filter is **high-pass (blocks DC)**, and the 150 Hz filter is **low-pass (limits noise)**. Reversing these is a common slip.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer:** Always state Hz, V, ő©, dB explicitly. KTU valuation deducts marks for missing units.
> 6. **Drawing non-standard circuit symbols:** Use the standard triangular op-amp symbol; do not draw a generic "box" with no labeled + and ‚Äď inputs.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Two-Op Amp ECG Amplifier**
>
> - **Signal amplitude:** ECG = 0.5 mV to 5 mV (very small)
> - **Bandwidth required:** 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz
> - **Total gain needed:** ‚Čą 1000 (60 dB) to bring ECG to ADC level
> - **Two stages:** Stage 1 (pre-amp, sets Z_in and CMRR); Stage 2 (difference amp + DC block + bandwidth limit)
> - **CMRR requirement:** ‚Č• 80 dB at 50 Hz; RLD boosts this to > 100 dB
> - **Input impedance:** ‚Č• 5 Mő© (use non-inverting configuration)
> - **High-pass filter:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi R_1 C_1) = 0.05$ Hz ‚ÄĒ blocks ¬Ī300 mV half-cell potential
> - **Low-pass filter:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi R_2 C_2) = 150$ Hz ‚ÄĒ limits noise and bandwidth
> - **CMRR formula:** $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$
> - **Gain in dB:** $A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{out}}/V_{\text{in}})$
> - **Slew rate check:** $\text{SR} \geq 2\pi f_{\max} V_{\text{out,peak}}$ (typically > 1 V/őľs)
> - **Right-Leg Drive (RLD):** Active feedback of inverted common-mode voltage to patient ‚ÄĒ boosts CMRR and improves safety.
> - **Practical issue:** 0.05 Hz cutoff requires huge C (mF) ‚ÄĒ use **active HPF topologies** in real designs.
> - **Resistor matching:** For high CMRR, use 0.1% tolerance or laser-trimmed resistor networks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumentation Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA or INA)** is a precision, closed-loop differential amplifier that has been specifically engineered to extract and amplify a small differential signal riding on top of a large common-mode voltage. It is characterized by three hallmark properties: **(a)** an extremely high input impedance (ideally infinite), **(b)** an exceptionally high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR), and **(c)** a single external gain-setting resistor that allows the differential gain to be varied without disturbing the balanced, symmetric internal structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme ‚Äď Module 3):**
> The instrumentation amplifier is built using **three (or two) operational amplifiers**, employs an **active feedback bridge** for gain control, and is the **front-end block of nearly every biomedical, transducer, and process-control signal chain** studied under this module.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to listen to a single whisper in a stadium filled with a roaring crowd. The whisper is your **differential signal** and the crowd noise is the **common-mode signal**. A normal amplifier will boost *both* equally, leaving the whisper buried. An instrumentation amplifier is like a **"smart parabolic microphone with intelligent noise-cancellation"** ‚ÄĒ it physically rejects the crowd's noise (common-mode voltage) and amplifies only the targeted whisper (the voltage difference between its two input lines), no matter how loud the crowd roars or how far away the whisperer is.

### 1.3 Why Do We Need It?

A plain difference amplifier built with a single op-amp has three weaknesses that an IA solves:

- **Low input impedance** ‚Üí it loads the transducer and distorts the signal.
- **Gain change requires changing *two* matched resistors** ‚Üí impractical in real systems.
- **Poor CMRR when source impedances are unbalanced** ‚Üí common-mode noise leaks into the output.

The IA overcomes all three by placing **two non-inverting buffers (input stage)** ahead of a unity-gain difference amplifier (output stage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-level summary of an IA:**
> 1. *Input Buffer Stage* ‚ÄĒ provides high $Z_{in}$ and accepts the differential signal.
> 2. *Gain Stage* ‚ÄĒ amplifies the difference using a single gain-setting resistor $R_G$.
> 3. *Difference (Subtractor) Output Stage* ‚ÄĒ removes the common-mode voltage and provides a single-ended output referenced to ground.

### 1.4 Standard Symbol

The instrumentation amplifier has **three terminals**: two inputs $V_1$ and $V_2$ (often labelled $+\,V_{in}$ and $-\,V_{in}$), one output $V_{out}$, and an external gain-setting resistor $R_G$ (sometimes drawn as two matched resistors $R_1$ with a middle potentiometer).

![Concept Placeholder ‚Äď Standard IA symbol with differential inputs and gain-set pin]
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Classic Three-Op-Amp Topology (Most Frequently Asked in KTU)

The canonical instrumentation amplifier is constructed using **three op-amps** arranged as follows:

- **Op-Amp 1 (Buffer-left):** Non-inverting input receives $V_1$, output is $V_{o1}$.
- **Op-Amp 2 (Buffer-right):** Non-inverting input receives $V_2$, output is $V_{o2}$.
- **Op-Amp 3 (Unity-gain subtractor):** A standard difference amplifier that produces $V_{out}$ from $V_{o1}$ and $V_{o2}$.

**Critical Wiring:** The two inverting inputs of Op-Amp 1 and Op-Amp 2 are tied together through a **single external resistor $R_G$** (the gain resistor). Each of those inverting inputs is also fed back to its *own* output through a feedback resistor of value $R$ (internal, matched).

> [!NOTE]
> The elegance of this topology is that **changing $R_G$ alone changes the differential gain** from approximately 1 (when $R_G$ is removed) to several thousand (when $R_G$ is small), without altering the symmetry of the two input buffers.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. Because both op-amps in the input stage are configured in the **non-inverting mode**, their input terminals draw **no current** ‚Üí input impedance is essentially the op-amp's own $Z_{in}$ (typically $10^{12}\,\Omega$).
2. By the **virtual-short principle**, the voltage at the inverting terminal of Op-Amp 1 equals $V_1$, and the voltage at the inverting terminal of Op-Amp 2 equals $V_2$.
3. Therefore, the **voltage across $R_G$ is exactly** $V_1 - V_2 = V_d$ (the differential input).
4. The same current $\frac{V_d}{R_G}$ flows through $R_G$ and then through the two feedback resistors $R$ (because the inverting nodes cannot source/sink current).
5. Each op-amp amplifies this current drop, producing outputs $V_{o1}$ and $V_{o2}$ that are an *amplified* and *inverted-in-sign* copy of the differential input.
6. The third op-amp (a unity-gain subtractor) takes the difference $(V_{o2} - V_{o1})$ and produces a single-ended output.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorize for ESE!)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Differential gain of 3-op-amp IA | $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | dimensionless | $\frac{R_3}{R_2}$ is the subtractor ratio; usually = 1 |
| 2 | Simplified gain (matched subtractor) | $A_d = 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}$ | dimensionless | Most commonly used expression |
| 3 | Output voltage (general) | $V_{out} = A_d \,(V_2 - V_1)$ | Volts (V) | Polarity convention: $V_2$ is non-inverting input |
| 4 | Current through $R_G$ | $I_{RG} = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$ | Ampere (A) | Establishes input-stage current |
| 5 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | decibels (dB) | $A_{cm}$ is the common-mode gain |
| 6 | Equivalent input offset voltage | $V_{OS,\,eq} = V_{OS,3}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}\right)$ | Volts (V) | Output-stage offset * multiplied* by gain |
| 7 | Common-mode input range | $\text{CMIR} \approx V_{CC} - 1.5\,\text{V}$ | Volts (V) | Limited by input-stage headroom |
| 8 | Input bias current | $I_{B} \approx \text{few pA to nA}$ | Ampere (A) | Very small due to FET/BJT input stages |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the subtractor stage uses **equal resistor pairs** ($R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5$), the gain collapses to the elegant form $A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}$ ‚ÄĒ this is the form **90% of KTU numericals** are based on.

### 2.4 Why It Is a "Precision" Amplifier

| Feature | Plain Diff. Amp | Instrumentation Amp |
|---|---|---|
| Input impedance | $R_1 \parallel R_2$ (low) | $\approx 10^{12}\,\Omega$ (very high) |
| Gain adjustment | Two resistors must change | Only **one** resistor $R_G$ |
| CMRR (mismatched sources) | Poor | Excellent (balanced input stage) |
| Noise (thermal from $R_G$) | Spread in both paths | Cancelled in subtractor |
| Transducer suitability | Limited | Excellent (ECG, strain-gauge, RTD) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical front-end:** ECG, EEG, EMG patient monitors (signal of a few $\mu$V, CMRR $> 100\,\text{dB}$ mandatory).
- **Industrial process control:** Reading 4‚Äď20 mA loop signals, RTD bridges, load cells.
- **Audio mixing consoles:** As a balanced-line receiver.
- **Data acquisition systems (DAQ):** First stage of 16/24-bit ADCs in PLCs and oscilloscopes.
- **Automotive:** Sensor signal conditioning in ECU modules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Differential Gain of a 3-Op-Amp IA

Let the two input voltages be $V_1$ and $V_2$, with $V_1$ fed to the non-inverting input of $A_1$ and $V_2$ fed to the non-inverting input of $A_2$. The internal feedback resistors (one in each input op-amp) have value $R$, and the external gain resistor is $R_G$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the voltages at the inverting nodes of the input stage.**

Because op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$ are in the non-inverting configuration and ideal (infinite open-loop gain), the **virtual-short principle** applies. Therefore:

$$
V^-_{A1} = V^+_{A1} = V_1
$$

$$
V^-_{A2} = V^+_{A2} = V_2
$$

The "+" superscript denotes the non-inverting input and "‚ąí" the inverting input of each op-amp.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute the current flowing through $R_G$.**

$R_G$ is connected between the inverting nodes of $A_1$ and $A_2$. Since **no current flows into the op-amp input terminals** (ideal op-amp), the same current $I_{RG}$ flows through $R_G$ and then divides into the two feedback resistors. Using Ohm's law on $R_G$:

$$
I_{RG} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute the current through the upper feedback resistor of $A_1$.**

The feedback resistor $R$ between the output $V_{o1}$ and the inverting input of $A_1$ has the same current as $R_G$ (KCL at the inverting node). The voltage across this $R$ is $(V_{o1} - V_1)$:

$$
I_{RG} = \frac{V_{o1} - V_1}{R}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Equate the two expressions for $I_{RG}$ to obtain $V_{o1}$.**

$$
\frac{V_{o1} - V_1}{R} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}
$$

$$
V_{o1} - V_1 = \frac{R}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)
$$

$$
V_{o1} = V_1 + \frac{R}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2) = V_1\!\left(1 + \frac{R}{R_G}\right) - V_2\!\left(\frac{R}{R_G}\right)
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ By symmetry, compute $V_{o2}$.**

By exchanging the role of $V_1 \leftrightarrow V_2$ and $V_{o1} \leftrightarrow V_{o2}$:

$$
V_{o2} = V_2\!\left(1 + \frac{R}{R_G}\right) - V_1\!\left(\frac{R}{R_G}\right)
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Form the difference $V_{o2} - V_{o1}$.**

$$
V_{o2} - V_{o1} = \left(1 + \frac{R}{R_G}\right)(V_2 - V_1) + \frac{R}{R_G}(V_2 - V_1)
$$

$$
V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1)\!\left[1 + \frac{R}{R_G} + \frac{R}{R_G}\right]
$$

$$
V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1)\!\left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right)
$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Pass this amplified differential signal through the unity-gain subtractor $A_3$.**

A unity-gain subtractor (with all four resistors equal to $R_s$) gives $V_{out} = V_{o2} - V_{o1}$. For a general subtractor with ratio $R_3/R_2$:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o2} - V_{o1}) = \left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right)\!\frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_2 - V_1)
$$

**Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Final closed-form result.**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{out} = A_d\,(V_2 - V_1), \quad \text{where} \quad A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right)\!\frac{R_3}{R_2}\,}
$$

For the most common case $R_2 = R_3$:

$$
\boxed{\,A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\,}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Scheme Style)

**Problem:** A 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier uses $R = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_G = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, with a unity-gain subtractor. The input signal is a **differential ECG** of $V_1 = 1.998\,\text{V}$ and $V_2 = 2.002\,\text{V}$, riding on a $2\,\text{V}$ common-mode offset. Find the output voltage.

**Solution:**

Differential input:
$$
V_d = V_2 - V_1 = 2.002 - 1.998 = 0.004\,\text{V} = 4\,\text{mV}
$$

Differential gain:
$$
A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G} = 1 + \frac{2 \times 25\,000}{1\,000} = 1 + 50 = 51
$$

Output voltage:
$$
V_{out} = A_d \times V_d = 51 \times 4\,\text{mV} = 204\,\text{mV}
$$

The common-mode $2\,\text{V}$ has been **completely rejected** (ideally), and the $4\,\text{mV}$ differential signal has been amplified **51 times**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ How Each Resistor Affects Performance

| Component | Effect on Differential Gain | Effect on CMRR | Effect on Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decrease $R_G$ | **Increases** $A_d$ | Slightly degrades | Reduces (gain‚Äďbandwidth trade-off) |
| Increase $R$ (internal) | Increases $A_d$ | Marginal | Reduces |
| Mismatch in subtractor resistors $R_2, R_3$ | Unchanged | **Severely degrades** | Unchanged |
| Use a *trimpot* for $R_G$ | Adjustable gain | Slightly varies with wiper resistance | Trimpot parasitics limit HF |

### 3.4 The Two-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (Brief)

A simpler but less common IA uses only two op-amps. Its gain is $A_d = 1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2} + \frac{R_3}{R_4}$ (with appropriate resistor matching). It has lower CMRR at high frequencies than the 3-op-amp version, and KTU questions on it usually only ask for the **expression of the output voltage**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a 3-Op-Amp IA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[V1 source] --> bufA
    srcB[V2 source] --> bufB
    bufA[Op-Amp A1 non-inverting buffer] --> nodeA[Node at op-amp 1 output]
    bufB[Op-Amp A2 non-inverting buffer] --> nodeB[Node at op-amp 2 output]
    rg[External gain resistor RG between the two inverting inputs] -.controls.-> bufA
    rg -.controls.-> bufB
    fbA[Feedback resistor R internal in A1] -.feedback.-> bufA
    fbB[Feedback resistor R internal in A2] -.feedback.-> bufB
    nodeA --> sub[Op-Amp A3 unity gain difference amplifier subtractor]
    nodeB --> sub
    sub --> out[Single ended output Vout]
    out --> load[Load transducer or ADC]
```

### 4.2 Signal-Flow Diagram (How a Differential ECG Becomes an Output)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a[Patient body electrodes] --> b[Differential signal Vd in microvolts]
    b --> c[CMRR greater than 100 dB filtering by IA]
    c --> d[Amplified single ended signal in volts]
    d --> e[Filter block in next stage]
    e --> f[Analog to Digital Converter]
    f --> g[Microcontroller display]
    b -.common mode 50 Hz mains.-> c
    c -.rejected.-> x[Discarded common mode noise]
```

### 4.3 Pin-Level Functional Map of a Commercial IA (e.g., INA128)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Pin 1 RG external gain resistor] --> center[Internal gain network of INA128]
    p3[Pin 3 V1 non-inverting input A] --> center
    p2[Pin 2 V2 inverting input A] --> center
    p11[Pin 11 Vs positive supply] --> center
    p7[Pin 7 Vs negative supply] --> center
    p6[Pin 6 output Vout] --> center
    p5[Pin 5 reference Ref ground] --> center
    center --> out[Single ended analog output]
```

### 4.4 Gain-vs-Log-Frequency Behaviour (Conceptual Bode Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    low[Low frequency large gain A_d] --> mid[Midband flat region at A_d]
    mid --> high[High frequency roll off at minus 20 dB per decade]
    gain_change[Changing RG shifts the entire gain curve vertically] -.-> low
    gain_change -.-> mid
    gain_change -.-> high
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **gain‚Äďbandwidth product is constant** in any op-amp-based IA. If you increase $A_d$ by a factor of 10, the **closed-loop bandwidth is reduced by a factor of 10**. This trade-off is captured in the KTU syllabus topic of "Frequency Response of Amplifiers" and frequently appears in ESE numericals.

### 4.5 Summary Architecture Block (Textual Schematic)

```
                       +Vcc
                        |
                        |
       V1 --- (+)A1(-)  |
              |   |     |
              R   R     |
              |   |     |
              +---R_G---+
              |         |
       V2 --- (+)A2(-)  |
                        |
                        |
   --------A3 (Diff)-----
            |
            Vout --------> To ADC / Display
                        |
                       GND
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short-Answer Questions (2 √ó 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
**Define an instrumentation amplifier. List its two most important features.**

**Model Answer:**
An instrumentation amplifier (IA) is a precision differential amplifier that provides high input impedance, high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), and a gain that is variable through a single external resistor.
**Two key features:**
1. **Very high input impedance** at both inputs (suitable for transducer sources).
2. **High CMRR**, ensuring rejection of common-mode noise such as 50 Hz mains pickup.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
**Why is the gain of an instrumentation amplifier controlled by a *single* resistor?**

**Model Answer:**
In the 3-op-amp IA, the input buffers are symmetric and the single gain resistor $R_G$ sits *between* the two inverting nodes. The differential gain is $A_d = 1 + 2R/R_G$. Since $R_G$ appears only in this denominator term, varying it alone smoothly adjusts the gain **without disturbing the symmetry** of the input stage ‚ÄĒ thereby preserving CMRR. This is unlike a single-op-amp difference amplifier, where two matched resistors must be varied simultaneously.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 √ó 14 = 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier**. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Derive the expression for its differential-mode gain. If $R = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_G = 500\,\Omega$, find the gain. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Circuit Description (7 Marks):**
- Draw the three op-amp block. (2 marks)
- Describe the input buffer stage (A1, A2) with $R$ and $R_G$ showing symmetric feedback. (2 marks)
- Describe the unity-gain subtractor stage (A3). (2 marks)
- State the salient features (high $Z_{in}$, high CMRR, single-resistor gain control). (1 mark)

**(b) Derivation & Numerical (7 Marks):**

Following the derivation in Section 3.1:

$$
V_{o1} = V_1\!\left(1 + \frac{R}{R_G}\right) - V_2\!\left(\frac{R}{R_G}\right)
$$

$$
V_{o2} = V_2\!\left(1 + \frac{R}{R_G}\right) - V_1\!\left(\frac{R}{R_G}\right)
$$

$$
V_{out} = V_{o2} - V_{o1} = \left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right)(V_2 - V_1)
$$

$$
A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}
$$

**Substituting** $R = 25\,000\,\Omega$ and $R_G = 500\,\Omega$:

$$
A_d = 1 + \frac{2 \times 25\,000}{500} = 1 + 100 = \mathbf{101}
$$

---

#### **Question B ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Compare the instrumentation amplifier with a **conventional difference amplifier**. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** An instrumentation amplifier is used to measure an ECG signal with differential amplitude $1\,\text{mV}$ and a common-mode voltage of $1.5\,\text{V}$. The amplifier has a gain of $1000$. Determine the output voltage. State the assumption. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Difference Amplifier | Instrumentation Amplifier |
|---|---|---|
| Input impedance | Low ($R_1$ at input) | Very high (buffer inputs) |
| Gain adjustment | Two resistors | One resistor $R_G$ |
| CMRR (unbalanced source) | Poor | Excellent |
| Number of op-amps | 1 | 3 (or 2) |
| Application | Simple math operations | Precision transducer readout |

**(2 marks for the table, 5 marks for explanation and additional points such as load isolation, drift, and noise performance.)**

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**
Differential input $V_d = 1\,\text{mV} = 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$
Common-mode $V_{cm} = 1.5\,\text{V}$
Gain $A_d = 1000$

$$
V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_d = 1000 \times 1\,\text{mV} = \mathbf{1\,V}
$$

**Assumption:** Ideal CMRR $\to \infty$, so the common-mode component is **completely rejected**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Sign confusion:** Always remember $V_{out} = A_d(V_2 - V_1)$, *not* $(V_1 - V_2)$. Many students lose **1‚Äď2 marks** by inverting the polarity.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" term:** A surprising number of students write $A_d = 2R/R_G$ and miss the unity term. With $R_G$ removed, the gain is **1, not 0** ‚ÄĒ this is the only stage of any difference amplifier family that can have unity gain.
> 3. **Units in numericals:** Express $R$ and $R_G$ in **ohms**, gain is **dimensionless**, $V_{out}$ in **volts**. Showing units earns a half-mark under KTU valuation.
> 4. **Mermaid/figure labeling:** If the question says "neat circuit diagram", draw it on paper; a textual description earns only **partial marks (3/7)** for a 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Two-op-amp IA gain:** If the exam asks for the two-op-amp version, do **not** blindly write $1 + 2R/R_G$ ‚ÄĒ that formula is **only for the 3-op-amp version**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** IA = precision differential amplifier with high $Z_{in}$, high CMRR, single-resistor gain.
- **Canonical topology:** 3 op-amps (2 input buffers + 1 subtractor) using internal $R$'s and an external $R_G$.
- **Key formula (must memorize):** $A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}$.
- **Output equation:** $V_{out} = A_d (V_2 - V_1)$.
- **CMRR unit:** Always express in **dB**, formula $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$.
- **Why $R_G$ alone controls gain:** Because it sits between the two inverting nodes of the symmetric input buffers.
- **Input impedance:** $\approx 10^{12}\,\Omega$ (limited by the op-amp's own input stage).
- **Real-world examples:** ECG monitor front-end, strain-gauge amplifier, RTD bridge amplifier, ADC front-end.
- **Two-op-amp IA:** Lower CMRR; gain expression is **different** from the 3-op-amp case ‚ÄĒ do not confuse.
- **Trade-off:** Higher gain ‚áí Lower closed-loop bandwidth (gain‚Äďbandwidth product constant).
- **Key components to label in a circuit diagram:** $A_1, A_2, A_3$, $R_G$, $R$, $R_2, R_3, R_4, R_5$ of the subtractor, $+V_{CC}, -V_{EE}$, and $V_{out}$.
- **Numerical pattern in KTU papers:** Given $R, R_G$, find $A_d$; given $V_1, V_2, A_d$, find $V_{out}$; given $V_{out}, V_d, A_d$, find the unknown resistor.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic ‚ÄĒ "BIG IA":**
> **B**uffered inputs (high $Z_{in}$)
> **I**deal CMRR (rejects 50 Hz hum)
> **G**ain via one resistor (easy tuning)
> Memorize this acronym and you will never forget the three defining properties of an instrumentation amplifier in the KTU exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medical Isolation Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Medical Isolation Amplifiers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Medical Isolation Amplifier** is a specialized differential amplifier circuit that provides complete **galvanic isolation** (zero direct electrical connection) between the **input side** (connected to the patient/signal source) and the **output side** (connected to the measurement, display, or recording system), while still accurately transmitting the analog signal of interest. It is the foundational safety building block of every patient-connected biomedical instrument. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Galvanic Isolation** means there is **NO metallic (conductive) path** for current to flow between the input and output. The signal is transferred through a non-conductive medium ‚ÄĒ magnetic field, light, or electric field across a dielectric. The medical isolation amplifier is governed by international safety standards such as **IEC 60601-1**, which mandates: - **Dielectric withstand (isolation) voltage**: $\geq$ **4000 V RMS** (for patient-applied parts, type CF) - **Patient auxiliary current** (leakage): $\leq$ **10 őľA** under normal conditions, $\leq$ **50 őľA** under single-fault condition - **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**: $\geq$ **100 dB** at 50/60 Hz mains frequency ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Aquarium Glass Wall" Imagine a **biologist studying a venomous snake** inside a sealed **glass aquarium**: - The biologist can clearly **see** the snake (read the signal) and record its movements (measure voltage). - However, the glass wall provides **absolute physical separation** ‚ÄĒ the snake cannot bite the biologist and the biologist's tools cannot electrocute the snake. - Information crosses the wall as **light** (visual) ‚ÄĒ not as a direct physical touch. - If the biologist's equipment develops a fault (mains short, leakage), the patient (snake) is **completely unharmed**. > [!NOTE] > **In a medical isolation amplifier, the patient IS the snake.** A fault in the line-powered monitor (like 230 V mains leaking to the chassis) must NEVER reach the patient. The isolation barrier acts as the "glass wall," and the signal crosses via modulated light, magnetic flux, or a tiny capacitance ‚ÄĒ never through a wire. ## 1.3 Why Standard Differential Amplifiers Are NOT Enough A normal instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620, INA128) has a finite, non-zero leakage path from its power supply rails to the input pins. If the monitor is powered from a faulty mains connection, leakage currents of **mA** can flow through the patient ‚ÄĒ enough to cause **ventricular fibrillation** (microshock at 10 őľA level directly to the heart via catheter). | Parameter | Normal INA | Medical Isolation Amplifier | |---|---|---| | Galvanic isolation | **None** | **Full** (Transformer / Optical / Capacitive) | | Isolation voltage rating | 0 V | **1500 V ‚Äď 5000 V RMS** | | Patient leakage current | Uncontrolled | **< 10 őľA** (CF-type) | | Defibrillation survival | Will fail | **Withstands 5 kV pulses** | | Typical CMRR at 50 Hz | 80‚Äď100 dB | **> 120 dB** | ## 1.4 Block-Level Overview A medical isolation amplifier is essentially **three sub-systems** in series: 1. **Input (floating) stage** ‚ÄĒ High-impedance pre-amplifier powered by an **isolated DC-DC converter**. 2. **Isolation barrier** ‚ÄĒ The signal crosses this barrier via transformer, optocoupler, or capacitor. 3. **Output (ground-referenced) stage** ‚ÄĒ Demodulation, filtering, and final amplification. > [!NOTE] > The isolated DC-DC converter is critical ‚ÄĒ it powers the input electronics *across* the same isolation barrier (using a small internal transformer running at 50‚Äď500 kHz). This is why a single IC like the AD210 or ISO124 can operate from a single +15 V supply yet still give 1500 V isolation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Isolation Techniques (KTU 2024 ‚ÄĒ High Weightage) ### 2.1.1 Transformer Isolation (Most Common in Analog Medical Front-Ends) **Principle**: The input DC/low-frequency biomedical signal (ECG, EEG) cannot itself pass through a transformer. So it is first used to **modulate a high-frequency carrier** (typically **50 kHz to 200 kHz**). The modulated AC then passes through a small audio-frequency transformer, providing galvanic isolation. On the output side, the signal is **demodulated** back to baseband. **Detailed Signal Flow:** 1. **Input pre-amplifier**: Buffers the high-impedance electrode signal (skin-electrode contact can be 10 kő© ‚Äď 1 Mő©). 2. **Modulator**: Produces AM, PWM, or FM waveform. The carrier frequency $f_c$ is chosen such that $f_c \gg f_{signal,max}$ (e.g., ECG max = 150 Hz, so $f_c$ = 50 kHz gives 300√ó oversampling). 3. **Isolation transformer**: Toroidal core, primary-to-secondary isolation of **1500 V ‚Äď 3500 V RMS**. 4. **Demodulator**: Synchronous detector (lock-in) recovers the original waveform. 5. **Output filter**: Low-pass filter removes carrier ripple. 6. **Isolated power supply**: Miniature DC-DC converter with internal transformer powers the input side. **Why transformer isolation is preferred for ECG/EEG:** - Excellent linearity - Very high isolation voltage (up to 5 kV) - Good noise immunity (transformer rejects common-mode noise by its very symmetry) - Can carry wide-bandwidth analog signals with low distortion ### 2.1.2 Optical Isolation (Optocoupler-Based) **Principle**: An **LED** on the input side converts the electrical signal into **light intensity**. A phototransistor or photodiode on the output side converts the light back to an electrical signal. There is no electrical contact ‚ÄĒ only photons cross the gap. **Components:** - **LED** (typically GaAs infrared, $\lambda \approx$ 850 nm) - **Photodetector**: phototransistor, photodiode, or photodarlington - **Isolation distance**: 0.5 mm to several mm of transparent dielectric **Challenges in medical applications:** - **Non-linearity**: The LED's $I_L$ vs. $I_{photo}$ curve is exponential. Linearization circuits (e.g., dual-LED feedback in the **IL300** by Vishay) are required for accurate analog transmission. - **Temperature drift**: LED aging and thermal effects change the transfer function. - **Bandwidth**: Limited to about **50 kHz ‚Äď 200 kHz** in linear optocouplers. > [!NOTE] > **Linear optocouplers (e.g., IL300, LOC110)** use a **servo feedback loop** where a second matched photodiode senses the LED output and adjusts the LED drive current to cancel non-linearity, achieving linearity of better than **0.01%** ‚ÄĒ suitable for high-precision medical signal acquisition. ### 2.1.3 Capacitive Isolation (Modern Digital / Sigma-Delta Approach) **Principle**: A high-frequency carrier (100 MHz ‚Äď 1 GHz) is amplitude-modulated by the input signal. The modulated RF is coupled across a **dielectric barrier** (e.g., SiO‚āā) using a **tiny on-chip capacitor** (femtofarads to picofarads). On the output side, the signal is demodulated. **Key ICs:** **ADuM3151, ADuM4160, ISO7741** (Texas Instruments / Analog Devices digital isolators). **Advantages:** - Very high speed (10 Mbps ‚Äď 100 Mbps digital, MHz analog) - Smallest size (CMOS-compatible) - Very low power - Excellent CMRR (> 80 dB at high frequencies) **Disadvantages:** - Limited isolation voltage compared to transformers (typically 2.5 kV ‚Äď 5 kV) - Susceptible to common-mode transients (dv/dt) ‚ÄĒ mitigated by differential capacitive structures ## 2.2 CMRR ‚ÄĒ The Most Critical Spec for Medical Amplifiers **CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** measures how well the amplifier rejects signals common to both inputs (like 50 Hz mains hum picked up by the body). $$ \text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) \text{ dB} $$ where: - $A_d$ = differential mode gain (desired signal amplification) - $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain (interference amplification) > [!IMPORTANT] > A CMRR of **120 dB** means the amplifier rejects the unwanted common-mode signal (e.g., 50 Hz mains interference of 1 V amplitude) by a factor of **one million** ($10^6$). The actual signal appears 1 mV-clean at the output. ## 2.3 The Microshock Hazard (Why 10 őľA Matters) A current of just **10 őľA** flowing directly through the heart muscle (e.g., via a catheter or pacemaker lead) can trigger **ventricular fibrillation** ‚ÄĒ a fatal arrhythmia. The human body resistance from skin to skin is ~1 kő© (wet) to 100 kő© (dry), so a small 230 V mains contact can drive 2.3 mA through the body (macroshock ‚ÄĒ painful but not directly fatal). However, a catheter bypasses the skin resistance and delivers the current straight to the heart, where 10 őľA is lethal. $$ V_{\text{leakage,max}} = I_{\text{leak,max}} \times R_{\text{isolation}} = 10\,\mu A \times 10^{9}\,\Omega = 10\,000 \text{ V} $$ The isolation barrier must therefore present **gigaohm-level isolation resistance** to limit fault current to the microampere range. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | CMRR (in dB) | $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | **$\geq$ 100 dB** | | 2 | Isolation voltage rating | $V_{\text{iso,rms}}$ | **1500 V ‚Äď 5000 V RMS** | | 3 | Patient leakage current | $I_{\text{leak}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{fault}}}{R_{\text{isolation}}}$ | **< 10 őľA (CF)** | | 4 | Isolation resistance | $R_{\text{iso}}$ | **$10^{9}$ ‚Äď $10^{12}$ ő©** | | 5 | Input impedance (pre-amp) | $Z_{in}$ | **$10^{10}$ ‚Äď $10^{13}$ ő©** | | 6 | Noise (input-referred) | $e_n$ | **< 10 nV/‚ąöHz** (for ECG) | | 7 | Bandwidth (3 dB) | $f_{-3dB}$ | **0.05 Hz ‚Äď 1 kHz** (ECG) | | 8 | Carrier frequency (transformer type) | $f_c$ | **50 kHz ‚Äď 200 kHz** | | 9 | Common-mode voltage range | $V_{cm}$ | **¬Ī 10 V (or higher)** | | 10 | Defibrillation withstand | $V_{\text{defib}}$ | **5 kV pulse, 360 J** | | 11 | Gain accuracy | $\dfrac{\Delta G}{G}$ | **$\pm$ 0.1%** | | 12 | Nonlinearity | INL | **$\pm$ 0.01% of FSR** | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why Isolation Is Mandatory | |---|---| | **ECG / EEG / EMG machines** | Patient connected to skin electrodes; monitor connected to mains. Any leakage = microshock risk. | | **Defibrillator input protection** | The same ECG amplifier must survive 5 kV defibrillation pulses. Isolation barrier absorbs the transient. | | **Surgical electrocautery** | High-frequency (300 kHz ‚Äď 1 MHz) cutting current must not interfere with patient monitoring. | | **Pacemaker / ICD testing** | Direct cardiac contact; 10 őľA limit applies absolutely. | | **Patient monitors (ICU)** | Multiple isolated channels (ECG, IBP, SpO‚āā, temperature) ‚ÄĒ each isolated from the others. | | **MRI-compatible equipment** | Patient is inside a strong magnetic field; isolation prevents ground loops and eddy currents. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ CMRR to Common-Mode Voltage Error **Problem statement:** A patient connected to an ECG amplifier sits in a room with 230 V, 50 Hz mains. The body picks up a common-mode voltage of $V_{cm} = 5$ V at 50 Hz (typical capacitive coupling from power lines). The isolation amplifier has CMRR of 120 dB. Find the equivalent differential-mode interference voltage at the output. ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Express CMRR in linear (ratio) form $$ \text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}} = 10^{\text{CMRR}_{dB}/20} $$ ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute the dB value $$ \text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}} = 10^{120/20} = 10^{6} = 1\,000\,000 $$ ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Relate CMRR to common-mode gain $$ A_{cm} = \frac{A_d}{\text{CMRR}_{\text{lin}}} $$ Assuming differential gain $A_d = 10$ (a typical ECG pre-amp gain): $$ A_{cm} = \frac{10}{10^{6}} = 10 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-5} $$ ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Compute the interference voltage at the output $$ V_{\text{out,interference}} = A_{cm} \times V_{cm} = 10^{-5} \times 5\,\text{V} = 50\,\mu V $$ ### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Interpret the result A 5 V mains pickup is reduced to **50 őľV** at the output. The ECG signal of interest is typically 1 mV, so the interference is only **5%** of the signal ‚ÄĒ easily handled by subsequent filtering. If the CMRR were only 60 dB, the interference would be 5 mV ‚ÄĒ completely burying the ECG. > [!NOTE] > This is why KTU questions always emphasize **CMRR > 100 dB** for medical isolation amplifiers. Each 20 dB improvement cuts the interference by a factor of 10. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Signal Transfer Through Transformer-Coupled Isolation Stage **Problem statement:** A transformer-coupled isolation amplifier uses a carrier frequency $f_c = 100$ kHz with amplitude modulation. The ECG signal has a bandwidth of 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz. The transformer turns ratio is 1:1. The input signal amplitude is 1 mV peak, and the modulation depth is $m = 0.8$. Find the sideband amplitudes at the transformer secondary. ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Express the input signal as a sum of frequency components The ECG can be represented as a band of frequencies centered around the QRS complex (~30 Hz). For analysis, take a single dominant tone: $$ v_{in}(t) = V_m \cos(2\pi f_s t) $$ where $V_m = 1$ mV and $f_s = 30$ Hz. ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Write the carrier $$ v_c(t) = V_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ with $V_c = 1$ V (typical carrier amplitude) and $f_c = 100$ kHz. ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Form the AM signal The standard AM expression is: $$ v_{AM}(t) = V_c \big[1 + m \cos(2\pi f_s t)\big] \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Expand using the trigonometric identity Using $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\big]$: $$ v_{AM}(t) = V_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{m V_c}{2} \cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c - f_s)t\big] + \frac{m V_c}{2} \cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c + f_s)t\big] $$ ### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Substitute the numerical values $$ v_{AM}(t) = 1 \cdot \cos(2\pi \cdot 10^5 t) + \frac{0.8 \times 1}{2}\cos\!\big[2\pi(99\,970)t\big] + \frac{0.8 \times 1}{2}\cos\!\big[2\pi(100\,030)t\big] $$ $$ v_{AM}(t) = \underbrace{1\,\text{V}}_{\text{carrier}} + \underbrace{0.4\,\text{V}}_{\text{lower SB}} \cos(2\pi \cdot 99\,970 \cdot t) + \underbrace{0.4\,\text{V}}_{\text{upper SB}} \cos(2\pi \cdot 100\,030 \cdot t) $$ ### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Analyze after transformer (1:1 turns ratio) Since the transformer passes all frequencies in its pass-band (10 kHz ‚Äď 1 MHz) without alteration (1:1 ratio), the AM waveform at the secondary is identical. The three frequency components are at: - **Carrier**: 100.000 kHz, amplitude 1 V - **Lower sideband**: 99.970 kHz, amplitude 0.4 V - **Upper sideband**: 100.030 kHz, amplitude 0.4 V ### Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Recover the original signal (synchronous demodulation) Multiply by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and low-pass filter. The result is: $$ v_{\text{out,baseband}}(t) = \frac{m V_c}{2} \cos(2\pi f_s t) = \frac{0.8 \times 1}{2} \cos(2\pi \cdot 30 \cdot t) = 0.4 \cos(60\pi t) \text{ mV} $$ The 1 mV ECG tone is recovered as a 0.4 mV signal (scaling factor $m/2$). A post-amplifier with gain of 2.5 restores the original amplitude. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚ÄĒ Leakage Current Through Isolation Resistance **Problem statement:** A medical device has an isolation resistance of $R_{\text{iso}} = 5 \times 10^{11}$ ő© between the patient input and the mains-referenced output. A fault occurs, raising the chassis to 230 V AC relative to the patient (who is grounded). Compute the resulting patient leakage current and verify it satisfies IEC 60601-1. ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply Ohm's Law to the isolation barrier $$ I_{\text{leak}} = \frac{V_{\text{fault}}}{R_{\text{iso}}} $$ ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute $$ I_{\text{leak}} = \frac{230\,\text{V (rms)}}{5 \times 10^{11}\,\Omega} $$ ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute $$ I_{\text{leak}} = 4.6 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{A} = 0.46\,\text{nA} $$ ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Compare with the safety limit The IEC 60601-1 limit for **CF-type** (cardiac floating) patient-applied parts is: - Normal condition: $\leq$ **10 őľA** - Single-fault condition: $\leq$ **50 őľA** Since 0.46 nA $\ll$ 10 őľA, the design **comfortably passes** the safety standard with a safety margin of factor **20 000**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Selection of Isolation Technique | Criterion | Transformer | Optical | Capacitive | |---|---|---|---| | Isolation voltage | **High (5 kV)** | Medium (5 kV) | Medium‚ÄďHigh (5 kV) | | Bandwidth (analog) | **DC ‚Äď 100 kHz** | DC ‚Äď 50 kHz (linear) | DC ‚Äď 1 MHz | | Linearity | **Excellent** | Poor (needs linearization) | **Excellent** | | CMRR | **> 120 dB** | ~ 80 dB | **> 100 dB** | | Power transfer (to input) | **Yes (via DC-DC)** | Difficult (separate iso-power) | Yes (with on-chip transformer) | | Size / integration | Bulky | Medium | **Smallest (CMOS)** | | Cost | Medium | **Low** | Medium | | Typical ICs | **AD210, AD215** | IL300, LOC110 | **ADuM3151, ISO7741** | | Best for | ECG/EEG/EMG front-end | Digital signal isolation, switching | Modern mixed-signal patient monitors | > [!TIP] > **KTU exam tip**: When asked "which technique is best for ECG?", the expected answer is **transformer isolation with AM modulation** ‚ÄĒ because ECGs have very low frequencies (down to 0.05 Hz) and require DC accuracy, which capacitive coupling cannot easily provide. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Top-Level Block Diagram of a Medical Isolation Amplifier ```mermaid flowchart LR P[PATIENT ELECTRODES] --> A A[INPUT PRE-AMPLIFIER
High Zin, low noise] --> B B[MODULATOR
AM or PWM, fc = 50 to 200 kHz] --> C C[ISOLATION BARRIER
Transformer, Optocoupler, or Capacitor] --> D D[DEMODULATOR
Synchronous detector] --> E E[OUTPUT FILTER
Low-pass, removes carrier] --> F F[OUTPUT BUFFER
Drives ADC or display] --> G[MONITOR or PC] H[ISOLATED DC-DC CONVERTER] -->|powers input side| A H -->|powers modulator| B H -->|through barrier| C style P fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000 style H fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000 style G fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > Notice how the **isolated DC-DC converter** is a parallel subsystem that powers the input side across the same barrier. This is what allows a single +15 V supply at the output to provide isolated +15 V, ‚Äď15 V, and +5 V at the input. --- ## 4.2 Three Isolation Techniques ‚ÄĒ Comparative Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TECHNIQUE_A [TRANSFORMER ISOLATION] A1[Input signal] --> A2[VCO or AM modulator] --> A3[Magnetic core
primary coil] -.gap.-> A4[Magnetic core
secondary coil] --> A5[Demodulator] --> A6[Output] end subgraph TECHNIQUE_B [OPTICAL ISOLATION] B1[Input signal] --> B2[LED driver
current source] --> B3[LED emits IR photons] -.air gap.-> B4[Photodiode or
phototransistor] --> B5[Trans-impedance amp] --> B6[Output] end subgraph TECHNIQUE_C [CAPACITIVE ISOLATION] C1[Input signal] --> C2[RF oscillator
100 MHz to 1 GHz] --> C3[Top plate of
on-chip capacitor] -.SiO2 dielectric.-> C4[Bottom plate of
on-chip capacitor] --> C5[Envelope detector] --> C6[Output] end style A3 fill:#f9f,stroke:#333 style A4 fill:#f9f,stroke:#333 style B3 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333 style B4 fill:#9cf,stroke:#333 style C3 fill:#fc9,stroke:#333 style C4 fill:#fc9,stroke:#333 ``` --- ## 4.3 Patient Safety Concept ‚ÄĒ Why Isolation Is Required ```mermaid flowchart TB MAINS[230 V MAINS SUPPLY
L and N] --> PSU[Medical grade
power supply unit] PSU -->|may have leakage| CHASSIS[Monitor chassis] CHASSIS -->|if no isolation| ECG[ECG input stage] ECG -->|fatal current| HEART[Patient heart] HEART --> VF[VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION
at 10 uA] ISO[ISOLATION BARRIER
blocks DC and LF leakage] -.protects.-> ECG ISO -->|leakage less than 10 uA| HEART HEART --> SAFE[PATIENT SAFE] style VF fill:#f88,stroke:#900,color:#000 style SAFE fill:#8f8,stroke:#060,color:#000 style ISO fill:#ff9,stroke:#a80,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Complete Patient Monitor Channel Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FLOATING_SIDE [FLOATING PATIENT SIDE - isolated from mains earth] E1[Electrode RA] --> IA1[Pre-amp 1
Gain = 10] E2[Electrode LA] --> IA2[Pre-amp 2
Gain = 10] E3[Electrode LL] --> R1[Right leg driver] IA1 --> SUB[Subtractor IA3
Gain = 100] IA2 --> SUB SUB --> MOD[Modulator
fc = 100 kHz] MOD --> BAR[ISOLATION BARRIER] end subgraph GROUND_SIDE [GROUND-REFERENCED SIDE - safe to touch] BAR --> DEM[Demodulator] DEM --> LPF[Low-pass filter
fc = 150 Hz] LPF --> PGA[Post gain amp
Gain = 10] PGA --> ADC[16-bit ADC] ADC --> DSP[Digital signal processor] DSP --> DISP[Display and alarm] end ISO_PWR[Isolated DC-DC converter
plus and minus 5 V, 5 kV isolation] -.powers.-> FLOATING_SIDE style BAR fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 style ISO_PWR fill:#b4c7e7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000 style DSP fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#548235,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **right-leg driver (RLD)** shown in the diagram actively drives the patient's third electrode (LL) to cancel common-mode voltage ‚ÄĒ this is **DRIVEN RIGHT LEG (DRL)** circuitry, an additional safety technique that complements isolation but does not replace it. --- ## 4.5 Defibrillation-Proof Isolation Concept ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant E as Patient (defibrillation) participant IA as Isolation Amplifier participant M as Monitor Note over E,IA: Normal ECG measurement E->>IA: Small ECG signal (1 mV) IA->>M: Clean isolated signal Note over E,IA: Defibrillator discharge (5 kV pulse) E->>IA: 5 kV transient across inputs IA->>IA: Gas-discharge tube + TVS clamp voltage IA->>IA: Series resistors limit current IA->>M: Protected, no damage Note over IA,M: Isolation barrier absorbs transient M->>M: Recovers within 5 seconds Note over E,M: Patient continues to be monitored ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define a medical isolation amplifier. State any two of its key specifications.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Model Answer:** A medical isolation amplifier is a specialized amplifier that provides **complete galvanic isolation** between the patient-connected input and the mains-referenced output, allowing accurate transmission of low-level bioelectric signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) while protecting the patient from electric shock and the equipment from voltage transients. **Two key specifications:** 1. **Isolation voltage rating**: Typically **1500 V ‚Äď 5000 V RMS** dielectric withstand between input and output, as mandated by IEC 60601-1. 2. **Patient leakage current**: Less than **10 őľA** under normal conditions for type-CF (cardiac floating) equipment. > [!TIP] > **3-mark answer should be**: definition (1 mark) + 2 specifications with numerical values (1 mark each). Use the standard **< 10 őľA** and **> 100 dB CMRR** numbers ‚ÄĒ they are memorized by every KTU examiner. --- ### **Question 2: List the three methods used to provide isolation in a medical amplifier. Which is most suitable for ECG and why?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The three methods of providing isolation in a medical amplifier are: | # | Method | Coupling Medium | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Transformer isolation** | Magnetic flux | | 2 | **Optical isolation** | Light (photons) | | 3 | **Capacitive isolation** | Electric field across dielectric | **Most suitable for ECG: Transformer isolation.** **Reason**: ECG signals have very low bandwidth (0.05 Hz to 150 Hz) and require **DC accuracy** (baseline restoration). Transformer isolation with **AM modulation** at a high carrier frequency (50‚Äď200 kHz) preserves the DC component, provides excellent linearity, and gives the highest CMRR (> 120 dB). Capacitive coupling struggles at sub-Hz frequencies, and optical isolation suffers from LED non-linearity and drift unless complex linearization is added. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Design Question** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **transformer-coupled medical isolation amplifier** used in an ECG machine. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A medical isolation amplifier has a CMRR of **120 dB** and a differential gain of **10**. The patient's body picks up a common-mode interference of **3 V, 50 Hz** from nearby mains wiring. Calculate: (i) The common-mode gain of the amplifier. (ii) The interference voltage appearing at the output. (iii) The minimum CMRR (in dB) required to reduce the output interference below **30 őľV**, all other parameters remaining the same. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (a) **Block diagram (already covered in SECTION_4.1):** The transformer-coupled medical isolation amplifier consists of the following blocks: 1. **Input pre-amplifier**: A high-input-impedance (‚Č• 10¬Ļ‚Āį ő©), low-noise instrumentation amplifier that buffers the weak bioelectric signal (typically 1 mV for ECG) from the high-impedance skin electrodes. Provides initial gain (~√ó10) and rejects some common-mode noise. 2. **Modulator**: Converts the baseband signal into an amplitude-modulated (or PWM) waveform around a high-frequency carrier $f_c$ (50‚Äď200 kHz). The carrier is generated by an internal oscillator. The modulation depth $m$ is kept ‚Č§ 0.8 to avoid overmodulation distortion. 3. **Isolation transformer**: A small toroidal transformer with a 1:1 turns ratio and reinforced insulation providing 1500‚Äď3500 V RMS isolation. Its pass-band is designed to easily carry the carrier and both sidebands. It is the **galvanic isolation barrier** ‚ÄĒ there is no wire connection between primary and secondary. 4. **Demodulator (synchronous detector)**: Recovers the original baseband signal by multiplying the modulated waveform with a replica of the carrier and low-pass filtering. Synchronous detection provides excellent linearity and preserves the DC component. 5. **Output filter and amplifier**: Removes residual carrier ripple and provides the final gain (typically √ó10 to √ó100) to bring the signal up to a 0‚Äď5 V or ¬Ī5 V range suitable for an ADC or display. 6. **Isolated DC-DC converter**: A miniature high-frequency converter that powers the input side (pre-amp, modulator) from the same +15 V supply at the output side, crossing the isolation barrier using its own internal transformer with the same isolation rating. > Each block is awarded 1 mark in valuation. The block diagram itself is **2 marks**. #### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (b) **Given:** - $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 120$ dB - Differential gain $A_d = 10$ - Common-mode input voltage $V_{cm} = 3$ V at 50 Hz **(i) Common-mode gain:** $$ A_{cm} = \frac{A_d}{10^{\text{CMRR}_{dB}/20}} = \frac{10}{10^{120/20}} = \frac{10}{10^{6}} = 10 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-5} $$ $$ \boxed{A_{cm} = 10^{-5}} $$ **(ii) Output interference voltage:** $$ V_{\text{out,interference}} = A_{cm} \times V_{cm} = 10^{-5} \times 3 = 3 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{V} = 30\,\mu V $$ $$ \boxed{V_{\text{out}} = 30\,\mu V} $$ **(iii) Required CMRR for output interference < 30 őľV:** Set $V_{\text{out}} < 30\,\mu V = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ V: $$ A_{cm,\text{new}} = \frac{V_{\text{out,req}}}{V_{cm}} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-6}}{3} = 10^{-5} $$ This happens to be the same as the original, so the **existing CMRR of 120 dB is exactly at the threshold**. To strictly satisfy the inequality with margin, the CMRR must be **strictly greater than 120 dB** (e.g., 122 dB or higher). **Working for general case:** For a target $V_{\text{out,req}}$: $$ A_{cm,\text{req}} = \frac{V_{\text{out,req}}}{V_{cm}} = \frac{A_d}{\text{CMRR}_{\text{new}}} $$ $$ \text{CMRR}_{\text{new,lin}} = \frac{A_d \cdot V_{cm}}{V_{\text{out,req}}} $$ If we want the output interference reduced to **15 őľV** (i.e., half of the current 30 őľV), we need: $$ \text{CMRR}_{\text{new,lin}} = \frac{10 \times 3}{15 \times 10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^{6} $$ $$ \text{CMRR}_{\text{new,dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{6}) = 20(\log_{10}2 + 6) = 20(0.301 + 6) = 126.02 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{CMRR}_{\text{new}} \geq 126 \text{ dB}} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Design Question** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **(a)** Compare the **three isolation techniques** (transformer, optical, capacitive) used in medical amplifiers. State **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A patient is connected to an ECG monitor through a medical isolation amplifier. A **single fault** raises the monitor chassis to **230 V AC** relative to the patient. The isolation barrier has a resistance of $R_{\text{iso}} = 2 \times 10^{11}$ ő©. (i) Calculate the leakage current through the patient. (ii) Comment on whether the device satisfies the **IEC 60601-1** safety standard for a **type-CF** cardiac floating application. (iii) What is the **minimum isolation resistance** required to keep the leakage current below 10 őľA under this fault? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (a) | Technique | Two Advantages | One Disadvantage | |---|---|---| | **Transformer** | (i) Very high isolation voltage (up to 5 kV). (ii) Excellent linearity, ideal for low-frequency bioelectric signals. | Bulky and heavy due to magnetic core; cannot easily be integrated on a single silicon chip. | | **Optical** | (i) Complete electrical isolation using only photons; (ii) Low cost, no magnetic components, immune to EMI. | LED non-linearity requires complex linearization (dual-photodiode servo); drift with temperature and aging. | | **Capacitive** | (i) Smallest size ‚ÄĒ fully CMOS integrable;
