(ii) Very high bandwidth (MHz range), suitable for modern digital isolators. | Sensitive to common-mode transients (dv/dt); limited dielectric strength compared to transformers. | #### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part (b) **Given:** - Fault voltage $V_{\text{fault}} = 230$ V (AC rms) - Isolation resistance $R_{\text{iso}} = 2 \times 10^{11}$ ő© **(i) Leakage current:** $$ I_{\text{leak}} = \frac{V_{\text{fault}}}{R_{\text{iso}}} = \frac{230}{2 \times 10^{11}} $$ $$ I_{\text{leak}} = 1.15 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{A} = 1.15\,\text{nA} $$ $$ \boxed{I_{\text{leak}} = 1.15 \text{ nA}} $$ **(ii) IEC 60601-1 compliance for type-CF (cardiac floating):** - Normal condition limit: **10 őľA** = $10 \times 10^{-6}$ A - Single-fault condition limit: **50 őľA** = $50 \times 10^{-6}$ A Calculated leakage = 1.15 nA $= 1.15 \times 10^{-9}$ A Compare: - $1.15 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A} \ll 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}$ (normal) - $1.15 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A} \ll 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}$ (single fault) **Conclusion**: The device **comfortably satisfies** IEC 60601-1 type-CF safety requirements with a safety margin of approximately **8700√ó** in the normal case and **43 000√ó** in the single-fault case. **(iii) Minimum isolation resistance for $I_{\text{leak}} < 10$ őľA:** $$ R_{\text{iso,min}} = \frac{V_{\text{fault}}}{I_{\text{leak,max}}} = \frac{230}{10 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ R_{\text{iso,min}} = 2.3 \times 10^{7}\,\Omega = 23\,\text{M}\Omega $$ $$ \boxed{R_{\text{iso,min}} = 23 \text{ M}\Omega} $$ This is an **extremely modest** requirement ‚ÄĒ virtually any practical isolation barrier easily exceeds it. Industrial medical isolation amplifiers typically provide **gigaohm** ($10^9$ ő©) or **teraohm** ($10^{12}$ ő©) isolation, giving huge safety margin. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:** > 1. **Confusing CMRR formula** ‚ÄĒ Students often write $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}(A_{cm}/A_d)$ (inverted!). The correct formula is $A_d / A_{cm}$. **One mark lost per wrong sign.** > 2. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear** before substituting into other equations. Always convert $\text{CMRR}_{dB} \to \text{CMRR}_{lin} = 10^{\text{dB}/20}$ first. > 3. **Mixing up isolation voltage and isolation resistance** ‚ÄĒ they are different quantities. Isolation voltage is the dielectric breakdown rating; isolation resistance is the leakage path resistance. > 4. **Not specifying "type-CF"** in leakage current questions ‚ÄĒ examiners expect the explicit IEC 60601-1 type (B, BF, or CF). > 5. **Omitting the block diagram** in part-(a) 7-mark questions ‚ÄĒ the diagram is typically **2 marks** of the 7. Always draw it, even if the description is brief. > 6. **Confusing AM modulation depth $m$ with carrier amplitude $V_c$** ‚ÄĒ the sideband amplitude is $mV_c/2$, not $mV_c$. > 7. **Using RMS vs peak inconsistently** ‚ÄĒ for sinusoids, peak = $\sqrt{2} \times$ RMS. State explicitly which is being used. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Medical Isolation Amplifiers (Module 3)** **Core Definition:** - A medical isolation amplifier provides **galvanic isolation** between patient and mains-referenced circuitry. - It protects the patient from **microshock** (10 őľA lethal to heart) and **macroshock** (mA range through body). **Three Isolation Methods:** - **Transformer** ‚ÄĒ best for ECG/EEG; uses AM modulation at 50‚Äď200 kHz carrier. - **Optical** ‚ÄĒ uses LED + photodetector; needs linearization for analog (IL300, LOC110). - **Capacitive** ‚ÄĒ modern CMOS-compatible; used in digital isolators (ADuM series). **Critical Specifications to Memorize:** - Isolation voltage: **1500 V ‚Äď 5000 V RMS** - Patient leakage current: **< 10 őľA (CF-type normal), < 50 őľA (single fault)** - CMRR: **> 100 dB** (medical); **> 120 dB** (premium) - Input impedance: **> 10¬Ļ‚Āį ő©** - Bandwidth for ECG: **0.05 Hz ‚Äď 150 Hz** - Defibrillation withstand: **5 kV, 360 J** **Key Formulas:** - $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ - $A_{cm} = A_d / 10^{\text{CMRR}_{dB}/20}$ - $V_{\text{out,int}} = A_{cm} \times V_{cm}$ - $I_{\text{leak}} = V_{\text{fault}} / R_{\text{iso}}$ - $R_{\text{iso,min}} = V_{\text{fault}} / I_{\text{leak,max}}$ **Block Diagram Order (must remember):** **Input pre-amp ‚Üí Modulator ‚Üí Isolation barrier ‚Üí Demodulator ‚Üí Output filter/buffer** **Plus: Isolated DC-DC converter (parallel branch)** **Standard ICs to Know:** - **AD210, AD215** ‚ÄĒ transformer isolation - **IL300, LOC110** ‚ÄĒ linear optocouplers - **ADuM3151, ISO7741** ‚ÄĒ capacitive digital isolators - **INA128, AD620** ‚ÄĒ pre-amp front-end (NOT isolated, just INAs) **Applications:** - ECG, EEG, EMG machines - Defibrillators (with surge protection) - Patient monitors (ICU, CCU) - Surgical electrocautery - MRI-compatible equipment **Safety Standards:** - **IEC 60601-1** ‚ÄĒ General medical electrical equipment safety - Type **B** (body), **BF** (body floating), **CF** (cardiac floating) ‚ÄĒ CF is the strictest - Patient auxiliary current: B = 100 őľA, BF = 100 őľA, CF = 10 őľA **Most Important Mnemonic:** > **"T-O-C"** ‚ÄĒ **T**ransformer, **O**ptical, **C**apacitive ‚ÄĒ the three isolation methods. **High-Yield Exam Topics:** - Block diagram of transformer-coupled isolation amplifier ‚ÄĒ guaranteed 7-mark question - CMRR-based calculation ‚ÄĒ frequently asked 7-mark question - Comparison of isolation techniques ‚ÄĒ favourite 7-mark essay question - Leakage current calculation ‚ÄĒ easy 3-mark question

topics.title: Safety Standards in Medical Electronic Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Safety Standards in Medical Electronic Amplifiers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Medical Electronic Amplifier Safety Standards** refer to the rigorously defined engineering protocols, electrical isolation techniques, leakage-current limits, grounding architectures, and regulatory frameworks (e.g., **IEC 60601-1**, **AAMI ES 60601-1**, **NFPA 99**, **IS 13450**) that govern the design, construction, and deployment of electronic amplifiers physically connected to a patient. The central goal is to prevent hazardous physiological effects‚ÄĒnamely **microshock** and **macroshock**‚ÄĒthat arise from unintended electrical current flowing through the body. In KTU parlance, a medical amplifier is *not* just an amplifier; it is a **Patient-Connected Medical Device (PCMD)** whose non-functional requirements (safety) are as critical as its functional requirements (gain, CMRR, bandwidth). The amplifier must amplify biopotential signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) faithfully **and** must never, under any single-fault condition, deliver a current that could induce ventricular fibrillation, burns, or neuromuscular stimulation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Seatbelt" Principle > [!NOTE] > **Analogy ‚ÄĒ Why we need medical-amplifier safety standards:** > Imagine driving a normal car versus driving an ambulance. A normal car has seatbelts; an ambulance has seatbelts **plus** airbags **plus** reinforced roll-cages **plus** crash-absorbing bumpers‚ÄĒbecause the *consequence* of failure is far higher. A medical amplifier is the *ambulance* of electronics. Even a tiny leakage current (microamperes) that would be imperceptible in a TV set can‚ÄĒbecause the patient may have a catheter directly inside the heart‚ÄĒtrigger **ventricular fibrillation** and death. **The Three Realms of Patient Vulnerability:** | Realm | Why patient is vulnerable | Typical scenario | |---|---|---| | **Skin intact (high resistance)** | Outer skin acts as a barrier (~10‚Āī‚Äď10‚ĀĶ ő©) | Routine ECG measurement | | **Skin breached (low resistance)** | Electrolyte gel, electrodes, or broken skin | EEG, EMG, external pacing | | **Direct cardiac contact (very low resistance)** | Catheter or pacing lead bypasses skin entirely | Cardiac catheterization, intracardiac ECG | The third realm is where **microshock** (currents as small as **10 ¬ĶA**) can be lethal. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Safety Thresholds (Memorize for KTU):** > - **Let-go threshold (men):** **20 mA** (60 Hz AC) > - **Let-go threshold (women):** **~15 mA** > - **Ventricular fibrillation threshold (macroshock):** **~100 mA for 1 s** > - **Ventricular fibrillation threshold (microshock):** **~10 ¬ĶA** applied directly to the heart > - **Maximum permissible earth leakage current (Class I, Normal Condition):** **5 mA** > - **Maximum permissible patient leakage current (Type CF, Normal Condition):** **10 ¬ĶA** > - **Maximum permissible patient leakage current (Type CF, Single Fault):** **50 ¬ĶA** ## 1.4 Key Regulatory Bodies and Standards > [!NOTE] > **Standard Mapping Table:** > - **IEC 60601-1** ‚ÄĒ International general standard for medical electrical equipment safety > - **AAMI ES 60601-1** ‚ÄĒ US adoption of IEC 60601-1 > - **NFPA 99** ‚ÄĒ Health Care Facilities Code (US) > - **IS 13450** ‚ÄĒ Indian Standard equivalent (BIS) > - **IEC 60601-1-2** ‚ÄĒ Collateral standard for **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Hazard Categories ### A. Macroshock - Current applied to the **body surface** (intact or broken skin). - Skin resistance limits the current to a few milliamperes for normal mains voltages. - Hazardous threshold: **> 1 mA** (perception), **> 100 mA** (fibrillation in 1 s). - Standard protection: Basic insulation + protective earth. ### B. Microshock - Current applied **directly to the heart** via catheter, pacing lead, or saline-filled catheter. - Skin resistance is **bypassed**; the heart is exposed to the full current. - Hazardous threshold: as low as **10 ¬ĶA** (1/10,000th of macroshock threshold). - Standard protection: **Galvanic isolation** with very low coupling capacitance; isolated patient connection referred to as a **"floating" input**. ## 2.2 The Three Leakage Currents (KTU Favourite) Leakage current is the unwanted current that flows through a protective earth conductor or through the patient to earth. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Leakage Currents Defined by IEC 60601-1:** 1. **Earth Leakage Current (I_earth):** Current flowing from the **live parts to the protective earth conductor** of the device's power cord. 2. **Enclosure (Touch/Chassis) Leakage Current (I_touch):** Current flowing from the **enclosure** to earth when a human touches it. 3. **Patient Leakage Current (I_patient):** Current flowing from any **patient connection** through the patient to earth. 4. **Patient Auxiliary Current (I_aux):** Current flowing between **two patient electrodes** that is *not* the intended functional current (e.g., bias currents). ## 2.3 Patient Connection Types (B, BF, CF) > [!NOTE] > **IEC 60601-1 Classification of Patient-Applied Parts:** | Type | Symbol | Allowed Patient Connection | Maximum Patient Leakage (NC) | Maximum Patient Leakage (SFC) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Type B** (Body) | ‚í∑ | External skin contact only (e.g., ultrasound) | **100 ¬ĶA** | **500 ¬ĶA** | | **Type BF** (Body, Floating) | ‚íĽ | External skin contact, but with **isolated (floating) input** | **100 ¬ĶA** | **500 ¬ĶA** | | **Type CF** (Cardiac, Floating) | ‚íł‚íĽ | **Direct cardiac contact permitted** (e.g., intracardiac ECG) | **10 ¬ĶA** | **50 ¬ĶA** | The **floating (F)** suffix means the patient circuit has no direct galvanic connection to earth‚ÄĒit is isolated by a transformer, optocoupler, or similar barrier. ## 2.4 Equipment Protection Classes | Class | Protection Method | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Class I** | Basic insulation **+ protective earth** | Most mains-powered medical equipment (defibrillators, monitors) | | **Class II** | **Double or reinforced insulation**, no reliance on earth | Some hand-held or portable devices | | **Class III** | Powered by **Safety Extra-Low Voltage (SELV)** ‚Č§ 25 V AC or 60 V DC | Battery-operated or internally powered devices (preferred for CF) | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations for the ESE:** | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V = I \cdot R$ | Ohm's Law for body current | V, A, ő© | | 2 | $I_{body} = \dfrac{V_{source}}{R_{skin} + R_{internal}}$ | Current through body for macroshock | A | | 3 | $I_{micro} = \dfrac{V_{source}}{R_{catheter} + R_{heart}}$ | Current through heart for microshock (skin bypassed) | A | | 4 | $V_{fibrillation} = I_{threshold} \cdot R_{path}$ | Voltage producing fibrillation | V | | 5 | $I_{leakage} = \dfrac{V_{mains}}{Z_{isolation}}$ | Leakage through imperfect isolation | A | | 6 | $Z_{iso} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C_{c}}$ | Isolation impedance of capacitive barrier | ő© | | 7 | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Rejection of common-mode (50 Hz mains) interference | dB | | 8 | $V_{out} = CMRR \cdot V_{common}$ | Residual output from imperfect CMRR | V | | 9 | $I_{iso,max} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{Z_{iso}}$ | Maximum current that can pass isolation barrier | A | | 10 | $P_{max,safe} = \dfrac{V_{rms}^2}{Z_{patient}}$ | Maximum safe power dissipation in patient path | W | > **Critical constant to remember:** **Fibrillation threshold for 50/60 Hz AC applied for >1 s is ~100 mA (macroshock) and ~10 ¬ĶA (microshock).** ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Defibrillator-protected inputs:** ECG amplifiers must survive and recover within **5 seconds** after a 360 J defibrillator pulse. This is achieved by gas-discharge tubes, transient-voltage suppressors (TVS), and current-limiting resistors. - **Patient isolation amplifier (e.g., AD210, ISO124):** Galvanic isolation of **5 kV RMS** with leakage of **< 1 ¬ĶA**. - **Hospital isolated power systems (IPS):** Ungrounded power distribution in operating rooms; first-fault current is limited to < 1 mA. - **Battery-powered CF-type devices:** Holter monitors use Class III operation to avoid earth entirely. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Microshock Threshold is 10 ¬ĶA The heart is a sensitive bioelectric organ. A 50/60 Hz current flowing directly through the myocardium during the **vulnerable period of ventricular repolarization** (the T-wave on ECG) can cause chaotic, asynchronous depolarization ‚ÄĒ **ventricular fibrillation**. **Step-by-step logical derivation:** **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Energy balance for fibrillation.** The heart tissue must absorb energy sufficient to depolarize a critical mass of ventricular cells (~75% of ventricular mass, per the "Critical Mass Hypothesis" by Zipes et al.). $$E_{fibrillation} = P_{heart} \cdot t_{exposure} = I^2 \cdot R_{path} \cdot t_{exposure}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Empirically determined threshold energy.** Experimental studies (Dalziel, 1970s) on animals and accident data show that the threshold for ventricular fibrillation is approximately: $$E_{fibrillation} \approx 50 \text{ to } 100 \,\mu J \text{ (microshock, direct cardiac application)}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Solve for current.** Given typical cardiac path resistance (catheter saline + myocardium) of $R_{path} \approx 1 \,k\Omega$ and exposure time of $t = 1 \,s$: $$I = \sqrt{\dfrac{E_{fibrillation}}{R_{path} \cdot t}}$$ $$I = \sqrt{\dfrac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{1000 \cdot 1}}$$ $$I = \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-10}} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \,A = 10 \,\mu A$$ **Therefore**, the **microshock threshold is ‚Čą 10 ¬ĶA**. This is why IEC 60601-1 mandates the patient leakage current for Type CF equipment be limited to **10 ¬ĶA** under normal conditions. ## 3.2 Derivation: Leakage Current Through Isolation Capacitance A medical isolation transformer (or optocoupler) has a finite parasitic capacitance $C_c$ between primary and secondary. At mains frequency $f = 50 \,Hz$, this capacitance forms an AC path for leakage current. **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Capacitive reactance:** $$X_C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C_c}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Total isolation impedance** (assuming series R is negligible for short paths): $$Z_{iso} \approx X_C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C_c}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Leakage current** for a fault voltage $V_{fault}$ (e.g., 230 V mains): $$I_{leakage} = \dfrac{V_{fault}}{Z_{iso}} = V_{fault} \cdot 2 \pi f C_c$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Insert numerical values.** Suppose $C_c = 10 \,pF$ (typical for a high-quality optocoupler), $V_{fault} = 230 \,V$, $f = 50 \,Hz$: $$I_{leakage} = 230 \cdot 2 \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12}$$ $$I_{leakage} = 230 \cdot 314.16 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12}$$ $$I_{leakage} = 7.23 \times 10^{-7} \,A = 0.723 \,\mu A$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Interpretation.** This leakage of $0.723 \,\mu A$ is **two orders of magnitude below** the 10 ¬ĶA microshock limit ‚úď. > [!NOTE] > **Design insight:** A well-designed isolation barrier (with $C_c \leq 15$ pF) easily meets the 10 ¬ĶA CF-patient-leakage requirement. KTU questions often ask you to verify this. ## 3.3 Derivation: Effective Resistance Model of the Human Body For macroshock analysis, the human body is modeled as a series combination of: - **Skin resistance** $R_{skin}$ (which depends on moisture, voltage, and contact area) - **Internal body resistance** $R_{int}$ (‚Čą 300 ő©, dominated by torso) - **Path-specific resistance** (limb-to-limb, hand-to-foot, etc.) **Standard ANSI/IEEE Std C95.1 model for hand-to-hand path:** $$R_{body} = R_{skin,A} + R_{int} + R_{skin,B}$$ For wet skin: $R_{skin} \approx 1\,k\Omega$; for dry skin: $R_{skin} \approx 100\,k\Omega$. **Step-by-step body current computation (dry-skin, hand-to-hand, 230 V):** $$R_{body} = 100\,k\Omega + 300\,\Omega + 100\,k\Omega \approx 200.3\,k\Omega$$ $$I_{body} = \dfrac{230}{200.3 \times 10^3} \approx 1.15\,mA$$ This is **above the perception threshold (~1 mA)** but **below the let-go threshold (~20 mA)** ‚ÄĒ uncomfortable but not lethal. **Same computation with wet skin (surgical setting):** $$R_{body} = 1\,k\Omega + 300\,\Omega + 1\,k\Omega = 2.3\,k\Omega$$ $$I_{body} = \dfrac{230}{2300} = 100\,mA$$ This is **at the fibrillation threshold** ‚ÄĒ lethal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Take-away:** Wet conditions in hospitals reduce skin resistance by a factor of ~100, turning a merely annoying current into a lethal one. This is why OR floors are non-conductive, staff wear anti-static shoes, and isolated power systems are mandated. ## 3.4 Derivation: Required CMRR to Reject 50 Hz Mains Interference A typical ECG amplifier must extract a 1 mV cardiac signal in the presence of up to 10 V of common-mode 50 Hz interference (from coupling through the body to mains). **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Required output noise from common-mode:** $$V_{cm,out} \leq 0.1 \times V_{signal} = 0.1 \times 1\,mV = 100\,\mu V$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Solve for CMRR:** $$CMRR_{dB} \geq 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{cm}}{V_{cm,out}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10}{100 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$ $$CMRR_{dB} \geq 20 \log_{10}(10^5) = 20 \times 5 = 100\,dB$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Practical result.** A medical ECG amplifier must achieve **CMRR ‚Č• 100 dB at 50 Hz**, which is why instrumentation amplifiers like the AD620, INA128, or isolated AD210 are used. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Isolated Medical ECG Amplifier > [!NOTE] > The diagram below shows the **galvanic isolation barrier** that separates the *patient side* (left) from the *system/earth side* (right). The barrier is the **single most important safety feature** of a medical amplifier. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Electrodes
RA LA LL] --> B[ESD and Defib
Protection Network] B --> C[Instrumentation
Amplifier INA128] C --> D[Isolation Barrier
Optical or Transformer
Isolation 5kV] D --> E[Output Stage
and Filter] E --> F[ADC and
Display System] D -.->|Galvanic Barrier| G[Isolated DC-DC
Converter for Patient Power] G --> C P[Mains 230V] --> Q[Class I Power Supply
with Earth Connection] Q --> F P -.leak.-> D classDef safety fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef barrier fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b38600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef safe fill:#e5f9e5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class B,C,G safety class D barrier class E,F safe ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Left zone (red):** Patient side. **No direct earth connection.** Powered only through the isolation barrier. - **Centre (yellow barrier):** The optocoupler or transformer that provides **galvanic isolation** (no wire crosses here). - **Right zone (green):** System side, connected to mains earth. The 50 Hz leakage path shown as a dotted red arrow is broken by the barrier. ## 4.2 Classification Hierarchy of Medical Amplifier Safety Features ```mermaid flowchart TB R[Medical Amplifier Safety] --> S1[Electrical Isolation] R --> S2[Leakage Current Control] R --> S3[Defibrillation Protection] R --> S4[EMC and EMI] R --> S5[Mechanical and Environmental] S1 --> S1a[Galvanic Isolation
Transformer] S1 --> S1b[Optical Isolation
Optocoupler] S1 --> S1c[Battery Power
Class III SELV] S2 --> S2a[Earth Leakage
less than 5 mA] S2 --> S2b[Enclosure Leakage
less than 100 microA] S2 --> S2c[Patient Leakage
Type CF less than 10 microA] S3 --> S3a[Gas Discharge Tube] S3 --> S3b[Transient Voltage
Suppressor TVS] S3 --> S3c[Current Limiting
Resistor Network] S4 --> S4a[Shielded Patient Cables] S4 --> S4b[Common Mode Choke] S4 --> S4c[Right Leg Drive Circuit] S5 --> S5a[IP Rating IPX4 splash proof] S5 --> S5b[Leakage Current Trip RCD] S5 --> S5c[Single Fault Condition Tests] classDef root fill:#dde7ff,stroke:#0033aa,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cat fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#cc7700,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#e7ffe7,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class R root class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 cat class S1a,S1b,S1c,S2a,S2b,S2c,S3a,S3b,S3c,S4a,S4b,S4c,S5a,S5b,S5c leaf ``` ## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Model for Patient Connection ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PatientSide["PATIENT SIDE (Floating, No Earth)"] PA[Patient Applied Part
Electrode / Catheter] ZP[Z_patient
Cardiac Path 1k ohm] end subgraph Barrier["ISOLATION BARRIER"] CC[C_c 10pF
Parasitic Capacitance] RISO[R_iso 10 G ohm
Isolation Resistance] end subgraph EarthSide["EARTH SIDE (Mains Referenced)"] ME[Mains Earth
230V 50Hz] end PA --- ZP ZP -- leaks through --> CC ZP -- leaks through --> RISO CC --- ME RISO --- ME classDef patient fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef earth fill:#e5e5ff,stroke:#0000cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef barrier fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b38600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 class PA,ZP patient class ME earth class CC,RISO barrier ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The patient side is connected to the earth side only through $C_c$ and $R_{iso}$. - $I_{leakage} \approx \dfrac{V_{mains}}{\sqrt{R_{iso}^2 + (1/\omega C_c)^2}}$ - The **lower** the $C_c$ and the **higher** the $R_{iso}$, the **safer** the design. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Question A1]** > **Q1. Define the terms *macroshock* and *microshock*. Why is microshock more dangerous despite the much smaller current involved? (3 marks)** > > **Model Answer:** > - **Macroshock:** An electrical shock delivered to the body through the **skin surface**. Typical hazardous current: **> 100 mA for 1 s** at 50 Hz. > - **Microshock:** An electrical shock delivered **directly to the myocardium** via catheters, pacing leads, or saline-filled electrodes that bypass the skin's natural resistance. > - **Why microshock is more dangerous:** The skin acts as a large series resistance ($\approx 100$ kő© dry) that **limits** the current entering the body for macroshock. In microshock, this resistance is **bypassed** by the conductive catheter pathway, so the entire current flows through cardiac tissue. Hence, currents as low as **10 ¬ĶA** can trigger ventricular fibrillation‚ÄĒthree orders of magnitude smaller than the macroshock threshold. > - Reference standard: **IEC 60601-1, Type CF limit = 10 ¬ĶA**. > **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Question A2]** > **Q2. List the three classes of medical equipment based on protection method, and state how each provides protection against electric shock. (3 marks)** > > **Model Answer:** > - **Class I:** Basic insulation **plus** a protective earth (PE) connection. The earth conductor provides a low-impedance fault path that trips a fuse or RCD. *Used in mains-powered monitors, defibrillators.* > - **Class II:** **Double or reinforced insulation**; no reliance on protective earth. Protection is provided by two independent layers of insulation. *Used in handheld devices like electronic thermometers.* > - **Class III:** Powered by **Safety Extra-Low Voltage (SELV) ‚Č§ 25 V AC rms or 60 V DC**. The supply itself is below hazardous voltage, so no additional protection is required. *Used in battery-operated Holter monitors, surgical cameras.* ## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- > **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Module 3, ESE Pattern]** > **Question A (14 Marks):** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **galvanically isolated ECG amplifier** and show how patient safety is ensured. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A medical amplifier has an isolation barrier capacitance of $C_c = 15$ pF and isolation resistance of $R_{iso} = 10^{12}$ ő©. Calculate the leakage current when the patient is exposed to a **230 V, 50 Hz** mains fault. Comment on whether the device meets the **Type CF (10 ¬ĶA) limit**. **(7 marks)** > > **Model Solution:** > > **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):** > > 1. **Patient electrodes (RA, LA, LL)** sense differential biopotential of ~1 mV. *(1 mark)* > 2. **Defibrillation protection** (gas discharge tube + TVS diode + current-limiting resistor) clamps the 5 kV defibrillator pulse. *(1 mark)* > 3. **Instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., INA128) provides high CMRR (>100 dB), high input impedance (>10 Gő©), and gain of ~1000. *(1 mark)* > 4. **Isolation barrier** (optocoupler or pulse transformer) provides **galvanic separation** of 5 kV RMS between patient and earth-referenced circuitry. *(2 marks)* > 5. **Isolated DC-DC converter** powers the patient-side electronics through the same barrier. *(1 mark)* > 6. **Output stage** delivers the signal to the system-side ADC and display, completely isolated from the patient. *(1 mark)* > > **Safety verification:** The barrier breaks the only current path between patient and earth, so the leakage current is limited by $C_c$ alone, easily satisfying the 10 ¬ĶA Type CF limit. > > --- > > **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical (7 marks):** > > **Given:** $C_c = 15$ pF, $R_{iso} = 10^{12}$ ő©, $V = 230$ V rms, $f = 50$ Hz. > > **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Capacitive reactance:** > > $$X_C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C_c} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 15 \times 10^{-12}}$$ > > $$X_C = \dfrac{1}{4.712 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.122 \times 10^{8} \, \Omega \approx 212.2 \,M\Omega$$ > > *(2 marks)* > > **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Total isolation impedance** (parallel combination, but $R_{iso} \gg X_C$, so $Z_{iso} \approx X_C$): > > $$Z_{iso} = \dfrac{R_{iso} \cdot X_C}{\sqrt{R_{iso}^2 + X_C^2}} \approx X_C = 2.122 \times 10^{8} \, \Omega$$ > > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Leakage current:** > > $$I_{leak} = \dfrac{V}{Z_{iso}} = \dfrac{230}{2.122 \times 10^{8}}$$ > > $$I_{leak} = 1.084 \times 10^{-6} \,A = 1.084 \,\mu A$$ > > *(2 marks)* > > **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Compliance check:** > > $$I_{leak} = 1.084 \,\mu A \quad \text{vs} \quad I_{limit,CF} = 10 \,\mu A$$ > > Since $1.084 < 10$, the device **meets** the Type CF normal-condition limit. ‚úĒ > > *(2 marks)* --- > **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Module 3, ESE Internal Choice]** > **Question B (14 Marks):** > **(a)** Explain the **IEC 60601-1 classification of patient-applied parts** (B, BF, CF) with leakage current limits under both normal and single-fault conditions. Why is the CF type mandatory for intracardiac use? **(7 marks)** > **(b)** An ICU patient is connected to an ECG monitor. The dry-skin hand-to-hand body resistance is 200 kő©, and the wet-skin resistance is 2 kő©. Compute the body current in both cases when the patient contacts a **230 V mains line**. Comment on the medical significance. **(7 marks)** > > **Model Solution:** > > **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ IEC 60601-1 Classification (7 marks):** > > | Type | Symbol | Patient Connection | Patient Leakage (NC) | Patient Leakage (SFC) | > |---|---|---|---|---| > | **B** | ‚í∑ | Earth-referenced; external body use | 100 ¬ĶA | 500 ¬ĶA | > | **BF** | ‚íĽ | **Floating (isolated)**; external body use | 100 ¬ĶA | 500 ¬ĶA | > | **CF** | ‚íł‚íĽ | **Floating; direct cardiac permitted** | **10 ¬ĶA** | **50 ¬ĶA** | > > *(4 marks for the table and explanation)* > > - The 'F' suffix indicates a **floating (galvanically isolated)** patient circuit. The patient side has no direct wire to earth; any current must cross an isolation barrier. > - **Why CF is mandatory for intracardiac use:** A catheter electrode directly contacts the myocardium. The 10 ¬ĶA limit under normal conditions is **two orders of magnitude stricter** than B/BF, because microshock fibrillation can occur at 10 ¬ĶA. A B-type device, with up to 100 ¬ĶA leakage, could deliver a lethal 100 ¬ĶA directly to the heart. *(3 marks)* > > **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Body Current Computation (7 marks):** > > **Given:** $V = 230$ V, $R_{dry} = 200$ kő©, $R_{wet} = 2$ kő©. > > **Case 1: Dry skin** > > $$I_{dry} = \dfrac{230}{200 \times 10^3} = 1.15 \times 10^{-3} \,A = 1.15 \,mA$$ > > This is just above the **perception threshold** (~1 mA). Uncomfortable tingling, but **below the let-go threshold (20 mA)** and far below fibrillation. > > *(3 marks)* > > **Case 2: Wet skin** > > $$I_{wet} = \dfrac{230}{2 \times 10^3} = 0.115 \,A = 115 \,mA$$ > > This is **above the macroshock fibrillation threshold** (~100 mA for 1 s). Highly dangerous. > > *(3 marks)* > > **Medical significance:** > - Skin moisture reduces resistance by a factor of 100, taking the current from "perceptible" to "lethal." > - This is why hospital operating rooms mandate **anti-static flooring**, **isolated power systems (IPS)**, and **residual-current devices (RCD)** that trip at ‚Č§ 30 mA. > - It also justifies the strict **earth-leakage limit of 5 mA** on medical equipment ‚ÄĒ to ensure that any fault current is *interrupted* before it can reach dangerous levels through wet skin. > > *(1 mark)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse "isolated" with "insulated":** *Isolated* means **galvanic separation** (no conductive path). *Insulated* means **covered with dielectric**. A device can be insulated but not isolated. > 2. **Don't quote "1 mA = microshock":** It is **10 ¬ĶA**, not 1 mA. Off-by-three-orders-of-magnitude is the most common mark-loss in this module. > 3. **Don't forget the units in numerical answers:** $1.084 \times 10^{-6}$ A **must be written as 1.084 ¬ĶA** for full credit. > 4. **CMRR for medical ECG must be ‚Č• 100 dB, not 80 dB.** Show the log calculation explicitly. > 5. **Type CF limit under single-fault is 50 ¬ĶA, not 100 ¬ĶA.** Memorize both NC and SFC limits. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - [x] **Macroshock** = through skin, threshold ‚Čą **100 mA for 1 s** - [x] **Microshock** = directly to heart, threshold ‚Čą **10 ¬ĶA** - [x] **IEC 60601-1** is the global standard for medical electrical equipment safety - [x] **AAMI ES 60601-1** is the US adoption; **IS 13450** is the Indian (BIS) equivalent - [x] **Three leakage currents:** Earth, Enclosure (Touch), Patient - [x] **Type B** (earth-referenced, body), **Type BF** (floating, body), **Type CF** (floating, cardiac) - [x] **CF patient leakage limit:** 10 ¬ĶA (NC), 50 ¬ĶA (SFC) - [x] **Class I** = Basic insulation + protective earth; **Class II** = Double insulation; **Class III** = SELV ‚Č§ 25 V AC - [x] **Galvanic isolation** is achieved by **optocouplers** or **isolation transformers** (typical 5 kV RMS, $C_c \leq 15$ pF) - [x] **Defibrillator protection** uses **GDT + TVS + current-limiting resistors**; recovery within 5 s - [x] **CMRR ‚Č• 100 dB at 50 Hz** is required for medical ECG amplifiers - [x] **Right-leg drive (RLD)** circuit actively reduces common-mode voltage on the patient - [x] **Isolated Power Systems (IPS)** in ORs limit first-fault current to < 1 mA - [x] **Body current formula:** $I = V / (R_{skin} + R_{int} + R_{skin})$ ‚ÄĒ wet skin drops $R_{skin}$ by 100√ó - [x] **Let-go thresholds:** 20 mA (men), 15 mA (women) at 50/60 Hz - [x] **NFPA 99** governs US health-care facilities; **IEC 60601-1-2** governs EMC **Mnemonic to remember the order:** "**B**efore **B**ody, **C**ardiac **F**loats" ‚ÄĒ B, BF, then CF, with F = floating.

topics.title: Phase Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Phase Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# PHASE DETECTORS ‚ÄĒ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Phase Detector (PD)** is a nonlinear signal-processing circuit (or functional block) that accepts **two periodic input signals** ‚ÄĒ typically a *reference signal* $v_1(t)$ and a *feedback / local-oscillator signal* $v_2(t)$ ‚ÄĒ and produces an **output voltage $v_d(t)$** whose **average (DC) value** is a function of the **instantaneous phase difference** $\Delta\phi = \phi_1 - \phi_2$ between them. In a phase-locked loop (PLL) it is functionally a **phase-to-voltage converter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCBRT403 ‚ÄĒ Module 3):** Phase Detectors form the first functional stage of the Phase-Locked Loop. Their DC transfer characteristic **$v_d = f(\Delta\phi)$** (called the *phase detector characteristic curve*) determines the **loop gain, lock range, and capture behaviour** of the entire PLL. A PD may also be designed to output **frequency difference information** (Phase-Frequency Detector).

The two input signals are generally of the same nominal frequency and are written as:

$$v_1(t) = V_{1}\sin(\omega_c t + \phi_1)$$

$$v_2(t) = V_{2}\sin(\omega_c t + \phi_2)$$

where $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ is the **carrier / centre angular frequency** in **rad/s**, and $\phi_1$, $\phi_2$ are the instantaneous phase angles in **radians (rad)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> **Real-world analogy ‚ÄĒ "Two Metronomes and a Stopwatch":**
> Imagine two students tapping a table with a metronome each. Their tapping represents two AC signals of the *same* nominal frequency. Now imagine a third person, holding a **stopwatch**, opens it the moment the *first student taps* and closes it the moment the *second student taps*. The **time recorded** on the stopwatch is directly proportional to the **phase difference** between the two tapping events.
> - If both tap *simultaneously* ‚Üí stopwatch reads **0** ‚Üí phase difference = **0¬į**.
> - If the second student is *a quarter-beat late* ‚Üí stopwatch reads a *small time* ‚Üí phase difference = **90¬į**.
> - If the second student is *exactly one full beat late* ‚Üí stopwatch reads the *full period* $T$ ‚Üí phase difference = **360¬į**.
> The **stopwatch reading (time) is the phase detector's output voltage.** This is exactly what a phase detector does: it *converts a timing/phase difference into a proportional voltage level.*

## 1.3 Why Phase Detectors Are Used in Instrumentation

Phase detectors are indispensable because they enable:

- **Phase-Locked Loops (PLL)** for frequency synthesis, demodulation (FM, PM), carrier recovery, and clock generation.
- **Motor speed & position control** (servo systems compare shaft encoder phase with reference).
- **Direction-of-rotation detection** in incremental encoders (using dual-channel quadrature).
- **Power-quality instrumentation** (e.g., detecting phase errors between voltage and current in grid monitoring).
- **Radar and sonar** (coherent detection of Doppler-shifted echoes).
- **Lock-in amplifiers** in measurement instrumentation, where phase-sensitive detection recovers tiny signals buried in noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constant / standard metric associated with the topic:**
> The unit of phase difference is the **radian (rad)** or **degree (¬į)**. The conversion is $2\pi \text{ rad} = 360¬į$. The detector's sensitivity is expressed in **V/rad** (volts per radian).

# PHASE DETECTORS ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Phase Detectors

Phase detectors are broadly divided into two families based on the signal domain they process:

| **Class** | **Sub-type** | **Output** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I. Analog (Multiplicative)** | Balanced modulator / four-quadrant multiplier | $v_d = K_d \sin(\Delta\phi)$ (sinusoidal) | High-frequency PLLs, coherent detectors |
| **II. Digital (Sequential)** | XOR gate PD | $v_d \propto \Delta\phi$ (triangular, 0‚ÄďŌÄ) | CMOS PLLs, simple lock indicators |
| | Edge-triggered JK flip-flop PD | $v_d \propto \Delta\phi$ (triangular, 0‚Äď2ŌÄ) | Wider lock range PLLs |
| | Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD) | Two outputs (UP / DN) with $\pm$ sign of $\Delta\phi$ and $\Delta\omega$ | Modern digital PLLs (charge-pump PLLs) |

## 2.2 Operating Principle ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic

### **A. Multiplier-Type (Analog) Phase Detector**

1. The two input signals $v_1(t)$ and $v_2(t)$ are **multiplied** by a four-quadrant analog multiplier (e.g., AD633, MC1496, Gilbert cell).
2. Using the trigonometric identity $2\sin A \sin B = \cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)$:
3. The product contains a **difference-frequency term** $\cos(\phi_1-\phi_2)$ and a **sum-frequency term** $\cos(2\omega_c t + \phi_1+\phi_2)$.
4. The sum-frequency term is **filtered out** by a low-pass filter (LPF).
5. The remaining low-frequency component is the **DC output** that depends on phase.

### **B. XOR Gate Phase Detector**

1. Both input signals (ideally square waves at 50 % duty cycle) are fed into an XOR gate.
2. The XOR output is **LOW (0)** when both inputs are equal, and **HIGH (1)** when they differ.
3. The **average (DC) value** of the XOR output is proportional to the phase difference.
4. Range of linear operation: $\Delta\phi \in [0,\; \pi/2]$ (i.e., 0¬į to 90¬į).
5. The transfer characteristic is a **triangle wave** with peak at $\Delta\phi = 90¬į$.

### **C. Edge-Triggered (JK Flip-Flop) Phase Detector**

1. A JK flip-flop is wired so that the *reference* drives **J = 1** and the *feedback* drives **K = 1** (or *clock*).
2. The flip-flop output Q is **set HIGH** on the reference edge and **reset LOW** on the feedback edge.
3. The output pulse width equals the **time delay** between the two edges, which is $\Delta t = \Delta\phi / \omega_c$.
4. Range of linear operation: $\Delta\phi \in [0,\; 2\pi]$ ‚ÄĒ **double the XOR range** and therefore a *wider lock range*.

### **D. Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD)**

1. Two D flip-flops are clocked by the reference and feedback respectively; a single AND gate *resets* both.
2. The outputs are **UP** (active when reference leads) and **DN / DOWN** (active when feedback leads).
3. The PFD **detects both phase AND frequency difference** ‚ÄĒ it can pull a far-off-frequency VCO into lock.
4. The output is a **three-state** (0, +1, ‚ąí1) current/charge-pump command.
5. **No dead zone** in the linear region (a critical advantage over XOR/flip-flop PDs).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity / Equation** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input signals | $v_1 = V_1\sin(\omega_c t + \phi_1)$, $v_2 = V_2\sin(\omega_c t + \phi_2)$ | V | Same nominal $\omega_c$ |
| 2 | Instantaneous phase error | $\Delta\phi = \phi_1 - \phi_2$ | rad | The PD's *input* |
| 3 | Multiplier-PD output (after LPF) | $v_d = K_d \sin(\Delta\phi)$ | V | Sinusoidal characteristic |
| 4 | Linearized small-signal gain | $K_d = \frac{V_1 V_2}{2}$ (multiplier constant) | V/rad | Slope at $\Delta\phi = 0$ |
| 5 | Approximation for small error | $v_d \approx K_d \cdot \Delta\phi \quad (\Delta\phi \ll 1\text{ rad})$ | V | Linear region |
| 6 | XOR-PD average output | $V_{d,avg} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{\Delta\phi}{\pi}$ | V | Valid for $0 \le \Delta\phi \le \pi/2$ |
| 7 | XOR-PD sensitivity | $K_{d,XOR} = \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi}$ | V/rad | Constant slope |
| 8 | JK-PD average output | $V_{d,avg} = V_{sat} \cdot \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi}$ | V | Valid for $0 \le \Delta\phi \le 2\pi$ |
| 9 | JK-PD sensitivity | $K_{d,JK} = \frac{V_{sat}}{2\pi}$ | V/rad | Half of XOR sensitivity, but **2√ó wider** range |
| 10 | PFD output (charge-pump) | $I_{cp} = \pm I_p \cdot \text{sgn}(\Delta\phi)$ (ideal) | A | Tri-state output |
| 11 | Lock condition | $v_d = 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \Delta\phi = 0 \text{ (multiplier type)}$ | ‚ÄĒ | Loop is *locked* |
| 12 | Phase-to-voltage conversion | $v_d = K_d \cdot \Delta\phi$ (linearised) | V | Foundation of PLL linear analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** A multiplier-type phase detector has a **sine** characteristic, *not a linear* one. Students who substitute $\sin(\Delta\phi)$ with $\Delta\phi$ for **large** phase errors lose marks in derivations. Always state the small-angle approximation **$\sin(x) \approx x$** explicitly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Phase detectors underpin:

- **Telecommunications:** Clock & data recovery (CDR) circuits in high-speed serial links (PCIe, USB, Ethernet) use PFDs.
- **Instrumentation:** Lock-in amplifiers use a multiplier-type PD to extract a tiny AC signal buried in noise by shifting it to DC.
- **Power systems:** Phase comparators in synchro-check relays prevent out-of-phase breaker closures.
- **Aerospace & defence:** Doppler radar receivers, GPS carrier tracking.
- **Industrial automation:** Resolver-to-digital converters in CNC machines for shaft-angle measurement with sub-arc-second accuracy.

# PHASE DETECTORS ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ Output of the Multiplier-Type Phase Detector

**Given:** Two sinusoidal input signals at the same nominal frequency $\omega_c$, with a phase difference $\Delta\phi = \phi_1 - \phi_2$.

$$v_1(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega_c t + \phi_1)$$

$$v_2(t) = V_2 \sin(\omega_c t + \phi_2)$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Multiply the two signals** using an ideal four-quadrant analog multiplier with gain constant $K_m$ (V/V):

$$v_{prod}(t) = K_m \cdot v_1(t) \cdot v_2(t)$$

$$v_{prod}(t) = K_m \cdot V_1 V_2 \sin(\omega_c t + \phi_1) \sin(\omega_c t + \phi_2)$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:**

$$\sin A \sin B = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Identify the two components:**

- **Difference-frequency (slow) term:**

$$\frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2} \cos\bigl[(\omega_c t + \phi_1) - (\omega_c t + \phi_2)\bigr] = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2} \cos(\phi_1 - \phi_2)$$

- **Sum-frequency (fast) term:**

$$-\frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2} \cos\bigl[2\omega_c t + \phi_1 + \phi_2\bigr]$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply the low-pass filter (LPF)** which removes the $2\omega_c$ term:

$$v_d(t) = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2} \cos(\phi_1 - \phi_2) = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2} \cos(\Delta\phi)$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Combine constants** into the PD gain $K_d$:

$$v_d = K_d \cos(\Delta\phi), \qquad K_d = \frac{K_m V_1 V_2}{2}$$

> **Note on the cosine vs sine convention:** Some textbooks write the inputs as $V_1\sin(\cdot)$ and $V_2\cos(\cdot)$, which yields $v_d = K_d \sin(\Delta\phi)$. The choice is arbitrary ‚ÄĒ it merely *shifts* the operating point. Both are correct; KTU exams accept either, provided the convention is declared.

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Linearise around the lock point** $(\Delta\phi \to 0)$. Using the small-angle approximation $\cos x \approx 1 - x^2/2$ (first order $\approx 1$) and the AC component of the error:

For small deviations $\Delta\phi \ll 1$ rad from the quadrature point ($\Delta\phi = \pi/2$):

$$\sin(\Delta\phi) \approx \Delta\phi \quad \Longrightarrow \quad v_d \approx K_d \cdot \Delta\phi$$

This is the **linearised phase detector gain** used in all PLL small-signal analyses.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Transfer Characteristic of the XOR Phase Detector

**Given:** Two square-wave inputs $v_1$ and $v_2$ at the same frequency $f_c$, with 50 % duty cycle, phase difference $\Delta\phi$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Determine the XOR output waveform.** The XOR gate outputs **HIGH** when $v_1 \neq v_2$ and **LOW** when $v_1 = v_2$.

- For $\Delta\phi = 0$: $v_1$ and $v_2$ are identical ‚áí XOR = 0 (constant LOW).
- For $\Delta\phi = \pi$: $v_2$ is exactly the inverse of $v_1$ ‚áí XOR = 1 (constant HIGH).
- For intermediate $\Delta\phi \in (0, \pi)$: XOR output is a pulse train at $2f_c$ with a **duty cycle** equal to $\Delta\phi / \pi$.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute the average (DC) value.** The average of a pulse train with amplitude $V_{CC}$ and duty cycle $D$ is:

$$V_{d,avg} = V_{CC} \cdot D = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{\Delta\phi}{\pi}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ State the range of validity.** Because the duty cycle $D \in [0, 1]$ maps to $\Delta\phi \in [0, \pi]$, the linear range is:

$$0 \le \Delta\phi \le \pi \quad \text{(i.e., 0¬į to 180¬į)}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Identify the slope (PD gain):**

$$K_{d,XOR} = \frac{dV_{d,avg}}{d\Delta\phi} = \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi} \quad \text{[V/rad]}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Triangular transfer characteristic.** The full characteristic is *triangular* with period $\pi$:

$$V_{d,avg}(\Delta\phi) = \begin{cases} \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi}\Delta\phi, & 0 \le \Delta\phi \le \pi \\[4pt] \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi}(2\pi - \Delta\phi), & \pi \le \Delta\phi \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

> **Insight:** The XOR PD has its **maximum** output (i.e., $V_{d,avg} = V_{CC}$) at $\Delta\phi = \pi$ (180¬į), and the **minimum** (0 V) at $\Delta\phi = 0$ *or* $2\pi$. The *lock point* of a PLL using an XOR PD is therefore at $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$ (90¬į), not zero ‚ÄĒ this is a frequently-asked viva question.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚ÄĒ Edge-Triggered JK Flip-Flop Phase Detector

**Given:** The flip-flop is *set* on the leading edge of $v_1$ and *reset* on the leading edge of $v_2$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Express pulse width.** The output Q is HIGH for a duration:

$$\Delta t = \frac{\Delta\phi}{\omega_c} = \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi f_c}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute the average value.** Treating Q as a single pulse of width $\Delta t$ with amplitude $V_{sat}$ in a period $T = 1/f_c$:

$$V_{d,avg} = V_{sat} \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{T} = V_{sat} \cdot \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ State the range.** Because Q cannot go negative and resets at the next leading edge of $v_1$:

$$0 \le \Delta\phi \le 2\pi \quad \text{(0¬į to 360¬į)}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ PD gain (sensitivity):**

$$K_{d,JK} = \frac{V_{sat}}{2\pi} \quad \text{[V/rad]}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Comparison with XOR PD:**

$$\frac{K_{d,XOR}}{K_{d,JK}} = \frac{V_{CC}/\pi}{V_{sat}/(2\pi)} = 2 \quad (\text{assuming } V_{CC} = V_{sat})$$

**Conclusion:** The XOR PD has *twice the sensitivity* of the JK PD but *half the linear range*. This is the key trade-off in PD design.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚ÄĒ Lock Condition and Static Phase Error of a PLL (1st-order)

A 1st-order PLL has **no integrator** in the loop filter, so the VCO control signal equals the PD output in steady state. At lock, $v_d = 0$ (multiplier type) or $v_d = V_{CC}/2$ (XOR type) ‚ÄĒ and the VCO frequency must equal the input frequency:

$$\omega_{VCO} = \omega_0 + K_{VCO} \cdot v_d = \omega_{in}$$

Solving for the static phase error:

$$\Delta\phi_{static} = \frac{\omega_{in} - \omega_0}{K_{VCO} \cdot K_d}$$

> **Engineering implication:** A 1st-order PLL **cannot track a frequency step with zero error**; it always settles to a *non-zero* static phase error. Higher-order loops (with integrators) eliminate this ‚ÄĒ a key reason PFD-based charge-pump PLLs use PI loop filters.

# PHASE DETECTORS ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a phase detector and state its primary function in a phase-locked loop.**
> **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A phase detector (PD) is a nonlinear circuit that compares two input periodic signals ‚ÄĒ a reference signal and a feedback (or local) signal ‚ÄĒ and produces an output voltage whose average (DC) value is a function of the phase difference $\Delta\phi$ between the two inputs. In a phase-locked loop (PLL), the phase detector is the **first functional block** whose output drives the loop filter and hence the VCO control input. It serves as a **phase-to-voltage converter** that converts a timing/phase error into a proportional DC level, enabling the loop to correct any frequency or phase mismatch between the input and the VCO output.

*(Scoring: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for function in PLL, 1 mark for naming the output quantity.)*

### **Q2. List any four types of phase detectors commonly used in electronic instrumentation.**
> **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four commonly used phase detector types are:

1. **Analog multiplier-type phase detector** (e.g., AD633, MC1496 based) ‚ÄĒ produces a sinusoidal $K_d \sin(\Delta\phi)$ characteristic.
2. **XOR-gate digital phase detector** ‚ÄĒ produces a triangular transfer characteristic over 0 to $\pi$ rad.
3. **Edge-triggered JK flip-flop phase detector** ‚ÄĒ produces a triangular characteristic over 0 to $2\pi$ rad.
4. **Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD)** ‚ÄĒ a three-state sequential logic circuit used with a charge pump; it detects both phase and frequency errors with no dead zone.

*(Scoring: 0.5 mark per correct type with brief distinguishing feature, 0.5 mark for naming the PFD as the modern standard.)*
