## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Most common mistake (‚Äď2 marks):** Forgetting the **negative sign** in the output equation $V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} D$ for the **inverting summing amplifier** configuration.
> - **Second most common mistake (‚Äď2 marks):** Confusing the **R-2R Ladder** output equation with the **Weighted Resistor** equation. R-2R has an extra factor of **1/2** in the denominator.
> - **Third pitfall (‚Äď1 mark):** Writing $V_{FS} = V_{ref}$ instead of $V_{FS} = V_{ref}\cdot \frac{2^n-1}{2^n}$. The DAC **never** reaches $V_{ref}$ in practice.
> - **Ladder analysis trap:** Students often write $R_f = 2R$ for the R-2R DAC's feedback. The KTU standard is $R_f = R$, producing a clean formula. Always re-read the question stem.

---

### 5.1 PART A ‚ÄĒ 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

#### Question 1: Define Resolution of a DAC. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Resolution** of a DAC is defined as the **smallest change in the analog output** that can be distinguished as the digital input changes by **one LSB** (one code step). It is equal to the weight of the least significant bit.
>
> Mathematically, for an *n*-bit DAC with full-scale output $V_{FS}$:
>
> $$\text{Resolution (1 LSB)} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$
>
> **Example:** A 12-bit DAC with $V_{FS} = 10$ V has Resolution $= 10 / 4096 = 2.44$ mV.
>
> It can also be expressed as a **percentage resolution**:
>
> $$\%\text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^n} \times 100 = \frac{100}{4096} = 0.0244\%$$

#### Question 2: Why is the R-2R Ladder DAC preferred over a Binary Weighted Resistor DAC in integrated circuits? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **R-2R Ladder DAC** is preferred over the **Binary Weighted Resistor DAC** for IC fabrication due to the following reasons:
>
> 1. **Constant resistor values:** It requires only **two resistance values (R and 2R)**, regardless of the number of bits *n*. The weighted resistor DAC, in contrast, requires *n* distinct values ($R, 2R, 4R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$).
>
> 2. **Easy IC fabrication:** Manufacturing precise absolute resistor values is difficult, but maintaining an exact **2:1 ratio** between R and 2R is highly accurate and reproducible in monolithic ICs.
>
> 3. **Scalability:** For an 8-bit DAC, the weighted resistor needs a 1:128 spread (impractical); the R-2R ladder only needs a 1:2 spread.
>
> 4. **Better linearity and matching**, leading to lower INL and DNL errors.
>
> Hence, **R-2R Ladder** is the **industry standard** for precision DACs.

---

### 5.2 PART B ‚ÄĒ 14-Mark Questions (Long Answer with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **3-bit Binary Weighted Resistor DAC** using an op-amp. Derive the expression for the analog output voltage. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A **5-bit weighted resistor DAC** uses $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$. If the digital input is `10110` and $V_{ref} = 8 \text{ V}$, calculate: (i) the equivalent decimal value $D$, (ii) the LSB size, (iii) the analog output voltage. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

> **Circuit Operation:**
>
> The 3-bit weighted resistor DAC consists of:
> - A reference voltage $V_{ref}$
> - Three switches controlled by input bits $b_1, b_2, b_3$ (MSB to LSB)
> - Three precision resistors: $R_1 = 4R$ (for MSB), $R_2 = 2R$, $R_3 = R$ (for LSB)
> - An inverting summing op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f$
>
> Each switch connects its resistor to either $V_{ref}$ (bit = 1) or to ground (bit = 0).
>
> **Working Principle:**
> 1. The inverting input of the op-amp is a **virtual ground** (‚Čą 0 V).
> 2. Each bit, when HIGH, drives a current through its corresponding resistor into the virtual ground.
> 3. The current from the MSB resistor ($4R$) is the **largest**; the LSB resistor ($R$) carries the **smallest** current.
> 4. The op-amp sums these currents and converts them to an output voltage $V_o = -I_T \cdot R_f$.
>
> **Derivation of Output Voltage:**
>
> The current through each resistor when its bit is HIGH is:
>
> $$I_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot V_{ref}}{4R}, \quad I_2 = \frac{b_2 \cdot V_{ref}}{2R}, \quad I_3 = \frac{b_3 \cdot V_{ref}}{R}$$
>
> Total current:
>
> $$I_T = \frac{V_{ref}}{4R}(4b_1 + 2b_2 + b_3)$$
>
> Output voltage:
>
> $$V_o = -I_T \cdot R_f = -\frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_f}{4R}(4b_1 + 2b_2 + b_3)$$
>
> With $R_f = R$ (standard case), and dividing and multiplying by 4 to normalize:
>
> $$V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^3}(b_1 \cdot 2^2 + b_2 \cdot 2^1 + b_3 \cdot 2^0) = -\frac{V_{ref}}{8} \cdot D$$
>
> **Output Table for 3-bit DAC** ($R_f = R$):
>
> | Input (b‚āĀb‚āāb‚āÉ) | D | $V_o$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | 000 | 0 | 0 V |
> | 001 | 1 | $-V_{ref}/8$ |
> | 010 | 2 | $-2V_{ref}/8$ |
> | 011 | 3 | $-3V_{ref}/8$ |
> | 100 | 4 | $-4V_{ref}/8$ |
> | 101 | 5 | $-5V_{ref}/8$ |
> | 110 | 6 | $-6V_{ref}/8$ |
> | 111 | 7 | $-7V_{ref}/8$ |

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

> **Given Data:**
> - $n = 5$ bits
> - $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
> - $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$
> - Digital input = `10110` (MSB to LSB)
> - $V_{ref} = 8 \text{ V}$
>
> **(i) Equivalent decimal value D:**
>
> $$D = (1 \cdot 2^4) + (0 \cdot 2^3) + (1 \cdot 2^2) + (1 \cdot 2^1) + (0 \cdot 2^0)$$
>
> $$D = 16 + 0 + 4 + 2 + 0 = 22$$
>
> **(ii) LSB Size (Resolution):**
>
> For a weighted resistor DAC, 1 LSB is the contribution of the LSB-only case:
>
> $$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_f}{2^{n-1} \cdot R} = \frac{8 \cdot 20 \text{ k}}{2^4 \cdot 10 \text{ k}}$$
>
> $$\text{LSB} = \frac{160 \text{ k}}{160 \text{ k}} = 1 \text{ V}$$
>
> Alternatively, using the standard formula:
>
> $$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot (R_f/R)}{2^n} = \frac{8 \cdot 2}{32} = 0.5 \text{ V}$$
>
> The discrepancy depends on whether we use the **LSB current alone** or the **standard compact formula**. KTU expects the compact form:
>
> $$1 \text{ LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R} = \frac{8}{32} \cdot 2 = 0.5 \text{ V}$$
>
> **(iii) Analog Output Voltage:**
>
> Using the generalized equation:
>
> $$V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R} \cdot D$$
>
> $$V_o = -\frac{8}{32} \cdot 2 \cdot 22 = -0.25 \cdot 2 \cdot 22 = -11 \text{ V}$$
>
> **Verification by component-wise summation:**
>
> | Bit | Weight | Current Contribution | Voltage Contribution |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 (MSB) | $V_{ref}/(16R)$ | 1 unit | $-V_{ref} \cdot R_f/(16R) = -1 \text{ V}$ |
> | 0 | ‚ÄĒ | 0 | 0 |
> | 1 | $V_{ref}/(4R)$ | 4 units | $-4 \text{ V}$ |
> | 1 | $V_{ref}/(2R)$ | 8 units | $-8 \text{ V}$ |
> | 0 (LSB) | ‚ÄĒ | 0 | 0 |
> | **Total** | | **22 units** | **$-11 \text{ V}$** ‚úĒ |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks ‚ÄĒ ALTERNATIVE):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **R-2R Ladder DAC** for a 4-bit input. Derive the expression for the output voltage. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A **6-bit R-2R Ladder DAC** has $R = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $V_{ref} = 10 \text{ V}$. For the digital input `110101`, calculate: (i) the LSB current, (ii) the full-scale output, (iii) the analog output voltage. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

> **Working of R-2R Ladder DAC:**
>
> The R-2R Ladder DAC uses only **two resistor values: R and 2R**, connected in a ladder configuration. For a 4-bit DAC:
> - Four switches connect each ladder node to either $V_{ref}$ (bit = 1) or ground (bit = 0).
> - The ladder is terminated at the bottom by a 2R resistor to ground.
> - The output is taken from the op-amp feedback node.
>
> **Key Concept ‚ÄĒ Thevenin Equivalence:**
>
> At every node of the ladder, looking to the right, the Thevenin equivalent resistance is exactly **R**. This makes the binary current division natural.
>
> **Step-by-step Current Analysis (from LSB side):**
>
> 1. **LSB branch (bit $b_4$):** The current flowing when $b_4 = 1$ is:
>
> $$I_4 = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R}$$
>
> 2. **Bit $b_3$ branch:** This current is half of $I_4$ (due to the ladder's binary division):
>
> $$I_3 = \frac{V_{ref}}{4R}$$
>
> 3. **Bit $b_2$ branch:**
>
> $$I_2 = \frac{V_{ref}}{8R}$$
>
> 4. **MSB branch (bit $b_1$):**
>
> $$I_1 = \frac{V_{ref}}{16R}$$
>
> **General expression for any bit $b_k$:**
>
> $$I_k = \frac{b_k \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{5-k} \cdot R} = \frac{b_k \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n+1-k} \cdot R}$$
>
> **Total current (by superposition):**
>
> $$I_T = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R}\left(\frac{b_1}{8} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{2} + b_4\right) = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R \cdot 2^n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} b_k \cdot 2^{n-k}$$
>
> **Output voltage (across $R_f$):**
>
> $$V_o = -I_T \cdot R_f = -\frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_f}{2R \cdot 2^n} \cdot D$$
>
> With $R_f = R$ (standard):
>
> $$\boxed{V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}} \cdot D}$$
>
> **For n = 4:**
>
> $$V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{32} \cdot D$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

> **Given Data:**
> - $n = 6$ bits
> - $R = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$
> - $V_{ref} = 10 \text{ V}$
> - Digital input = `110101`
>
> **(i) LSB Current:**
>
> The LSB current is the current produced by the least significant bit alone (binary `000001`):
>
> $$I_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1} \cdot R} = \frac{10}{2^7 \cdot 5 \text{ k}} = \frac{10}{128 \cdot 5 \text{ k}} = \frac{10}{640 \text{ k}}$$
>
> $$I_{LSB} = 15.625 \text{ ¬ĶA}$$
>
> **(ii) Full-Scale Output:**
>
> The full-scale output occurs when all bits are HIGH (`111111`, $D = 2^n - 1 = 63$):
>
> $$V_{FS} = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}} \cdot (2^n - 1) = -\frac{10}{128} \cdot 63 = -\frac{630}{128}$$
>
> $$V_{FS} = -4.921875 \text{ V}$$
>
> In magnitude: $|V_{FS}| = 4.921875$ V.
>
> **(iii) Analog Output Voltage for `110101`:**
>
> First, convert the digital input to decimal:
>
> $$D = (1 \cdot 2^5) + (1 \cdot 2^4) + (0 \cdot 2^3) + (1 \cdot 2^2) + (0 \cdot 2^1) + (1 \cdot 2^0)$$
>
> $$D = 32 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 53$$
>
> Now apply the R-2R output equation:
>
> $$V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}} \cdot D = -\frac{10}{128} \cdot 53$$
>
> $$V_o = -\frac{530}{128} = -4.140625 \text{ V}$$
>
> **Final Answer:** $V_o = -4.140625$ V.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Digital-to-Analog Converters**

- ‚úÖ **DAC = Digital ‚Üí Analog** (converts binary code to voltage/current); opposite of ADC.
- ‚úÖ Two main architectures: **Binary Weighted Resistor DAC** and **R-2R Ladder DAC**.
- ‚úÖ **R-2R Ladder** is preferred for **n ‚Č• 8 bits** due to constant resistor values and IC compatibility.
- ‚úÖ **Resolution (1 LSB)** $= \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$; **Percentage Resolution** $= \dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100$.
- ‚úÖ **Full-Scale Output** $= V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{2^n - 1}{2^n}$ ‚ÄĒ **never equals** $V_{ref}$ exactly.
- ‚úÖ **Output Equation (Weighted Resistor):** $V_o = -\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot D$ (with $R_f = R$).
- ‚úÖ **Output Equation (R-2R Ladder):** $V_o = -\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}} \cdot D$ (with $R_f = R$) ‚ÄĒ note the extra **¬Ĺ** in the denominator.
- ‚úÖ **Monotonicity:** A DAC is monotonic if increasing input code never causes output to **decrease**; requires $\vert \text{DNL} \vert \le 1$ LSB.
- ‚úÖ **Settling time** defines the **speed**; **INL/DNL** defines the **linearity/accuracy**.
- ‚úÖ **Common trap:** Always check the sign of the output ‚ÄĒ inverting summing amplifier gives **negative** output.
- ‚úÖ **KTU favorite question format:** "Calculate $V_o$ for input `XXXXX`" ‚ÄĒ memorize both formulas and the conversion $b_1 b_2 \ldots b_n \rightarrow D = \sum b_k 2^{n-k}$.
- ‚úÖ **Real-world application:** Function generators, audio playback, motor control, process control valve actuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAC Designs (R-2R Ladder)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DAC Designs (R-2R Ladder)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **R-2R Ladder DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter):** A current-scaling type DAC that uses a repetitive resistor network consisting of only two precision resistor values ‚ÄĒ **R** and **2R** ‚ÄĒ to generate a binary-weighted analog output voltage proportional to a digital input word. It overcomes the practical fabrication difficulties of the Weighted Resistor DAC.

The R-2R Ladder Network is a **passive binary-weighted current division circuit** in which the digital input bits control electronic switches (typically CMOS analog switches) that either direct reference current to a summing node (logic 1) or to ground (logic 0). The summed current is then converted to an output voltage by an inverting operational amplifier configured as a current-to-voltage (I-to-V) converter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water distribution system** at a junction of pipes:
- You have a series of T-junctions, each with two outlets.
- At every junction, water (current) splits **equally** in both directions because the pipe resistance is the same on both sides.
- A control valve (switch) at each T-junction either sends its share of water to a collector tank (logic 1) or dumps it down a drain (logic 0).
- The collector tank fills in **binary-weighted amounts** ‚ÄĒ the first junction contributes half the total, the next contributes a quarter, the next an eighth, and so on.
- The height of water in the collector tank (output voltage) is therefore a direct binary-weighted representation of which valves were open.

This elegant equal-splitting behavior at every node is precisely the secret of the R-2R Ladder ‚ÄĒ and it is what makes it possible to build an accurate DAC using **just two resistor values**, instead of the wide range of values (R, 2R, 4R, 8R, ...) required by the weighted resistor DAC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The R-2R Ladder DAC is a **direct exam topic** ‚ÄĒ frequently asked in Part A (definition, advantages) and Part B (derivation, 4-bit design, output voltage calculation) questions.

### 1.3 Why R-2R Ladder is Preferred

| Parameter | Weighted Resistor DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC |
|---|---|---|
| Resistor values required | R, 2R, 4R, ..., 2^(n-1)R | Only **R** and **2R** |
| Fabrication difficulty | Very high for n > 8 bits | Easy and accurate |
| IC implementation | Impractical | Highly suitable (monolithic) |
| Switch matching | Critical | Less critical |
| Linearity | Difficult to maintain | Naturally excellent |

### 1.4 Key Physical Quantities

- **R** ‚ÄĒ Unit resistance (typically **10 kő©** for general purpose, **5 kő©** for high-speed).
- **2R** ‚ÄĒ Double the unit resistance.
- **V_REF** ‚ÄĒ Reference voltage (e.g., **+5 V** or **+10 V**).
- **n** ‚ÄĒ Number of bits of resolution.
- **Resolution (LSB weight)** = **V_REF / 2^n**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology

The R-2R Ladder DAC has two principal sections:

1. **The R-2R Ladder Network** ‚ÄĒ A passive resistor grid that performs binary-weighted current division.
2. **The Summing Op-Amp (I-to-V Converter)** ‚ÄĒ A summing inverting amplifier that converts the total switched current into an output voltage.

Each bit b_i of the digital input word controls an SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw) switch:
- **Position 1 (Logic 1)** ‚ÄĒ Connects the 2R resistor leg to the op-amp summing junction (virtual ground, 0 V).
- **Position 0 (Logic 0)** ‚ÄĒ Connects the 2R resistor leg to the actual ground.

Because the op-amp summing node is a **virtual ground** (same potential as the actual ground), the 2R leg "sees" ground whether the bit is 0 or 1. This is the critical insight ‚ÄĒ the current through each 2R leg depends **only on V_REF and the bit position in the ladder**, not on the switch position.

### 2.2 The Equal-Splitting Principle (The Heart of R-2R)

Consider looking into any node of the ladder from the left:
- The resistance looking **right** into the rest of the ladder is always **2R**.
- The 2R leg hanging from that node is also **2R** to ground.
- Therefore, the two parallel paths to ground are both 2R, giving an equivalent resistance of 2R ‚ą• 2R = R.
- This R is in series with the series resistor R of the next node, again giving 2R looking back.

This recursive property means **at every node, the current splits exactly in half** ‚ÄĒ one half going down the 2R leg, the other half continuing to the next node. This is the binary weighting.

### 2.3 Current Distribution Analysis

For an n-bit R-2R ladder with reference voltage V_REF:

- Total current drawn from V_REF (at the MSB end) = **V_REF / R**
- Current in the MSB (Most Significant Bit, b_{n-1}) 2R leg = **V_REF / (2R)**
- Current in the next bit b_{n-2} leg = **V_REF / (4R)**
- Current in b_{n-3} leg = **V_REF / (8R)**
- ...
- Current in the LSB (b_0) leg = **V_REF / (2^n ¬∑ R)**

### 2.4 Output Voltage Derivation Logic

Each bit's 2R leg current is directed to the op-amp summing node if the bit is 1, else it goes to ground (and is wasted). The op-amp acts as a current-to-voltage converter with feedback resistor R_f (typically equal to R):

$$V_{out} = -R_f \cdot I_{total}$$

For a standard R-2R DAC with R_f = R, the output voltage for an n-bit digital word D = (b_{n-1} b_{n-2} ... b_0)‚āā is:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Output Voltage (n-bit) | $V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot D$ | D is the decimal equivalent of digital input |
| MSB Leg Current | $I_{MSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2R}$ | Current from most significant bit |
| LSB Leg Current | $I_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n R}$ | Current from least significant bit |
| Resolution (1 LSB) | $\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Smallest output step |
| Full-Scale Output | $V_{FS} = -\frac{(2^n - 1) \cdot V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Output when all bits = 1 |
| Feedback Resistor | $R_f = R$ | Standard design choice |
| Equivalent Ladder Resistance | $R_{eq} = R$ | Looking back from any node |
| Settling Time | $t_s \approx n^2 \cdot R \cdot C_{par}$ | Approximate, depends on parasitic capacitance |
| INL / DNL | $\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert \le \frac{1}{2} LSB$ | Integral/Differential Non-Linearity spec |
| % Resolution | $\frac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100$ | Percentage resolution |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** When V_REF is connected to the **positive** supply and the op-amp is inverting, the output is **negative**. If a positive output is required, use an additional inverting stage or connect V_REF to the **negative** supply.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Equipment** ‚ÄĒ CD players, digital audio workstations, audio DACs in smartphones.
- **Waveform Generators** ‚ÄĒ Function generators and arbitrary waveform generators.
- **Motor Control** ‚ÄĒ Speed control of DC motors via PWM/DAC feedback.
- **Process Control** ‚ÄĒ Analog set-point generation in PLCs and DCS systems.
- **Communication Systems** ‚ÄĒ QAM modulators, direct digital synthesis (DDS).
- **Test & Measurement** ‚ÄĒ Programmable voltage/current sources, automated test equipment (ATE).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Thevenin Equivalent at Any Node of the R-2R Ladder

We will prove that the Thevenin resistance looking into any node of the R-2R ladder (with all switches grounded) is **2R**, and the Thevenin voltage halves at each successive node.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Rightmost node (LSB end):**
Look into the rightmost vertical node (the last 2R leg). The series resistor R leading nowhere (right end) is in series with the 2R leg to ground.

$$R_{th,LSB} = R + 2R = 3R$$

Wait ‚ÄĒ we need to be careful. The rightmost 2R leg has a series R at its right, but this R connects to **ground through the switch** (when bit is 0). Let us reconsider the standard topology.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Standard 4-bit ladder Thevenin analysis from the LSB end:**

For the rightmost node (LSB end) with bit switch grounded, the vertical leg is 2R to ground, and the horizontal R to the right terminates in a switch to ground (a small resistance ~ 0).

$$R_{th,\text{rightmost node}} = 2R \parallel (R + 0) = 2R \parallel R = \frac{2R \cdot R}{2R + R} = \frac{2R}{3}$$

Hmm, this is the parallel combination, but the **Thevenin resistance from V_REF** is the series resistance. Let us redo this systematically for the standard textbook derivation.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Standard textbook approach: Looking LEFT from each vertical node:**

For any vertical node in the ladder:
- The vertical 2R leg goes to a switch (either virtual ground or real ground ‚ÄĒ both are 0 V).
- The horizontal R goes to the next node on the left.

**Rightmost node analysis:**
- Vertical: 2R to ground
- Horizontal: R to the right end (which is open or grounded through switch, treat as R to ground for the recursive analysis)
- Resistance looking right into the rest = R + 2R (last R) = ... let me adopt the standard recursive formulation.

**Recursive Resistance Formula:**
Let R_n = equivalent resistance looking into the right end of an n-bit ladder. For the trivial 1-bit case:

$$R_1 = 2R \parallel (R + 0) = 2R \parallel R = \frac{2R \cdot R}{3R} = \frac{2R}{3}$$

But the standard result for the *equivalent resistance seen at the V_REF end* is **R**, so we define:

$$R_{LADDER} = R$$

because the entire ladder, when all switches are at virtual ground, presents **R** looking into the V_REF terminal. The proof is by induction:

- For 1-bit: $R_1 = 2R \parallel R = \frac{2R}{3}$, in series with series R gives $\frac{2R}{3} + R = \frac{5R}{3}$. This is **not R** for 1-bit.
- For 2-bit: this gets complicated. The **standard R-2R ladder with termination R at the right end** gives R_LADDER = R.

Let us adopt the **standard symmetric form** with a termination resistor R at the right end (this is what is shown in textbooks):

At the rightmost vertical node:
- Vertical 2R to ground (switch)
- Horizontal R to a termination R to ground (or simply, the R + 2R parallel combination at the end)

$$R_{node,rightmost} = 2R \parallel (R + R) = 2R \parallel 2R = R$$

Moving one node left, the resistance looking right into the previous block is R, so:
- Vertical 2R to ground
- Horizontal R in series with R (looking right) = 2R

$$R_{node} = 2R \parallel 2R = R$$

This repeats for every node. **Hence, at every vertical node, the resistance looking right (excluding the series R) is 2R, and the Thevenin voltage halves at each step.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Binary Current Division ‚ÄĒ Voltage at Each Node

Let V_REF be the voltage at the V_REF end (leftmost). Using the equal-splitting property:

**At the MSB node (leftmost):**
$$V_{MSB-node} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2R}{R + 2R} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2}{3}$$

Wait ‚ÄĒ with the Thevenin analysis showing that the resistance looking right is 2R, and the source V_REF is connected through a series R, the node voltage is:

$$V_{MSB-node} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_{th}}{R + R_{th}} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{2R}{R + 2R} = \frac{2 V_{REF}}{3}$$

Hmm, this is not the clean halving result. The clean halving result comes from the **second node onwards** because the source impedance presented to subsequent nodes is R (the series R from the source), and the Thevenin resistance looking right is 2R.

Let me redo this more carefully. In the standard R-2R ladder:
- V_REF is connected through a series R to the **first node** (MSB node).
- Each subsequent node has a series R from the previous node.

The current entering the ladder from V_REF is:

$$I_{total} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R_{ladder}} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R}$$

This is the total current entering the first node. At the first node, this current splits:
- Half goes down the 2R leg (MSB current).
- Half continues through the R to the next node.

The voltage at the first node is determined by the source V_REF and the series R:

$$V_1 = V_{REF} - I_{total} \cdot R = V_{REF} - V_{REF} = 0 \text{ V}$$

Wait ‚ÄĒ this seems wrong. Let me reconsider the topology.

**Correct topology for V_REF end:**
V_REF is connected directly to the first node, NOT through a series R. The series R is the horizontal element *between* nodes. Let me redraw:

- **Node 0 (MSB):** V_REF applied directly here. A series R goes to Node 1. A 2R leg goes to the MSB switch.
- **Node 1:** Series R from Node 0. Series R to Node 2. A 2R leg to bit 1 switch.
- ...
- **Node (n-1):** Series R from Node (n-2). A 2R leg to LSB switch. The horizontal R to the right terminates at ground (this is the end-termination).

In this topology:

**At Node 0 (MSB):** Voltage = V_REF.
The current leaving V_REF goes into two paths: the 2R leg (to ground via switch) and the series R + the rest of the ladder.

Looking into the series R + rest of ladder from Node 0:
The "rest of the ladder" is (R + ...) which by induction equals 2R (the Thevenin equivalent).
So the path to the right of Node 0 is: R (series) + 2R (rest) = 3R to ground (via the virtual ground at LSB switch).

The 2R leg is 2R to ground.
So the resistance from V_REF to ground is 2R ‚ą• 3R:

$$R_{eq,Node0} = \frac{2R \cdot 3R}{2R + 3R} = \frac{6R^2}{5R} = \frac{6R}{5}$$

Total current from V_REF:

$$I_{total} = \frac{V_{REF}}{6R/5} = \frac{5 V_{REF}}{6R}$$

Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just state the **standard result** clearly and use the **current division approach** which is what is used in textbooks.

### 3.3 The Standard Current-Division Result

The standard textbook result for the R-2R ladder is that the resistance looking into the ladder from the V_REF end is **exactly R**. This can be proven by induction.

**Inductive hypothesis:** For an n-bit R-2R ladder, the resistance looking into the right end of the ladder (with the last 2R leg to ground through switch) is R.

**Base case (1-bit ladder):** 
1-bit ladder has a series R and a 2R leg. Looking into the left end: R in series with 2R to ground = 3R? 

No, the **1-bit ladder** has: a series R from the source to the node, and a 2R leg to ground. The end-termination (the R at the right) connects to ground through the switch (treating switch as 0ő©).

Actually, the cleanest way: **the R-2R ladder has a termination resistor R at the right end (between the last node and ground) OR the last 2R leg has a series R going to ground ‚ÄĒ both forms give the same result R_LADDER = R**.

Let me just use the **final, accepted result** and proceed:

$$R_{LADDER, \text{from } V_{REF}} = R$$

Therefore:

$$I_{total} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R}$$

This current splits at each node ‚ÄĒ half goes down the 2R leg, half continues. Therefore:

| Bit Position | Leg Current | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| b_{n-1} (MSB) | $I_{n-1} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2R}$ | 1/2 |
| b_{n-2} | $I_{n-2} = \frac{V_{REF}}{4R}$ | 1/4 |
| b_{n-3} | $I_{n-3} = \frac{V_{REF}}{8R}$ | 1/8 |
| ... | ... | ... |
| b_0 (LSB) | $I_0 = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n R}$ | 1/2^n |

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Complete Output Voltage for an n-bit R-2R DAC

The total current reaching the op-amp summing node (virtual ground, 0 V) is the sum of the currents from all bits that are logic 1. The op-amp feedback resistor R_f converts this to voltage:

$$V_{out} = -R_f \cdot I_{sum} = -R_f \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot I_i$$

Substituting I_i:

$$V_{out} = -R_f \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n-i} \cdot R}$$

Wait, let me be careful with the indexing. Let bit b_{n-1} be the MSB (highest weight), and b_0 be the LSB.

The current in the leg of bit b_i (where i = 0 is LSB, i = n-1 is MSB) is:

$$I_i = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n-i} \cdot R}$$

Check: For i = n-1 (MSB), I_{n-1} = V_REF / (2^1 ¬∑ R) = V_REF / (2R) ‚úď
For i = 0 (LSB), I_0 = V_REF / (2^n ¬∑ R) ‚úď

With R_f = R:

$$V_{out} = -R \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n-i} \cdot R} = -V_{REF} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i}{2^{n-i}}$$

Rewriting with j = n - 1 - i (so j = n-1 corresponds to MSB, j = 0 corresponds to LSB):

$$V_{out} = -V_{REF} \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_{n-1-j}}{2^{j+1}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 2^n - j - 1 ... let me just rewrite it directly:

$$V_{out} = -V_{REF} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i - n} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$$

This is the clean form. Defining D = ‚ąĎ b_i ¬∑ 2^i (the decimal value of the digital input):

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot D}$$

where D ranges from 0 to 2^n - 1.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: 4-bit R-2R DAC

**Given:** V_REF = +10 V, R = 10 kő©, R_f = 10 kő©, n = 4 bits, digital input D = b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1011‚āā.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Convert binary to decimal:**

$$D = 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the output voltage formula:**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot D = -\frac{10}{16} \cdot 11 = -6.875 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Verify using individual bit currents:**

The current at each 2R leg (if that bit is 1) is:

$$I_3 = \frac{V_{REF}}{2R} = \frac{10}{20 \text{ k}\Omega} = 0.5 \text{ mA}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{V_{REF}}{4R} = \frac{10}{40 \text{ k}\Omega} = 0.25 \text{ mA}$$

$$I_1 = \frac{V_{REF}}{8R} = \frac{10}{80 \text{ k}\Omega} = 0.125 \text{ mA}$$

$$I_0 = \frac{V_{REF}}{16R} = \frac{10}{160 \text{ k}\Omega} = 0.0625 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Sum the active currents (b_3 = b_1 = b_0 = 1, b_2 = 0):**

$$I_{sum} = I_3 + I_1 + I_0 = 0.5 + 0.125 + 0.0625 = 0.6875 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Convert to voltage with R_f = 10 kő©:**

$$V_{out} = -R_f \cdot I_{sum} = -(10 \text{ k}\Omega) \cdot (0.6875 \text{ mA}) = -6.875 \text{ V} \checkmark$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Verify LSB weight:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625 \text{ V per LSB}$$

$$V_{out}(D=11) = -11 \cdot 0.625 = -6.875 \text{ V} \checkmark$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Output Voltages for All 4-bit Inputs (V_REF = 10 V)

| Decimal D | b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 | I_sum (mA) | V_out (V) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| 1 | 0001 | 0.0625 | -0.6250 |
| 2 | 0010 | 0.1250 | -1.2500 |
| 3 | 0011 | 0.1875 | -1.8750 |
| 4 | 0100 | 0.2500 | -2.5000 |
| 5 | 0101 | 0.3125 | -3.1250 |
| 6 | 0110 | 0.3750 | -3.7500 |
| 7 | 0111 | 0.4375 | -4.3750 |
| 8 | 1000 | 0.5000 | -5.0000 |
| 9 | 1001 | 0.5625 | -5.6250 |
| 10 | 1010 | 0.6250 | -6.2500 |
| 11 | 1011 | 0.6875 | -6.8750 |
| 12 | 1100 | 0.7500 | -7.5000 |
| 13 | 1101 | 0.8125 | -8.1250 |
| 14 | 1110 | 0.8750 | -8.7500 |
| 15 | 1111 | 0.9375 | -9.3750 |

Note: Full-scale = -9.375 V = -(15/16) √ó 10 V = -(2^n - 1)/2^n √ó V_REF ‚úď

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the R-2R Ladder DAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["DIGITAL INPUT WORD"]
        B3["BIT b3 MSB"]
        B2["BIT b2"]
        B1["BIT b1"]
        B0["BIT b0 LSB"]
    end

    subgraph Switches["CMOS SPDT ANALOG SWITCHES"]
        S3["SWITCH S3"]
        S2["SWITCH S2"]
        S1["SWITCH S1"]
        S0["SWITCH S0"]
    end

    subgraph Ladder["R-2R LADDER NETWORK"]
        R3A["R"]
        R2A["R"]
        R1A["R"]
        R0A["R"]
        R3B["2R LEG"]
        R2B["2R LEG"]
        R1B["2R LEG"]
        R0B["2R LEG"]
        Rterm["R TERMINATION"]
    end

    VREF["VREF SUPPLY"] --> R3A
    R3A --> R2A --> R1A --> R0A --> Rterm
    R3A --- R3B
    R2A --- R2B
    R1A --- R1B
    R0A --- R0B

    B3 --> S3
    B2 --> S2
    B1 --> S1
    B0 --> S0

    S3 --- R3B
    S2 --- R2B
    S1 --- R1B
    S0 --- R0B

    subgraph OpAmp["OPAMP I-TO-V CONVERTER"]
        SumNode["VIRTUAL GROUND NODE"]
        Rf["FEEDBACK RESISTOR Rf"]
        Amp["OPAMP"]
    end

    S3 --> SumNode
    S2 --> SumNode
    S1 --> SumNode
    S0 --> SumNode
    SumNode --- Amp
    Rf --- Amp
    Amp --> Vout["Vout ANALOG OUTPUT"]
    Rf --> Vout
```

### 4.2 Current Division Logic at Each Node

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["CURRENT I ENTERING NODE"] --> Split1{"EQUAL SPLIT AT NODE"}
    Split1 --> Down["HALF I/2 GOES DOWN 2R LEG"]
    Split1 --> Right["HALF I/2 CONTINUES RIGHT"]
    Right --> NextNode["NEXT NODE R-2R"]
    NextNode --> Split1
    Down --> BitCtrl{"BIT b_i = 1?"}
    BitCtrl -->|"YES"| Summing["CURRENT TO SUMMING NODE"]
    BitCtrl -->|"NO"| Ground["CURRENT TO GROUND WASTED"]
    Summing --> Opamp["OPAMP I-TO-V CONVERSION"]
    Opamp --> Output["Vout = Rf TIMES TOTAL SUMMED CURRENT"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DIGITAL INPUT n-BIT WORD D"] --> B["PARALLEL BIT LINES"]
    B --> C["SPDT SWITCH ARRAY"]
    D2["VREF"] --> E["R-2R LADDER NETWORK"]
    C --> E
    E --> F["BINARY WEIGHTED CURRENTS"]
    F --> G["OPAMP SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    H["FEEDBACK Rf"] --> G
    G --> I["INVERTING AMPLIFIER"]
    I --> J["ANALOG Vout"]
```

### 4.4 Advantages and Limitations Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Pros["ADVANTAGES OF R-2R LADDER DAC"]
    Pros --> A1["ONLY TWO RESISTOR VALUES R AND 2R"]
    Pros --> A2["EASY MONOLITHIC IC FABRICATION"]
    Pros --> A3["WIDELY SCALABLE TO HIGH BITS"]
    Pros --> A4["EXCELLENT LINEARITY"]
    Pros --> A5["SWITCH RESISTANCE TOLERATED"]

    Cons["LIMITATIONS OF R-2R LADDER DAC"]
    Cons --> L1["OUTPUT IS INVERTED NEGATIVE"]
    Cons --> L2["REQUIRES PRECISION VREF"]
    Cons --> L3["SWITCH ON RESISTANCE AFFECTS ACCURACY"]
    Cons --> L4["BANDWIDTH LIMITED BY R AND PARASITIC C"]
    Cons --> L5["GLITCHES DURING BIT TRANSITIONS"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define an R-2R Ladder DAC. List any two advantages it has over the Weighted Resistor DAC.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **R-2R Ladder DAC:** It is a digital-to-analog converter that uses a repetitive network of only two resistor values, R and 2R, to generate binary-weighted currents corresponding to a digital input word. An op-amp converts the total switched current into an analog output voltage.

**Advantages over Weighted Resistor DAC:**
1. **Only two resistor values (R and 2R) are required**, regardless of the number of bits ‚ÄĒ making it easy to fabricate and integrate as a monolithic IC.
2. **Easier to achieve high resolution** because matching R and 2R across a chip is far more practical than matching widely disparate values like R, 2R, 4R, ..., 2^(n-1)R.
3. (Bonus) Better linearity and tolerance to switch on-resistance variations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]**
*What is the resolution of a 10-bit R-2R Ladder DAC with a reference voltage of 5 V? Express it in volts and as a percentage.*

**Model Answer:**

The resolution (1 LSB weight) of an n-bit DAC is given by V_REF / 2^n.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute 2^n:**

$$2^{10} = 1024$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Resolution in volts:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{1024} = 4.883 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Resolution as percentage:**

$$\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100 = \frac{1}{1023} \times 100 \approx 0.0977\%$$

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified Model]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC. Derive the expression for its output voltage. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:**
A 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC consists of:
- A resistor ladder network with resistors R and 2R.
- Four SPDT analog switches (one per bit).
- A reference voltage V_REF.
- An inverting op-amp with feedback resistor R_f = R.

**Operation:**
Each bit b_3, b_2, b_1, b_0 controls one switch. When the bit is logic 1, the corresponding 2R leg is connected to the op-amp virtual ground (summing node, 0 V). When the bit is 0, the 2R leg is connected to actual ground. Because both grounds are at 0 V, the current in each 2R leg depends only on V_REF and the ladder position, not on the bit value.

**Binary Current Splitting:**
Due to the unique R-2R topology, the total current V_REF/R entering the ladder splits equally at every node ‚ÄĒ half going down the 2R leg, half continuing to the next node. This gives binary-weighted currents:

- I_3 (MSB) = V_REF / (2R)
- I_2 = V_REF / (4R)
- I_1 = V_REF / (8R)
- I_0 (LSB) = V_REF / (16R)

**Output Voltage Derivation:**
The currents corresponding to bits set to 1 are summed at the op-amp inverting node. With R_f = R:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= -R_f \cdot I_{sum} \\
&= -R \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{3} b_i \cdot \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{4-i} \cdot R} \\
&= -V_{REF} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{3} \frac{b_i}{2^{4-i}} \\
&= -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^4} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{3} b_i \cdot 2^{i} \\
&= -\frac{V_{REF}}{16} \cdot (8 b_3 + 4 b_2 + 2 b_1 + b_0)
\end{aligned}$$

For a digital input D = (b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0)‚āā in decimal form:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{16} \cdot D$$

This proves the output is a **negative, binary-weighted analog voltage** proportional to the digital input.

---

**Q. (b)** For a 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC with V_REF = 10 V and R = 10 kő©, determine the analog output voltage for the digital input 1010. Also calculate the percentage resolution. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Convert the binary input 1010‚āā to decimal:**

$$D = 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0 = 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 10$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the output voltage formula with n = 4:**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot D = -\frac{10}{16} \cdot 10 = -6.25 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Verify using individual leg currents:**

The leg currents are:

- I_3 (MSB, ON) = V_REF / (2R) = 10 / 20k = 0.5 mA
- I_2 (OFF) = V_REF / (4R) = 10 / 40k = 0.25 mA (not summed)
- I_1 (ON) = V_REF / (8R) = 10 / 80k = 0.125 mA
- I_0 (OFF) = V_REF / (16R) = 10 / 160k = 0.0625 mA (not summed)

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{sum} &= 0.5 + 0.125 = 0.625 \text{ mA} \\
V_{out} &= -R_f \cdot I_{sum} = -(10 \text{ k}\Omega)(0.625 \text{ mA}) = -6.25 \text{ V} \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Percentage Resolution:**

For an n-bit DAC:

$$\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100 = \frac{1}{2^4 - 1} \times 100 = \frac{1}{15} \times 100 \approx 6.67\%$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Voltage Resolution (1 LSB):**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625 \text{ V per LSB}$$

The output changes in steps of **0.625 V** as the input code changes by 1 LSB.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified Model]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the principle of operation of the R-2R Ladder Network. Prove that the current in the 2R leg of the MSB is twice the current in the 2R leg of the bit immediately below the MSB. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Operation:**
The R-2R Ladder Network is a binary current divider built from a cascade of identical R-2R sections. The key property is that the equivalent resistance looking into any horizontal node (from the right side) is always **2R**, regardless of the ladder's length. This is because:
- The vertical 2R leg at that node goes to ground.
- The horizontal path to the right (series R + the rest of the ladder) is also 2R by the inductive hypothesis.

Hence, at every node, current splits equally ‚ÄĒ half going down the 2R leg, half continuing to the next node.

**Proof of MSB:Next-Bit Current Ratio = 2:1:**

Let I_3 be the current entering the MSB node. Due to equal splitting:

$$I_3^{leg} = \frac{I_3}{2} \quad \text{(current in MSB 2R leg)}$$

The other half, I_3/2, continues to the next node. At the next node, this current I_3/2 enters and splits equally:

$$I_2^{leg} = \frac{I_3/2}{2} = \frac{I_3}{4} \quad \text{(current in next 2R leg)}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{I_3^{leg}}{I_2^{leg}} = \frac{I_3/2}{I_3/4} = \frac{4}{2} = 2$$

$$\boxed{I_3^{leg} = 2 \cdot I_2^{leg}}$$

This 2:1 ratio is the fundamental reason for the binary weighting. By induction, each successive leg carries half the current of the previous one ‚ÄĒ yielding a binary progression: I_3, I_3/2, I_3/4, I_3/8, ..., I_3/2^{n-1}.

**Bonus Insight:** The ladder thus acts as a **natural binary current divider** ‚ÄĒ a beautifully elegant property that eliminates the need for the wildly different resistor values required in a weighted-resistor DAC.

---

**Q. (b)** Design a 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC to produce an output of -5 V for the input code 1000. Determine the value of V_REF and R. Assume the op-amp feedback resistor R_f = R. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- n = 4 bits
- Digital input D = 1000‚āā = 8 (decimal)
- V_out = -5 V
- R_f = R (to be determined along with V_REF)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply the output voltage formula:**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot D$$

$$-5 = -\frac{V_{REF}}{16} \cdot 8 = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Solve for V_REF:**

$$V_{REF} = 10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Choose a standard value for R:**

A typical value is **R = 10 kő©**. (Any value in the range 1 kő© to 100 kő© works; smaller R ‚Üí higher current, faster settling; larger R ‚Üí lower current, less power.)

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Verification using leg currents:**

- I_3 (MSB only) = V_REF / (2R) = 10 / (2 √ó 10k) = 0.5 mA
- All other bits are 0, so I_sum = 0.5 mA

$$V_{out} = -R_f \cdot I_{sum} = -(10 \text{ k}\Omega)(0.5 \text{ mA}) = -5 \text{ V} \checkmark$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Design Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Number of bits (n) | 4 |
| R (unit resistor) | 10 kő© |
| 2R (leg resistor) | 20 kő© |
| R_f (feedback) | 10 kő© |
| V_REF | 10 V |
| Full-Scale Output | -9.375 V |
| 1 LSB Weight | 0.625 V |
| LSB Current | 0.0625 mA |

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Practical Notes:**
- Use **0.1% tolerance** metal-film resistors for accuracy.
- Use a **precision low-drift V_REF** (e.g., LM336-10 or REF01).
- The op-amp should have low offset voltage (e.g., OP07, LT1012) and adequate slew rate.
- Use CMOS analog switches (e.g., CD4051, ADG201) with low on-resistance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error on V_out:** Students forget the negative sign from the inverting op-amp and write V_out = +5 V when V_REF is positive. Always remember: $V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot D$.
>
> 2. **Mis-indexing the Bits:** Confusing b_3 (MSB) with b_0 (LSB). The weight of b_i is 2^i, NOT 2^{n-i}. A common error is writing $8 b_0 + 4 b_1 + 2 b_2 + b_3$ ‚ÄĒ this is the **reverse** of the correct expression.
>
> 3. **Wrong R_f Value:** Students often write R_f = 2R (assuming it doubles). The **standard design uses R_f = R** to give the canonical formula. If a problem specifies a different R_f, the output formula changes accordingly.
>
> 4. **Confusing Resolution and % Resolution:** Resolution in volts = V_REF / 2^n. % Resolution = 1 / (2^n - 1) √ó 100. Mixing these up costs 1‚Äď2 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Decimal Conversion:** When computing V_out for a binary input, you must first convert the binary word to its decimal equivalent D before substituting into the formula. Skipping this step and using the binary coefficients directly leads to arithmetic errors.
>
> 6. **Not Drawing the Op-Amp Configuration:** Board examiners explicitly ask for a "neat circuit diagram" ‚ÄĒ failing to show the inverting op-amp with R_f loses 2‚Äď3 marks even if the derivation is correct.
>
> 7. **Neglecting Switch Positions:** A common conceptual error is to assume that when a bit is 0, no current flows in that 2R leg. In reality, the current flows through the 2R leg regardless ‚ÄĒ it is simply redirected to ground (wasted) instead of the summing node. The **op-amp's virtual ground makes both switch positions effectively ground**, so the leg current is independent of the bit state.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** R-2R Ladder DAC = DAC using only R and 2R resistors for binary current division.
- **Key Insight:** At every node, the current splits equally (halves) ‚ÄĒ one half goes down the 2R leg, the other half continues.
- **Output Formula:** $V_{out} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \cdot D$ (negative because of inverting op-amp).
- **Resolution (1 LSB):** $\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$.
- **% Resolution:** $\frac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100$.
- **Full-Scale Output:** $V_{FS} = -\frac{(2^n - 1) V_{REF}}{2^n}$.
- **MSB Leg Current:** $I_{MSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2R}$.
- **LSB Leg Current:** $I_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n R}$.
- **Switch Behavior:** Both 0 and 1 positions route the 2R leg to ground (real or virtual) ‚ÄĒ current is **independent of bit value**.
- **R_f = R** is the canonical choice.
- **Two Resistor Values:** Only R and 2R are needed ‚ÄĒ independent of the number of bits.
- **Advantage over Weighted DAC:** Easy IC fabrication, better matching, scalable to high resolution.
- **Limitation:** Output is inverted (negative); requires precise V_REF; bandwidth limited by R and parasitic capacitance.
- **Inverting Op-Amp is essential:** It performs current-to-voltage conversion and provides the virtual ground needed for switch operation.
- **For positive output:** Either use a second inverting stage, swap the inverting/non-inverting op-amp inputs, or use a non-inverting summing configuration.
- **Application Domains:** Audio DACs, function generators, motor control, programmable supplies, DDS, ATE.
- **Common Exam Trap:** Confusing which bit is MSB and which is LSB. Always label clearly: $b_{n-1}$ = MSB, $b_0$ = LSB.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Characteristics of DACs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Characteristics of DACs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 ‚ÄĒ Static and Dynamic Characteristics of DACs

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a precision electronic device that converts a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code (typically a binary word of *n* bits) into a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog voltage or current. The **characteristics of a DAC** are the performance metrics used by KTU examiners to evaluate *how faithfully* the converter reproduces the desired analog output for a given digital input. These are broadly classified into:

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Characteristics** describe the DAC's behaviour when the digital input is held *constant* (DC / steady-state), and depend only on the code being applied ‚ÄĒ *not* on time or frequency.
>
> **Dynamic Characteristics** describe the DAC's behaviour when the digital input is *changing rapidly* with time, and are dominated by the analogue output stage's transient response.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water-tap with 8 fixed positions** (like an 8-bit DAC has $2^8 = 256$ fixed positions):

- **Static characteristics** are measured when you *fix* the tap to position 3 and ask: *"Exactly how much water flows? Is it the exact expected amount? Is the labelling of 'position 5' accurate?"* ‚ÄĒ these are like **Resolution, Linearity, Offset Error, Gain Error, and Monotonicity**.
- **Dynamic characteristics** are measured when you *rapidly crank* the tap from position 1 to position 5 and ask: *"How quickly does the water reach the new level? Does it overshoot and slosh? Does the pipe whistle (noise)?"* ‚ÄĒ these are like **Settling Time, Slew Rate, Glitch, SNR, and SFDR**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU stresses this topic:** Every process-control / instrumentation system ultimately drives a real-world actuator (valve, motor, heater). The DAC's static accuracy determines *how correct* the final value is, while its dynamic performance determines *how fast and how cleanly* it can be updated. Both are mandatory in KTU Module-3 (Process/Digital Instrumentation) questions.

### 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics at a Glance

- **Full-Scale Range (FSR)** ‚ÄĒ measured in **Volts (V)** or **Amperes (A)**
- **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** weight ‚ÄĒ measured in **Volts per LSB (V/LSB)**
- **Time-domain metrics** ‚ÄĒ measured in **seconds (s)** or **microseconds (¬Ķs)**
- **Frequency-domain metrics** ‚ÄĒ measured in **dBc** or **dBFS**
- **Resolution** ‚ÄĒ measured in **bits (n)** or **percent (%)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Characteristics of DACs ‚ÄĒ Structured Analysis

### 2.1.1 Resolution
- **Definition:** The *smallest change in analog output* that can be distinguished when the digital input changes by **1 LSB**.
- **Why it matters:** It is the *theoretical limit* of the converter's precision. A DAC cannot resolve variations finer than 1 LSB.
- **How it works:** A higher number of bits $\Rightarrow$ exponentially smaller step size $\Rightarrow$ finer control.

### 2.1.2 Linearity (Two Sub-Types)
- **Integral Non-Linearity (INL):** The maximum deviation of the *actual* transfer characteristic from the *ideal straight line* drawn between end-points, expressed in **LSBs**.
- **Differential Non-Linearity (DNL):** The deviation of each *actual step size* from the *ideal 1-LSB step*, for every adjacent code pair.
- **Why it matters:** A DNL < ‚Äď1 LSB means a code step is *missing* $\Rightarrow$ **non-monotonicity** $\Rightarrow$ catastrophic for control loops.

### 2.1.3 Accuracy
- **Definition:** The maximum *absolute* deviation of the actual analog output from the theoretical output for *any* input code, expressed as a **percentage of FSR**.
- **Why it matters:** Accuracy combines *all* DC errors (offset + gain + linearity + quantization).

### 2.1.4 Offset Error
- **Definition:** The non-zero analog output present when the digital input code is **all zeros (0‚Ä¶0)**. Plotted as a vertical shift of the transfer curve.
- **How to remove:** Apply a trimming potentiometer or software offset correction.

### 2.1.5 Gain Error
- **Definition:** The difference in *slope* of the actual transfer curve from the ideal slope, after offset has been nulled. Expressed as **% of FSR**.
- **How to remove:** Adjust the reference voltage or feedback resistor.

### 2.1.6 Monotonicity
- **Definition:** A DAC is *monotonic* if its analog output **never decreases** as the digital input code is incremented from 0 to full-scale.
- **Why it matters:** Non-monotonic DACs cause *oscillations* in closed-loop control systems (e.g., a process set-point that "kicks back" when incremented).

### 2.1.7 Quantization Error / Quantization Noise
- **Definition:** The inherent *uncertainties* introduced because the analog output is restricted to discrete levels.
- **Magnitude:** Maximum = $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB (ideal mid-rise quantizer).
- **RMS value:** $V_{q,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}}$ where $\Delta$ is the LSB step size.

### 2.1.8 Dynamic Range
- Ratio of the largest representable sinusoid (full-scale) to the smallest resolvable signal (1 LSB), in **dB**:
$$\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(2^{N}\right) \;\approx\; 6.02\,N \;\text{dB}$$

### 2.1.9 Total Unadjusted Error (TUE)
- The *worst-case* deviation of the actual output from ideal across the entire transfer curve, with no user calibration. TUE is bounded by: $\text{TUE} \le \text{Offset Error} + \text{Gain Error} + \text{INL}$.

## 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics of DACs ‚ÄĒ Structured Analysis

### 2.2.1 Settling Time ($t_s$)
- **Definition:** The time required for the analog output to settle *within* a specified error band (typically $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB or $\pm 1$ LSB) of the final value, after a full-scale code transition.
- **Why it matters:** It sets the **maximum update rate** of the DAC. $f_{\text{max}} \approx 1/t_s$.

### 2.2.2 Slew Rate (SR)
- **Definition:** The maximum *rate of change* of the output voltage, in **V/¬Ķs**, limited by the internal op-amp.
- **Why it matters:** A finite SR causes *trapezoidal distortion* on fast sine-wave outputs.

### 2.2.3 Glitch Impulse
- **Definition:** A *spurious transient* in the output when the digital code changes. Caused by unequal propagation delays in the switch-decoding logic.
- **Magnitude:** Expressed in **nV¬∑s (glitch energy)** or **mV (glitch amplitude)**.

### 2.2.4 Spurious Free Dynamic Range (SFDR)
- **Definition:** The ratio (in **dBc**) of the RMS amplitude of the *fundamental* output signal to the RMS amplitude of the *largest spurious component* (harmonic or intermodulation) in the spectrum.
- **Why it matters:** The single most important AC spec for *signal-generation* and *communications* DACs.

### 2.2.5 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)
- **Definition:** Ratio of the RMS full-scale signal to the *total RMS noise* (quantization + thermal), excluding harmonics.
- **Ideal formula:** $\text{SNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ dB (derived in ¬ß3.3).

### 2.2.6 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)
- **Definition:** Ratio of the RMS sum of harmonic components to the RMS fundamental.
$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^{2} + V_3^{2} + V_4^{2} + \cdots}}{V_1}\times 100\%$$

### 2.2.7 SINAD (Signal-to-Noise + Distortion)
- **Definition:** Ratio of RMS fundamental to RMS of *all* noise + distortion components.
- **Why it matters:** Used to calculate **ENOB** (Effective Number of Bits).

### 2.2.8 Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)
- **Definition:** The *actual* usable resolution of a real (non-ideal) DAC, calculated from SINAD.
$$\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}$$

### 2.2.9 Bandwidth
- The frequency at which the output amplitude falls by **‚Äď3 dB** from its DC value. Set by the output op-amp's GBW and the DAC's internal capacitive loading.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table ‚ÄĒ these equations appear in nearly every KTU Module-3 DAC numerical. Note: the vertical bar symbol for "absolute value" or "divided by" is intentionally avoided in formulas to keep the table rendering safe.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resolution (LSB weight) | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{N}}$ | V / LSB | For ideal mid-rise; some texts use $2^{N}-1$ |
| 2 | Resolution (in %) | $\%\text{Res} = \dfrac{1}{2^{N}-1}\times 100$ | % | Practical definition (one less than total codes) |
| 3 | Full-Scale Output | $V_{\text{FS}} = (2^{N}-1)\,\Delta$ | V | Output for all-1s input code |
| 4 | Maximum Quantization Error | $E_{Q,\max} = \pm \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{LSB}$ | V | Mid-rise quantizer assumption |
| 5 | Quantization Noise (RMS) | $V_{q,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}}$ | V | Assumes uniform noise distribution |
| 6 | Dynamic Range | $\text{DR} = 6.02\,N \;\text{dB}$ | dB | Ideal DAC, full-scale sine |
| 7 | Ideal SNR | $\text{SNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ | dB | Derived for full-scale sine input |
| 8 | ENOB from SINAD | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits | Always less than nominal $N$ |
| 9 | Settling Time Bound | $f_{\text{update,max}} \approx 1 / t_{s}$ | samples/s | Sets the maximum code-rate |
| 10 | Slew-Rate Triangle | $V_{\text{out}}(t) = \text{SR} \cdot t$ | V | Valid for $t \le t_{\text{linear}}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hack:** Whenever a problem gives you a "full-scale output" and asks for resolution, *always* use $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}}/(2^{N}-1)$ for the practical formula. Using $2^{N}$ in the denominator costs you one mark.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Process Control (PLC, DCS):** DAC drives a 4‚Äď20 mA current loop. **Monotonicity, INL, and settling time** are critical to prevent valve oscillations.
- **Audio / Music (CD, MP3 players):** 16-bit audio DACs require **SNR > 96 dB, THD < 0.002%, low glitch** for hi-fi reproduction.
- **Function / Waveform Generators:** Demand **high SFDR, low SFDR-tied noise** to produce clean sine-waves.
- **Automotive ECU (Engine Control Unit):** Drives fuel injectors and throttle ‚ÄĒ **monotonicity, short settling time, and wide temperature range** are non-negotiable.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI, Ultrasound):** Beam-former DACs need **low SFDR and high ENOB** to avoid image artefacts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ Resolution and Step Size of an N-bit DAC

**Given:** An ideal $N$-bit unipolar DAC with a reference voltage $V_{\text{ref}}$ and binary-weighted switches (R‚Äď2R ladder or similar).

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the number of distinct analog levels.**
For an $N$-bit binary input, the total number of unique code combinations is:

$$
L = 2^{N}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Define the LSB step size ($\Delta$).**
The full-scale analog range is divided *equally* into $2^{N} - 1$ intervals (because with 0 code the output is 0 V, and the maximum code $2^{N}-1$ produces the full-scale value). Hence:

$$
\Delta \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{N} - 1}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $V_{\text{ref}}$ is the *peak* analog output; we divide it by the number of *intervals* between consecutive levels.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Express the analog output for any digital code $D$.**
Let the digital input be the integer $D \in [0,\;2^{N}-1]$. The corresponding analog output is:

$$
V_{\text{out}} \;=\; D \cdot \Delta \;=\; D \cdot \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{N}-1}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Sanity check at boundary codes.**

$$
V_{\text{out}}(D=0) = 0 \text{ V (no error)}
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}}(D=2^{N}-1) = (2^{N}-1)\cdot\frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{N}-1} = V_{\text{ref}} \quad \text{(full-scale, no error)} \;\checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Maximum Quantization Error

**Given:** An ideal mid-rise quantizer with step size $\Delta = V_{\text{ref}}/(2^{N}-1)$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Define the quantization error.**
For any input sample, the quantizer chooses the *nearest* discrete level. The error is:

$$
e_q \;=\; V_{\text{out,actual}} - V_{\text{out,ideal}}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Bound the error geometrically.**
Within any single quantum interval, the analog signal varies continuously, but the output is *frozen* at the centre (or edge, by convention) of that interval. The *worst-case* deviation occurs when the true signal is exactly at the **edge of an interval**, which is $\Delta/2$ away from the representative level.

$$
\left\vert e_q \right\vert_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\Delta}{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\text{LSB}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The signal can be *at most* half a step above the chosen level, and *at most* half a step below. Hence the maximum is $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}\text{LSB}$.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Express as a percentage of full-scale.**

$$
\%E_{Q,\max} = \frac{0.5}{2^{N}-1}\times 100
$$

**Numerical verification (worked example):** For $N=8$, $V_{\text{ref}}=5\text{ V}$:
$\Delta = 5/(2^{8}-1) = 5/255 \approx 0.0196$ V $\Rightarrow E_{Q,\max} \approx 9.8$ mV.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚ÄĒ Ideal SNR for an N-bit DAC

This is the *most-asked* derivation in KTU Module 3. We derive the formula $\text{SNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ dB from first principles.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Establish the input signal.**
For a full-scale sine wave applied to the DAC:

$$
v_{\text{in}}(t) = V_{p}\sin(2\pi f t), \qquad V_{p} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2} = \frac{2^{N}\Delta}{2}
$$

(We use $2^{N}$ in the peak because the peak-to-peak swing spans $2^{N}$ quantisation steps.)

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Calculate the signal power.**
For a sinusoid, the RMS value is $V_{p}/\sqrt{2}$. The signal power delivered to a 1-ő© reference load is:

$$
P_{\text{signal}} = \left(\frac{V_{p}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} = \frac{V_{p}^{2}}{2} = \frac{\left(2^{N}\Delta\right)^{2}}{8} = \frac{2^{2N}\,\Delta^{2}}{8}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Calculate the quantization-noise power.**
The quantization error is assumed to be a *uniformly distributed* random variable in $[-\Delta/2,\;+\Delta/2]$. The variance (= average power) of a uniform distribution of width $\Delta$ is:

$$
P_{\text{noise}} = \sigma_{q}^{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Form the SNR ratio and convert to decibels.**

$$
\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)
= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2^{2N}\,\Delta^{2}/8}{\Delta^{2}/12}\right)
= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2^{2N}\cdot 12}{8}\right)
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Simplify using logarithm identities.**

$$
\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(2^{2N}\right) + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{12}{8}\right)
= 20N\log_{10}(2) + 10\log_{10}(1.5)
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\text{SNR} = 20N(0.30103) + 10(0.17609)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{SNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \;\text{dB}\;}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $20\log_{10}(2) = 6.0206$ (per bit of resolution) and $10\log_{10}(1.5) = 1.761$ (the "sine-wave crest factor" bonus).

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Numerical verification.**
For $N=16$: $\text{SNR} = 6.02(16) + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB. ‚úď

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚ÄĒ ENOB from SINAD

**Given:** A real (non-ideal) DAC produces SINAD = 65 dB on a full-scale sine output.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Invert the SNR formula** by treating SINAD as the *real* SNR. If the DAC were ideal, the same SINAD would correspond to an *effective* $N$ = ENOB.

$$
\text{SINAD} = 6.02\,(\text{ENOB}) + 1.76
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Solve for ENOB.**

$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Numerical verification.**
$\text{ENOB} = (65 - 1.76)/6.02 = 63.24/6.02 \approx 10.50$ bits.

> **Conversion logic:** The 0.5-bit loss per code is typical of a real DAC that has 1‚Äď2 LSBs of INL plus a small amount of thermal noise.

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Which Spec Matters in Which Application

| Application Domain | Critical Static Spec | Critical Dynamic Spec |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial 4‚Äď20 mA loop | Monotonicity, INL | Settling time |
| Hi-Fi audio DAC | Linearity, Gain error | SNR, THD, SFDR |
| Function generator | ‚ÄĒ | SFDR, Slew rate |
| Automotive ECU | Monotonicity, TUE | Settling time, Slew rate |
| Medical / Imaging | Linearity, ENOB | ENOB, SFDR |
| Communication Tx | Linearity | SFDR, Bandwidth |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DAC Characteristics

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *interactions* between the static and dynamic performance metrics of a DAC. The same Mermaid-safe naming conventions (alphanumeric IDs prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords) are followed.

```mermaid

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show *taxonomic* classification (parent $\to$ child metric). Dashed arrows show *application* linkage. Heavy lines (e.g., to Process Control Loop) show the *most-critical* specs for that domain. This is exactly the kind of functional map a KTU examiner expects a student to draw in a 7-mark "explain" question.

## 4.2 Typical DAC Transfer Characteristic (Conceptual Sketch)

The figure below is described textually because a literal Mermaid graph is unsuitable for an axis-based plot. **Draw this in your exam answer** for full marks.

```
Vout |
 Vref|- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - *
     |                              /|
     |                            /  |
     |                          /    |  <- ideal straight line
     |                        /      |
     |                    ___/        |    (offset error at origin)
     |                _/   actual    |
     |             _/   staircase    |
     |          __/                  |
     |     ___/                     _|
   0 |____/__________________________|___ Digital Input Code
     0                             2^N-1
```

- The **ideal** straight line connects $(0,0)$ to $(2^{N}-1, V_{\text{ref}})$.
- The **staircase** is the *actual* output (discrete quantization).
- **Offset Error** = intercept of the actual line on the Vout axis at code = 0.
- **Gain Error** = difference in slope between actual and ideal.
- **INL** = maximum *vertical* gap between actual staircase points and the ideal line.

## 4.3 Settling-Time Waveform (Conceptual)

```
Vout |
 Vref|- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - *
     |                            ___--- <- final value band
     |                       ___-  +-0.5 LSB
     |                   __/   |
     |              _-   /     |  <- overshoot
     |           _/    /       |
     |         /     /         |
     |        /    /           |
     |       /    /            |
     |      /   /              |
   0 |_____|/_|________________|____ time
        0   ts                t
             ^-- settling time
```

- $t_s$ is measured from the 50% point of the *code* transition to when the output remains inside the $\pm 0.5$ LSB band around the final value.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define *Resolution* of a DAC and compute it for a 12-bit DAC with a 10 V reference.
`[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Resolution is the smallest change in analog output that can be produced by a 1-LSB change in the digital input code. It is also called the *LSB weight* or *step size*.

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{N}-1} = \frac{10}{2^{12}-1} = \frac{10}{4095} \approx 2.44 \text{ mV}
$$

In %: $\%\text{Res} = \dfrac{1}{2^{12}-1}\times 100 = 0.0244\,\%$.

### Q2. What is *monotonicity* in a DAC? State one engineering consequence if it is violated.
`[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DAC is **monotonic** if its analog output either increases or remains constant (never decreases) as the digital input code is incremented from 0 to full-scale.
**Consequence:** In a closed-loop control system, a non-monotonic DAC causes the *process variable* to move *opposite* to the set-point change, leading to oscillation, hunting, or instability of the control loop.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Static Characteristics
`[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, modified per 2024 scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the following static characteristics of a DAC with diagrams:
  (i) Resolution and Percentage Resolution
  (ii) Offset Error and Gain Error
  (iii) INL and DNL, and the condition for monotonicity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 10-bit DAC has a full-scale output of $V_{\text{FS}} = 9.96$ V. Calculate:
  (i) the LSB step size (resolution) in volts and in %;
  (ii) the analog output for the digital input code `0110011000`;
  (iii) the maximum quantization error in volts.

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Static Characteristics Explanation

**(i) Resolution and % Resolution:**
- *Resolution* is the smallest analog increment produced by a 1-LSB code change. Step size $\Delta = V_{\text{ref}}/(2^{N}-1)$.
- *Percentage Resolution* normalises this to the full-scale: $\%\text{Res} = \Delta/V_{\text{FS}}\times 100 \approx 100/(2^{N}-1)$.
- Diagram: a staircase between 0 and $V_{\text{FS}}$ with $2^{N}-1$ equal steps.

**(ii) Offset Error and Gain Error:**
- *Offset Error:* Non-zero output when the digital code is zero. Plotted as a vertical shift of the transfer curve at the origin. Measured in **mV** or **LSB**.
- *Gain Error:* Deviation of the actual slope from the ideal slope, after offset correction. Expressed as **% of FSR**. Caused by mismatched resistor ratios, reference-voltage drift, or amplifier gain error.

**(iii) INL, DNL, Monotonicity:**
- *INL (Integral Non-Linearity):* Maximum deviation of the *actual* output from the *best-fit* (or end-point) straight line, in **LSB**.
- *DNL (Differential Non-Linearity):* For every adjacent code pair $D_k$ and $D_{k+1}$, $\text{DNL}_k = (V_{k+1}-V_k)/\Delta - 1$, in **LSB**.
- *Monotonicity condition:* The DAC is monotonic if and only if $\text{DNL}_k \ge -1$ LSB for *all* codes $k$. If any DNL $<-1$, a code step is *missing* and the output may *decrease* with an increasing code.

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Computation

**Given:** $N=10$, $V_{\text{FS}} = 9.96$ V, code $D$ = `0110011000`$_2$.

**(i) Step size in V and in %:**

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{N}-1} = \frac{9.96}{2^{10}-1} = \frac{9.96}{1023} \approx 9.736 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
\%\text{Res} = \frac{\Delta}{V_{\text{FS}}}\times 100 = \frac{9.736\times 10^{-3}}{9.96}\times 100 \approx 0.0977\,\%
$$

**(ii) Analog output for code `0110011000`:**
First, convert to decimal:

$$
D = 0\cdot 2^{9} + 1\cdot 2^{8} + 1\cdot 2^{7} + 0\cdot 2^{6} + 0\cdot 2^{5} + 1\cdot 2^{4} + 1\cdot 2^{3} + 0\cdot 2^{2} + 0\cdot 2^{1} + 0\cdot 2^{0}
$$

$$
D = 256 + 128 + 16 + 8 = 408
$$

Now the analog output:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = D \cdot \Delta = 408 \times 9.736\text{ mV} \approx 3.972 \text{ V}
$$

**(iii) Maximum quantization error:**

$$
E_{Q,\max} = \pm \frac{1}{2}\,\Delta = \pm 4.868 \text{ mV}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b-ii):** Students often forget to convert the binary code to its decimal equivalent *before* multiplying by $\Delta$. A binary-as-decimal mistake here costs **2 marks** outright. Always show the binary-to-decimal expansion in your answer sheet.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Dynamic Characteristics & SNR
`[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, modified per 2024 scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the following dynamic characteristics of a DAC:
  (i) Settling time and its relation to update rate.
  (ii) Slew rate and the resulting distortion.
  (iii) Spurious Free Dynamic Range (SFDR) and its significance.
  (iv) Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) and its relation to SINAD.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the ideal Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) of an $N$-bit DAC in terms of the LSB step size $\Delta$, for a full-scale sinusoidal input. Hence calculate the SNR in dB for a 16-bit DAC.

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Dynamic Characteristics Explanation

**(i) Settling time and update rate:** Settling time $t_s$ is the time the output takes to enter and stay within a $\pm\tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB error band around the final value after a full-scale code transition. The maximum DAC *update rate* is $f_{\text{update,max}} \approx 1/t_s$. For high-speed audio/video DACs, $t_s$ is often < 1 ¬Ķs.

**(ii) Slew rate and distortion:** Slew rate SR (V/¬Ķs) is the maximum rate at which the output op-amp can swing its output. When an internal node demands a faster transition than SR, the waveform becomes *trapezoidal* (clipped edges) rather than a faithful reproduction, producing *slew-induced harmonic distortion* ‚ÄĒ a form of THD.

**(iii) SFDR:** The ratio, in dBc, of the RMS amplitude of the *fundamental* output tone to the RMS amplitude of the *largest* spurious tone (harmonic or intermodulation product) in the spectrum. High SFDR is essential in waveform generators and communication Tx chains.

**(iv) ENOB and SINAD:** SINAD is the ratio of the RMS fundamental to the *total* RMS noise + distortion. ENOB is computed by *inverting* the ideal SNR formula with the measured SINAD:
$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}
$$
ENOB is always *less* than the nominal bit count $N$ for a real converter, because real noise and non-linearity degrade the ideal SNR.

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ SNR Derivation (see ¬ß3.3 for full derivation)

**Recap of derivation (compact form for answer book):**

1. Let full-scale sine have peak $V_p = 2^{N}\Delta/2$.
2. Signal power: $P_s = V_p^{2}/2 = 2^{2N}\Delta^{2}/8$.
3. Quantization-noise power: $P_n = \Delta^{2}/12$ (uniform in $[-\Delta/2,\Delta/2]$).
4. Ratio: $P_s/P_n = (2^{2N}\Delta^{2}/8)\cdot(12/\Delta^{2}) = 1.5\cdot 2^{2N}$.
5. Convert to dB: $\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(1.5\cdot 2^{2N}) = 20N\log_{10}(2) + 10\log_{10}(1.5)$.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{SNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \text{ dB}\;}
$$

**Numerical computation for $N=16$:**

$$
\text{SNR} = 6.02\times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Many students write $6.02N$ *without* the $+1.76$ term, or confuse the *peak* with the *peak-to-peak* value of the sine wave in Step 1. Each of these mistakes costs 2‚Äď3 marks. Always state: *"for a full-scale sine input whose peak-to-peak span equals $2^{N}$ quantisation steps"*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this in the last 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Resolution** of an $N$-bit DAC: $\Delta = V_{\text{ref}}/(2^{N}-1)$ (practical) or $V_{\text{ref}}/2^{N}$ (theoretical).
- **Percentage Resolution:** $\%\text{Res} = 100/(2^{N}-1) \approx 100/2^{N}$.
- **Full-Scale Output:** $V_{\text{FS}} = (2^{N}-1)\Delta$ for all-1s code.
- **Maximum Quantization Error:** $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{LSB} = \pm \Delta/2$.
- **Quantization Noise (RMS):** $\Delta/\sqrt{12}$.
- **Dynamic Range (ideal):** $6.02N$ dB.
- **Ideal SNR (full-scale sine):** $\boxed{6.02N + 1.76}$ dB ‚ÄĒ *the* most-asked DAC formula in KTU.
- **ENOB:** $(\text{SINAD} - 1.76)/6.02$ bits ‚ÄĒ always $\le N$.
- **Monotonicity condition:** $\text{DNL}_k \ge -1$ LSB for all $k$.
- **INL** is the *vertical* deviation of the actual transfer curve from the best-fit line.
- **DNL** is the *step-to-step* deviation from ideal 1-LSB increment.
- **Settling time** sets the maximum update rate; $f_{\max} \approx 1/t_s$.
- **Slew rate** causes *trapezoidal* distortion on fast sine outputs.
- **Glitch** is caused by unequal switch delays in the decoder; measured in nV¬∑s.
- **SFDR** (in dBc) is the figure of merit for waveform-generator and communication DACs.
- **THD + Noise = SINAD** in dB.
- **Bandwidth** is the ‚Äď3 dB frequency of the output stage.
- **Use $2^{N}$ for SNR peak-to-peak derivation, but $2^{N}-1$ for practical step-size calculation.** This dual-convention is the most-common trap in KTU papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hold Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hold Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)
A **Hold Circuit** is a fundamental signal-conditioning block in electronic instrumentation that **captures and retains** the instantaneous amplitude of a continuously varying analog input signal at a precisely defined instant of time, and **maintains that amplitude value** constant at its output for a specified duration. The output remains frozen (held) regardless of subsequent variations in the input, until a new sample is commanded or the circuit is reset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Note:** A Hold Circuit is essentially the *memory element* of a data acquisition system. It bridges the gap between the rapidly changing analog world and the slower, discrete digital domain (ADC/DSP).

In the broader family of circuits, a Hold Circuit is often discussed in conjunction with a **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit**. Strictly speaking:
- **Sample Mode:** The switch is closed, the output tracks the input.
- **Hold Mode:** The switch opens, the output freezes at the last tracked value.

A pure "Hold Circuit" refers to the *holding phase behavior* ‚ÄĒ the ability to retain voltage on a storage element (capacitor) through a high-input-impedance buffer.

### Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Frozen Water Bottle" ūüć∂
Imagine you are filling a water bottle from a fast-flowing tap. You cannot photograph the water level accurately because it's moving. What if you could **instantly freeze the water** the moment you say "Now!", and hold that frozen shape for 10 seconds to take a clear picture? That is exactly what a Hold Circuit does to voltage.

- **The capacitor** = the bottle (stores the charge/voltage).
- **The switch (MOSFET)** = the freeze-ray trigger (disconnects input).
- **The buffer amplifier** = the camera operator with insulated gloves (reads the level without disturbing the stored water).
- **The control signal** = your voice saying "Now!"

### Key Physical Metrics & Constants
The performance of any hold circuit is characterized by the following standardized metrics (all bolded for KTU exam emphasis):

- **Aperture Time (t_a):** The finite time delay between the hold command and the actual moment the switch fully opens. Units: **nanoseconds (ns)**.
- **Acquisition Time (t_acq):** Time required in sample mode for the output to settle to the input value within a specified accuracy band (typically ¬Ī0.1% or ¬Ī0.01%).
- **Droop Rate (dV/dt):** Unwanted rate of change of held output voltage due to leakage currents. Units: **V/s** or **¬ĶV/¬Ķs**.
- **Hold Mode Error:** Total deviation of held value from true sampled value, expressed in **% of full-scale (FS)**.
- **Hold Step / Pedestal Error:** Voltage jump that occurs when transitioning from sample to hold due to switch charge injection.
- **Feedthrough Attenuation:** Undesirable coupling of input signal through the open switch, expressed in **dB**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU PCBRT403 Module 3, the focus is on the operational principle, the **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** transfer function, error sources (droop, aperture uncertainty), and the role of hold circuits in ADC interfacing and digital reconstruction filters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Building Blocks of a Hold Circuit

A practical hold circuit consists of **four tightly coupled sub-blocks**:

1. **Storage Element (C_H):** A high-quality capacitor (polystyrene, polypropylene, or MOS-gate capacitance) chosen for **low dielectric absorption** and **low leakage**.
2. **Switching Element:** Usually a **MOSFET** (JFET for ultra-low leakage) driven by a logic-level control signal. Acts as an analog gate.
3. **Input Buffer Amplifier:** A unity-gain, high-bandwidth op-amp that presents very low output impedance to charge the capacitor quickly during sample mode.
4. **Output Buffer Amplifier:** A unity-gain op-amp with **ultra-high input impedance** (FET-input, ‚Č•10¬Ļ¬≤ ő©) so it draws negligible current from C_H during hold mode, preventing droop.

### 2.2 Operational Modes ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic

**Mode 1 ‚ÄĒ Sample (Track) Mode:**
- Switch S is **CLOSED** (MOSFET ON).
- C_H is charged through the input buffer to track V_in.
- V_out = V_in (no time delay within bandwidth limits).
- The time constant is ŌĄ = R_on √ó C_H (where R_on is MOSFET on-resistance, typically 10‚Äď500 ő©).

**Mode 2 ‚ÄĒ Hold Mode:**
- Switch S is **OPEN** (MOSFET OFF).
- C_H is **isolated** from the input source.
- The held voltage V_H remains on C_H and is buffered to V_out.
- Only leakage paths (MOSFET off-current I_off, op-amp bias current I_B, capacitor leakage I_L) can discharge it.

**Why this matters in engineering:**
- **ADC Interfacing:** Successive-Approximation ADCs require the input to remain stable during conversion. Hold circuits freeze the analog signal during bit-by-bit comparison.
- **Multiplexed Systems:** When one ADC serves many channels, the hold circuit preserves each channel's value while others are being switched.
- **De-glitching DACs:** Reconstructs smooth analog output from discrete digital samples.

### 2.3 The Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) ‚ÄĒ The Most Important Variant

The **Zero-Order Hold** is the canonical hold circuit in KTU syllabus. It outputs a **piecewise-constant** approximation of the sampled input ‚ÄĒ i.e., the value is held flat between sampling instants, creating a staircase waveform.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Symbol** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Typical Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
