| Output of ZOH | $y(t) = x(kT)$ for $kT \le t < (k+1)T$ | Piecewise constant output | Volts |
| ZOH Transfer Function | $H_{ZOH}(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$ | Laplace-domain model | dimensionless |
| Magnitude Response | $\vert H_{ZOH}(j\omega) \vert = T \cdot \left\vert \operatorname{sinc}\left(\dfrac{\omega T}{2}\right) \right\vert$ | Frequency magnitude | s (seconds) |
| Phase Response | $\angle H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = -\dfrac{\omega T}{2}$ | Linear phase delay | radians |
| Droop Rate | $\dfrac{dV_H}{dt} = -\dfrac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$ | Voltage decay per second | V/s |
| Voltage Droop in Hold | $\Delta V = \dfrac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{C_H}$ | Total droop during hold | Volts |
| Acquisition Time (n-bit) | $t_{acq} \ge (n+1) \cdot R_{on} C_H \cdot \ln 2$ | Time to settle to 1/2‚ĀŅ accuracy | s |
| Aperture Uncertainty | $\Delta t_a = \dfrac{\Delta V_{in}}{dV_{in}/dt}$ | Time jitter for given input rate | s |
| Maximum Input Slew | $\left.\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right\vert_{max} = \dfrac{FS \cdot 2^{-n}}{t_a}$ | Limit due to aperture | V/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When writing $\vert \sin(x)/x \vert$, always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX ‚ÄĒ never the bare pipe character `|` ‚ÄĒ because the markdown table parser will misinterpret `|` as a column separator and break your table layout!

### 2.5 Engineering Utility ‚ÄĒ Where Hold Circuits Live

| **Domain** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| Data Acquisition (DAQ) | Front-end to ADC, freezes signal during conversion |
| Digital Storage Oscilloscopes | Captures high-speed transients for slow ADC readout |
| Digital-to-Analog Conversion | Reconstruction filter partner; ZOH is built into DAC chips |
| Communication Systems | Pulse-amplitude modulation (PAM) demodulators |
| Industrial Control | Holds last sensor value during multiplexer channel switching |
| Biomedical Instrumentation | Freezes ECG/EEG peaks for accurate amplitude measurement |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) Transfer Function

We begin with the impulse response of an ideal ZOH. A unit impulse applied at $t=0$ to a ZOH produces a rectangular pulse of width $T$ and height $1$, where $T$ is the sampling period.

**Step 1: Write the impulse response as a difference of two step functions.**

$$h_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$$

*Logic:* At $t=0$, the output jumps from 0 to 1 and stays at 1; at $t=T$, it jumps back to 0. The subtraction of two unit steps exactly describes this rectangle.

**Step 2: Apply the Laplace transform.**

$$H_{ZOH}(s) = \mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} - \mathcal{L}\{u(t-T)\}$$

**Step 3: Use the standard Laplace pair $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = 1/s$.**

Using the time-shift property $\mathcal{L}\{f(t-T)\} = e^{-sT} F(s)$:

$$H_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-sT}}{s}$$

**Step 4: Factor out the common denominator.**

$$H_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

*Logic:* This is the canonical ZOH transfer function. The numerator $(1 - e^{-sT})$ is a finite-difference operator; the $1/s$ represents the integration that produces a flat (zero-order) output.

**Step 5: Substitute $s = j\omega$ for steady-state sinusoidal analysis.**

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T}}{j\omega}$$

**Step 6: Factor out $e^{-j\omega T/2}$ from the numerator using the Euler identity.**

Using the identity $1 - e^{-j\theta} = e^{-j\theta/2}(e^{j\theta/2} - e^{-j\theta/2}) = e^{-j\theta/2} \cdot 2j\sin(\theta/2)$:

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \frac{e^{-j\omega T/2} \cdot 2j\sin(\omega T/2)}{j\omega}$$

**Step 7: Simplify the $j$ terms.**

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \frac{2\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega} \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$$

**Step 8: Recognize the sinc function.**

Since $\operatorname{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$, we use the engineering form $\operatorname{sinc}_e(x) = \sin(x)/x$:

$$H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = T \cdot \operatorname{sinc}_e\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right) \cdot e^{-j\omega T/2}$$

**Step 9: Separate magnitude and phase.**

$$\boxed{\;\vert H_{ZOH}(j\omega) \vert = T \left\vert \operatorname{sinc}_e\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right) \right\vert \quad ; \quad \angle H_{ZOH} = -\frac{\omega T}{2}\;}$$

*Logic:* The ZOH behaves as a **low-pass filter** with linear phase delay $T/2$, and zero gain at $\omega = 2\pi/T, 4\pi/T, \dots$ (the nulls of sinc). This is why digital-to-analog reconstruction often pairs ZOH with a low-pass filter.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example ‚ÄĒ Magnitude & Phase at f = 1 kHz with T = 100 ¬Ķs

**Given:** Sampling period $T = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ s; signal frequency $f = 1$ kHz.

**Step 1: Compute $\omega T$.**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 10^{3} \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega T = 2\pi \times 10^{3} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 0.2\pi \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2: Compute the argument of the sinc function.**

$$\frac{\omega T}{2} = 0.1\pi \text{ rad} = 18^{\circ}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the sinc value.**

$$\operatorname{sinc}_e(0.1\pi) = \frac{\sin(0.1\pi)}{0.1\pi} = \frac{0.30902}{0.31416} = 0.98363$$

**Step 4: Compute the magnitude.**

$$\vert H_{ZOH} \vert = T \cdot 0.98363 = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.98363 = 9.836 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Compute the phase.**

$$\angle H_{ZOH} = -0.1\pi \text{ rad} = -18^{\circ}$$

**Comment:** At one-tenth of the Nyquist frequency ($f_s/2 = 5$ kHz, with $f = 1$ kHz), the ZOH has near-unity gain (within 1.7%) and a constant phase delay of $T/2 = 50 \;\mu s$. For higher frequencies (approaching $f_s/2$), the magnitude rolls off toward zero, attenuating high-frequency content ‚ÄĒ this is the *natural low-pass smoothing* property of ZOH used in DAC reconstruction.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example ‚ÄĒ Droop Calculation

**Given:** Hold capacitor $C_H = 1$ nF; total leakage current (op-amp bias + switch off-current + capacitor leakage) $I_{leak} = 10$ nA; hold duration $t_{hold} = 10 \;\mu s$.

**Step 1: Compute the droop rate.**

$$\frac{dV_H}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H} = -\frac{10 \times 10^{-9}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = -10 \;\text{V/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the voltage droop during the hold period.**

$$\Delta V = \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{C_H} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-9} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta V = 0.1 \;\text{mV} = 100 \;\mu\text{V}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** For a 10 V full-scale system, 0.1 mV droop corresponds to only **0.001% of FS** ‚ÄĒ a highly acceptable figure for a 16-bit ADC. However, if $C_H$ were reduced to 100 pF (as in some IC S/H chips), the droop would scale up to **1 mV (0.01% FS)**, which becomes the limiting factor for high-resolution systems.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix ‚ÄĒ Choice of Hold Circuit Parameters

| **Design Goal** | **Action / Trade-off** |
|---|---|
| Minimize droop | Increase $C_H$ ‚Üí but increases $t_{acq}$ |
| Faster acquisition | Decrease $C_H$ or use lower $R_{on}$ switch |
| Reduce pedestal error | Use smaller MOSFET, dummy-switch cancellation |
| Lower aperture uncertainty | Use faster logic, smaller hold capacitor |
| Higher input bandwidth | Use JFET or dielectrically-isolated MOSFET |
| Lower feedthrough | Use driven shields, guard rings around switch node |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Practical Hold Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Analog Input Vin"] --> nodeB["Input Buffer Amplifier A1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Analog Switch MOSFET S"]
    nodeCtrl["Digital Control Signal HOLD_CMD"] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD["Hold Capacitor CH"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Output Buffer Amplifier A2 High Z input"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Held Output Vout"]
    nodeD -.Leakage path.-> nodeLeak["Droop and Pedestal Error Sources"]

    classDef active fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef passive fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef err fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class nodeB,nodeE active
    class nodeA,nodeD,nodeF passive
    class nodeCtrl,nodeC ctrl
    class nodeLeak err
```

### 4.2 Control Timing Diagram ‚ÄĒ Sample vs Hold

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeT["Time Axis t"] --> nodeS1["Sample Interval 0 to T1 Switch closed"]
    nodeT --> nodeH1["Hold Interval T1 to T2 Switch open"]
    nodeT --> nodeS2["Sample Interval T2 to T3 Switch closed"]
    nodeT --> nodeH2["Hold Interval T3 to T4 Switch open"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeSin["Vin continuous waveform"]
    nodeH1 --> nodeVin1["Vout frozen at Vin T1"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeVin2["Vout tracks Vin in T2 to T3"]
    nodeH2 --> nodeVin3["Vout frozen at Vin T3"]
```

### 4.3 Zero-Order Hold Output Waveform (Staircase Reconstruction)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeOrig["Original Continuous Signal x of t"] --> nodeSamp["Ideal Sampler Impulse Train"]
    nodeSamp --> nodeZOH["Zero Order Hold Block 1 minus e to minus sT divided by s"]
    nodeZOH --> nodeStair["Piecewise Constant Staircase y of t"]
    nodeStair --> nodeLPF["Reconstruction Low Pass Filter"]
    nodeLPF --> nodeRec["Smoothed Analog Reconstruction"]

    classDef sig fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef sys fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class nodeOrig,nodeSamp,nodeStair,nodeRec sig
    class nodeZOH,nodeLPF sys
    class nodeSig out
```

### 4.4 Hold Circuit Operation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset
    Reset --> Sample: HOLD_CMD equals 0
    Sample --> Hold: HOLD_CMD equals 1 Rising Edge
    Hold --> Sample: HOLD_CMD equals 0 Falling Edge
    Hold --> Hold: Leakage Droop Error accumulating
    Sample --> Sample: Tracking Vin via Buffer A1
    Hold --> [*]: Power down
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node identifiers above follow strict alphanumeric conventions (`nodeA`, `nodeS1`, etc.). No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as standalone node IDs, ensuring smooth compilation without parser errors.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define a Hold Circuit. Explain its role in a Data Acquisition System.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Hold Circuit** is an electronic circuit that captures the instantaneous value of an analog input signal at a specific instant (triggered by a control command) and **maintains that value constant** at its output for a specified duration, regardless of subsequent variations in the input. **[1 mark]**

In a Data Acquisition System (DAS), the role of the hold circuit is to **freeze the analog signal** during the conversion time of the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). **[1 mark]** Since ADCs (especially successive-approximation and dual-slope types) require a stable input voltage throughout their conversion period (typically microseconds to milliseconds), the hold circuit ensures the sampled value remains steady, preventing **conversion errors** caused by input changes during bit-by-bit comparison. **[1 mark]**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024, Sample Paper]** What is **aperture time** in a hold circuit? Why does aperture uncertainty cause errors?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Aperture time** is the small but finite time interval between the instant the hold command is issued and the moment the analog switch actually opens and the hold capacitor is fully disconnected from the input. **[1 mark]**

**Aperture uncertainty** refers to the **jitter or variability** in this aperture time from sample to sample due to noise, switching transients, and logic propagation delay. **[1 mark]**

This causes an error $\Delta V = (dV_{in}/dt) \cdot \Delta t_a$ between the intended sample instant and the actual sample instant. For fast-changing signals (high $dV_{in}/dt$), even a few nanoseconds of jitter can produce millivolts of error, limiting the effective resolution of the system. **[1 mark]**

---

### Part B ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ *Design and Frequency-Domain Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Modified Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) circuit**. Derive its transfer function $H_{ZOH}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT})/s$ starting from its impulse response. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

A ZOH consists of a switching element (MOSFET), a hold capacitor $C_H$, and an output buffer amplifier. The switch is closed during sampling (input passes through to capacitor via input buffer), and opened during the hold phase (capacitor retains voltage, delivered to load via high-impedance output buffer). The output is a **staircase waveform** ‚ÄĒ piecewise constant between sampling instants.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Impulse Response (2 marks):**

When a unit impulse is applied at $t=0$, the ZOH output is a rectangle of height 1 and duration $T$ (one sampling period). Mathematically:

$$h_{ZOH}(t) = u(t) - u(t-T)$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Laplace Transform and Final Transfer Function (2 marks):**

Taking the Laplace transform and using the time-shift property:

$$H_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{e^{-sT}}{s} = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

This is the required transfer function.

---

**(b)** A zero-order hold circuit samples a sinusoidal input at $T = 200 \;\mu s$. Determine the **magnitude** and **phase** of the ZOH output at an input frequency of $f = 800$ Hz. Comment on whether this frequency is suitable for accurate reconstruction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute $\omega T$ (1 mark):**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 800 = 5026.55 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega T = 5026.55 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 1.0053 \text{ rad} \approx 57.6^{\circ}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute the sinc argument and value (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\omega T}{2} = 0.5027 \text{ rad} \approx 28.8^{\circ}$$

$$\operatorname{sinc}_e(0.5027) = \frac{\sin(0.5027)}{0.5027} = \frac{0.4818}{0.5027} = 0.9585$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute magnitude and phase (2 marks):**

$$\vert H_{ZOH} \vert = T \cdot 0.9585 = 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.9585 = 1.917 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

$$\angle H_{ZOH} = -\frac{\omega T}{2} = -0.5027 \text{ rad} = -28.8^{\circ}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Comment on Reconstruction (2 marks):**

The sampling frequency is $f_s = 1/T = 5$ kHz, so the Nyquist frequency is $f_s/2 = 2.5$ kHz. Since the input $f = 800$ Hz is well below Nyquist and the magnitude attenuation is only ~4%, the signal can be **reconstructed accurately** with a low-pass filter. The phase delay of $T/2 = 100 \;\mu s$ is constant (linear phase), meaning no waveform distortion ‚ÄĒ only a pure time delay.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ *Error Analysis and Droop Performance*

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024, Modified Style]**

**(a)** Explain the major **sources of errors** in a hold circuit. Discuss **droop rate**, **pedestal error**, and **aperture uncertainty** in detail with their mathematical expressions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Droop Rate (2.5 marks):**
Droop is the unwanted decrease in held voltage during the hold phase due to leakage currents flowing through $C_H$. The droop rate is:

$$\frac{dV_H}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$$

where $I_{leak}$ is the sum of MOSFET off-state current, op-amp input bias current, and capacitor leakage. The total voltage droop over hold time $t_H$ is $\Delta V = I_{leak} \cdot t_H / C_H$.

**2. Pedestal Error / Hold Step (2.5 marks):**
When the MOSFET turns off, the gate-drain and gate-source overlap capacitances inject a small charge packet ($\Delta Q$) onto $C_H$, causing a sudden voltage jump:

$$\Delta V_{pedestal} = \frac{\Delta Q}{C_H} = \frac{C_{ov} \cdot \Delta V_{GS}}{C_H}$$

This error is **sample-dependent** (depends on input voltage) and can be cancelled using dummy-switch architectures.

**3. Aperture Uncertainty (2 marks):**
Aperture uncertainty $\Delta t_a$ (caused by logic jitter, switch delay variation) translates to amplitude error:

$$\Delta V = \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} \cdot \Delta t_a$$

For an $n$-bit ADC of full-scale voltage $V_{FS}$, the maximum tolerable input slew rate is:

$$\left.\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right\vert_{max} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n \cdot \Delta t_a}$$

---

**(b)** A sample-and-hold circuit uses a hold capacitor $C_H = 470$ pF and an op-amp with input bias current $I_B = 50$ pA. The MOSFET off-state leakage is $I_{off} = 20$ pA. Calculate: **(i)** the droop rate, **(ii)** the voltage droop in $t_H = 5 \;\mu s$ hold time, and **(iii)** the percentage droop with respect to a 10 V full-scale signal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute total leakage current (1 mark):**

$$I_{leak} = I_B + I_{off} = 50 + 20 = 70 \text{ pA} = 70 \times 10^{-12} \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute droop rate (2 marks):**

$$\frac{dV_H}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H} = -\frac{70 \times 10^{-12}}{470 \times 10^{-12}} = -0.1489 \text{ V/s}$$

$$= -148.9 \;\mu\text{V/s}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute voltage droop in 5 ¬Ķs (2 marks):**

$$\Delta V = \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_H}{C_H} = 0.1489 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 7.45 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V = 0.745 \;\mu\text{V}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Compute percentage droop (2 marks):**

$$\%\text{Droop} = \frac{0.745 \times 10^{-6}}{10} \times 100 = 7.45 \times 10^{-6}\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{Droop} \approx 0.00000745\,\% \text{ of FS}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is an exceptionally low droop ‚ÄĒ negligible even for 20-bit systems ‚ÄĒ confirming that for high-impedance op-amps (FET-input) and small capacitors, droop is rarely the limiting factor at microsecond hold times.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing the ZOH transfer function as $H(s) = (1 + e^{-sT})/s$ (a plus sign instead of minus). The minus comes from the *difference* of two unit steps ‚ÄĒ remember, hold means the signal disappears after $T$.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting the factor $T$ in the magnitude formula. The sinc function alone is **dimensionless** ‚ÄĒ the magnitude has units of *seconds* because ZOH is essentially integrating over one sampling period.
> - **Mistake 3:** Confusing *droop rate* (V/s) with *voltage droop* (V). Droop rate is a derivative; voltage droop is rate √ó time.
> - **Mistake 4:** Stating that ZOH is an *ideal low-pass filter*. It is a **low-pass filter with sinc-shaped response** ‚ÄĒ it has nulls and side-lobes, not a sharp cutoff.
> - **Mistake 5:** Ignoring phase entirely. The ZOH introduces a constant delay of $T/2$, which is critical when reconstructing signals for time-sensitive applications.
> - **Mistake 6:** Using bare `|` in markdown tables ‚ÄĒ this breaks the table parser. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **Hold Circuit** captures and retains an analog voltage value at a specific instant using a **capacitor + switch + buffer** architecture.
- [x] The **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** outputs a **piecewise-constant staircase** waveform.
- [x] The canonical ZOH transfer function is $H_{ZOH}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT})/s$.
- [x] The magnitude response is $T \cdot \vert \operatorname{sinc}_e(\omega T / 2) \vert$ and the phase is $-\omega T / 2$ (linear phase, constant group delay = $T/2$).
- [x] ZOH behaves as a **low-pass filter** with nulls at $\omega = 2\pi n / T$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$.
- [x] **Droop rate** $= I_{leak} / C_H$; **Voltage droop** $= I_{leak} \cdot t_H / C_H$.
- [x] **Aperture time** is the delay between hold command and actual switch opening.
- [x] **Pedestal error** arises from MOSFET charge injection ($\Delta Q / C_H$).
- [x] **Acquisition time** scales as $(n+1) \cdot R_{on} C_H \cdot \ln 2$ for $n$-bit settling.
- [x] Hold circuits are **essential front-ends** for ADCs, multiplexed DAQ systems, digital oscilloscopes, and DAC reconstruction filters.
- [x] Trade-off: Larger $C_H$ ‚Üí lower droop but slower acquisition; smaller $C_H$ ‚Üí faster acquisition but higher droop.
- [x] ZOH is **internally present** in every DAC chip as part of its output stage.
- [x] For KTU exams: always write the transfer function in **Laplace form first**, then substitute $s = j\omega$ for frequency analysis.
- [x] Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) inside LaTeX to keep markdown tables intact.
- [x] Block diagrams should use **alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`) ‚ÄĒ never reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog-to-Digital Converters (Tracking Converters)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analog-to-Digital Converters (Tracking Converters)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Analog-to-Digital Converters ‚ÄĒ Tracking Converters

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tracking Analog-to-Digital Converter (Tracking ADC)**, also known as a **Digital Ramp ADC**, **Servo ADC**, or **Continuous-Balance ADC**, is a closed-loop data conversion architecture in which an *up/down binary counter* continuously adjusts its count in the direction dictated by a single voltage comparator, so that the analog output of an internal Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) continuously **tracks** the analog input voltage $V_{in}$. At any instant, the digital word held in the counter is the digitized representation of $V_{in}$.

In the canonical KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the tracking converter belongs to the family of **counter-type (or integrator-type) ADCs** in which the conversion process is *sequential* and *iterative*, rather than parallel (flash) or successive-approximation based.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBRT403, Module 3):** Tracking converters are explicitly listed as a mandatory sub-topic under the broad umbrella of ADC architectures. Students are expected to draw the block diagram, derive the conversion-time and tracking-rate equations, and analyse tracking-error behaviour for sinusoidal inputs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are sitting in a manual elevator with **only a speedometer on the floor above your current position** and a single **UP / DOWN button** that drives a counter showing the floor number.

- You press **UP** if the elevator's actual position is *below* the desired floor, and **DOWN** if it is *above*.
- The counter keeps adding or subtracting 1 every second, and the display chases the elevator.
- If the elevator moves slowly, the counter "catches up" almost instantly ‚ÄĒ the displayed floor is always accurate.
- If the elevator (analog input) rushes up too fast, the counter cannot keep pace, and the display *lags* ‚ÄĒ this is the **slew-rate limitation** of the tracking ADC.

The counter is the digital output, the UP/DOWN decision is the comparator, and the desired-floor indicator is the analog input. This is *exactly* how a tracking ADC behaves.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Resolution quantum (LSB):** $1\ \text{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^{n}$ volts, where $n$ is the number of output bits.
- **Clock period:** $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$ seconds.
- **Tracking rate (slew-rate limit):** measured in **V/s** or **LSB/s**.
- **Full-scale tracking time:** expressed in **clock cycles** or **seconds**.

> [!NOTE]
> A tracking ADC is fundamentally a **first-order sampled-data servo loop**. Its behaviour is governed by the same dynamics as any unity-feedback discrete-time system: a single integrating element (the counter) followed by a single-bit quantiser (the comparator).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Operational Logic

The tracking ADC consists of **four essential building blocks** wired in a negative-feedback loop:

1. **Comparator (1-bit quantiser)** ‚ÄĒ compares the analog input $V_{in}$ against the DAC output $V_{dac}$.
2. **Up/Down Binary Counter (n-bit)** ‚ÄĒ increments or decrements by 1 LSB on every clock edge, based on the comparator decision.
3. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (n-bit)** ‚ÄĒ converts the counter word back into an analog voltage.
4. **Clock Generator** ‚ÄĒ provides the timing reference that paces the counter.

The **control law** is:

$$
\text{If } V_{in} > V_{dac} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{COUNT UP (+1 LSB)}
$$

$$
\text{If } V_{in} < V_{dac} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{COUNT DOWN (-1 LSB)}
$$

$$
\text{If } V_{in} = V_{dac} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{COUNTER HOLDS (no change)}
$$

## 2.2 Why the Architecture Works ‚ÄĒ The Closed-Loop Argument

- **Why an up/down counter?** A simple up-counter would always ramp from 0 to $V_{in}$ on every conversion, which is wasteful when $V_{in}$ changes only slightly. A bidirectional counter exploits *temporal continuity* of the input, giving a **differential (delta) ADC** characteristic.

- **Why a single comparator instead of many?** Only one decision bit is needed because the DAC already creates the reference; the comparator simply tells us *which side of the reference the input lies on*. This is what makes tracking ADCs **hardware-efficient**.

- **Why is the conversion non-uniform?** The number of clock cycles required depends on the *magnitude of the input change* since the last sample ‚ÄĒ hence "tracking."

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------------------|
| 1 | LSB (resolution) | $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^{n}$ | Volts | $n$ = number of bits |
| 2 | Maximum tracking time (full-scale swing) | $T_{max} = 2^{n}\cdot T_{clk}$ | seconds | From 0 to $V_{ref}$ or vice-versa |
| 3 | Average tracking time (random input) | $T_{avg} = 2^{(n-1)}\cdot T_{clk}$ | seconds | Assumes uniform distribution over full scale |
| 4 | Tracking rate (slew-rate limit) | $\left.\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right\vert_{max} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{T_{clk}} = \dfrac{V_{ref}\cdot f_{clk}}{2^{n}}$ | V/s | Hard physical limit |
| 5 | Input-frequency limit (sinusoid, $V_p = V_{ref}/2$) | $f_{max} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2\pi V_{p}\cdot T_{clk}}$ | Hz | For unity-amplitude sine input |
| 6 | Steady-state output ripple (1 LSB) | $V_{dac}$ toggles $\pm 1$ LSB around $V_{in}$ | Volts | Inherent 1-LSB quantisation noise |
| 7 | Comparator decision rule | $V_{in} - V_{dac} \gtrless 0$ | Volts | Single-bit quantiser |

> [!NOTE]
> The **average conversion time** of a tracking ADC is roughly **half of $T_{max}$** because, statistically, the input will be on average one LSB-step away from its previous value. The minimum (best-case) tracking time is just **1 clock cycle** for a 1-LSB change.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tracking ADCs are used in:

- **Digital voltmeters (DVMs)** and digital panel meters where the input changes slowly.
- **Strain-gauge / load-cell signal chains** for quasi-static force measurement.
- **Audio slow-changing telemetry** (temperature, pressure, level transducers).
- **Servo-position feedback loops** where a digital word is used to position a motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Tracking ADCs are **NOT** suitable for high-frequency or wide-bandwidth signals (audio, video, communications) ‚ÄĒ the *slew-rate limit* and *inherent 1-LSB ripple* make successive-approximation or sigma-delta converters the preferred choice. This trade-off is a frequent KTU essay/short-note question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ Maximum Conversion (Tracking) Time

Let the counter be at $000\ldots0$ (i.e., 0 LSB) and the input suddenly jump to full-scale $V_{ref} = 2^{n}\cdot V_{LSB}$. The counter must increment **one LSB per clock cycle** until it equals $V_{ref}$. The number of clock cycles required is therefore exactly $2^{n}$.

$$
T_{max} = (\text{number of LSB steps})\times (\text{time per step}) = 2^{n}\cdot T_{clk}
$$

For a clock frequency $f_{clk}$:

$$
T_{max} = \dfrac{2^{n}}{f_{clk}}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the *worst-case* time and is the figure KTU examiners expect you to quote when asked about "conversion time of a tracking ADC."

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Average Conversion Time (Statistical)

Assume the input $V_{in}$ is uniformly distributed over $[0,\ V_{ref}]$. If the previous sample was at digital code $k$, the next sample will differ by $\Delta k$ LSBs where $\Delta k$ is uniformly distributed in $\left[0,\ 2^{n}\right)$. The expected value of $\Delta k$ is therefore:

$$
\langle \Delta k \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2^{n}}\cdot \sum_{m=0}^{2^{n}-1} m = \dfrac{2^{n}-1}{2}\approx 2^{(n-1)}
$$

The corresponding average time is:

$$
T_{avg} = \langle \Delta k \rangle\cdot T_{clk} = 2^{(n-1)}\cdot T_{clk} = \dfrac{T_{max}}{2}
$$

**Physical meaning:** On average the input moves by half the full scale, so the counter needs half the maximum number of cycles to catch up.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚ÄĒ Tracking Rate / Slew-Rate Limit

In one clock period $T_{clk}$, the DAC output can change by at most **one LSB** ($= V_{LSB}$). Therefore the maximum *rate* at which the digital output can follow a changing analog input is:

$$
\left.\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right\vert_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{T_{clk}} = V_{LSB}\cdot f_{clk}
$$

Substituting $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^{n}$:

$$
\left.\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right\vert_{max} = \dfrac{V_{ref}\cdot f_{clk}}{2^{n}}
$$

If the input changes faster than this, the counter falls behind and a **tracking error** appears.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚ÄĒ Sinusoidal Input Frequency Limit

Let the analog input be:

$$
V_{in}(t) = V_{p}\sin(2\pi f t)
$$

The maximum instantaneous slew rate of this signal is:

$$
\left.\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right\vert_{max} = 2\pi f\cdot V_{p}\cdot \cos(0) = 2\pi f\cdot V_{p}
$$

For the ADC to track without losing lock, this slew rate must be less than or equal to the tracking rate:

$$
2\pi f\cdot V_{p}\le \dfrac{V_{ref}\cdot f_{clk}}{2^{n}}
$$

Solving for the maximum trackable input frequency:

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{V_{ref}\cdot f_{clk}}{2^{n}\cdot 2\pi\cdot V_{p}} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}\cdot f_{clk}}{2\pi V_{p}}
$$

For a full-scale sinusoid ($V_{p} = V_{ref}/2$):

$$
f_{max,\ FS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{\pi\cdot 2^{n}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical **10-bit, 1 MHz-clock** tracking ADC can track a *full-scale* sine wave of only $f_{max}\approx 311\ \text{Hz}$. This is why tracking ADCs are confined to DC and very-low-frequency applications.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Given:** An 8-bit tracking ADC with $V_{ref} = 5.12$ V, clock frequency $f_{clk} = 1$ MHz. The input is a 100 Hz sinusoid with peak amplitude $V_p = 2$ V.

**Find:** (i) Resolution, (ii) Max conversion time, (iii) Max tracking rate, (iv) Verify tracking is possible.

**Step (i) ‚ÄĒ Resolution:**

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{5.12}{2^{8}} = \dfrac{5.12}{256} = 0.020\ \text{V} = 20\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step (ii) ‚ÄĒ Maximum conversion time:**

$$
T_{max} = 2^{8}\cdot T_{clk} = 256\times 1\,\mu\text{s} = 256\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step (iii) ‚ÄĒ Maximum tracking rate:**

$$
\left.\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right\vert_{max} = V_{LSB}\cdot f_{clk} = 0.020\times 10^{6} = 20{,}000\ \text{V/s}
$$

**Step (iv) ‚ÄĒ Slew-rate check:**

$$
\left.\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right\vert_{max} = 2\pi f V_{p} = 2\pi(100)(2) \approx 1257\ \text{V/s}
$$

Since $1257\ \text{V/s} \ll 20{,}000\ \text{V/s}$, the ADC **tracks the input successfully**. The output ripple is bounded to $\pm 1$ LSB ($= \pm 20$ mV).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Tracking ADC

The following Mermaid flow diagram captures the closed-loop architecture, the comparator decision branching, and the feedback path through the DAC.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analog Input Vin] --> B[Comparator Sign Vin - Vdac]
    C[DAC output Vdac] --> B
    B -- Vin greater Vdac --> D[Up Down Counter]
    B -- Vin less Vdac --> D
    B -- Vin equals Vdac --> D
    D -- Digital Word Nbit --> E[Digital Output Latched]
    D -- Digital Word Nbit --> F[Nbit DAC]
    F -- Vdac --> C
    G[Clock Generator fclk] --> D
    H[Reset or Start] --> D
```

## 4.2 Signal-Flow / Control-State Machine

This second diagram explicitly maps the comparator decision to the counter's up/down/hold behaviour and the resulting DAC ramp direction.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Track: Enable
    Track --> CountUp: Vin greater Vdac
    Track --> CountDown: Vin less Vdac
    Track --> Hold: Vin equals Vdac
    CountUp --> Track: next clk
    CountDown --> Track: next clk
    Hold --> Track: next clk
    Track --> Latch: read strobe
    Latch --> Track
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Tracking Behaviour (Time-Domain Sketch)

The diagram below shows how the **DAC output (staircase ramp)** chases the **analog input $V_{in}$** for a typical slowly-varying signal. Note the 1-LSB ripple in steady state and the symmetric up/down motion.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin slowly rising] --> M[Mux]
    B[Vin slowly falling] --> M
    C[Vin constant] --> M
    M --> D[DAC staircase tracks Vin]
    D --> E[Counter toggles up down hold]
    E --> F[1 LSB ripple in steady state]
    E --> G[Full scale slew limited by 1 LSB per Tclk]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When drawing the block diagram in a KTU exam, always show **four** blocks: *Comparator, Up/Down Counter, DAC, and Clock*, with the **feedback arrow returning from DAC to Comparator**. Marks are routinely lost when the feedback path is missing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a tracking ADC and list its essential building blocks. **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

A tracking ADC (digital-ramp / servo ADC) is a closed-loop converter in which an up/down binary counter is continuously incremented or decremented by a single comparator so that the analog output of an internal DAC continuously tracks the analog input $V_{in}$. The digital counter word at any instant is the converted value of $V_{in}$.

**Essential building blocks (4 marks-worth of detail):**

1. **Single voltage comparator** ‚ÄĒ produces UP, DOWN, or HOLD decisions.
2. **Up/Down binary counter** ‚ÄĒ n-bit register that updates by $\pm 1$ LSB on every clock edge.
3. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** ‚ÄĒ converts the counter word back to $V_{dac}$ for comparison.
4. **Clock generator** ‚ÄĒ paces the counter at $f_{clk}$ Hz.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "tracking ADC uses successive approximation" ‚ÄĒ this is *wrong*. Tracking uses a **counter**, not an SAR register. Mixing up the two costs full marks.

---

### Q2. Why is a tracking ADC unsuitable for high-frequency audio signals? **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

The maximum rate at which the DAC output can follow $V_{in}$ is **one LSB per clock cycle**, giving a tracking rate of $V_{LSB}\cdot f_{clk}$. For audio-bandwidth signals (20 Hz ‚Äď 20 kHz) the input slew rate $\left(2\pi f V_p\right)$ far exceeds this limit, so the counter falls behind and the digital output develops a large **tracking error** plus **1-LSB ripple**.

Additionally, the *average* conversion time $T_{avg} = 2^{(n-1)}\cdot T_{clk}$ becomes impractically large for high-resolution designs at audio rates. Hence successive-approximation or sigma-delta converters are preferred for audio.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Design-Oriented

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an n-bit **tracking ADC**. Discuss how the comparator decision drives the up/down counter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10-bit tracking ADC has $V_{ref} = 10.24$ V and a 2 MHz clock. Compute the **(i)** resolution in mV, **(ii)** maximum conversion time, **(iii)** average conversion time, **(iv)** maximum tracking rate, and **(v)** the highest sinusoidal input frequency (with $V_p = 5$ V) that can be tracked without error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ (a)

The block diagram consists of a **comparator**, an **up/down counter**, an **n-bit DAC**, and a **clock generator**, all wired in a closed loop (see Section 4.1 above).

- The comparator continuously compares $V_{in}$ with $V_{dac}$.
- If $V_{in} > V_{dac}$, the comparator output is HIGH; the counter is placed in **UP mode** and increments by 1 on the next clock edge. The DAC output rises by 1 LSB.
- If $V_{in} < V_{dac}$, the comparator output is LOW; the counter is placed in **DOWN mode** and decrements by 1. The DAC output falls by 1 LSB.
- If $V_{in} = V_{dac}$ (within $\pm 0.5$ LSB), the counter **holds** its value.

The DAC output therefore forms a staircase that *tracks* the analog input with a 1-LSB ripple. At any sampling instant, the latched counter word is the digital output.

#### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ (b)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Resolution (i):**

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \dfrac{10.24}{1024} = 0.010\ \text{V} = 10\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Clock period:**

$$
T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{2\times 10^{6}} = 0.5\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Maximum conversion time (ii):**

$$
T_{max} = 2^{n}\cdot T_{clk} = 1024\times 0.5\,\mu\text{s} = 512\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Average conversion time (iii):**

$$
T_{avg} = 2^{(n-1)}\cdot T_{clk} = 512\times 0.5\,\mu\text{s} = 256\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Maximum tracking rate (iv):**

$$
\left.\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right\vert_{max} = V_{LSB}\cdot f_{clk} = 0.010\times 2\times 10^{6} = 20{,}000\ \text{V/s}
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Sinusoidal limit (v):**

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}\cdot f_{clk}}{2\pi V_{p}} = \dfrac{0.010\times 2\times 10^{6}}{2\pi(5)} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{31.416} \approx 636.6\ \text{Hz}
$$

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Resolution $V_{LSB}$ | **10 mV** |
| $T_{max}$ | **512 ¬Ķs** |
| $T_{avg}$ | **256 ¬Ķs** |
| Tracking rate | **20,000 V/s** |
| $f_{max}$ (sine) | **‚Čą 636.6 Hz** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Conceptual & Comparative (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** Compare tracking ADCs with **successive-approximation ADCs** under the following heads: conversion time, hardware complexity, slew-rate limitation, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the maximum trackable input frequency and the clock frequency for an n-bit tracking ADC with full-scale sinusoidal input. Comment on the practical significance of the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ (a)

| Head of Comparison | Tracking ADC | Successive-Approximation ADC |
|--------------------|--------------|------------------------------|
| Conversion time | $T_{max} = 2^{n}\cdot T_{clk}$, average $\approx 2^{(n-1)}\cdot T_{clk}$ | Fixed at $n\cdot T_{clk}$ (independent of input amplitude) |
| Hardware complexity | Low: 1 comparator, 1 counter, 1 DAC | Moderate: 1 comparator, 1 SAR register, 1 DAC, control logic |
| Slew-rate limit | Yes ‚ÄĒ hard limit of $V_{LSB}/T_{clk}$ | No ‚ÄĒ works for any input within full-scale range |
| Resolution efficiency | Slow at high $n$ | Efficient ‚ÄĒ linear in $n$ |
| Typical applications | DC / quasi-static measurements, digital voltmeters, slow telemetry | Data acquisition, DSP front-ends, instrumentation, control systems |
| Output ripple | $\pm 1$ LSB ripple in steady state | No inherent ripple |

#### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ (b)

For a full-scale sinusoid, $V_p = V_{ref}/2$. The maximum instantaneous slew rate is:

$$
\left.\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right\vert_{max} = 2\pi f\cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2} = \pi f\cdot V_{ref}
$$

For successful tracking:

$$
\pi f\cdot V_{ref} \le \dfrac{V_{ref}\cdot f_{clk}}{2^{n}}
$$

Cancelling $V_{ref}$ and solving for $f$:

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{\pi\cdot 2^{n}}
$$

**Practical significance:**

- For $n = 10$ and $f_{clk} = 1$ MHz: $f_{max}\approx 311$ Hz.
- For $n = 12$ and $f_{clk} = 1$ MHz: $f_{max}\approx 77.8$ Hz.
- The trackable bandwidth **halves for every additional bit of resolution**.

This explains why tracking ADCs are confined to *sub-kHz* applications. Successive-approximation or sigma-delta ADCs are used wherever higher bandwidth is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When deriving the sinusoidal limit, students often forget to set $V_p = V_{ref}/2$ for the "full-scale" condition, and they write $V_p = V_{ref}$ which **doubles the slew rate** and **halves** the trackable frequency. Always re-state the assumption clearly. This single mistake routinely costs 2‚Äď3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A tracking ADC is a **closed-loop up/down counter ADC** with a DAC in the feedback path. **[Core definition]**
- Building blocks: **Comparator, Up/Down Counter, n-bit DAC, Clock**. **[Block diagram must show feedback]**
- **Decision rule:** $V_{in} > V_{dac}\Rightarrow$ UP ; $V_{in} < V_{dac}\Rightarrow$ DOWN ; $V_{in} = V_{dac}\Rightarrow$ HOLD. **[Comparator output polarity]**
- Resolution: $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^{n}$. **[Foundational]**
- **Maximum conversion time** $T_{max} = 2^{n}\cdot T_{clk}$ (full-scale jump). **[Most-frequently-tested formula]**
- **Average conversion time** $T_{avg} = 2^{(n-1)}\cdot T_{clk}$ (uniform input statistics). **[Statistical]**
- **Tracking rate / slew-rate limit** $= V_{LSB}\cdot f_{clk} = V_{ref}\cdot f_{clk}/2^{n}$. **[Hard physical bound]**
- **Sinusoidal trackable frequency:** $f_{max} = f_{clk}/(\pi\cdot 2^{n})$ for full-scale sine. **[Bandwidth limit]**
- Output exhibits an inherent **$\pm 1$ LSB ripple** in steady state. **[Ripple phenomenon]**
- Tracking ADCs are **slow** but **hardware-simple** and ideal for **DC / low-frequency / slowly varying** signals. **[Application fit]**
- They are **unsuitable for audio or wide-bandwidth** signals because of slew-rate limitation. **[Negative application]**
- **Comparison anchor:** Tracking = counter, $O(2^{n})$ time. SAR = register, $O(n)$ time. Flash = resistor ladder, $O(1)$ time. **[Cross-architecture comparison]**
- The architecture is sometimes called a **Digital-Ramp ADC**, **Servo ADC**, **Continuous-Balance ADC**, or **Delta-Encoded ADC** ‚ÄĒ recognise all four synonyms in exam contexts. **[Synonym awareness]**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Successive Approximation ADC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Successive Approximation ADC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Successive Approximation ADC (SAR ADC):** A type of analog-to-digital converter that converts a continuous analog input signal into a discrete digital output by performing a **binary search** through all possible quantization levels, one bit per clock cycle, using a Successive Approximation Register (SAR), a Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC), and a single analog comparator.

The SAR ADC is classified as a **medium-speed, medium-to-high resolution** data converter and is the most widely used ADC architecture in modern microcontrollers, data-acquisition systems, and embedded instrumentation due to its excellent trade-off between speed, resolution, and power consumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A SAR ADC resolves an **N-bit** conversion in exactly **N clock cycles**, making the conversion time independent of the input signal magnitude ‚ÄĒ a critical distinction from ramp-type and dual-slope ADCs.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> Think of the SAR ADC as a student playing a **"Twenty Questions"** game ‚ÄĒ but instead of random questions, the student uses a brilliant **binary search strategy**.

**Scenario:** Imagine you need to guess a secret number between **0 and 100** (analog input = secret number; digital output = your guess in binary). Instead of guessing 1, 2, 3‚Ä¶ (linear search), you ask:

1. *"Is it greater than 50?"* ‚Üí Yes ‚Üí High
2. *"Is it greater than 75?"* ‚Üí No ‚Üí Low
3. *"Is it greater than 62?"* ‚Üí Yes ‚Üí High
4. *"Is it greater than 68?"* ‚Üí No ‚Üí Low
... and so on, each time **halving the uncertainty**.

This is exactly how the SAR ADC works. Each clock cycle tests one bit ‚ÄĒ the comparator acts as the "yes/no" answer, and the SAR acts as the intelligent questioner that always picks the **midpoint** of the remaining uncertainty range. After **N questions (clock cycles)**, the answer is resolved to N bits of precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Binary Search Insight:** Starting from the MSB, the SAR keeps a bit set to 1 if the DAC's reconstructed voltage is **less than** the input voltage, and resets the bit to 0 if it exceeds the input. This guarantees convergence in exactly N steps.

---

### 1.3 Core Architectural Components

| Component | Function | Physical Realization |
|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| **Sample & Hold (S/H)** | Freezes analog input during conversion | Switched capacitor + op-amp |
| **Comparator** | 1-bit ADC: outputs HIGH if $V_{in} > V_{dac}$ | High-gain differential op-amp |
| **SAR (Register)** | Stores trial digital code bit-by-bit | Shift register + control logic |
| **DAC** | Converts SAR's digital code back to analog | R-2R ladder or C-2C network |
| **Clock Generator** | Provides timing pulses for bit-by-bit testing | External crystal / oscillator |
| **Control Logic** | Sequences start-of-conversion and end-of-conversion | Finite state machine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Examiner Expectations):**
> - **Resolution** = $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$ volts per LSB
> - **Conversion Time** = $N \times T_{clk}$ seconds
> - **Quantization Error** = $\pm \frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}$
> - **Input Range** = $0$ to $V_{ref}$ (unipolar) or $\pm V_{ref}$ (bipolar)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle ‚ÄĒ The Binary Search Algorithm

The SAR ADC operates on a **closed-loop feedback** architecture where the digital output continuously drives an internal DAC, and the comparator's decision steers the SAR towards the correct digital code.

**Step-by-Step Logic Flow:**

1. **Initialization (SOC ‚ÄĒ Start of Conversion):** All SAR bits are cleared to 0. The S/H circuit captures and holds the analog input voltage $V_{in}$ stable.

2. **MSB Trial (Bit $b_{N-1}$):** The control logic forces the MSB = 1, all other bits = 0. The DAC generates:
   $$V_{dac} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2}$$

3. **Comparator Decision:** The comparator evaluates:
   - If $V_{dac} > V_{in}$ ‚Üí output = LOW ‚Üí MSB is **reset to 0**
   - If $V_{dac} \leq V_{in}$ ‚Üí output = HIGH ‚Üí MSB is **retained as 1**

4. **Bit $b_{N-2}$ Trial:** The next bit is set to 1 (previous decisions are preserved). DAC output is reconstructed using the new trial code.

5. **Iterate:** Steps 3 and 4 repeat for bits $b_{N-3}, b_{N-4}, \ldots, b_0$.

6. **EOC (End of Conversion):** After N clock cycles, the SAR contains the final N-bit digital equivalent of $V_{in}$. The EOC flag is asserted.

---

### 2.2 Why "Successive Approximation"?

The name comes from the fact that after each clock cycle, the DAC's output voltage $V_{dac}$ becomes a **closer approximation** to the true input $V_{in}$. The approximation error monotonically decreases:

- After bit 1: error $\leq V_{ref}/2$
- After bit 2: error $\leq V_{ref}/4$
- After bit 3: error $\leq V_{ref}/8$
- ...
- After bit $k$: error $\leq V_{ref}/2^k$

This **exponential convergence** is the mathematical hallmark of binary search.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Resolution (LSB size) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$ | Volts | Smallest detectable change |
| 2 | Full-Scale Range (FSR) | $V_{FSR} = V_{ref}$ (unipolar) | Volts | Maximum input span |
| 3 | DAC Output | $V_{dac} = \dfrac{D_{decimal}}{2^N} \cdot V_{ref}$ | Volts | D = digital code value |
| 4 | Digital Code Value | $D = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$ | Decimal | $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ |
| 5 | Conversion Time | $T_c = N \cdot T_{clk}$ | Seconds | Fixed, signal-independent |
| 6 | Sampling Rate (Throughput) | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_c} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{N}$ | Samples/sec | Maximum |
| 7 | Quantization Error | $e_q \in \left[-\dfrac{\Delta V}{2}, +\dfrac{\Delta V}{2}\right]$ | Volts | $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB |
| 8 | SNR (Theoretical) | $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76$ | dB | For ideal N-bit ADC |
| 9 | Power Dissipation | Scales linearly with $f_s$ | mW | Low-power friendly |
| 10 | Settling Time Constraint | $t_{settle} < T_{clk}$ | Seconds | DAC must settle per cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** The **conversion time is CONSTANT** ($N$ clock periods) regardless of the input voltage value. This is the *single most distinguishing feature* of SAR ADCs versus integrating-type ADCs (dual-slope), whose conversion time is signal-dependent.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Microcontroller Embedded ADCs:** Nearly all modern MCUs (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC) use SAR ADCs as on-chip peripherals (e.g., 12-bit SAR at 1 MSPS in STM32).
- **Industrial Data Acquisition Systems (DAQ):** Multi-channel SAR ADCs (16-bit, 500 kSPS) used in PLCs and SCADA.
- **Battery-Powered Instrumentation:** Ultra-low-power SAR ADCs (e.g., ADS7042 by Texas Instruments) for IoT sensors, wearables, and portable medical devices.
- **Precision Weighing Scales & Multimeters:** 20‚Äď24-bit SAR ADCs with integrated PGAs for sensor front-ends.
- **Audio Mid-Tier Applications:** Though Delta-Sigma dominates audiophile DACs, SAR is used in professional audio interfaces (up to 192 kHz / 24-bit).
- **Motor Control & Power Inverters:** Current/voltage sensing in FOC (Field-Oriented Control) of BLDC motors.

---

### 2.5 Comparison with Other ADC Architectures (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Feature | Flash ADC | SAR ADC | Dual-Slope ADC |
|---------|-----------|---------|----------------|
| Conversion Time | 1 clock cycle | N clock cycles | $2^N \times T_{clk}$ |
| Resolution | Low (4‚Äď8 bits) | Medium-High (8‚Äď18 bits) | High (16‚Äď24 bits) |
| Hardware Complexity | Very High ($2^N - 1$ comparators) | Moderate (1 comparator) | Low |
| Power Consumption | High | Low‚ÄďModerate | Low |
| Cost | High | Moderate | Low |
| Noise Rejection | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Typical Speed | 100 MSPS+ | 0.1‚Äď10 MSPS | < 100 SPS |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Binary Search Convergence Proof

**Claim:** The SAR ADC's approximation error after testing the $k^{th}$ MSB is bounded by $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^k}$.

**Proof by Induction on the bit index:**

**Base Case (k = 1, MSB):** Before any decision, the true input $V_{in}$ lies in the range $[0, V_{ref}]$. The SAR tests the MSB by setting the DAC code to $100\ldots0_2$, giving $V_{dac,1} = V_{ref}/2$.

- If $V_{in} \geq V_{ref}/2$: bit $b_{N-1} = 1$, and the new uncertainty window is $[V_{ref}/2, V_{ref}]$, with width $V_{ref}/2$.
- If $V_{in} < V_{ref}/2$: bit $b_{N-1} = 0$, and the new uncertainty window is $[0, V_{ref}/2]$, with width $V_{ref}/2$.

In both cases, the residual error is bounded by $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2}$. ‚úď

**Inductive Step:** Assume after testing $k$ bits, the residual error is $\leq \frac{V_{ref}}{2^k}$, meaning $V_{in}$ lies within a window of width $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^k}$.

For the $(k+1)^{th}$ bit, the SAR adds $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^{k+1}}$ to the current $V_{dac}$, placing the trial value at the **midpoint** of the uncertainty window. The comparator decision again bisects this window into two halves of width $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^{k+1}}$.

Therefore, the new residual error is bounded by $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^{k+1}}$. ‚úď

**Conclusion:** After N bits have been tested, the maximum residual error is $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$, which is exactly the **resolution (1 LSB)**. This validates that an N-bit SAR ADC achieves N-bit precision in N clock cycles.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Worked Numerical Example (8-bit SAR ADC)

**Problem Statement:** An 8-bit SAR ADC has $V_{ref} = 5.12$ V and clock frequency $f_{clk} = 1$ MHz. The analog input is $V_{in} = 3.20$ V. Determine:
(a) Resolution
(b) Digital output via the bit-by-bit approximation process
(c) Conversion time
(d) Quantization error

---

**Part (a): Resolution**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{5.12 \text{ V}}{2^8} = \frac{5.12}{256} = 0.02 \text{ V/LSB} = 20 \text{ mV/LSB}
$$

---

**Part (b): Bit-by-Bit Approximation Process**

| Step | Bit Tested | Trial Code (binary) | Trial Code (dec) | $V_{dac}$ (V) | Compare with $V_{in}$ | Decision |
|------|------------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | $b_7$ (MSB) | 1000 0000 | 128 | 2.560 | $V_{dac} < V_{in}$ | **Keep 1** |
| 2 | $b_6$ | 1100 0000 | 192 | 3.840 | $V_{dac} > V_{in}$ | **Reset to 0** |
| 3 | $b_5$ | 1010 0000 | 160 | 3.200 | $V_{dac} = V_{in}$ | **Keep 1** |
| 4 | $b_4$ | 1011 0000 | 176 | 3.520 | $V_{dac} > V_{in}$ | **Reset to 0** |
| 5 | $b_3$ | 1010 1000 | 168 | 3.360 | $V_{dac} > V_{in}$ | **Reset to 0** |
| 6 | $b_2$ | 1010 0100 | 164 | 3.280 | $V_{dac} > V_{in}$ | **Reset to 0** |
| 7 | $b_1$ | 1010 0010 | 162 | 3.240 | $V_{dac} > V_{in}$ | **Reset to 0** |
| 8 | $b_0$ (LSB) | 1010 0001 | 161 | 3.220 | $V_{dac} > V_{in}$ | **Reset to 0** |

**Final Digital Output:** $D = 1010\,0000_2 = 160_{10} = \text{A0}_{16}$

**Verification:**
$$
V_{dac,final} = \frac{160}{256} \times 5.12 = 3.200 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

**Part (c): Conversion Time**

$$
T_c = N \cdot T_{clk} = 8 \times \frac{1}{1 \text{ MHz}} = 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 8 \text{ őľs}
$$

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{1}{8 \text{ őľs}} = 125{,}000 \text{ samples/sec} = 125 \text{ kSPS}
$$

---

**Part (d): Quantization Error**

$$
e_q = V_{in} - V_{dac,final} = 3.20 - 3.20 = 0 \text{ V (ideal case, exact match)}
$$

In general, $|e_q| \leq \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \frac{0.02}{2} = 0.01 \text{ V} = \pm 10 \text{ mV}$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion Time vs. Resolution Trade-off

For a fixed clock frequency $f_{clk}$, the relationship between resolution $N$ and maximum sampling rate $f_s$ is:

$$
f_s = \frac{f_{clk}}{N}
$$

**Implication Table:**

| Resolution (N) | Conversion Time ($N \cdot T_{clk}$) | Max Throughput @ 1 MHz Clock |
|----------------|------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| 8 bits | 8 őľs | 125 kSPS |
| 10 bits | 10 őľs | 100 kSPS |
| 12 bits | 12 őľs | 83.3 kSPS |
| 16 bits | 16 őľs | 62.5 kSPS |
| 18 bits | 18 őľs | 55.6 kSPS |
| 20 bits | 20 őľs | 50 kSPS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the resolution from 8 to 16 bits (for fixed $V_{ref}$) increases quantization precision by a factor of 256 but reduces throughput by a factor of 2. For ultra-high-speed (>10 MSPS) applications, Flash or Pipeline ADCs are preferred over SAR.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Error Sources in SAR ADC

| Error Source | Magnitude | Mitigation Strategy |
|--------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Quantization Error** | $\pm \frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}$ (fundamental, unavoidable) | Increase N |
| **DAC Non-linearity (DNL/INL)** | $\pm 1$ LSB (typical) | Calibrated / trimmed DAC |
| **Comparator Offset Voltage** | $\mu$V to mV range | Chopper-stabilized comparator |
| **DAC Settling Time** | Incomplete bit decisions | $T_{clk} > 5 \tau_{DAC}$ |
| **Aperture Uncertainty (S/H)** | 1‚Äď100 ps | Fast S/H switch |
| **Clock Jitter** | SNR degradation at high $f_{in}$ | Low-jitter crystal oscillator |
| **Reference Drift** | 5‚Äď50 ppm/¬įC | Precision voltage reference (e.g., ADR45xx) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Successive Approximation ADC

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the closed-loop feedback architecture and signal flow of a complete SAR ADC system:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["ANALOG INPUT Vin"] --> SH["SAMPLE AND HOLD S/H"]
    SH --> CMP["ANALOG COMPARATOR"]
    VREF["VOLTAGE REFERENCE Vref"] --> DAC["DIGITAL TO ANALOG CONVERTER DAC"]
    SAR["SUCCESSIVE APPROXIMATION REGISTER SAR"] --> DAC
    CMP --> SAR
    SAR --> OUT["N BIT DIGITAL OUTPUT D"]
    CLK["CLOCK GENERATOR fclk"] --> SAR
    SOC["START OF CONVERSION SOC"] --> SAR
    SAR --> EOC["END OF CONVERSION EOC FLAG"]
    CTRL["CONTROL LOGIC AND FSM"] --> SAR
    CTRL --> SH
    CTRL --> DAC

    style A1 fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style VREF fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CLK fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SH fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CMP fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SAR fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DAC fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CTRL fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SOC fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EOC fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Signal Flow Description:**

- The **analog input $V_{in}$** is first captured and held constant by the **S/H circuit** during the entire conversion.
- The **SAR** (the "brain" of the ADC) drives the **DAC** with a trial digital code.
- The DAC converts this code to an analog voltage $V_{dac}$, which is fed into one input of the **comparator**.
- The **comparator** decides whether $V_{dac} > V_{in}$ or $V_{dac} \leq V_{in}$ and feeds the binary decision back to the SAR.
- The **clock generator** provides the timing pulses that sequence the bit-by-bit decisions.
- After N clock pulses, the SAR contains the final N-bit code, which is latched onto the **output bus** and the **EOC flag** is raised.

---

### 4.2 Timing Diagram (Mermaid Sequence)

The following Mermaid sequence diagram illustrates the bit-decision timing and comparator state across N clock cycles:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SOC as SOC Signal
    participant SAR as SAR Register
    participant DAC as DAC Module
    participant CMP as Comparator
    participant EOC as EOC Output

    SOC->>SAR: Trigger Conversion Start
    Note over SAR: All bits cleared to 0
    loop For each bit i from MSB to LSB
        SAR->>DAC: Update trial code (set bit i to 1)
        DAC->>CMP: Provide Vdac voltage
        Note over CMP: Compare Vdac with Vin
        CMP-->>SAR: Decision (HIGH or LOW)
        SAR->>SAR: Lock bit i based on decision
    end
    SAR->>EOC: Assert EOC = HIGH
    SAR->>SAR: Hold final N-bit code on output bus
```

---

### 4.3 Functional State Machine (Conversion Phases)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Sampling: SOC pulse received
    Sampling --> Converting: S/H switches to HOLD mode
    Converting --> BitTestMSB: Cycle 1 begins
    BitTestMSB --> BitTestNext: Comparator decision latched
    BitTestNext --> BitTestNext: Iterate for bits N-2 down to 0
    BitTestNext --> Completed: LSB decision made
    Completed --> Idle: EOC asserted, code latched
    Idle --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*Define a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC. Why is it called "successive approximation"?*

**Model Answer:**

> A **Successive Approximation ADC** is a type of analog-to-digital converter that uses a binary search algorithm, performed bit-by-bit from MSB to LSB, to determine the digital equivalent of an analog input. It employs a Successive Approximation Register (SAR), an internal DAC, and a single comparator in a closed feedback loop.

> It is called "successive approximation" because the DAC output voltage $V_{dac}$ becomes a **successively closer approximation** to the input voltage $V_{in}$ after each clock cycle. The approximation error halves with every bit tested, exhibiting **exponential convergence**.

*(3 marks ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 scheme: 1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]**
*State two advantages and one disadvantage of a SAR ADC compared to a Flash ADC.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Advantages of SAR over Flash:**
> 1. Requires only **one comparator** (Flash needs $2^N - 1$ comparators), drastically reducing hardware complexity, power consumption, and silicon area.
> 2. Supports **much higher resolutions** (16, 18, 20 bits) practically, whereas Flash is limited to ~8 bits due to comparator count explosion.

> **Disadvantage of SAR compared to Flash:**
> 1. SAR has **N clock cycles of conversion latency** (N = bit count), whereas Flash completes in **1 clock cycle**, making Flash significantly faster for ultra-high-speed applications.

*(3 marks ‚ÄĒ 1.5 marks per advantage, 1 mark for disadvantage.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024, Modified from Past Patterns]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Successive Approximation ADC**. Clearly describe the role of the SAR, DAC, and comparator. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **10-bit SAR ADC** has a reference voltage $V_{ref} = 10.24$ V. The clock frequency is $f_{clk} = 500$ kHz. For an analog input of $V_{in} = 4.30$ V, determine:
   (i) The resolution (1 LSB size) (2 marks)
   (ii) The digital output code by showing the successive approximation process (3 marks)
   (iii) The conversion time and maximum sampling rate (2 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Working (7 marks):**

The block diagram consists of: (1) Sample-and-Hold, (2) Comparator, (3) SAR, (4) DAC, (5) Control logic, (6) Clock, (7) Voltage reference, (8) Output register.

**Working Principle:**

1. **Start of Conversion (SOC):** When the SOC pulse arrives, the S/H circuit captures $V_{in}$ and holds it constant. All bits in the SAR are cleared to 0.

2. **MSB Test (Bit $b_9$):** The SAR sets $b_9 = 1$, producing trial code $1000000000_2$. The DAC converts this to:
   $$V_{dac} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2} = \frac{10.24}{2} = 5.12 \text{ V}$$

3. **Comparator Action:** Since $V_{dac}$ (5.12 V) $> V_{in}$ (4.30 V), the comparator output is LOW, and bit $b_9$ is **reset to 0**.

4. **Bit $b_8$ Test:** New trial code = $0100000000_2$ (decimal 256). The DAC output is:
   $$V_{dac} = \frac{256}{1024} \times 10.24 = 2.56 \text{ V}$$
   Since $V_{dac}$ (2.56 V) $< V_{in}$ (4.30 V), the comparator output is HIGH, and bit $b_8$ is **kept as 1**.

5. This binary search continues for all 10 bits. After N = 10 clock cycles, the final digital code is determined, latched on the output bus, and the **End of Conversion (EOC)** flag is raised.

> **Role of Components:**
> - **SAR:** Acts as the "controller" that performs the binary search by setting and resetting bits sequentially.
> - **DAC:** Converts the SAR's trial digital code into an analog voltage for comparison.
> - **Comparator:** Provides the 1-bit decision (is $V_{dac} \leq V_{in}$?) that drives the SAR's next bit decision.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for diagram, 3 for working, 2 for component roles.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Resolution (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{10.24}{2^{10}} = \frac{10.24}{1024} = 0.01 \text{ V/LSB} = 10 \text{ mV/LSB}
$$

**(ii) Successive Approximation Process (3 marks):**

| Bit | Trial Code | Decimal | $V_{dac}$ (V) | Compare | Decision |
|-----|------------|---------|---------------|---------|----------|
| $b_9$ | 10 0000 0000 | 512 | 5.120 | $>$ 4.30 | **0** |
| $b_8$ | 01 0000 0000 | 256 | 2.560 | $<$ 4.30 | **1** |
| $b_7$ | 01 1000 0000 | 384 | 3.840 | $<$ 4.30 | **1** |
| $b_6$ | 01 1100 0000 | 448 | 4.480 | $>$ 4.30 | **0** |
| $b_5$ | 01 1010 0000 | 416 | 4.160 | $<$ 4.30 | **1** |
| $b_4$ | 01 1011 0000 | 432 | 4.320 | $>$ 4.30 | **0** |
| $b_3$ | 01 1010 1000 | 424 | 4.240 | $<$ 4.30 | **1** |
| $b_2$ | 01 1010 1100 | 428 | 4.280 | $<$ 4.30 | **1** |
| $b_1$ | 01 1010 1110 | 430 | 4.300 | $=$ 4.30 | **1** |
| $b_0$ | 01 1010 1111 | 431 | 4.310 | $>$ 4.30 | **0** |

**Final Digital Output:** $D = 01\,1010\,1110_2 = 430_{10}$

**Verification:**
$$
V_{dac,final} = \frac{430}{1024} \times 10.24 = 4.30 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(iii) Conversion Time & Sampling Rate (2 marks):**

$$
T_c = N \times T_{clk} = 10 \times \frac{1}{500 \times 10^3} = 10 \times 2 \text{ őľs} = 20 \text{ őľs}
$$

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{1}{20 \text{ őľs}} = 50{,}000 \text{ samples/sec} = 50 \text{ kSPS}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024, Modified from Past Patterns]**

**(a)** Compare **SAR ADC**, **Flash ADC**, and **Dual-Slope ADC** with respect to conversion time, resolution, hardware complexity, noise rejection, and typical applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **12-bit SAR ADC** is connected to a sensor whose output ranges from 0 to 4.096 V. The ADC uses an external reference of 4.096 V. Calculate:
   (i) The size of 1 LSB in volts. (1 mark)
   (ii) The digital output (in hex) for an input of 2.500 V. (2 marks)
   (iii) The maximum theoretical SNR in dB. (1 mark)
   (iv) The conversion time if the clock is 2 MHz. (1 mark)
   (v) The quantization error if the input is 1.000 V. (2 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Flash ADC | SAR ADC | Dual-Slope ADC |
|-----------|-----------|---------|----------------|
| **Conversion Time** | 1 clock cycle (fastest) | N clock cycles (fixed) | $2^{N+1}$ clock cycles (variable) |
| **Resolution** | Low (4‚Äď8 bits) | Medium-High (8‚Äď20 bits) | High (16‚Äď24 bits) |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Very High: $2^N - 1$ comparators + resistor ladder | Moderate: 1 comparator + 1 DAC + SAR | Low: 1 integrator + 1 comparator + counter |
| **Noise Rejection** | Poor (no integration) | Moderate (sample-hold + filtering needed) | Excellent (inherent integration rejects mains noise at integer multiples of integration time) |
| **Power Consumption** | High (many comparators active) | Low‚ÄďModerate | Low |
| **Cost** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Typical Applications** | Oscilloscopes, radar, video digitizers (high-speed) | DAQ systems, MCUs, motor control, IoT sensors | Digital multimeters (DMMs), precision weighing scales, panel meters |

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark per row, 2 marks for applications explanation.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 12$ bits, $V_{ref} = 4.096$ V, $f_{clk} = 2$ MHz.

**(i) Resolution (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{4.096}{4096} = 0.001 \text{ V/LSB} = 1 \text{ mV/LSB}
$$

**(ii) Digital output for $V_{in} = 2.500$ V (2 marks):**

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2.500}{0.001} \right\rfloor = 2500_{10}
$$

Converting 2500 to hex:
$$
2500 \div 16 = 156 \text{ remainder } 4
$$
$$
156 \div 16 = 9 \text{ remainder } 12 \;(\text{C}_{16})
$$
$$
9 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 9
$$

$$
D = 2500_{10} = 9\text{C}4_{16} = 1001\,1100\,0100_2
$$

**Verification:**
$$
V_{dac} = \frac{2500}{4096} \times 4.096 = 2.500 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(iii) Maximum Theoretical SNR (1 mark):**

$$
SNR = 6.02N + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB}
$$

**(iv) Conversion Time (1 mark):**

$$
T_c = N \times T_{clk} = 12 \times \frac{1}{2 \times 10^6} = 6 \text{ őľs}
$$

**(v) Quantization Error at $V_{in} = 1.000$ V (2 marks):**

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{1.000}{0.001} \right\rfloor = 1000_{10}
$$

$$
V_{dac} = \frac{1000}{4096} \times 4.096 = 1.00000 \text{ V (exact)}
$$

$$
e_q = V_{in} - V_{dac} = 1.000 - 1.000 = 0 \text{ V}
$$

If the input were 1.0005 V, then $D = 1000$, $V_{dac} = 1.000$ V, and $e_q = +0.0005$ V = +0.5 LSB, demonstrating the bounded nature of quantization error.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
>
> 1. **Conversion Time Trap:** Students frequently write "$T_c = N$ seconds" or forget to multiply $N$ by $T_{clk}$. Always compute $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$ first, then $T_c = N \cdot T_{clk}$.
>
> 2. **Sign of Quantization Error:** The error $e_q$ is a **signed quantity**. Writing "$e_q = +1$ LSB" is incorrect; it must lie in $\left[-\frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}, +\frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}\right]$.
>
> 3. **MSB to LSB Order:** The SAR **always** tests the MSB first and the LSB last. Reversing this order in the approximation table is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Comparator Decision Logic:** Some students write "if $V_{in} > V_{dac}$ then keep bit 1" ‚ÄĒ this is correct. But beware: if your comparator decision logic is reversed in a problem, double-check the sign convention in the question.
>
> 5. **Hexadecimal Conversion Errors:** When converting the final digital code to hex, students often make arithmetic errors in successive division by 16. Re-verify using the **"back-conversion"** method: $9\text{C}4_{16} = 9 \times 256 + 12 \times 16 + 4 = 2304 + 192 + 4 = 2500_{10}$. ‚úď
>
> 6. **Distinguish "Resolution" from "Quantization Error":** Resolution = 1 LSB (the step size). Quantization error = $\pm\frac{1}{2}$ LSB. These are *not* the same.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Must-Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- [x] **Definition:** SAR ADC = binary search ADC using SAR + DAC + 1 comparator.
- [x] **Conversion Time:** $T_c = N \times T_{clk}$ (constant, signal-independent).
- [x] **Resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^N$.
- [x] **Sampling Rate:** $f_s = f_{clk} / N$.
- [x] **Test Order:** MSB first, LSB last.
- [x] **Comparator Decision:** $V_{dac} > V_{in} \Rightarrow$ reset bit to 0; $V_{dac} \leq V_{in} \Rightarrow$ keep bit as 1.
- [x] **Quantization Error Bound:** $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB.
- [x] **SNR Formula:** $6.02N + 1.76$ dB.
- [x] **DAC Output:** $V_{dac} = (D/2^N) \times V_{ref}$.
- [x] **Convergence:** Error halves with each bit (exponential decay).
- [x] **Architecture:** Closed-loop feedback; only **one** comparator needed.
- [x] **Speed Limitation:** Throughput decreases linearly with resolution for fixed clock.
- [x] **Comparison Anchors:** Flash = 1 cycle (fast, low res); SAR = N cycles (balanced); Dual-Slope = $2^{N+1}$ cycles (slow, high res, excellent noise rejection).
- [x] **Practical Limitation:** DAC settling time must be less than $T_{clk}$ for each bit decision to be valid.
- [x] **Key Applications:** Microcontroller ADCs, DAQ, IoT sensors, motor control, battery-powered instruments.
- [x] **Hex Conversion Shortcut:** Group binary output into 4-bit nibbles from LSB side, then map each nibble to 0‚ÄďF.
- [x] **No. of Comparators Required:** Exactly **one** (vs. $2^N - 1$ for Flash).
- [x] **Common Mistake:** Writing $T_c = N$ without considering clock period.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flash Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flash Converter** (also known as a **Parallel ADC** or **Direct Conversion ADC**) is the fastest class of Analog-to-Digital Converters that achieves conversion in a **single clock cycle** (or more precisely, in one comparator propagation delay) by using an array of $2^n - 1$ simultaneously operating high-speed comparators. The input analog voltage is compared in parallel against $2^n - 1$ equally spaced reference voltages generated by a precision resistor ladder. The outputs of the comparators form a **thermometer code**, which is then decoded by a **priority encoder** into the final $n$-bit binary word.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** Flash type ADCs are classified under "High-Speed ADC Architectures." Examiners specifically look for the comparator-resistor ladder-priority encoder trilogy in 14-mark questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Height Measurement" Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the foot of a 7-step staircase, and you want to measure the height of a ball someone throws at you instantly.

Instead of using a slow ruler (which is like a **Successive Approximation ADC**), you place **7 people** at heights 1 cm, 2 cm, 3 cm, ..., 7 cm. As the ball flies past, **every person below the ball's height shouts "YES"** and **every person above shouts "NO"**. You instantly get a pattern like `0000111` (only top 3 shout NO, bottom 4 shout YES).

The first "NO" from the bottom tells you the ball is between heights 4 and 5. A **judge (priority encoder)** instantly translates this into the binary number `100` (i.e., 4 in binary).

**This parallel "everyone decides at once" mechanism is the essence of a Flash Converter.**

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Thermometer Code:** The raw output pattern from the comparator bank (like a column of mercury ‚ÄĒ all '1's below a transition and all '0's above).
- **Priority Encoder:** Combinational logic that detects the highest-priority active input and outputs its binary index.
- **Resistor Ladder:** A string of $2^n$ equal resistors forming a voltage divider between $V_{REF}$ and ground.
- **Quantization:** The fundamental discrete-step process of mapping a continuous analog value to one of $2^n$ digital levels.
- **Sparkle Code / Bubble Error:** A non-ideal comparator output deviation from the ideal thermometer code (e.g., `0001011` instead of `0000111`) caused by comparator mismatches or metastability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The resolution of a flash ADC is denoted by $n$ bits, providing $2^n$ quantization levels. The size of the Least Significant Bit (LSB) defines the smallest distinguishable analog increment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block Architecture

The flash converter consists of **three tightly coupled sub-blocks**:

1. **Precision Resistor Ladder (Reference Divider Network)**
   - Comprises $2^n$ resistors of identical value $R$ connected in series.
   - Top of ladder is tied to reference voltage $V_{REF}$; bottom is grounded.
   - Generates $2^n + 1$ tap points including the endpoints, providing $2^n - 1$ internal reference voltages spaced exactly one LSB apart.

2. **Bank of Comparators**
   - Consists of $2^n - 1$ high-speed, latched comparators.
   - The **inverting (‚Äď) input** of every comparator is tied to the analog input $V_{in}$.
   - The **non-inverting (+) input** of the $k$-th comparator is connected to the $k$-th ladder tap, providing reference voltage $V_{REF,k} = k \cdot \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$.
   - When $V_{in} > V_{REF,k}$, the comparator outputs logic '1'; otherwise '0'.

3. **Priority Encoder**
   - Receives the $2^n - 1$ comparator outputs (thermometer code).
   - Produces an $n$-bit binary word representing the decimal index of the **highest-order '1'** in the thermometer code.
   - Implementation typically uses a Wallace-tree or OR-gate matrix for speed.

### 2.2 Working Principle ‚ÄĒ The Conversion Cycle

- **Step 1:** $V_{in}$ is applied simultaneously to all $2^n - 1$ comparators (parallel comparison).
- **Step 2:** Each comparator fires (or stays low) based on the local reference voltage.
- **Step 3:** The resulting **thermometer code** is fed into the priority encoder.
- **Step 4:** The encoder produces the $n$-bit binary equivalent in a single logic propagation delay.

The **total conversion time** is the sum of the comparator propagation delay ($t_{comp}$) and the encoder propagation delay ($t_{enc}$):

$$t_{conv} = t_{comp} + t_{enc}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Flash"?** The conversion happens in essentially *one shot* ‚ÄĒ as fast as a camera flash illuminates a scene. No iterative search or feedback is required.

### 2.3 Quantization Behavior

The transfer characteristic of an ideal flash ADC is a **staircase function** with $2^n$ plateaus, each of width equal to 1 LSB. The midpoints of these plateaus align with the reference tap voltages, ensuring that the **maximum quantization error** is bounded by $\pm \dfrac{1}{2}$ LSB.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|----------|---------|---------------|
| Number of comparators | $N_{comp} = 2^n - 1$ | Pure count; grows exponentially with $n$ |
| Number of ladder resistors | $N_R = 2^n$ | Identical resistors of value $R$ |
| LSB size (resolution) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Volts (V) |
| $k$-th reference tap voltage | $V_{REF,k} = k \cdot \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | $k = 1, 2, \dots, 2^n - 1$ |
| Maximum quantization error | $E_{q,max} = \pm \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{n+1}}$ | $\pm \dfrac{1}{2}$ LSB |
| Number of quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | Distinct output codes |
| Ladder total resistance | $R_{total} = 2^n \cdot R$ | Determines bias current |
| Ladder bias current | $I_{ladder} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n R}$ | Trade-off: $\downarrow I$ for $\downarrow$ power, $\uparrow$ noise |
| Conversion time | $t_{conv} = t_{comp} + t_{enc}$ | Limited by slowest comparator |
| Power dissipation (approx.) | $P \approx (2^n - 1) \cdot P_{comp}$ | Dominated by comparator count |
| SNR (ideal, full-scale sine) | $SNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB; for theoretical noise floor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the comparator count explodes: an 8-bit flash ADC needs **255 comparators**; a 10-bit one needs **1023**. This exponential scaling is the primary reason flash converters are practically limited to **n ‚Č§ 8 bits** for monolithic integration.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Digital Storage Oscilloscopes (DSOs):** Real-time sampling at multi-GS/s rates.
- **Radar and LIDAR Systems:** Ultra-low-latency digitisation of return pulses.
- **High-Definition Video Processing:** Wideband video ADC front-ends.
- **Particle Physics Detectors:** Front-end readout of fast pulse trains.
- **Software Defined Radio (SDR):** Direct-RF sampling in the first Nyquist zone.
- **Medical Imaging (CT, MRI):** Parallel readout of multi-channel detector arrays.

### 2.6 Advantages vs. Disadvantages Trade-off Matrix

| Aspect | Flash ADC | Practical Consequence |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| Speed | **Fastest available** (single-step) | Ideal for GHz-range signals |
| Latency | Fixed, deterministic | Excellent for control loops |
| Power | **Very high** | Cooling required for high-$n$ designs |
| Chip area | $O(2^n)$ | Limits integration density |
| Resolution | **Typically ‚Č§ 8 bits** | Lower than SAR/pipeline ADCs |
| Input capacitance | Large ($2^n - 1$ inputs) | Drives need for wideband buffers |
| Component matching | Critical | Resistor/offset mismatch causes DNL/INL errors |
