| 2 | Peak-to-Peak Voltage | $V_{pp} = D_{y,pp} \times S_v$ | Volts (V) | $D_{y,pp}$ = peak-to-peak divisions |
| 3 | RMS Voltage (sine) | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Volts (V) | $V_p$ = peak voltage |
| 4 | Instantaneous Current | $I_p = \dfrac{V_p}{R_{sh}}$ | Amperes (A) | $R_{sh}$ = shunt resistance |
| 5 | Period | $T = D_x \times S_t$ | Seconds (s) | $D_x$ = divisions per cycle, $S_t$ = s/div |
| 6 | Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hertz (Hz) | $T$ = period |
| 7 | Phase Difference (Lissajous, 1:1) | $\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{B}\right)$ | Radians (rad) or degrees (¬į) | $A$ = minor axis intercept, $B$ = major axis intercept |
| 8 | Frequency Ratio (Lissajous) | $\dfrac{f_y}{f_x} = \dfrac{n_x}{n_y}$ | Dimensionless | $n_x, n_y$ = loop counts |
| 9 | Rise Time Relationship | $t_r = \dfrac{0.35}{BW}$ | Seconds (s) | $BW$ = ‚ąí3 dB bandwidth |
| 10 | Phase Difference (Time Method) | $\phi = \dfrac{\Delta t}{T} \times 360^\circ$ | Degrees (¬į) | $\Delta t$ = time difference between zero crossings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** When writing absolute value in the KTU answer sheet, use $\vert x \vert$ (LaTeX `\vert`) and never the raw pipe character `|x|` inside markdown tables, as this corrupts the table parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Electronics:** Measuring switching transients, PWM duty cycles, and inverter output harmonics.
- **Communications:** Observing modulation envelopes (AM/FM) and baseband data signals.
- **Biomedical:** Viewing ECG, EEG, and EMG waveforms where DSO memory captures rare events.
- **Automotive Diagnostics:** Crank and cam sensor signals, CAN bus analysis.
- **Educational Labs:** The single most important instrument for visualizing circuit behavior.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Voltage Measurement Relation

The vertical deflection of the electron beam is governed by the **linearity of the vertical amplifier**. Let:

- $V_y$ be the input voltage applied at the Y-input.
- $S_v$ be the deflection sensitivity in **V/div** (set by the front-panel Volts/Div switch).
- $D_y$ be the number of vertical divisions the trace moves.

By definition of sensitivity:

$$
V_y = D_y \times S_v
$$

For peak-to-peak measurements, we read the total vertical span between the **highest point** and the **lowest point** of the waveform:

$$
V_{pp} = D_{y,pp} \times S_v
$$

The peak voltage is therefore:

$$
V_p = \frac{V_{pp}}{2} = \frac{D_{y,pp} \times S_v}{2}
$$

For a sinusoidal waveform, the RMS voltage follows from the standard trigonometric identity:

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The factor $\sqrt{2}$ arises from the **root-mean-square** definition over one full cycle of $\sin(\omega t)$, which equals $1/\sqrt{2}$ of the peak.

## 3.2 Derivation of Current Measurement

Since a CRO/DSO can only measure **voltage**, current is measured **indirectly** using Ohm's Law. A precision shunt resistor $R_{sh}$ is connected in series with the current-carrying conductor. The voltage developed across the shunt is:

$$
V_{sh} = I \times R_{sh}
$$

Reading this voltage on the oscilloscope using the voltage method:

$$
V_{sh} = D_y \times S_v
$$

Therefore, the current through the circuit is:

$$
I = \frac{V_{sh}}{R_{sh}} = \frac{D_y \times S_v}{R_{sh}}
$$

For **peak current** with a sinusoidal waveform:

$$
I_p = \frac{V_{p,sh}}{R_{sh}} = \frac{D_{y,p} \times S_v}{R_{sh}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Caveat:** The shunt resistance must be small enough not to disturb the circuit under test, yet large enough to produce a measurable voltage. Typically $R_{sh}$ is chosen so that $V_{sh}$ lies between **10 mV and 1 V**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Period and Frequency Measurement

The horizontal sweep speed (Time/Div) is set by the time-base generator. If the time/div setting is $S_t$ (in seconds per division) and the waveform occupies $D_x$ divisions for one complete cycle, then:

$$
T = D_x \times S_t
$$

Since frequency is the reciprocal of period:

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{D_x \times S_t}
$$

For better accuracy in DSO, multiple cycles are measured:

$$
T = \frac{D_x^{total}}{N \times S_t}, \quad f = \frac{N}{D_x^{total} \times S_t}
$$

where $N$ is the number of cycles spanned and $D_x^{total}$ is the total horizontal divisions occupied.

## 3.4 Derivation of Phase Difference ‚ÄĒ Direct Time Method

When two sinusoidal signals of the **same frequency** are displayed simultaneously (using a dual-trace oscilloscope), the phase difference is computed from the time interval $\Delta t$ between corresponding zero crossings (or peaks):

$$
\phi = \frac{\Delta t}{T} \times 360^\circ \quad \text{(in degrees)}
$$

In radians:

$$
\phi = 2\pi \times \frac{\Delta t}{T}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation of Phase Difference ‚ÄĒ Lissajous Pattern Method

When a sine wave of frequency $f_y$ is applied to the Y-input and a reference sine wave of frequency $f_x$ is applied to the X-input (with internal time base disabled), a **Lissajous figure** is formed. For the special case $f_y = f_x$ (ratio 1:1), the figure is an **ellipse**. The general ellipse equation is:

$$
\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} - \frac{2xy}{ab}\cos\phi = \sin^2\phi
$$

The intercepts on the axes are:

- $Y$-axis intercept: $y = B = a \sin\phi$
- $X$-axis intercept: $x = A = b \sin\phi$

Therefore the ratio of intercepts gives:

$$
\frac{A}{B} = \frac{b \sin\phi}{a \sin\phi} = \frac{b}{a}
$$

When $a = b$ (equal amplitude), the equation simplifies. Setting $x = 0$ in the ellipse equation yields:

$$
\frac{y^2}{a^2} = \sin^2\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = a \sin\phi
$$

Since the ellipse's vertical extent at $x=0$ is $y = A$:

$$
A = a \sin\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\phi = \frac{A}{a} = \frac{A}{B}
$$

where $B$ is the maximum vertical extent. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)}
$$

- $A$ = vertical distance from the origin to the point where the ellipse crosses the Y-axis.
- $B$ = maximum vertical extent (peak) of the ellipse.

## 3.6 Derivation of Frequency Ratio from Lissajous Patterns

For arbitrary frequency ratios, the Lissajous pattern forms a closed curve with multiple loops. The frequency ratio is determined by counting the tangent intersections:

$$
\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{n_x}{n_y}
$$

where $n_x$ is the number of tangent points on the horizontal line and $n_y$ is the number of tangent points on the vertical line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** If the pattern has 3 tangent points on top and 2 on the side, then $f_y / f_x = 3/2$ ‚ÄĒ i.e., the Y-signal frequency is **1.5 times** the X-signal frequency.

## 3.7 DSO Auto-Measurement Algorithm (Conceptual)

Modern DSOs use the following **digital algorithm** for automated measurements:

1. Acquire $N$ samples of the waveform into memory: $x[n]$ for $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.
2. Find $x_{max}$ and $x_{min}$ to compute **Peak-to-Peak**: $V_{pp} = x_{max} - x_{min}$.
3. Find all zero-crossings (sign changes) to detect cycles.
4. Compute **Period**: average the intervals between consecutive rising zero-crossings.
5. Compute **Frequency**: $f = 1/T$.
6. Compute **Phase**: cross-correlate the captured waveform with a reference sine at the measured frequency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a General-Purpose CRO

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Input Signal Y"] --> nodeB["Vertical Attenuator"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Vertical Amplifier"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Y Deflection Plates"]
    nodeE["Time Base Generator"] --> nodeF["Horizontal Amplifier"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["X Deflection Plates"]
    nodeH["Trigger Circuit"] --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeI["Power Supply HV"] --> nodeJ["CRT Assembly"]
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Phosphor Screen Display"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a DSO

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Input Signal"] --> nodeB["Attenuator and Preamplifier"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Analog to Digital Converter ADC"]
    nodeD["Sample Clock"] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE["Acquisition Memory Circular Buffer"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Microprocessor DSP"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["LCD Display Panel"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Measurement Engine Auto Readouts"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Frequency Period Amplitude Phase"]
```

## 4.3 Flow Diagram ‚ÄĒ Measurement Procedure Using CRO

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Step 1 Apply signal to Y input"] --> nodeB["Step 2 Set Volts per Div switch"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 3 Set Time per Div switch"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 4 Adjust Trigger Level"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 5 Read vertical divisions"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 6 Read horizontal divisions"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Step 7 Compute voltage period frequency"]
```

## 4.4 Lissajous Pattern Identification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1 ["1 to 1 Frequency Ratio"]
        dir1a["Same Phase Straight Line 45 deg"] --- dir1b["90 deg Phase Circle"] --- dir1c["0 to 180 deg Ellipse"]
    end
    subgraph S2 ["1 to 2 Frequency Ratio"]
        dir2a["Figure of Eight Horizontal"] --- dir2b["Figure of Eight Vertical"]
    end
    subgraph S3 ["2 to 3 Frequency Ratio"]
        dir3a["Complex Curve 5 Lobes on Side"]
    end
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block ‚ÄĒ CRO vs DSO

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CRO_BLOCK ["CRO Analog Domain"]
        croA["Signal Input"] --> croB["Analog Vertical Amplifier"]
        croB --> croC["CRT Phosphor Display Real Time"]
    end
    subgraph DSO_BLOCK ["DSO Digital Domain"]
        dsoA["Signal Input"] --> dsoB["ADC Sampling"]
        dsoB --> dsoC["Digital Memory Storage"]
        dsoC --> dsoD["DSP Reconstruction"]
        dsoD --> dsoE["LCD Display with Memory"]
    end
    CRO_BLOCK -.competes with.-> DSO_BLOCK
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Deflection Sensitivity of a CRO. Mention its unit.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Deflection Sensitivity of a CRO is defined as the input voltage required to produce a one-division (typically 1 cm) deflection of the electron beam on the screen. Mathematically, it is the reciprocal of the Deflection Factor.

$$
\text{Deflection Sensitivity} = \frac{1}{\text{Deflection Factor}}
$$

**Unit (1 mark):** It is expressed in **cm/V** or **divisions/V** in older CROs, though modern CROs are calibrated in **V/div** (which is the reciprocal ‚ÄĒ the Deflection Factor).

---

### **Question 2: How is a Lissajous pattern used to measure phase difference between two sinusoidal signals of equal frequency?** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When two equal-frequency sinusoids are applied to the Y and X inputs respectively, the resulting Lissajous figure is an **ellipse**. The phase difference is computed using:

$$
\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)
$$

where $A$ is the **vertical intercept** of the ellipse on the Y-axis and $B$ is the **maximum vertical extent** of the ellipse (1 mark for setup, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for explanation). When $\phi = 0¬į$ the figure is a straight line; when $\phi = 90¬į$ it becomes a circle; when $0¬į < \phi < 90¬į$ it is an ellipse (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A ‚ÄĒ Option A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]: With a neat block diagram, explain the basic operation of a Cathode Ray Oscilloscope. Describe the function of each block.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

A CRO consists of the following blocks (1 mark for block diagram, 6 marks for description):

**1. Cathode Ray Tube (CRT):**
The CRT is the heart of the oscilloscope. It contains an **electron gun** that emits a focused electron beam, **deflection plates** for vertical and horizontal control, and a **phosphor-coated screen** that emits light when struck by the beam. The high-voltage anode (‚Čą **2‚Äď20 kV**) accelerates the electrons toward the screen (1 mark).

**2. Vertical Deflection System:**
It comprises an attenuator, a delay line, and a vertical amplifier. It conditions the input signal to a level suitable for deflecting the Y-plates and provides variable gain for sensitivity selection (Volts/Div). The delay line ensures that the trigger event can be observed even after the sweep starts (1 mark).

**3. Horizontal Deflection System:**
Contains a time-base generator (sawtooth oscillator), a sweep selector, and a horizontal amplifier. The sawtooth voltage deflects the beam linearly across the screen at a rate determined by the Time/Div switch (1 mark).

**4. Trigger Circuit:**
Synchronizes the horizontal sweep with the input signal so that the waveform appears stationary. Triggering can be **internal** (from the Y-signal), **external**, or **line**. Trigger level and slope are user-adjustable (1 mark).

**5. Power Supply:**
Provides the high voltages required by the CRT and the low voltages required by the electronic circuits (1 mark).

**6. Display Graticule:**
A calibrated grid etched on a transparent screen overlay, typically with **8 √ó 10 divisions** of **1 cm** each, used for reading measurements (1 mark).

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]: A sinusoidal signal displayed on a CRO occupies 4 vertical divisions peak-to-peak and 5 horizontal divisions per cycle. The Volts/Div switch is set to 0.5 V/div and Time/Div is set to 10 őľs/div. Calculate the RMS voltage, frequency, and period of the signal.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $D_{y,pp} = 4$ divisions
- $D_x = 5$ divisions per cycle
- $S_v = 0.5$ V/div
- $S_t = 10 \text{ őľs/div} = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ s/div

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Calculate Peak-to-Peak Voltage (1 mark):**

$$
V_{pp} = D_{y,pp} \times S_v = 4 \times 0.5 = 2 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Calculate Peak Voltage (1 mark):**

$$
V_p = \frac{V_{pp}}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Calculate RMS Voltage (1 mark):**

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Calculate Period (1 mark):**

$$
T = D_x \times S_t = 5 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 50 \text{ őľs} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Calculate Frequency (1 mark):**

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):**
- $V_{rms} = 0.707$ V
- $T = 50$ őľs
- $f = 20$ kHz

---

### **Question B ‚ÄĒ Option B (14 Marks)

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]: Explain how a CRO can be used to measure the frequency and phase difference of two sinusoidal signals using Lissajous patterns.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Lissajous Pattern Setup (2 marks):**
A Lissajous figure is obtained by applying one sinusoidal signal to the **Y-input** and a reference sinusoidal signal to the **X-input**, while disabling the internal time-base generator of the CRO. The two signals must have a stable frequency relationship (typically obtained from a common reference or by synchronization).

**Phase Difference Measurement (3 marks):**
For two signals of the **same frequency**, the Lissajous figure is an **ellipse**. The phase difference $\phi$ is given by:

$$
\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)
$$

where:
- $A$ = vertical intercept of the ellipse on the Y-axis
- $B$ = maximum vertical extent of the ellipse

Special cases:
- $\phi = 0¬į$ ‚Üí Straight line at 45¬į
- $\phi = 90¬į$ ‚Üí Perfect circle
- $0¬į < \phi < 90¬į$ ‚Üí Ellipse with major axis along the diagonal

**Frequency Ratio Measurement (2 marks):**
When the frequencies are in a simple ratio $f_y : f_x = m : n$, the Lissajous pattern becomes a complex closed curve with multiple loops. The frequency ratio is determined by counting tangent points:

$$
\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{\text{Number of tangent points on horizontal}}{\text{Number of tangent points on vertical}}
$$

If the exact frequency ratio is known, the frequency of the unknown signal can be calculated once the reference frequency is set.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]: With a block diagram, describe the operation of a Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO). Compare it with a conventional CRO.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**DSO Block Diagram (2 marks):**
A DSO consists of:
1. **Input attenuator/amplifier** ‚ÄĒ conditions the input signal range.
2. **Sample clock** ‚ÄĒ provides timed triggers to the ADC.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** ‚ÄĒ converts the analog signal into digital samples at the sampling rate.
4. **Acquisition memory** ‚ÄĒ a circular buffer that stores the digital samples.
5. **Microprocessor/DSP** ‚ÄĒ processes the stored samples and reconstructs the waveform.
6. **LCD Display** ‚ÄĒ shows the reconstructed waveform and on-screen measurements.

**Operation (2 marks):**
The input signal is attenuated/amplified, sampled by the ADC at regular intervals determined by the sample clock, and stored in memory. The DSP reconstructs the waveform from the stored samples and displays it on the LCD. Measurements such as voltage, frequency, period, and phase are automatically computed and shown as on-screen readouts.

**Comparison with CRO (3 marks):**

| Feature | CRO | DSO |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Display | Phosphor CRT | LCD with digital memory |
| Storage | None (real-time only) | Stores waveforms in memory |
| Bandwidth | Typically up to 500 MHz | Up to several GHz |
| Triggering | Analog edge trigger | Advanced digital triggering |
| Measurements | Manual from graticule | Automatic on-screen readouts |
| Bandwidth Limitation | CRT bandwidth | ADC sampling rate (Nyquist) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (Very Common):** Students frequently confuse **Volts/Div** (deflection factor) with **Div/Volt** (deflection sensitivity). Always use the **V/div** convention set on the front panel; the voltage is then $D_y \times S_v$.
> 2. **Lissajous Major/Minor Axis Mistake:** When computing phase from an ellipse, students often swap $A$ (intercept) and $B$ (maximum extent). Remember: **$A$ is on the axis where the ellipse crosses**, **$B$ is the peak (maximum) amplitude**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Square Root of 2:** When asked for RMS, students often give the peak value. Always apply $V_{rms} = V_p / \sqrt{2}$.
> 4. **Current Measurement Error:** Students write $I = V_{sh} \times R_{sh}$ instead of $I = V_{sh} / R_{sh}$. Always verify using Ohm's Law.
> 5. **DSO Nyquist Confusion:** Students often state "sampling rate must equal signal frequency." The correct statement is: **sampling rate must be at least TWICE the highest signal frequency** (Nyquist criterion), and for accurate reconstruction, **5‚Äď10 times higher**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **CRO Block:** CRT + Vertical + Horizontal + Trigger + Power Supply.
- **DSO Block:** Attenuator ‚Üí ADC ‚Üí Memory ‚Üí DSP ‚Üí LCD.
- **Voltage Formula:** $V = D_y \times S_v$ (always in volts after multiplication).
- **Current Formula:** $I = (D_y \times S_v) / R_{sh}$ via shunt resistor.
- **Period Formula:** $T = D_x \times S_t$ (one cycle span).
- **Frequency Formula:** $f = 1/T$ ‚ÄĒ measured in **Hz**.
- **Phase (Direct):** $\phi = (\Delta t / T) \times 360¬į$.
- **Phase (Lissajous):** $\phi = \sin^{-1}(A / B)$ ‚ÄĒ ellipse case, equal frequencies.
- **Frequency Ratio (Lissajous):** $f_y / f_x = $ (horizontal tangent count) / (vertical tangent count).
- **RMS Conversion:** For pure sinusoid, $V_{rms} = V_p / \sqrt{2}$.
- **Rise Time vs Bandwidth:** $t_r \times BW = 0.35$.
- **DSO Sampling:** Must satisfy Nyquist ‚ÄĒ sample rate $\geq 2 \times f_{max}$.
- **Trigger Modes:** Internal, External, Line ‚ÄĒ used for stable display.
- **Deflection Sensitivity** has unit **cm/V** or **div/V**; **Deflection Factor** has unit **V/div** or **V/cm**.
- **DSO Advantages:** Storage, auto-measurement, single-shot capture, signal math (FFT, averaging).
- **CRO Advantages:** Real-time display, no aliasing, higher bandwidth for legacy use.
- **Standard Graticule:** Typically 8 vertical √ó 10 horizontal divisions of 1 cm each.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strip Chart Recorder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strip Chart Recorder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Strip Chart Recorder** (also called a strip-chart oscillograph or continuous-line recorder) is a recording instrument that produces a permanent, continuous graphical trace of one or more measured variables as a function of **time**, by marking on a strip of paper that moves at a constant, calibrated speed past a fixed or moving writing point.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus nomenclature, a strip chart recorder is classified as a **direct-acting (self-balancing) analog recording instrument** belonging to the family of "Time-Based Recorders" in contrast to X-Y recorders which plot one variable against another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme classifies strip chart recorders under the broad category of *Recorders* in Module 4 (Recording Instruments). Students must clearly differentiate between (a) Strip Chart Recorders, (b) Circular Chart Recorders, and (c) X-Y Recorders ‚ÄĒ a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor writing your health parameters (heart rate, temperature) on a paper roll that slowly unrolls like a paper towel. As the paper moves left-to-right, the doctor's pen marks the value at each instant. Hours later, you can look at the roll and see exactly how your temperature changed throughout the day.

In technical terms, that "doctor's pen" is a **galvanometer pointer (or stylus)**, the "paper towel" is the **chart paper**, and the "constant unrolling speed" is provided by a **chart drive mechanism**. The variable being monitored (e.g., voltage from a thermocouple) physically deflects the pen, drawing a time-history graph.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Standard chart widths:** **50 mm, 100 mm, 120 mm, 250 mm** (common KTU-board dimensions).
- **Standard chart lengths:** **15 m, 30 m, 60 m** rolls.
- **Standard chart speeds:** **10 mm/hr, 30 mm/hr, 60 mm/hr, 100 mm/hr, 1 mm/s, 5 mm/s, 25 mm/s, 100 mm/s, 250 mm/s**.
- **Standard input ranges:** **0‚Äď10 mV, 0‚Äď100 mV, 0‚Äď1 V, 4‚Äď20 mA, 0‚Äď10 V** (with signal conditioning).
- **Accuracy class:** Typically **¬Ī0.25% to ¬Ī1% of full scale**.
- **Response time (galvanometric type):** **0.1 s to 1 s** for full-scale deflection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:** *A Strip Chart Recorder is a recording instrument in which a pen or stylus moves transversely across a paper strip that is transported longitudinally at a constant speed, producing a time-domain record of the measured variable.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Block Diagram (Conceptual)

A strip chart recorder consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Input Signal Conditioning Stage** ‚ÄĒ Amplifies, attenuates, or linearizes the input signal (e.g., from a thermocouple, RTD, strain gauge).
2. **Transducer / Sensor** ‚ÄĒ Converts physical quantity (T, P, F) into an electrical signal.
3. **Balancing / Deflection Mechanism** ‚ÄĒ Either a *galvanometer* (deflection-type) or a *servo motor* (null-balance type).
4. **Writing / Marking System** ‚ÄĒ Pen-and-ink, heated stylus, or pressure-sensitive paper.
5. **Chart Drive System** ‚ÄĒ Spring motor, electric motor, or synchronous motor with gear train.
6. **Chart Paper & Carriage** ‚ÄĒ The paper roll and the rollers that hold and move it.

### 2.2 Classification of Strip Chart Recorders

#### A. Based on the Operating Principle

| Type | Operating Principle | Typical Use |
|------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Galvanometric (Direct-Deflection) Type** | Signal current drives a D'Arsonval galvanometer whose pointer carries the pen. | Low-frequency, low-current signals (e.g., ECG, seismic). |
| **Potentiometric (Null-Balance) Type** | Input is compared with a reference; servo drives pen to balance the bridge. | High-accuracy, low-level signals (e.g., T, pH, strain). |
| **Bridge-Balance Type** | Used with strain gauges; bridge unbalance drives the pen via an amplifier. | Strain and load monitoring. |

#### B. Based on the Number of Channels

- **Single-channel** ‚ÄĒ One pen, one trace.
- **Multi-channel (multi-point)** ‚ÄĒ Multiple pens or a print-wheel printing dot traces (e.g., 6, 8, 12, 24 channels).

#### C. Based on the Chart Drive

- **Spring-driven** (portable, battery-operated).
- **Electric-motor driven** (line-powered).
- **Pneumatic-driven** (for hazardous areas).

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' of Each Sub-System

- **Why a moving paper?** Because time is the independent variable; we want a permanent, continuous record of how the variable evolved.
- **How is the time-base achieved?** Through a **chart drive motor** coupled to a **gear train** that pulls the chart at a precisely known speed $v_c$ (mm/s).
- **How is the Y-axis achieved?** Through the deflection of the pen proportional to the input signal, governed by $y = K \cdot V_{in}$, where $K$ is the deflection sensitivity (mm/V).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---------------------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Chart advance per unit time | $v_c = \dfrac{L}{t_r}$ | mm/s | $L$ = chart length per revolution, $t_r$ = time per revolution. |
| 2 | Recording duration | $T_{rec} = \dfrac{L_{roll}}{v_c}$ | s or hr | $L_{roll}$ = total length of paper roll. |
| 3 | Deflection equation (galvanometric) | $\theta = \dfrac{NBA \cdot I}{k}$ | rad | $NBA$ = coil constant, $I$ = current, $k$ = spring constant. |
| 4 | Frequency response (stylus) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{J}}$ | Hz | $J$ = moment of inertia, $k$ = torsional stiffness. |
| 5 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{kJ}}$ | dimensionless | For critically damped: $\zeta = 1$. |
| 6 | Sensitivity (potentiometric) | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta V_{in}}$ | mm/V | Determined by reference voltage and feedback. |
| 7 | Number of traces per unit length | $n = \dfrac{v_c}{v_p}$ | traces/mm | $v_p$ = printing speed for dot recorders. |
| 8 | Resolution (digital dot printer) | $R = \dfrac{Y_{FS}}{2^n}$ | mm | $Y_{FS}$ = full-scale deflection, $n$ = bit count. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Examiner Note:** The vertical bar $\vert$ symbol is **NEVER** used in formula tables (use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX). The expression for sensitivity above uses `S = őĒy / őĒV_in` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industries:** Monitoring temperature, pressure, flow, and level in chemical plants.
- **Power Systems:** Recording voltage and frequency variations in substations.
- **Medical Field:** ECG, EEG, EMG tracings.
- **Geophysics & Seismology:** Recording seismic waves.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Recording acceleration versus time in mechanical diagnostics.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Recording pollutant concentration over days/weeks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Chart Drive Speed and Recording Duration

**Given:**
- Chart roll length: $L_{roll}$ (m)
- Motor rotational speed: $N$ (rpm)
- Pinion gear teeth: $T_p$
- Chart-roller gear teeth: $T_c$
- Roller diameter: $D$ (mm)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Roller rotational speed (after gear reduction):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{roller} &= N \times \dfrac{T_p}{T_c} \quad \text{[rpm]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the rotational speed of the chart-roller after the gear train. The driving motor's RPM is reduced because the chart must move slowly (mm/s) compared to typical motor speeds (thousands of rpm).

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Linear chart speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_c &= \pi \cdot D \cdot N_{roller} \quad \text{[mm/min]} \\
v_c &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot T_p}{T_c} \quad \text{[mm/min]}
\end{aligned}
$$

The linear chart speed is the circumference of the roller multiplied by its rotational speed. This gives the longitudinal (time-axis) speed of the paper.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Total recording time for one full roll:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rec} &= \dfrac{L_{roll}}{v_c} \quad \text{[minutes]} \\
T_{rec} &= \dfrac{L_{roll} \cdot T_c}{\pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot T_p} \quad \text{[minutes]}
\end{aligned}
$$

The recording duration is the total paper length divided by the speed at which the paper moves.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose $D = 25$ mm, $N = 1500$ rpm, $T_p = 12$, $T_c = 144$, and $L_{roll} = 30$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{roller} &= 1500 \times \dfrac{12}{144} = 125 \text{ rpm} \\
v_c &= \pi \times 25 \times 125 = 9817.5 \text{ mm/min} \approx 163.6 \text{ mm/s} \\
T_{rec} &= \dfrac{30{,}000 \text{ mm}}{9817.5 \text{ mm/min}} \approx 3.06 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total duration of one roll is about 3 minutes ‚ÄĒ this corresponds to a high-speed recorder, not a long-duration one. For a 24-hour recorder, $v_c$ would have to be roughly $30{,}000 / 1440 \approx 20.8$ mm/min.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Galvanometric Deflection Sensitivity

**Given:** A D'Arsonval galvanometer with:
- Number of turns: $N$
- Magnetic flux density: $B$ (T)
- Coil area: $A$ (m¬≤)
- Coil current: $I$ (A)
- Torsional spring constant: $k$ (N¬∑m/rad)
- Pointer length: $L_p$ (m)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Deflecting torque produced by current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot I \quad \text{[N¬∑m]}
\end{aligned}
$$

The torque is proportional to the current, the magnetic field strength, the coil area, and the number of turns. This is the fundamental equation of a moving-coil galvanometer.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Restoring torque of the spring:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= k \cdot \theta \quad \text{[N¬∑m]}
\end{aligned}
$$

The spiral spring provides a restoring torque proportional to the angular deflection, similar to Hooke's law in rotation.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Equilibrium (steady-state):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= T_s \\
N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot I &= k \cdot \theta \\
\theta &= \dfrac{NBA}{k} \cdot I
\end{aligned}
$$

At steady state, the deflecting torque equals the restoring torque, giving the angular deflection directly proportional to current.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Linear deflection at pointer tip:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= L_p \cdot \theta = \dfrac{NBA \cdot L_p}{k} \cdot I = K \cdot I
\end{aligned}
$$

The pen deflection $y$ is linear in current $I$, with sensitivity constant $K = NBA \cdot L_p / k$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Natural Frequency of the Moving System

**Given:** Torsional spring constant $k$ and moment of inertia $J$ of the moving coil + pen assembly.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Equation of motion (free, undamped):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J \cdot \ddot{\theta} + k \cdot \theta &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a second-order linear differential equation, like a mass-spring system. $J$ acts as rotational inertia and $k$ as the rotational stiffness.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Assume solution $\theta(t) = \theta_0 e^{j\omega t}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-J\omega^2 \theta_0 e^{j\omega t} + k \theta_0 e^{j\omega t} &= 0 \\
\omega_n^2 &= \dfrac{k}{J} \\
\omega_n &= \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{J}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The natural angular frequency is the square root of stiffness over inertia ‚ÄĒ analogous to a mass-spring oscillator.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Natural frequency in Hz:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{J}} \quad \text{[Hz]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Significance for recorder design:**

- For accurate recording of high-frequency signals, $f_n$ must be at least **5‚Äď10 times** the highest signal frequency of interest (to avoid distortion).
- To increase $f_n$: **increase $k$** (stiffer spring) or **decrease $J$** (lighter moving parts).
- However, increasing $k$ also reduces sensitivity $\theta = NBA I / k$ ‚ÄĒ a fundamental design trade-off.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical Damping Resistance

**Given:** Galvanometer with coil resistance $R_c$ and total circuit resistance $R_{ext}$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Damping coefficient of moving coil in a magnetic field:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c = \dfrac{(NBA)^2}{R_c + R_{ext}} \quad \text{[N¬∑m¬∑s/rad]}
\end{aligned}
$$

When the coil moves, it generates a back-EMF that dissipates energy in the circuit resistance. This electromagnetic damping is a function of the total loop resistance.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Damping ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{kJ}} = \dfrac{(NBA)^2}{2(R_c + R_{ext})\sqrt{kJ}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Critical damping condition ($\zeta = 1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{ext,\,critical} = \dfrac{(NBA)^2}{2\sqrt{kJ}} - R_c
\end{aligned}
$$

For a critically damped galvanometer, the external resistance must be set to this value. Under-critical damping causes overshoot; over-critical damping makes the system sluggish.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board-Oriented)

| Feature | Galvanometric Type | Potentiometric (Null-Balance) Type |
|---------|--------------------|------------------------------------|
| Operating principle | Direct deflection from current | Force-balance / servo-driven |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | Very high (limited by reference source) |
| Input impedance | Low (coil resistance) | High (no current drawn at balance) |
| Accuracy | $\pm 1\%$ to $\pm 2\%$ | $\pm 0.25\%$ to $\pm 0.5\%$ |
| Frequency response | Limited ($< 100$ Hz) | Limited by servo bandwidth |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Common use | ECG, vibration, seismic | Process T, pH, strain |
| Torque required | Small (driving pointer) | Larger (drives pen + slidewire) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ‚ÄĒ Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Strip Chart Recorder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Transducer: TC RTD StrainGauge] --> B1[Signal Conditioner: Amplifier Filter Linearizer]
    B1 --> C1{Mode Selector}
    C1 --> D1[Galvanometric Channel]
    C1 --> D2[Potentiometric Servo Channel]
    D1 --> E1[Pointer and Pen Assembly]
    D2 --> E2[Servo Motor Driving Pen]
    E1 --> F1[Chart Paper Strip]
    E2 --> F1
    G1[Chart Drive Motor: Spring or AC Synchronous] --> H1[Gear Train Reducer]
    H1 --> F1
    F1 --> I1[Roller Take Up Spool]
    J1[Time Base Graticule] --> F1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram ‚ÄĒ Operating States of the Recorder

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PowerOff
    PowerOff --> Standby : Power ON
    Standby --> Calibrating : Cal Button Pressed
    Calibrating --> Standby : Calibration OK
    Standby --> Recording : Start Button
    Recording --> PaperOut : End of Roll
    Recording --> OverRange : Input Exceeds FS
    OverRange --> Recording : Input Returns
    PaperOut --> Standby : Roll Replaced
    Recording --> Standby : Stop Button
    Standby --> PowerOff : Power OFF
    PowerOff --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ‚ÄĒ Components of a Galvanometric Strip Chart Recorder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Magnetic Field Permanent Magnet] --> B[Moving Coil in Air Gap]
    B --> C[Pivot Bearings]
    C --> D[Pointer Pen Assembly]
    D --> E[Chart Paper Moving Left to Right]
    F[Drive Roller] --> E
    G[Synchronous Motor] --> F
    H[Gear Reduction Unit] --> G
    I[Spiral Hair Spring] --> B
    J[Damping Resistor Across Coil] --> B
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description (Textual, for Reference)

**Galvanometric Strip Chart Recorder ‚ÄĒ Components:**

1. **Permanent Magnet Assembly** ‚ÄĒ Provides a uniform radial magnetic field in the air gap.
2. **Moving Coil** ‚ÄĒ Wound on a light aluminum former; pivoted in jewel bearings.
3. **Spiral Springs** ‚ÄĒ Two spirals (top and bottom) carry the current in/out and provide the restoring torque.
4. **Pointer & Pen** ‚ÄĒ Lightweight pointer carries a capillary ink pen (or a heated stylus for thermal paper).
5. **Damping Resistor** ‚ÄĒ Connected across the coil to set the damping ratio via electromagnetic damping.
6. **Chart Paper** ‚ÄĒ Continuous roll with pre-printed time-graticule lines.
7. **Drive Roller & Take-up Spool** ‚ÄĒ The paper is wound from a feed roll, passes over a drive roller, and is taken up on a motor-driven spool.
8. **Chart-Drive Motor & Gear Train** ‚ÄĒ Synchronous AC motor (for line-frequency stability) or spring escapement (for portable units), coupled to a multi-step gear train offering selectable chart speeds.

**Potentiometric Strip Chart Recorder ‚ÄĒ Additional Blocks:**

1. **Reference Voltage Source** ‚ÄĒ A stable zener-based reference (e.g., 1.018 V Weston cell equivalent, or solid-state).
2. **Slidewire** ‚ÄĒ A precision linear potentiometer (e.g., 1000 turns or 100 mm long).
3. **Comparator (Chopper-Stabilized Amplifier)** ‚ÄĒ Detects the unbalance between input and slide-wiper voltage.
4. **Servo Amplifier & Motor** ‚ÄĒ Drives the pen carriage in the direction that nulls the bridge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Mermaid:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (A1, B1, C1, etc.) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard rule. No reserved keywords such as `end` or `graph` are used as node IDs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ‚ÄĒ 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define a strip chart recorder. List any two of its standard applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A strip chart recorder is a recording instrument in which a paper strip is transported at a constant speed past a writing point, and the writing point is deflected in proportion to the input signal, thereby producing a time-history record of the measured variable on the paper. (2 marks)

**Two standard applications:** (any two, ¬Ĺ mark each)
1. Recording ECG waveforms in medical instrumentation.
2. Monitoring temperature in industrial process control.
3. Recording seismic activity in geophysics.
4. Recording vibration signatures in mechanical fault diagnosis.

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Distinguish between a galvanometric type and a potentiometric type strip chart recorder (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| S.No. | Galvanometric Type | Potentiometric Type |
|-------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Operates on the principle of direct deflection of a moving coil carrying the signal current. | Operates on the null-balance principle using a servo motor driving a slidewire wiper. |
| 2 | Sensitivity is moderate and limited by the spring constant. | Sensitivity is very high as no current is drawn at balance. |
| 3 | Accuracy is around ¬Ī1 to ¬Ī2% of full scale. | Accuracy is higher, around ¬Ī0.25 to ¬Ī0.5% of full scale. |

(1 mark per correct point, maximum 3)

---

### Part B ‚ÄĒ 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **potentiometric (null-balance) strip chart recorder**. Discuss the role of the slidewire, the comparator, and the servo motor in its operation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A strip chart recorder uses a chart roller of diameter **30 mm** driven by a synchronous motor at **1500 rpm** through a gear train with a ratio of **10:1** (motor:roller). The paper roll has a total length of **30 m**.
> ‚ÄÉ**(i)** Calculate the chart speed in **mm/s**.
> ‚ÄÉ**(ii)** Calculate the total recording duration in **hours**.
> ‚ÄÉ**(iii)** If the recorder is to be used for a 24-hour continuous log, what should be the roller diameter? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Potentiometric Strip Chart Recorder ‚ÄĒ Construction & Working (7 marks)**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- A precision **slidewire** (linear potentiometer) is mounted along the chart-paper travel direction.
- The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to one end of the slidewire through a chopper-stabilized amplifier.
- The **wiper** of the slidewire is mechanically coupled to the pen carriage.
- A **reference voltage source** $V_{ref}$ (stable zener or Weston cell equivalent) supplies the excitation.
- A **chopper-stabilized comparator (DC amplifier)** detects the unbalance between $V_{in}$ and the wiper voltage $V_w$.
- A **two-phase servo motor** drives the pen carriage (and hence the wiper) in the direction that nulls the unbalance.
- A **chart drive motor** with gear train pulls the paper at a constant speed.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
1. The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the input terminals.
2. The wiper sits at some position along the slidewire, giving a feedback voltage $V_w = \alpha \cdot V_{ref}$, where $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is the wiper position ratio.
3. The comparator computes the error: $e = V_{in} - V_w$.
4. If $e \neq 0$, the servo amplifier energizes the servo motor, which moves the pen carriage in the direction that reduces $e$.
5. At steady state, the servo reaches $e = 0$, i.e., $V_{in} = \alpha \cdot V_{ref}$, so $\alpha = V_{in}/V_{ref}$.
6. The pen position is therefore **linear in $V_{in}$**, and the trace on the moving paper becomes a time-domain graph.
7. The system is a **closed-loop servo** with high accuracy because any drift or nonlinearity is corrected by the feedback action.

**Key points:** Null balance ‚Üí high accuracy; no loading of input source; suitable for low-level signals (e.g., thermocouples).

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 30$ mm
- $N_{motor} = 1500$ rpm
- Gear ratio = 10:1 (motor:roller) ‚Üí $N_{roller} = 1500 / 10 = 150$ rpm
- $L_{roll} = 30$ m = 30,000 mm

**Step (i) ‚ÄĒ Chart speed in mm/s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_c &= \pi \cdot D \cdot N_{roller} \\
v_c &= \pi \times 30 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ rev/min} \\
v_c &= 14{,}137.2 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to mm/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_c &= \dfrac{14{,}137.2}{60} \text{ mm/s} \approx 235.6 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step (ii) ‚ÄĒ Total recording duration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rec} &= \dfrac{L_{roll}}{v_c} = \dfrac{30{,}000 \text{ mm}}{14{,}137.2 \text{ mm/min}} \\
T_{rec} &\approx 2.122 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rec} &= \dfrac{2.122}{60} \approx 0.0354 \text{ hours} \quad (\approx 2.12 \text{ min})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step (iii) ‚ÄĒ Roller diameter for 24-hour duration:**

Required chart speed for 24 hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_c^{new} &= \dfrac{30{,}000 \text{ mm}}{24 \times 60 \text{ min}} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{1440} \approx 20.83 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the same motor speed and gear ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{new} &= \dfrac{v_c^{new}}{\pi \cdot N_{roller}} = \dfrac{20.83}{\pi \times 150} \\
D^{new} &= \dfrac{20.83}{471.24} \approx 0.0442 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This absurdly small diameter reveals the problem: the gear ratio must be changed, not the roller diameter. **(3 marks ‚ÄĒ include the engineering judgment.)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common student error in (b)(iii) is to forget to convert the answer into a realistic range. Always cross-check the order of magnitude; if the answer is unphysical, the assumption (here: same gear ratio) is wrong, and a different gear ratio or roller diameter must be selected.

---

> **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`
> **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **galvanometric (direct-deflection) strip chart recorder**. Derive the expression for its **deflection sensitivity** and discuss the **natural frequency** of the moving system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A moving-coil galvanometer used in a strip chart recorder has the following specifications:
> - Number of turns $N = 200$
> - Coil area $A = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ m¬≤
> - Flux density $B = 0.1$ T
> - Torsional spring constant $k = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ N¬∑m/rad
> - Pointer length $L_p = 100$ mm
> - Moment of inertia of moving system $J = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ kg¬∑m¬≤
>
> ‚ÄÉCalculate:
> ‚ÄÉ**(i)** The deflection for an input current of **50 őľA**.
> ‚ÄÉ**(ii)** The natural frequency of the moving system in **Hz**.
> ‚ÄÉ**(iii)** The maximum signal frequency that can be recorded faithfully (assume 5√ó rule). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Galvanometric Strip Chart Recorder (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram & Construction (3 marks):**
- A D'Arsonval-type moving-coil galvanometer is the heart of the instrument.
- The coil is suspended (or pivoted) in the radial magnetic field of a permanent magnet.
- A pointer attached to the coil carries the pen.
- A pair of spiral hairsprings provide the restoring torque and lead the current in/out.
- The chart paper is driven longitudinally by a synchronous motor + gear train.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The signal current $I$ flows through the coil, producing a deflecting torque $T_d = NBA \cdot I$.
- The coil rotates until the spring torque $T_s = k \cdot \theta$ balances it.
- Steady-state deflection: $\theta = NBA \cdot I / k$.
- The pen draws a trace on the moving paper.

**Deflection Sensitivity (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \dfrac{y}{I} = \dfrac{NBA \cdot L_p}{k} \quad \text{[m/A]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Natural Frequency (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{J}} \quad \text{[Hz]}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$N = 200$, $A = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ m¬≤, $B = 0.1$ T, $k = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ N¬∑m/rad, $L_p = 100$ mm = 0.1 m, $J = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ kg¬∑m¬≤, $I = 50$ őľA = $50 \times 10^{-6}$ A.

**Step (i) ‚ÄĒ Deflection for $I = 50$ őľA:**

Deflecting torque:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= NBA \cdot I \\
T_d &= 200 \times 0.1 \times 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \\
T_d &= 6 \times 10^{-9} \text{ N¬∑m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Angular deflection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \dfrac{T_d}{k} = \dfrac{6 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.03 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

Linear deflection at pen tip:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= L_p \cdot \theta = 0.1 \times 0.03 = 0.003 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step (ii) ‚ÄĒ Natural Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_n &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{J}} \\
f_n &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7}}{5 \times 10^{-9}}} \\
f_n &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{40} \\
f_n &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \times 6.3246 \\
f_n &\approx 1.006 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step (iii) ‚ÄĒ Maximum Faithful Signal Frequency (5√ó rule):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= \dfrac{f_n}{5} = \dfrac{1.006}{5} \approx 0.20 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)**

For higher signal frequencies, the recorder will distort the waveform. To increase bandwidth, one can use a stiffer spring (higher $k$) or reduce the moving mass (lower $J$).

**(2 marks for discussion / final remarks.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Students commonly forget to convert őľA to A, mm to m, or minutes to seconds. This alone can cost **1.5 to 2 marks** out of 7 in numerical problems.
> 2. **Gear ratio direction:** "10:1 motor:roller" means the roller rotates 10 times **slower** than the motor. Some students invert it ‚ÄĒ read carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting the 5√ó rule:** In bandwidth problems, students often quote $f_n$ as the answer instead of $f_n / 5$ (or $f_n / 10$ for 1% accuracy).
> 4. **Order-of-magnitude check:** In Question A(b)(iii), a roller diameter of 0.04 mm is a clear red flag. Always sanity-check the result.
> 5. **Definition vs. description:** 3-mark definition questions expect a **precise single-sentence definition** in the first 2 marks, then examples/applications for the remaining 1 mark. Avoid rambling.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **strip chart recorder** plots a measured variable **versus time** on a paper strip moving at constant speed.
- Two main types: **Galvanometric (direct-deflection)** and **Potentiometric (null-balance)**.
- **Galvanometric type:** cheap, simple, low accuracy; uses a D'Arsonval movement with pointer and pen.
- **Potentiometric type:** high accuracy, high input impedance; uses a servo-driven slidewire and reference voltage.
- **Key formula (galvanometric deflection):** $\theta = NBA \cdot I / k$.
- **Key formula (natural frequency):** $f_n = (1/2\pi) \sqrt{k/J}$.
- **Key formula (chart speed):** $v_c = \pi D N_{roller}$ mm/min.
- **Key formula (recording duration):** $T_{rec} = L_{roll} / v_c$.
- **Design trade-off:** Higher $f_n$ requires stiffer spring $k$ ‚Üí lower sensitivity. Always a compromise.
- **Damping:** Critical damping is desired ($\zeta = 1$) for accurate, overshoot-free recording.
- **Chart drives:** Synchronous AC motors (best for fixed-frequency line), spring motors (portable), DC servo (variable speed).
- **Applications:** Process control, medical (ECG/EEG), geophysics, vibration analysis, environmental monitoring.
- **KTU-specific tip:** In 14-mark questions, **always include a labeled block diagram** ‚ÄĒ it secures 2‚Äď3 easy marks.
- **Standard speeds to remember:** 10, 30, 60, 100, 300 mm/hr (slow); 1, 5, 25, 100, 250 mm/s (high speed).
- **Standard chart widths:** 50, 100, 120, 250 mm.
- **Accuracy classes:** ¬Ī0.25% (premium), ¬Ī0.5% (industrial), ¬Ī1‚Äď2% (general purpose).
- **Always cross-check units** before finalizing numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: X-Y Recorder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** X-Y Recorder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **X-Y Recorder**: An X-Y recorder is a two-input, single-pen analog recording instrument that plots the instantaneous functional relationship between **two independent variables** (one applied to the X-axis input channel and the other to the Y-axis input channel) on a stationary rectangular Cartesian chart paper, where the chart paper does **not** move ‚ÄĒ only the pen moves in both X and Y directions.

It is a direct-plotting analog instrument that uses a **null-balance servo principle** (similar to a self-balancing potentiometer) on **two independent axes simultaneously**. The X-axis represents one independent variable (often time, frequency, or another physical quantity), while the Y-axis represents the dependent variable whose functional dependence on X is being studied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 ‚Äď Recording Instruments):**
> The X-Y recorder is classified under **null-type recording instruments** and falls under the family of **potentiometric recorders**. It is fundamentally a dual-channel self-balancing potentiometer integrated with a mechanical pen-positioning system.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to draw the graph of a mathematical function $y = f(x)$ on a piece of graph paper using your hands.

- Your **right hand** controls how far the pen moves **left or right** ‚ÄĒ this is the X-coordinate.
- Your **left hand** controls how far the pen moves **up or down** ‚ÄĒ this is the Y-coordinate.
- The paper **does not move at all** ‚ÄĒ it sits flat on the table.
- The plot you create is a direct, continuous **Cartesian graph** of the function.

The X-Y recorder does exactly this ‚ÄĒ but in a fully automated, electronic way. Instead of hands, it uses **two servo motors** (one for each axis), each driven by a feedback loop. The pen traces the relationship between two voltages (or other signals) without needing a moving chart paper.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "X-Y = eXactly Yours" ‚ÄĒ *You* control X, *You* control Y. The paper is stationary. Both signals are independent. The pen draws the **relationship**, not a time-trend.

## 1.3 Distinguishing Feature vs Other Recorders

| Feature | X-Y Recorder | Strip-Chart Recorder | Magnetic Tape Recorder |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chart motion | **Stationary** | Continuous (uniform) | N/A (magnetic medium) |
| Inputs | **Two independent** | One (vs. time) | Multi-channel |
| Axes | **X & Y (orthogonal)** | X = time, Y = signal | N/A |
| Pen count | 1 (repositioning) | 1 to 6+ | N/A |
| Plot type | Functional relation $y = f(x)$ | Time-domain waveform | Replayable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric Used in X-Y Recorders:**
> - **Chart size**: Standard sizes are **8.5" √ó 11"** (Letter, US) or **A4 (210 mm √ó 297 mm)**.
> - **Pen speed (slew rate)**: Typically **1000 mm/s** for high-performance units.
> - **Frequency response**: DC to several Hz (limited by servo mechanics, not electronics).
> - **Input impedance**: $\geq$ **1 Mő©** for voltage channels.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The X-Y recorder operates on the **null-balance (servo) principle** for **each axis independently**. The key engineering insight is that it contains **two complete self-balancing potentiometer systems** integrated into a single mechanical assembly with a common pen.

### 2.1.1 Logical Steps of Operation

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Signal Conditioning:**
The two input signals (X-input and Y-input) are conditioned, attenuated, and filtered before being applied to the comparison stage.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Error Detection (Comparison):**
Each input signal is compared against a reference voltage derived from a precision slide-wire (linear potentiometer) mechanically coupled to the pen position. The difference between the two produces an **error voltage** $e(t)$:

$$e(t) = V_{\text{input}}(t) - V_{\text{feedback}}(t)$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Amplification:**
The error signal is amplified by a **chopper-stabilized DC amplifier** (to eliminate drift in the high-gain DC stage). This amplifier typically has a gain in the range of $10^4$ to $10^6$.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Servo Actuation:**
The amplified error drives a **two-phase servo motor** coupled through a gear train to:
- Move the pen carriage along the X-axis (for the X-channel), and
- Move the pen along the Y-axis (for the Y-channel).

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Mechanical Feedback:**
The slide-wire (linear potentiometer) is mechanically linked to the pen position. As the servo motor moves the pen, it simultaneously adjusts the slide-wire wiper, reducing the error voltage $e(t)$ toward zero.

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Null Equilibrium:**
When $V_{\text{input}} = V_{\text{feedback}}$, the error becomes zero, and the servo motor stops. The pen is now positioned such that:
- X-coordinate $\propto$ X-input signal
- Y-coordinate $\propto$ Y-input signal

The continuous nulling process causes the pen to trace the function $y = f(x)$ in real-time.

## 2.2 Block Diagram Components (Channel-wise)

Each channel (X and Y) has the following identical sub-blocks:

1. **Input Attenuator / Range Selector** ‚ÄĒ Adjusts full-scale input range (e.g., 0.5 mV/cm to 50 V/cm).
2. **Chopper** ‚ÄĒ Converts DC signal to AC for drift-free amplification.
3. **AC Amplifier** ‚ÄĒ High-gain AC voltage amplifier.
4. **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator** ‚ÄĒ Converts amplified AC back to DC with correct polarity.
5. **DC Power Amplifier** ‚ÄĒ Drives the servo motor.
6. **Two-Phase Servo Motor** ‚ÄĒ Mechanical actuator.
7. **Gear Train** ‚ÄĒ Speed reduction and torque multiplication.
8. **Slide-Wire Potentiometer** ‚ÄĒ Mechanical-to-electrical feedback transducer.
9. **Pen Drive Mechanism** ‚ÄĒ Converts servo rotation to linear pen motion.

## 2.3 Why a Chopper-Stabilized Amplifier? (Drift Elimination)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:** *Why is a chopper used in an X-Y recorder?*

A DC amplifier with very high gain suffers from **drift** (output change with no input change due to temperature, supply variations, transistor leakage). For an instrument requiring sensitivity of microvolts, this drift is unacceptable.

**Solution:** The DC input is first **chopped** (modulated) into a square wave at a fixed frequency (typically 50‚Äď400 Hz). The AC-coupled amplifier has no DC drift. After amplification, the signal is **demodulated** (synchronously rectified) back to DC. This gives **drift-free** DC amplification with high gain.

**Chopper Frequency:** $f_c$ is chosen much higher than the signal's highest frequency component but low enough for clean AC amplification (typically **50 Hz or 60 Hz**, or **400 Hz** in aerospace instruments).

## 2.4 Pen-Drive Mechanism (Mechanical Coupling)

The pen-drive mechanism converts the rotational motion of the servo motor shaft into linear motion of the pen.

**Common Mechanisms:**

| Mechanism | Working Principle | Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Lead-screw** | Rotating screw translates carriage | High accuracy, limited speed |
| **Rack-and-pinion** | Pinion gear engages linear rack | Higher speed, less precision |
| **Capstan-belt** | Belt driven by motor pulley | Smooth, lower backlash |
| **Linear servo** | Direct linear motor (rare) | Highest speed, expensive |

The pen is mounted on a **carriage** that rides on precision guide rails. The X-axis servo moves the carriage horizontally, while the Y-axis servo moves the pen vertically along the carriage.

## 2.5 The Slide-Wire Potentiometer (Linear Pot)

> [!NOTE]
> **Slide-Wire (Linear Potentiometer):** A precision resistance element with a uniform voltage gradient. The wiper is mechanically tied to the pen. As the pen moves, the wiper samples a position-dependent fraction of the reference voltage. This voltage is the **negative feedback** to the comparator.

The slide-wire is a **non-inductively wound** resistance wire of high linearity (typically $\pm 0.1\%$) stretched between two terminals across a stable reference voltage. Its length equals the chart width (e.g., 250 mm for an A4 recorder).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Symbol | Description | Units / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = V_{\text{in}}(t) - V_{\text{fb}}(t)$ | Error signal at comparator | **Volts (V)** |
| 2 | $V_{\text{fb}}(x) = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Feedback voltage vs. pen position $x$ | Volts |
| 3 | $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = K_m \cdot e_{\text{amp}}$ | Servo motor speed proportional to amplified error | rad/s |
| 4 | $x(t) = \int \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} \cdot r \, dt$ | Pen position (linear) from motor angle | **mm** or **cm** |
| 5 | $T_s \approx \dfrac{1}{2 \zeta \omega_n}$ | Settling time (2% band) for 2nd-order servo | **seconds** |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\bigg\vert_{\text{chart}}$ | Chart sensitivity (slope of plotted line) | unitless |
| 7 | $V_{\text{fs}} = \text{Full-scale range}$ | Selected full-scale input range | **V** |
| 8 | $f_{-3\text{dB}} = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ | Servo bandwidth ($-3$ dB point) | **Hz** |
| 9 | $\zeta$ | Damping ratio (typically $0.6$‚Äď$0.8$ for critical damping) | unitless |
| 10 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{\text{fs}}}{N_{\text{bits}}}$ | For digital X-Y recorders | **V/bit** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary / Critical Conditions for Servo Stability:**
> - For **critically damped** response: $\zeta = 1$ (no overshoot, fastest non-oscillatory response).
> - For **optimal plot quality** (no ringing): $\zeta \approx 0.7$ (slight under-damping).
> - For **stability**, the open-loop gain must be such that the closed-loop system has all poles in the **left-half s-plane** (Routh-Hurwitz criterion).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Where X-Y Recorders are Used in Industry & Labs:**
> 1. **B-H Curve tracing** for magnetic materials (H on X-axis, B on Y-axis).
> 2. **Transistor characteristic curves** ($I_C$ vs. $V_{CE}$, with $I_B$ stepped).
> 3. **Diode & Zener diode V-I characteristics**.
> 4. **Antenna radiation pattern plotting** (signal strength vs. angular position).
> 5. **Stress-Strain curves** in materials testing (load vs. elongation).
> 6. **Frequency response plots** of filters and amplifiers (gain vs. frequency).
> 7. **Pressure-Volume (P-V) diagrams** of thermodynamic cycles (e.g., Otto, Diesel).
> 8. **Lissajous figures** for phase measurement of two AC signals.
> 9. **Thermocouple calibration curves**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Pen Position

### 3.1.1 Setup and Notation

Let the X-axis input voltage be $V_x(t)$ and the Y-axis input voltage be $V_y(t)$. Each input is connected to its respective **null-balance servo loop**.

**Define:**
- $x(t)$ = horizontal pen position (in cm, measured from chart origin)
- $y(t)$ = vertical pen position (in cm, measured from chart origin)
- $L_x$ = full-scale horizontal chart length
- $L_y$ = full-scale vertical chart length
- $V_{\text{ref},x}$ = reference voltage across the X slide-wire
- $V_{\text{ref},y}$ = reference voltage across the Y slide-wire

### 3.1.2 Feedback Voltage from Slide-Wire

The slide-wire is a uniform linear potentiometer. The voltage tapped at the wiper is linearly proportional to the pen displacement.

For the X-axis:

$$V_{\text{fb},x}(t) = V_{\text{ref},x} \cdot \frac{x(t)}{L_x}$$

For the Y-axis:

$$V_{\text{fb},y}(t) = V_{\text{ref},y} \cdot \frac{y(t)}{L_y}$$

This is because the wiper divides the resistance in the same ratio as the pen divides the mechanical length of the slide-wire.

### 3.1.3 Error Signal Generation

The error signal is the difference between the input voltage and the feedback voltage.

$$e_x(t) = V_x(t) - V_{\text{fb},x}(t) = V_x(t) - V_{\text{ref},x} \cdot \frac{x(t)}{L_x}$$

$$e_y(t) = V_y(t) - V_{\text{fb},y}(t) = V_y(t) - V_{\text{ref},y} \cdot \frac{y(t)}{L_y}$$

### 3.1.4 Amplification and Motor Drive

The error is amplified by a gain $A$ (very high, in the range of $10^4$‚Äď$10^6$). The amplified error drives a two-phase servo motor whose angular velocity is proportional to the amplified error:

$$\frac{d\theta_x}{dt} = K_m \cdot A \cdot e_x(t) = K_m \cdot A \cdot \left[V_x(t) - V_{\text{ref},x} \cdot \frac{x(t)}{L_x}\right]$$

where $K_m$ is the motor's torque-speed constant (in rad/V¬∑s).

### 3.1.5 Mechanical Coupling (Linear Position from Angular Position)

The motor shaft drives the pen carriage through a gear train with effective radius $r$ (or lead-screw pitch). The linear pen velocity is:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{r \cdot d\theta_x}{dt}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = r \cdot K_m \cdot A \cdot \left[V_x(t) - V_{\text{ref},x} \cdot \frac{x(t)}{L_x}\right]$$

### 3.1.6 Steady-State Solution

At **steady state** (i.e., the pen has reached equilibrium, the error is zero, and the motor is stationary), we have:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = 0$$

This implies the bracketed term must equal zero:

$$V_x(t) - V_{\text{ref},x} \cdot \frac{x_{\text{ss}}}{L_x} = 0$$

Solving for the steady-state pen position $x_{\text{ss}}$:

$$V_x(t) = V_{\text{ref},x} \cdot \frac{x_{\text{ss}}}{L_x}$$

$$x_{\text{ss}} = L_x \cdot \frac{V_x(t)}{V_{\text{ref},x}}$$

This proves that the **pen position is directly proportional to the input voltage** ‚ÄĒ confirming the linear plotting action.

### 3.1.7 Final Plotted Relationship

Combining both axes:

$$x(t) = L_x \cdot \frac{V_x(t)}{V_{\text{ref},x}}, \quad y(t) = L_y \cdot \frac{V_y(t)}{V_{\text{ref},y}}$$

If we eliminate $t$ between $x$ and $y$ (i.e., if $V_y$ is functionally related to $V_x$), the trace on the chart represents the functional curve $y = f(x)$ in **Cartesian form**.

## 3.2 Transfer Function and Dynamic Analysis

### 3.2.1 Linearized System Equation

Define the closed-loop gain $G = A \cdot K_m \cdot r$ (with units of $\text{s}^{-1}\text{V}^{-1}$). The servo equation becomes:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} + G \cdot \frac{V_{\text{ref},x}}{L_x} \cdot x(t) = G \cdot V_x(t)$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE**. The time constant is:

$$\tau_x = \frac{L_x}{G \cdot V_{\text{ref},x}}$$

### 3.2.2 Step Response

For a step input $V_x(t) = V_0 \cdot u(t)$ (where $u(t)$ is the unit step), the pen position evolves as:

$$x(t) = x_{\text{ss}} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_x}\right)$$

with $x_{\text{ss}} = L_x \cdot V_0 / V_{\text{ref},x}$.

The pen reaches **63.2%** of its final position in one time constant $\tau_x$ and **98.2%** in $4\tau_x$ (practical settling time).

### 3.2.3 Second-Order Refinement

In practice, the servo has inertia and damping. A more realistic second-order model is:

$$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n \frac{dx}{dt} + \omega_n^2 x = \omega_n^2 \cdot L_x \cdot \frac{V_x(t)}{V_{\text{ref},x}}$$

For a step input, the response is:

$$x(t) = x_{\text{ss}} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \sin\left(\omega_d t + \phi\right)\right]$$

where:
- $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ ‚ÄĒ **damped natural frequency**
- $\phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$ ‚ÄĒ **phase angle**

**KTU Examiner's Note:** The damping ratio $\zeta$ is critical. For an X-Y recorder to plot cleanly without oscillation:
- $\zeta < 0.5$: Pen oscillates around the true position (over-plotted, fuzzy traces)
- $\zeta \approx 0.7$: Optimal ‚ÄĒ slight overshoot, fast settling
- $\zeta > 1$: Sluggish, slow response (no oscillation, but pen lags signal)

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An X-Y recorder is used to plot a B-H curve. The X-axis full-scale range is set to **$V_{\text{fs},x} = 5 \text{ V}$** corresponding to a chart width of **$L_x = 25 \text{ cm}$**. The slide-wire reference voltage is **$V_{\text{ref},x} = 5.0 \text{ V}$**.

If the magnetic field strength signal (H) provides an X-input of $V_x = 2.5$ V, find the pen's horizontal position on the chart.

### Solution:

Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the relevant equation (derived above):

$$x_{\text{ss}} = L_x \cdot \frac{V_x}{V_{\text{ref},x}}$$

Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute the values:

$$x_{\text{ss}} = 25 \text{ cm} \cdot \frac{2.5 \text{ V}}{5.0 \text{ V}}$$

Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Simplify:

$$x_{\text{ss}} = 25 \text{ cm} \cdot 0.5 = 12.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Result:** The pen is positioned at **$x = 12.5$ cm** from the left edge of the chart, which is exactly at the **midpoint** of the chart width. This makes physical sense, as the input voltage is exactly half of the full-scale range.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** If $V_x = 5$ V (full-scale), $x = 25$ cm (right edge). If $V_x = 0$ V, $x = 0$ cm (left edge). Linear scaling confirmed.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: X-Y Recorder vs Y-T Strip Chart

| Parameter | X-Y Recorder | Y-T Strip-Chart Recorder |
|---|---|---|
| Independent variables | **Two** (X, Y) | **One** (Y vs. time) |
| Chart movement | None (stationary) | **Uniform** at fixed speed |
| Pen drives | **Two servos** (X, Y) | **One servo** (Y) + chart motor |
| Use case | $y = f(x)$ functional plots | Time-domain waveforms |
| Frequency response | DC to ~5 Hz (typical) | DC to ~10 Hz (depends on chart speed) |
| Cost | Higher (dual servo) | Lower (single servo) |
| Maintenance | Complex (two servo systems) | Simpler |
| Example use | Transistor V-I curves | ECG, EEG, seismic signals |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an X-Y Recorder

The X-Y recorder contains two parallel signal-processing channels. The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete signal and mechanical-feedback flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph XAXIS["X AXIS CHANNEL"]
        XIN["X Input Signal Vx"] --> XATT["X Attenuator and Range Selector"]
        XATT --> XCHOP["X Chopper Modulator"]
        XCHOP --> XAMP["X AC Amplifier High Gain"]
        XAMP --> XDEM["X Phase Sensitive Demodulator"]
        XDEM --> XDC["X DC Power Amplifier"]
        XDC --> XMOT["X Servo Motor Two Phase"]
        XMOT --> XGEAR["X Gear Train"]
        XGEAR --> XCAR["X Pen Carriage Horizontal Motion"]
        XCAR --> XPEN["Pen Position Update X"]
        XCAR -. Mechanical Link .-> XSW["X Slide Wire Potentiometer"]
        XSW -. Feedback Voltage .-> XERR["X Error Comparator"]
        XIN --> XERR
        XERR --> XCHOP
    end

    subgraph YAXIS["Y AXIS CHANNEL"]
        YIN["Y Input Signal Vy"] --> YATT["Y Attenuator and Range Selector"]
        YATT --> YCHOP["Y Chopper Modulator"]
        YCHOP --> YAMP["Y AC Amplifier High Gain"]
        YAMP --> YDEM["Y Phase Sensitive Demodulator"]
        YDEM --> YDC["Y DC Power Amplifier"]
        YDC --> YMOT["Y Servo Motor Two Phase"]
        YMOT --> YGEAR["Y Gear Train"]
        YGEAR --> YPEN["Y Pen Vertical Motion"]
        YPEN --> PENPOS["Pen Position Update Y"]
        YPEN -. Mechanical Link .-> YSW["Y Slide Wire Potentiometer"]
        YSW -. Feedback Voltage .-> YERR["Y Error Comparator"]
        YIN --> YERR
        YERR --> YCHOP
    end

    XCAR --> CHART["Stationary Chart Paper"]
    YPEN --> CHART

    XPEN --> FINAL["Final Traced Curve y = f of x"]
    PENPOS --> FINAL
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Servo Control Block Diagram

The following block diagram represents **one axis** (X or Y) of the recorder as a closed-loop control system ‚ÄĒ this is the standard form required for KTU control-system analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Input V in"] --> SUM[("Summing Junction")]
    SUM --> CHOP["Chopper Modulator"]
    CHOP --> ACAMP["AC Amplifier Gain A1"]
    ACAMP --> DEM["Demodulator"]
    DEM --> DCAMP["DC Power Amplifier Gain A2"]
    DCAMP --> MOTOR["Servo Motor Km"]
    MOTOR --> GEAR["Gear Train r"]
    GEAR --> PEN["Pen Position x"]
    PEN --> CHART2["Chart Paper Trace"]
    PEN -. Position Feedback .-> SLIDE["Slide Wire Vfb equals Vref times x over L"]
    SLIDE --> SUM
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        I1["Vx Input Terminal"]
        I2["Vy Input Terminal"]
        I3["Range Selector Switches"]
        I4["Input Attenuator Network"]
    end

    subgraph ELECTRONICS["ELECTRONIC SUBSYSTEM"]
        E1["Chopper Circuit"]
        E2["AC Amplifier Stage"]
        E3["Demodulator"]
        E4["DC Power Driver"]
    end

    subgraph MECHANICAL["MECHANICAL SUBSYSTEM"]
        M1["Two-Phase Servo Motor X"]
        M2["Two-Phase Servo Motor Y"]
        M3["X Gear Train"]
        M4["Y Gear Train"]
        M5["Carriage Guide Rails"]
        M6["Pen Holder"]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK["FEEDBACK SUBSYSTEM"]
        F1["X Slide-Wire Potentiometer"]
        F2["Y Slide-Wire Potentiometer"]
        F3["Reference Voltage Source"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        O1["Stationary Chart Paper"]
        O2["Pen Ink Cartridge"]
        O3["Plotted Curve Display"]
    end

    I1 --> I3 --> I4 --> E1
    I2 --> I3
    E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4
    E4 --> M1 --> M3 --> M5 --> M6
    E4 --> M2 --> M4 --> M6
    M3 -. drives .-> F1
    M4 -. drives .-> F2
    F3 --> F1
    F3 --> F2
    F1 -. feedback .-> E1
    F2 -. feedback .-> E1
    M6 --> O1
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> M6
```

## 4.4 Signal Waveform Timing Diagram (Chopper Action)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DC Input Signal Vin"] --> B["Chopper Switch"]
    C["Chopping Frequency fc 50 Hz Square Wave"] --> B
    B --> D["Modulated AC Square Wave"]
    D --> E["AC Amplifier with Transformer Coupling"]
    E --> F["Amplified AC Signal"]
    F --> G["Phase Sensitive Demodulator"]
    C --> G
    G --> H["DC Output V out with Correct Polarity"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The DC input is "chopped" into an AC square wave by the chopper (typically a photomodulator or field-effect transistor driven at 50/60 Hz). The transformer-coupled AC amplifier provides high gain with no DC drift. The phase-sensitive demodulator recovers the DC signal with the correct polarity, preserving the sign of the input.

---
