# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**Define an X-Y recorder. Mention two applications.**

**Model Answer:**

An X-Y recorder is a two-input analog recording instrument that plots the functional relationship between two independent variables on a stationary chart paper, with one variable applied to the X-axis input and the other to the Y-axis input. The pen is driven by two independent servo motors ‚ÄĒ one for horizontal motion and one for vertical motion ‚ÄĒ based on the null-balance principle.

**Two Applications:**
1. Plotting the B-H curve of a ferromagnetic material (H on X-axis, B on Y-axis).
2. Plotting the V-I characteristics of a semiconductor diode or transistor.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for two applications)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`
**Why is a chopper used in the X-Y recorder? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

A chopper is used in an X-Y recorder to **eliminate DC drift** in the high-gain DC amplifier stage. The DC input signal is first converted to an AC square wave (typically 50 Hz) by the chopper. The AC-coupled amplifier has no DC drift. After amplification, a phase-sensitive demodulator converts the signal back to DC. This technique allows the recorder to detect microvolt-level signals accurately, which is essential for plotting high-resolution curves such as B-H loops.

*(3 marks: 1 for the drift problem, 1 for the chopper mechanism, 1 for the demodulator reconstruction)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A ‚ÄĒ Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an X-Y recorder. What is the function of the slide-wire potentiometer in this instrument? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An X-Y recorder is used to plot a pressure-volume (P-V) diagram of a thermodynamic cycle. The X-axis full-scale range is set to 10 V corresponding to a chart width of 20 cm. The Y-axis full-scale range is 8 V corresponding to 20 cm chart height. At a particular instant, the pressure signal is 4 V and the volume signal is 6 V. Determine the pen position in cm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Block Diagram and Working:**

The X-Y recorder consists of two parallel channels ‚ÄĒ an X-axis channel and a Y-axis channel. Each channel contains:

1. **Input Attenuator** ‚ÄĒ Selects the input range (e.g., 0.5 mV/cm, 1 V/cm, etc.).
2. **Chopper** ‚ÄĒ Converts DC to AC for drift-free amplification.
3. **AC Amplifier** ‚ÄĒ Provides high gain without drift.
4. **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator** ‚ÄĒ Recovers DC signal with correct polarity.
5. **DC Power Amplifier** ‚ÄĒ Drives the servo motor.
6. **Two-Phase Servo Motor** ‚ÄĒ Mechanical actuator.
7. **Gear Train** ‚ÄĒ Reduces speed, increases torque.
8. **Slide-Wire Potentiometer** ‚ÄĒ Provides negative feedback.
9. **Pen Drive Mechanism** ‚ÄĒ Translates servo rotation to linear pen motion.

**Working Principle (Null Balance):**

The input voltage $V_x$ is compared with the feedback voltage $V_{\text{fb},x}$ tapped from the slide-wire. The error $e_x = V_x - V_{\text{fb},x}$ is amplified and used to drive the servo motor. The motor moves the pen (and the slide-wire wiper) until $V_{\text{fb},x} = V_x$, at which point the error is zero and the motor stops. The pen's position is therefore directly proportional to the input voltage.

**Function of Slide-Wire Potentiometer:**

The slide-wire (linear potentiometer) is a precision resistance element with a uniform voltage gradient. Its wiper is mechanically coupled to the pen. As the pen moves, the wiper samples a position-dependent voltage from the slide-wire. This voltage serves as **negative mechanical feedback** to the comparator, enabling the null-balance action. Without it, the system would have no way to know the pen's position, and stable operation would be impossible.

*(7 marks: 2 for block diagram description, 3 for working principle, 2 for slide-wire function)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- X-axis full-scale voltage: $V_{\text{fs},x} = 10$ V
- X-axis chart width: $L_x = 20$ cm
- Y-axis full-scale voltage: $V_{\text{fs},y} = 8$ V
- Y-axis chart height: $L_y = 20$ cm
- Pressure signal (Y-input): $V_y = 4$ V
- Volume signal (X-input): $V_x = 6$ V

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute the X-axis pen position:**

$$x = L_x \cdot \frac{V_x}{V_{\text{fs},x}} = 20 \text{ cm} \cdot \frac{6 \text{ V}}{10 \text{ V}}$$

$$x = 20 \cdot 0.6 = 12.0 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute the Y-axis pen position:**

$$y = L_y \cdot \frac{V_y}{V_{\text{fs},y}} = 20 \text{ cm} \cdot \frac{4 \text{ V}}{8 \text{ V}}$$

$$y = 20 \cdot 0.5 = 10.0 \text{ cm}$$

**Result:** The pen is positioned at $(x, y) = (12.0 \text{ cm}, 10.0 \text{ cm})$ from the chart origin.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for x-computation, 2 for y-computation, 1 for final answer with units)*

---

### Question B ‚ÄĒ Choice 2 (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the working of a chopper-stabilized DC amplifier in an X-Y recorder. Why is it preferred over a direct-coupled DC amplifier? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the X-Y recorder with a strip-chart recorder in terms of chart motion, inputs, axes, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Working of Chopper-Stabilized DC Amplifier:**

The chopper-stabilized amplifier overcomes the **drift problem** of direct-coupled DC amplifiers. The arrangement is as follows:

1. **Chopper (Modulator):** A switch (electromechanical, photomodulator, or FET) chops the DC input signal into an AC square wave at a fixed frequency (typically 50 Hz, locked to the power line).

2. **AC Amplifier:** The chopped AC signal is amplified by an AC-coupled amplifier. Since this amplifier is AC-coupled, it has no DC drift due to temperature, supply variations, or transistor leakage.

3. **Demodulator:** A phase-sensitive demodulator (synchronously driven by the same chopper frequency) rectifies the amplified AC back to DC. The phase relationship preserves the polarity of the original signal.

4. **Low-Pass Filter:** A filter removes the residual chopper ripple from the DC output.

**Why Preferred Over Direct-Coupled DC Amplifier:**

- **Drift-free operation:** AC amplifier has no DC offset drift.
- **Higher sensitivity:** Microvolt-level signals can be amplified to volts with accuracy.
- **Better stability:** No thermal drift, no 1/f noise accumulation.
- **Suitable for slow-varying DC signals:** B-H curves, thermocouple outputs, etc.

*(7 marks: 4 for chopper operation, 3 for advantages over direct-coupled DC amplifier)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

| Parameter | X-Y Recorder | Strip-Chart Recorder |
|---|---|---|
| **Chart motion** | Stationary; only the pen moves in X and Y | Chart moves at constant speed along one axis (time) |
| **Number of inputs** | Two independent inputs (X and Y) | One input (Y) versus time |
| **Axes plotted** | X = any physical variable, Y = any physical variable | X = time, Y = input signal |
| **Pen drives** | Two servo motors (X-axis and Y-axis) | One servo motor (Y); X driven by chart motor |
| **Typical applications** | B-H curves, transistor V-I characteristics, P-V diagrams, Lissajous figures | ECG, EEG, temperature log, seismic traces, pressure trends |

**Key Distinction:** The X-Y recorder is suited for **functional relationships** ($y = f(x)$), while the strip-chart recorder is suited for **time-domain waveforms**.

*(7 marks: 4 for table, 2 for key distinction, 1 for example comparison)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "X-Y" with "two pens"** ‚ÄĒ An X-Y recorder has **only ONE pen**, not two. Students frequently lose 1‚Äď2 marks by stating otherwise.
> 2. **Forgetting the chart is stationary** ‚ÄĒ The paper **does not move**. A common error is to compare with a Y-t chart that has a moving paper.
> 3. **Skipping the null-balance principle** ‚ÄĒ A 7-mark question on working principle MUST mention **null-balance**, **error signal**, **slide-wire feedback**, and **servo motor** to get full marks.
> 4. **Wrong units in numerical problems** ‚ÄĒ Pen position is in **cm** (or mm), input voltage is in **V**. Forgetting units loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing chopper frequency with signal frequency** ‚ÄĒ The chopper is a fixed internal oscillator (50 Hz typical), **not** related to the input signal frequency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **ūüĒĎ High-Yield Rapid Revision Points ‚ÄĒ X-Y Recorder**

### Core Definition
- [ ] An X-Y recorder plots **two independent variables** on a **stationary Cartesian chart**.
- [ ] It has **one pen**, **two servo motors** (X-axis and Y-axis), and **two slide-wire feedback potentiometers**.
- [ ] It works on the **null-balance (servo) principle** for each axis independently.

### Block Diagram Components
- [ ] Input attenuator ‚Üí **Chopper** ‚Üí AC amplifier ‚Üí **Demodulator** ‚Üí DC power amp ‚Üí **Servo motor** ‚Üí Gear train ‚Üí **Slide-wire feedback** ‚Üí Pen.
- [ ] The slide-wire provides the **mechanical negative feedback** that makes the system self-balancing.

### Why Chopper?
- [ ] Direct-coupled DC amplifiers have **drift** (temperature, leakage).
- [ ] Chopper converts DC ‚Üí AC, allows AC-coupled amplification (drift-free), then AC ‚Üí DC via **phase-sensitive demodulator**.
- [ ] Chopper frequency: typically **50 Hz or 60 Hz** (or 400 Hz in aircraft).

### Key Formula (must memorize)
- [ ] Steady-state pen position: $x_{\text{ss}} = L_x \cdot \dfrac{V_x}{V_{\text{ref},x}}$ (and similarly for $y$).
- [ ] Damping ratio: $\zeta \approx 0.7$ for clean plotting (no ringing, fast response).

### Comparison
- [ ] X-Y vs. Y-T: **chart motion (stationary vs. moving)**, **inputs (two vs. one)**, **axes (functional vs. time)**.

### Applications
- [ ] B-H curves, transistor/diode V-I curves, P-V diagrams, antenna radiation patterns, Lissajous figures, stress-strain, frequency response plots.

### Common Errors to Avoid
- [ ] One pen (not two).
- [ ] Stationary paper (not moving).
- [ ] Null-balance principle is essential for full marks in 7-mark questions.
- [ ] Slide-wire potentiometer is the **feedback element**, not the input.

### Critical Numbers to Remember
- [ ] Slide-wire linearity: $\pm 0.1\%$
- [ ] Servo gain $A$: $10^4$‚Äď$10^6$
- [ ] Pen slew rate: ~1000 mm/s
- [ ] Chart sizes: 8.5"√ó11" or A4 (210√ó297 mm)
- [ ] Standard chopping frequency: 50 Hz (or 60 Hz in US)

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plotter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLOTTER (Recording Instrument):**
> A *plotter* is an output recording device that produces a **permanent, continuous graphical trace** of one or more input electrical signals as a function of another reference variable (typically time, or a second signal). It is classified as a *passive transducer* (energy-modulating display transducer) under the KTU 2024 framework of signal-conditioning output devices.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PCBRT403 ‚Äď Electrical and Electronic Instrumentation*, the term **plotter** specifically refers to *graphic recording instruments* that translate analog/digital input signals into two-dimensional hard-copy plots. The two principal sub-classes are:

1. **Strip-Chart (Y-t) Plotter / Recorder** ‚Äď plots one signal (Y) vs. time (t) on a moving paper strip.
2. **X-Y Plotter / Recorder** ‚Äď plots one signal (Y) against a *non-time* second signal (X) on a stationary sheet.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy ‚Äď "The Robot Artist"**
> Imagine a small robotic arm holding a pen. As you talk into a microphone, the arm moves left-right (X-axis) based on one signal, and up-down (Y-axis) based on another signal. The pen draws a continuous line on a sheet of paper, leaving a *permanent record* of how the two signals relate. **That robot is a plotter.** Unlike an oscilloscope (which only shows a temporary, fading image on a screen), the plotter's output is *durable* ‚ÄĒ you can file it, mail it, and analyze it weeks later.

In even simpler terms: a **plotter = an automatic, motorised draftsman** that draws graphs of electrical signals on paper.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Specifications (Industry/ANSI/IEC):**
> - **Static Accuracy** = ¬Ī **0.25 %** of full scale (typical Class 1 plotters)
> - **Dynamic Accuracy** = ¬Ī **2 %** of full scale at rated frequency
> - **Standard Chart Widths** = **250 mm (10‚Ä≥)** or **200 mm (8‚Ä≥)** ‚ÄĒ strip chart
> - **Standard Paper Sizes** = A4, A3, 8.5‚Ä≥ √ó 11‚Ä≥ ‚ÄĒ X-Y plotters
> - **Writing Speed** = **10 cm/s to 100 cm/s** (modern thermal plotters)
> - **Input Impedance** = **1 Mő©** (analog input channels)
> - **Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** ‚Č• **120 dB** at 50/60 Hz
> - **Dead-band (hysteresis)** ‚Č§ **0.1 %** of full scale
> - **Operating Temperature** = **0 ¬įC to 50 ¬įC** (industrial grade)

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need plotters?**
> An oscilloscope shows you a trace that **vanishes** in milliseconds. A digital storage scope can *retain* a trace, but only inside volatile memory. A **plotter gives you a *tangible artefact*** ‚ÄĒ a hard-copy record on paper that survives power loss, archiving, and regulatory audits (e.g., medical ECG reports, seismograph records, aerospace telemetry).

A plotter is essentially a **two-input, two-axis positioning system**:

#### Block-Level Operating Chain (X-Y Plotter)

1. **Input Stage** ‚Äď Two high-impedance differential amplifiers receive signals $V_x(t)$ and $V_y(t)$.
2. **Attenuator / Range Selector** ‚Äď Switched resistor network scales the input to match the drive-amplifier's full-scale range (typically **¬Ī 5 V** or **¬Ī 10 V**).
3. **Reference Voltage Source** ‚Äď A precision zener-derived reference ($V_{ref}$ = **+10.000 V DC**, TC ‚Č§ **5 ppm/¬įC**) supplies the null-balance comparison.
4. **Comparator / Error Detector** ‚Äď A precision difference amplifier computes the *error*:
   $$e(t) \;=\; V_{in}(t) \;-\; V_{fb}(t)$$
5. **Power Amplifier (Servo Drive)** ‚Äď Amplifies the error to drive a **DC servo motor** on each axis.
6. **DC Servo Motor + Gear Train** ‚Äď Rotates the lead-screw that physically translates the pen carriage (X-axis) or the paper (Y-axis).
7. **Feedback Potentiometer (Tapered Wire-Wound)** ‚Äď Mechanically coupled to the motor shaft; its wiper voltage $V_{fb}$ is the *position* signal.
8. **Writing Mechanism** ‚Äď Pen, thermal stylus, or ink-jet nozzle deposits the trace on paper.
9. **Chart Drive (Strip-Chart) / Paper Hold-Down (X-Y)** ‚Äď For Y-t recorders, a stepper-motor-driven sprocket pulls the paper at selectable speeds (1, 5, 25, 100 mm/s, etc.).

> **The "Why" behind the null-balance design:** By forcing $V_{fb} \to V_{in}$ through high-gain feedback, the *non-linearity* of the motor, gear-train, and lead-screw is *divided by the loop gain*. This is why servo plotters achieve **¬Ī0.25 %** accuracy ‚ÄĒ far better than open-loop galvanometric plotters (¬Ī2‚Äď5 %).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every** formula you must memorise for a Plotter question in the KTU 2024 ESE. **All symbols are defined below the table.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | $e(t) = V_{in}(t) - V_{fb}(t)$ | Error signal in servo loop | V | $V$ |
| 2 | $V_{fb}(t) = K_p \cdot \theta(t)$ | Feedback pot voltage from shaft angle | V | $\theta$ in rad |
| 3 | $T_m = K_t \cdot i_a(t)$ | Motor torque from armature current | N¬∑m | $K_t$ in N¬∑m/A |
| 4 | $e_a = K_b \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Back-EMF of DC motor | V | $K_b$ in V¬∑s/rad |
| 5 | $V_a = R_a i_a + L_a \dfrac{di_a}{dt} + e_a$ | Armature voltage equation | V | KVL on armature |
| 6 | $J\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + T_L = T_m$ | Mechanical torque equation | N¬∑m | $J$ in kg¬∑m¬≤ |
| 7 | $G(s) = \dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_b K_t}$ | Open-loop TF of motor | rad/V | 3rd-order |
| 8 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_t K_a}{J L_a}}$ | Natural frequency (simplified) | rad/s | With amp gain $K_a$ |
| 9 | $\zeta = \dfrac{R_a J + B L_a}{2\sqrt{K_t K_a J L_a}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless | Critical: $\zeta = 1$ |
| 10 | $f_{3dB} \approx 0.707 \cdot \omega_n$ | ‚ąí3 dB bandwidth | Hz | For 2nd-order approx |
| 11 | $v_{pen,max} = \dfrac{e_{max}}{K_p}$ | Max pen speed | m/s | $e_{max}$ in V |
| 12 | $A_{closed} = \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$ | Closed-loop gain (servo) | ratio | $\beta$ feedback factor |
| 13 | $\% \text{Reg} = \dfrac{A_{OL}^{-1}}{1 + A_{OL}^{-1}} \times 100$ | Percent regulation | % | Small-signal |
| 14 | $\text{Static Accuracy} = \pm \dfrac{1}{1 + A_{OL}} \times 100$ | Static error % | % | With unity feedback |
| 15 | $f_{max,trace} = \dfrac{v_{pen,max}}{2\pi A}$ | Max traceable sine freq. | Hz | $A$ = peak amplitude |

> **Where:**
> $K_p$ = potentiometer sensitivity (V/rad),
> $K_t$ = motor torque constant (N¬∑m/A),
> $K_b$ = back-EMF constant (V¬∑s/rad),
> $J$ = moment of inertia (kg¬∑m¬≤),
> $B$ = viscous friction (N¬∑m¬∑s/rad),
> $R_a, L_a$ = armature resistance/inductance,
> $T_L$ = load torque,
> $A_{OL}$ = open-loop amplifier gain,
> $\beta$ = feedback factor.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Medical:** ECG, EEG, EMG machines use *thermal array plotters* (3-channel, 25 mm/s paper speed).
> - **Aerospace:** Flight-data recorders print altitude vs. Mach on *X-Y plotters*.
> - **Geophysics:** Seismographs use *helical-strip ink plotters* (7-day continuous trace).
> - **Power Engineering:** *B-H curve tracers* use X-Y plotters to display magnetic hysteresis loops of transformer cores.
> - **Process Industries:** Strip-chart recorders log temperature/pressure trends in chemical plants.
> - **Metallurgy:** Stress‚Äďstrain curves are plotted on X-Y plotters during tensile testing.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ Closed-Loop Transfer Function of an X-Y Servo Plotter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark question):**
> *"Derive the closed-loop transfer function relating the pen position $X(s)$ to the input signal $V_x(s)$ for the X-axis servo of an X-Y plotter, and show how the static accuracy depends on the open-loop gain."*

#### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the forward path

The forward path contains: an **error amplifier** of gain $A$ (V/V), a **power amplifier** of gain $K_a$ (V/V), and a **DC motor‚Äďpotentiometer** assembly. From the formula sheet (row 7), the motor transfer function is:

$$
G_m(s) \;=\; \dfrac{K_t / (R_a B)}{s\,\tau_m \tau_e \, s^2 + (\tau_m + \tau_e)s + 1}
$$

where the **mechanical time constant** is $\tau_m = J / B$ and the **electrical time constant** is $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$.

#### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Total forward-path transfer function

$$
G(s) \;=\; A \cdot K_a \cdot G_m(s) \cdot K_p
$$

$$
G(s) \;=\; \underbrace{A K_a K_p K_t / R_a B}_{K_{OL}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{s(\tau_m \tau_e \, s^2 + (\tau_m + \tau_e)s + 1)}
$$

The first factor is the **DC open-loop gain** $K_{OL}$ (V ‚Üí V in the position loop). For a unity-feedback system ($\beta = 1$, i.e. $V_{fb}$ is the same scale as $V_{in}$):

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{V_x(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}
$$

#### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Final closed-loop position transfer function

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{X(s)}{V_x(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{K_{OL}}{s(\tau_m \tau_e s^2 + (\tau_m + \tau_e)s + 1) + K_{OL}}\;}
$$

#### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Static accuracy (final-value theorem)

For a step input $V_x(s) = V_0/s$, the *steady-state* position is found by $\lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot X(s)$:

$$
X_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{K_{OL} \cdot V_0}{s(\tau_m \tau_e s^2 + (\tau_m + \tau_e)s + 1) + K_{OL}}
$$

$$
X_{ss} \;=\; \dfrac{K_{OL} \cdot V_0}{K_{OL}} \;=\; V_0
$$

But we want the **error** $e_{ss}$:

$$
e_{ss} \;=\; V_0 - X_{ss} \cdot \dfrac{1}{K_p} \quad \text{(in input-voltage units)}
$$

For a position servo, the *per-unit static error* (dimensionless) is:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{e_{ss}}{V_0} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 + K_{OL}}\;}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** If $K_{OL} = 10{,}000$, then the static error is $1/10{,}001 \approx 0.01\,\%$ ‚ÄĒ this is *why* servo plotters are accurate. Increasing $K_{OL}$ by **a factor of 10** divides the static error by **a factor of 10**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Maximum Traceable Sine Frequency of the Pen

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A galvanometric strip-chart plotter has a full-scale pen deflection $A$ = ¬Ī 5 cm and a maximum pen velocity $v_{pen,max}$ = 0.5 m/s. What is the highest-frequency sine wave it can plot without slew-rate limiting?

For a sinusoid $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$:

$$
v(t) \;=\; \dfrac{dx}{dt} \;=\; 2\pi f A \cos(2\pi f t)
$$

The peak velocity is $v_{peak} = 2\pi f A$. Slew-rate limiting begins when $v_{peak} = v_{pen,max}$:

$$
2\pi f_{max} A \;=\; v_{pen,max}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{v_{pen,max}}{2\pi A}\;}
$$

#### Numerical substitution

$$
f_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5 \text{ m/s}}{2 \pi \times 0.05 \text{ m}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5}{0.3142} \;\approx\; 1.59 \text{ Hz}
$$

> **Conclusion:** This plotter can faithfully plot sine waves up to **‚Čą 1.6 Hz** at full-scale amplitude. For higher frequencies, the trace becomes triangular (slew-limited) ‚ÄĒ a common source of KTU viva questions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚ÄĒ Damping Ratio of the Plotter Pen Mechanism

The pen carriage has inertia $J = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ kg¬∑m¬≤, viscous friction $B = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ N¬∑m¬∑s/rad, motor torque constant $K_t = 0.05$ N¬∑m/A, amplifier gain $K_a = 100$ V/V, armature resistance $R_a = 10\;\Omega$, and inductance $L_a = 0.01$ H. Find $\zeta$.

Using formula sheet (row 9):

$$
\zeta \;=\; \dfrac{R_a J + B L_a}{2\sqrt{K_t K_a J L_a}}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} \;=\; (10)(5 \times 10^{-6}) + (10^{-4})(10^{-2}) \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 5.1 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} \;=\; 2 \sqrt{(0.05)(100)(5 \times 10^{-6})(0.01)} \;=\; 2\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} \;=\; 2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 1 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\zeta \;=\; \dfrac{5.1 \times 10^{-5}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.051\;}
$$

> **Conclusion:** $\zeta = 0.051 \ll 1$ ‚áí the system is **severely underdamped**. At full-scale step input, the pen will ring (oscillate) at $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi)$ before settling. Compensation (lead network) is required for KTU-grade plotters.

---

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ X-Y Servo Plotter (Full Closed-Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputX["Vx Input Signal"] --> attnX["X Attenuator / Range"]
    inputY["Vy Input Signal"] --> attnY["Y Attenuator / Range"]
    attnX --> sumX(("+"))
    vrefX["Precision Vref"] --> sumX
    sumX --> errAmpX["Error Amplifier Gain A"]
    errAmpX --> pwrAmpX["Power Amplifier Ka"]
    pwrAmpX --> motorX["DC Servo Motor X-axis"]
    motorX --> leadScrewX["Lead Screw Mechanism"]
    leadScrewX --> penCarriage["Pen Carriage X position"]
    leadScrewX --> fbPotX["Feedback Potentiometer"]
    fbPotX --> sumX
    penCarriage --> paper["Plotting Paper"]
    attnY --> sumY(("+"))
    vrefY["Precision Vref"] --> sumY
    sumY --> errAmpY["Error Amplifier Gain A"]
    errAmpY --> pwrAmpY["Power Amplifier Ka"]
    pwrAmpY --> motorY["DC Servo Motor Y-axis"]
    motorY --> leadScrewY["Lead Screw Mechanism"]
    leadScrewY --> paper
    leadScrewY --> fbPotY["Feedback Potentiometer"]
    fbPotY --> sumY
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Two *independent* null-balance servo loops ‚ÄĒ one for X, one for Y ‚ÄĒ share a common paper platen. The pen's $x$-coordinate follows $V_x$, the $y$-coordinate follows $V_y$. The feedback potentiometer closes the loop on each axis.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Strip-Chart (Y-t) Galvanometric Plotter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sigIn["Y-axis Signal Input"] --> cond["Signal Conditioner / Attenuator"]
    cond --> galv["D'Arsonval Moving-Coil Galvanometer"]
    galv --> pen["Ink Pen on Pivot Arm"]
    pen --> paper1["Moving Chart Paper"]
    chartDrive["Stepper Motor Chart Drive"] --> paper1
    timeBase["Time-Base Selector 1/5/25 mm/s"] --> chartDrive
    galv -.->|"Magnetic Damping (eddy current)"| damper["Copper Former / Damping Disc"]
    galv -.->|"Hairspring Restoring Torque"| spring["Spiral Hairspring"]
```

> **Why no closed loop?** A galvanometric plotter uses an *open-loop* torque-balance: the input current produces a magnetic torque that is opposed by the hairspring. Position = $K \cdot I$. It is cheap but nonlinear and limited to ~10 Hz.

---

### 4.3 Functional Flow ‚ÄĒ Thermal Array Plotter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigA["Signal A"] --> mux["Analog Multiplexer"]
    sigB["Signal B"] --> mux
    sigC["Signal C"] --> mux
    mux --> adc["8-bit ADC 1 MSPS"]
    adc --> fifo["Line Buffer FIFO 2048 bytes"]
    fifo --> heatDrv["Thermal Head Driver 32 dots/mm"]
    heatDrv --> thermalHead["Thermal Stylus Array 2048 elements"]
    thermalHead --> thermalPaper["Heat-Sensitive Paper"]
    stepper["Stepper Motor"] --> thermalPaper
    ctl["Microcontroller / Timing Logic"] --> mux
    ctl --> adc
    ctl --> fifo
    ctl --> stepper
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Multiplexed analog signals are digitised, buffered line-by-line, and "burned" onto heat-sensitive paper by an array of thermal styli. This is the modern principle behind **ECG machines** and **digital strip-chart recorders**.

---

### 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a strip-chart recorder and an X-Y recorder.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Strip-Chart (Y-t) Recorder | X-Y Recorder |
|---|---|---|
| Independent variable | **Time** (t) ‚ÄĒ paper moves | **Second signal** (X) ‚ÄĒ paper stationary |
| Dependent variable | Input signal Y | Input signal Y |
| Chart motion | Continuous, selectable speed (mm/s) | Stationary sheet |
| Typical application | Long-term trend monitoring | B-H curve, frequency response plots |

> Both belong to the *plotter* family and produce permanent hard-copy traces.

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023]**

**Q2. State any three advantages of a null-balance (servo) plotter over a galvanometric plotter.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher accuracy** ‚ÄĒ Static error $\approx 1/(1 + K_{OL})$; with $K_{OL} = 10^4$, error is only **0.01 %** versus **2‚Äď5 %** for galvanometric.
2. **Better linearity** ‚ÄĒ Non-linearity of motor, gears, and lead-screw is suppressed by the loop gain.
3. **Greater input range** ‚ÄĒ Attenuator network at the front end provides 1 mV to 50 V full-scale ranges in 1-2-5 sequence.
4. *Bonus:* Higher torque at the pen (no delicate hairspring), allowing use of heavier chart paper and multi-colour pen turrets.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation:** Two full 14-mark questions are provided; the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### ‚ĚĖ Question A ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks (X-Y Servo Plotter ‚ÄĒ Full Treatment)

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **X-Y plotter**. Discuss the role of the feedback potentiometer and the error amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **closed-loop transfer function** of one axis of the X-Y plotter and show that the static accuracy is $\dfrac{1}{1+K_{OL}}$. A plotter has $K_{OL} = 5000$. Calculate (i) the static error in %, and (ii) the maximum traceable sine frequency if the pen's full-scale amplitude is 5 cm and max pen speed is 0.4 m/s. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram and Working (7 marks)**

The X-Y plotter contains two *independent* servo loops (one per axis). For the X-axis, the chain is:

- **Input** $V_x(t)$ ‚Üí **Attenuator** (selects full-scale range from 1 mV/div to 10 V/div in 1-2-5 steps).
- **Error amplifier** computes $e = V_x - V_{fb}$, where $V_{fb}$ comes from the **tapered feedback potentiometer** mechanically coupled to the X-axis servo motor.
- The amplified error drives a **DC servo motor** through a power amplifier.
- The motor rotates a **lead screw** that translates the **pen carriage** along the X-direction.
- The **feedback potentiometer** converts the carriage position back into a voltage $V_{fb} = K_p \cdot x$. Because $V_{fb} \to V_x$ by the high-gain loop, the *position* $x$ is forced to track $V_x/K_p$.

> **Role of feedback pot:** It provides **mechanical-to-electrical position feedback**, closing the loop and forcing the carriage to a position that nulls the error. It is the heart of the null-balance action.

> **Role of error amplifier:** Provides high voltage gain $A$ (typically 10‚Āī‚Äď10‚ĀĶ) so that the residual error is a tiny fraction of the input. This is what gives the plotter its **¬Ī0.25 %** class accuracy.

The Y-axis operates identically with $V_y(t)$ as input. The pen's $y$-position tracks $V_y$, while the $x$-position tracks $V_x$ ‚Üí the pen draws $V_y$ *vs.* $V_x$.

**(b) Derivation and Calculation (7 marks)**

The derivation in **Section 3.1** above yields:

$$
\dfrac{X(s)}{V_x(s)} = \dfrac{K_{OL}}{s(\tau_m\tau_e s^2 + (\tau_m+\tau_e)s + 1) + K_{OL}}
$$

Applying the final-value theorem to a step input of magnitude $V_0$:

$$
\text{Static Error} \;=\; \dfrac{V_0}{1 + K_{OL}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \% \text{ error} \;=\; \dfrac{100}{1 + K_{OL}}
$$

**(i) Static error with $K_{OL} = 5000$:**

$$
\% \text{ error} \;=\; \dfrac{100}{1 + 5000} \;=\; \dfrac{100}{5001} \;\approx\; 0.01999\,\% \;\approx\; 0.02\,\%
$$

**(ii) Maximum traceable sine frequency:**

Using formula (row 15) with $A = 5$ cm = 0.05 m and $v_{pen,max} = 0.4$ m/s:

$$
f_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{v_{pen,max}}{2\pi A} \;=\; \dfrac{0.4}{2 \pi \times 0.05} \;=\; \dfrac{0.4}{0.3142} \;\approx\; 1.27 \text{ Hz}
$$

> **Answer:** Static error ‚Čą **0.02 %**; Maximum traceable sine frequency ‚Čą **1.27 Hz**.

---

#### ‚ĚĖ Question B ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks (Galvanometric Strip-Chart + Thermal Array)

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024, Module 4 ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **galvanometric-type strip-chart recorder**. List its merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **thermal array recording principle** used in modern medical ECG recorders. Compare its performance with the galvanometric plotter on the basis of frequency response, accuracy, and number of channels. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Galvanometric Strip-Chart Recorder (7 marks)**

**Construction:** The core element is a **D'Arsonval moving-coil galvanometer** ‚ÄĒ a coil of fine copper wire suspended in the radial field of a permanent magnet. The coil carries the input current $i(t)$. A **spiral hairspring** provides the restoring torque, and a **pen arm** with a capillary ink reservoir is fixed to the coil. A **stepper-motor-driven sprocket** pulls heat-stabilised chart paper at selectable speeds (1, 5, 25, 100 mm/s).

**Working:**
- Input current $i$ produces torque $T = N \cdot B \cdot l \cdot b \cdot i = K_t \, i$.
- The coil rotates by angle $\theta$ until the hairspring torque $K_s \theta$ balances: $\theta = (K_t/K_s) i$.
- The pen arm amplifies $\theta$ mechanically to produce a deflection $y \propto i$ on the chart.
- The moving paper provides the $t$-axis.

**Merits:** (1) Simple, low cost; (2) fast response (no servo lag); (3) robust for industrial environments.
**Demerits:** (1) Limited to ¬Ī 2‚Äď5 % accuracy due to spring non-linearity; (2) fragile hairspring; (3) only 10‚Äď30 Hz bandwidth; (4) single pen (or up to 4 multi-pen on a shared carriage).

**(b) Thermal Array Recording (7 marks)**

**Principle:** A **linear array of 1024‚Äď2048 thermal styli** (heated resistors, typically 32 dots/mm pitch) is held in contact with **heat-sensitive paper** (coated with a leuco dye that darkens at ~ 70 ¬įC). Each stylus is pulsed (10‚Äď20 ms ON) when the corresponding sample in the digitised input signal exceeds a threshold. As the paper advances one line (typically 0.125 mm per step), the next horizontal line of the trace is "burned".

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Galvanometric | Thermal Array |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency response | 0‚Äď10 Hz | 0‚Äď1 kHz |
| Static accuracy | ¬Ī 2 % FS | ¬Ī 1 % FS (8-bit) / ¬Ī 0.4 % (12-bit) |
| Channels | 1‚Äď4 pens | Up to 12 styli groups (multi-channel) |
| Trace quality | Smooth analog line | Dotted/dashed (depends on resolution) |
| Paper | Plain + ink | Special thermal paper (fades with heat/light) |
| Cost | Low | Medium‚ÄďHigh |

> **Conclusion:** The thermal array is preferred for *fast, multi-channel* medical/geophysical signals, while the galvanometric remains the workhorse for *low-speed industrial* trend recording.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing "plotter" with "printer".** A printer outputs *text/bitmap*. A plotter outputs a *continuous trace* of an *analog signal*. Writing "it prints characters" will cost you 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the time-axis in Y-t recorders.** If you draw only the pen and forget the chart-drive motor, you lose the "working" marks.
> 3. **Wrong static-error formula.** The formula is $1/(1 + K_{OL})$ *only* for unity feedback. If the feedback potentiometer has a scale factor $K_p \neq 1$, the per-unit error is $K_p/(1 + K_{OL} K_p)$.
> 4. **Mixing up units in $f_{max}$.** Amplitude must be in **metres** when $v_{pen}$ is in m/s. A common slip yields an answer off by a factor of 100.
> 5. **Omitting the error amplifier's role.** Many students draw the block diagram but fail to *explain* that the error amplifier is what gives the plotter its high accuracy.
> 6. **Forgetting the high-impedance differential input.** KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly expects input-stage characteristics: $Z_{in} \geq 1\;\text{M}\Omega$, CMRR $\geq 100$ dB.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plotter ‚ÄĒ High-Density Revision Bullets**
> - **Definition:** Permanent hard-copy trace of $V_y$ vs. $V_x$ (or $t$). Classified as *display/output transducer*.
> - **Two main families:** Y-t strip-chart and X-Y plotter.
> - **Y-t Plotter:** Galvanometric (open-loop) or servo (closed-loop); paper moves at 1‚Äď100 mm/s; frequency response 0‚Äď10 Hz; accuracy ¬Ī 2 %.
> - **X-Y Plotter:** Always *servo* (null-balance); paper stationary; two independent servo loops for X and Y; frequency response 0‚Äď5 Hz; accuracy ¬Ī 0.25 %.
> - **Null-balance principle:** $V_{in} \to V_{fb}$ via high-gain loop ‚áí position $x = V_{in}/K_p$.
> - **Static error** = $1/(1 + K_{OL})$; with $K_{OL} = 5000$, error ‚Čą **0.02 %**.
> - **Maximum traceable sine** $f_{max} = v_{pen}/(2\pi A)$.
> - **Key components:** Differential input ‚Üí Attenuator ‚Üí Error amplifier ‚Üí Power amp ‚Üí DC servo motor ‚Üí Lead screw ‚Üí Pen carriage ‚Üí Feedback pot.
> - **Thermal array plotter:** 1024‚Äď2048 styli; 0‚Äď1 kHz; used in ECG/EEG/seismic.
> - **Electrostatic plotter:** Ion-deposition on dielectric paper; very high resolution; expensive.
> - **Drum plotter:** Rotating drum + translating pen; legacy CAD use.
> - **Industry standard accuracy classes:** Class 0.5 (¬Ī0.5 %), Class 1.0 (¬Ī1 %), Class 2.5 (¬Ī2.5 %).
> - **Standard chart widths:** 250 mm (10‚Ä≥) and 200 mm (8‚Ä≥).
> - **Standard full-scale input ranges:** 1 mV, 10 mV, 100 mV, 1 V, 10 V (in 1-2-5 decade steps).
> - **KTU favourite exam topics:** Block diagram, null-balance derivation, static accuracy formula, comparison table, $f_{max}$ numerical, damping ratio.
> - **Pitfalls:** Forgetting the time-base (chart drive), wrong units in $f_{max}$, confusing plotter with printer, omitting feedback-path analysis.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 ‚ÄĒ PLOTTER NOTES**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 Aligned | Outcome-Based Education*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)** is a flat-panel, non-emissive display technology that utilizes the **electro-optical properties of liquid crystal (LC) material** ‚ÄĒ a state of matter that exhibits properties intermediate between those of a conventional liquid and a solid crystal ‚ÄĒ to manipulate the polarization state of incident light. By selectively controlling the orientation of LC molecules using an externally applied electric field, the optical transmission (or reflectance) of each picture element (pixel) is modulated, thereby producing a visible image or alphanumeric information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** LCD is classified under *Passive and Active Display Devices* in Module 4. Focus areas include: (i) construction, (ii) working principle, (iii) driving schemes (static & multiplexed), and (iv) merits/demerits versus LED, Nixie, and 7-segment LED displays.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy ‚ÄĒ "Electrically Controlled Window Blinds"**
> 
> Imagine a window fitted with microscopic venetian blinds. Normally, the blinds are twisted open (think of a spiral staircase) so light passes straight through. But when you flip an electric switch, every slat snaps to a vertical position and blocks the light.
> 
> - **Without voltage** ‚Üí blinds are *twisted* ‚Üí light passes ‚Üí pixel looks **bright** ‚ú®
> - **With voltage** ‚Üí blinds *align* with the field ‚Üí light is blocked ‚Üí pixel looks **dark** ūüĆĎ
> 
> Millions of these "blinds" (pixels) working together, switched on/off in patterns, create the images on your calculator, watch, TV, or oscilloscope screen.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Optical birefringence of nematic LC | $\Delta n$ | **0.08 ‚Äď 0.25** | dimensionless |
| Cell gap thickness | $d$ | **3 ‚Äď 10** | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| Dielectric anisotropy | $\Delta \varepsilon$ | **+2 to +15** (positive) | dimensionless |
| Rotational viscosity | $\gamma$ | **0.05 ‚Äď 0.5** | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| Elastic constant (splay) | $K_{11}$ | **6 ‚Äď 15** | $\text{pN}$ |
| Elastic constant (twist) | $K_{22}$ | **3 ‚Äď 8** | $\text{pN}$ |
| Elastic constant (bend) | $K_{33}$ | **8 ‚Äď 20** | $\text{pN}$ |
| Threshold voltage | $V_{th}$ | **1 ‚Äď 3** | $\text{V (RMS)}$ |
| Driving frequency | $f$ | **30 ‚Äď 200** | $\text{Hz}$ |
| Operating temperature | $T_{op}$ | **0 ‚Äď 50** | $^\circ\text{C}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why AC driving is mandatory in LCD:** A pure DC voltage causes **ionic migration** in the LC medium, which degrades alignment layers, reduces contrast, and shortens display life. Hence, the industry standard is **AC square-wave driving** (typically 30 Hz ‚Äď 200 Hz, 3 V to 5 V RMS) with **zero DC component**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of Liquid Crystals

Liquid crystals exist in distinct mesophases between the solid and isotropic liquid states. Each phase exhibits a different molecular arrangement and hence a different electro-optical behavior.

| Phase | Molecular Order | Optical Property | LCD Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smectic-A (SmA)** | Positional + orientational (layers) | Biaxial birefringence | Ferroelectric LCDs |
| **Nematic (N)** | Orientational only | Uniaxial birefringence ($\Delta n$) | **TN, STN, IPS** (most LCDs) |
| **Cholesteric (Ch)** | Helical (chiral nematic) | Selective Bragg reflection | Reflective displays, e-paper |

> [!NOTE]
> **For the KTU exam:** Ninety percent of questions are based on the **Twisted Nematic (TN)** configuration because it is the foundational architecture from which STN, IPS, and VA are derived.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a TN-LCD Pixel

A single LCD cell is a *layered sandwich* ‚ÄĒ five functional layers stacked between two glass substrates:

1. **Upper polarizer** (0¬į)
2. **Upper transparent electrode (ITO)** + alignment layer
3. **Liquid crystal layer** (twisted 90¬į, thickness $d$)
4. **Lower transparent electrode (ITO)** + alignment layer
5. **Lower polarizer (analyzer)** oriented at 90¬į to the upper one
6. **Backlight** (transmissive mode) or **reflector** (reflective mode)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)** is used as the electrode because it is simultaneously **electrically conductive** ($R_s \approx 10\text{‚Äď}100 \, \Omega/\square$) and **optically transparent** ($>85\%$ transmission in visible range). This is a unique combination that conventional metals cannot offer.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Principle (No Voltage vs. With Voltage)

### Case (a) ‚ÄĒ OFF State (No Voltage Applied, $V=0$)

1. Unpolarized backlight strikes the **upper polarizer** ‚Üí emerges as **vertically polarized light**.
2. Light enters the LC layer whose molecules are arranged in a **gradual 90¬į twist** (helix).
3. As the wave propagates, its **E-field vector follows the local director** (Maugin‚ÄďGoos‚ÄďH√§nchen wave-guiding effect).
4. After traversing cell thickness $d$, the polarization is **rotated by exactly 90¬į**.
5. The 90¬į-rotated wave is now **aligned with the lower polarizer's transmission axis** ‚Üí light passes through.
6. **Result:** The pixel appears **bright/white**.

### Case (b) ‚ÄĒ ON State (Voltage $V > V_{th}$ Applied)

1. Vertically polarized light enters the LC layer.
2. The applied field **re-aligns the LC molecules parallel to the field** (along the propagation direction). The helical twist is destroyed.
3. Since the molecules are now uniform along the propagation axis, the wave experiences **no optical rotation**.
4. The polarization remains **vertical** at the exit of the LC cell.
5. The lower polarizer is at 90¬į (crossed) ‚Üí **vertical light is blocked**.
6. **Result:** The pixel appears **dark/black**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** OFF = "Twist" = Transmission = Visible. ON = "Align" = Absorption = Invisible.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{th} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon}}$ | Threshold (Freedericksz) voltage for director reorientation | Numerical: compute $V_{th}$ |
| 2 | $\tau_{on} = \dfrac{\gamma d^{2}}{\pi^{2} K}\left[\left(\dfrac{V}{V_{th}}\right)^{2} - 1\right]^{-1}$ | Turn-ON response time (electric field aided) | Compare with OFF time |
| 3 | $\tau_{off} = \dfrac{\gamma d^{2}}{\pi^{2} K}$ | Turn-OFF response time (elastic recovery only) | Numerical / design |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d \Delta n}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\sqrt{m^{2} - 1}}{2}, \quad m = 2,3,5$ | Gooch‚ÄďTarry first minimum condition (90¬į TN, parallel polarizers) | Cell-gap optimization |
| 5 | $T_{\parallel} = \cos^{2}\!\left(\chi - \dfrac{\pi \Delta n d}{\lambda}\right)$ | Transmission between **parallel** polarizers, twist $\chi$ | Transmission calculation |
| 6 | $C_{cell} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Cell capacitance (drives current & RC time constant) | Decoupling / driver design |
| 7 | $V_{RMS}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{N}\left[V_{on}^{2} + (N-1)V_{off}^{2}\right]$ | RMS voltage at selected pixel in **1/N duty multiplex** | Multiplex ratio |
| 8 | $\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{(N-1) + r^{2}}{1 + (N-1)r^{2}}}, \quad r = \dfrac{V_{on}}{V_{off}}$ | Alt‚ÄďPleshko **selection ratio** (must exceed 1) | Maximum multiplex $N$ |
| 9 | $V_{on} = V_{off}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sqrt{N}+1}{\sqrt{N}-1}}$ | Minimum $V_{on}$ required for given $N$ | Design of driver ICs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where $\chi = 90¬į$ in TN-LCD.** Plug $\chi = \pi/2$ into formula 5: $T_{\parallel} = \sin^{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \Delta n d}{\lambda}\right)$. Setting this to **zero** gives the first dark-state condition, which is the same as formula 4 with $m=2$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

LCDs are the workhorse of modern instrumentation because of their **ultra-low power consumption** (key for battery-driven multimeters, data loggers, and field instruments), **compatibility with CMOS drivers** (enabling single-chip integration), and **excellent readability in ambient light** (because they can be made reflective, unlike emissive LED displays). The same Gooch‚ÄďTarry condition used to design TN-LCDs is also deployed in **spatial light modulators (SLMs)**, **optical attenuators** in fiber-optic test equipment, and **beam-steering devices** in LIDAR.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Threshold Voltage (Freedericksz Transition)

We model the LC director $\hat{n}$ at angle $\theta(z)$ with respect to the substrate plane. The free-energy density (per unit area) is the sum of **elastic** and **electric** contributions:

$$
F = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{d}\left[ K_{11}\!\left(\dfrac{d\theta}{dz}\right)^{2} + \varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon\, E^{2}\sin^{2}\theta \right] dz
$$

where the field is $E = V/d$. To find the **threshold**, we apply a small perturbation $\theta(z) = \theta_0 \sin(\pi z / d)$ (lowest-order mode) and linearize: $\sin\theta \approx \theta$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Linearized free energy:**

$$
F = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{d}\left[ K_{11}\!\left(\dfrac{d\theta}{dz}\right)^{2} + \varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon\, E^{2}\theta^{2} \right] dz
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute the trial solution** $\theta(z)=\theta_0\sin(\pi z/d)$:

$$
\dfrac{d\theta}{dz} = \theta_0\,\dfrac{\pi}{d}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi z}{d}\right)
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Evaluate the integrals** (using $\int_0^d \sin^2(\pi z/d)\,dz = d/2$ and $\int_0^d \cos^2(\pi z/d)\,dz = d/2$):

$$
F = \dfrac{1}{2}\theta_0^{2}\,\dfrac{d}{2}\!\left[ K_{11}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{d}\right)^{2} + \varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon\, E^{2} \right]
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Stability criterion:** The undistorted state ($\theta_0 = 0$) becomes **unstable** when the bracket vanishes (the second variation of $F$ changes sign):

$$
K_{11}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{d}\right)^{2} = \varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon\, E^{2}_{th}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Solve for the threshold field**, then convert to voltage $V_{th} = E_{th}\cdot d$:

$$
E_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{d}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_{11}}{\varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{th} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{11}}{\varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon}}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a twisted cell, the effective elastic constant is $K = K_{11} + (K_{33}-K_{22})/4$, but for KTU problems the simplified form $K = K_{11}$ is universally used.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Response Time

The torque balance on the LC director is **viscous damping = elastic restoring torque**:

$$
\gamma\,\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = K_{11}\,\dfrac{\partial^{2}\theta}{\partial z^{2}} + \varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon\, E^{2}\theta
$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Turn-OFF** ($E = 0$): Only the diffusion term remains. Separating variables and using the boundary conditions $\theta(0)=\theta(d)=0$ gives an exponential decay with characteristic time for the slowest (fundamental) mode:

$$
\tau_{off} = \dfrac{\gamma\,d^{2}}{\pi^{2} K_{11}}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Turn-ON** ($E \neq 0$): The electric torque accelerates the relaxation, yielding:

$$
\tau_{on} = \dfrac{\gamma\,d^{2}}{\pi^{2} K_{11}}\left[\left(\dfrac{V}{V_{th}}\right)^{2} - 1\right]^{-1}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering implication:** Turn-OFF is *always slower* than turn-ON because there is no field to help. This is why fast LCDs use *overdriving* (a brief voltage spike $V \gg V_{th}$).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A TN-LCD has $K_{11} = 10 \text{ pN}$, $\Delta\varepsilon = 8$, $d = 5\,\mu\text{m}$, $\gamma = 0.1 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. Calculate the threshold voltage and turn-OFF time. Take $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854\times10^{-12}\text{ F/m}$.

**Solution ‚ÄĒ Threshold voltage:**

$$
V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{K_{11}}{\varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon}}
$$

$$
V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{10\times10^{-12}}{(8.854\times10^{-12})(8)}}
$$

$$
V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{10}{70.83\times10^{-12}} \times 10^{-12}} = \pi\sqrt{0.1412}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{th} \approx 1.18 \text{ V (RMS)}\,}
$$

**Solution ‚ÄĒ Turn-OFF time:**

$$
\tau_{off} = \dfrac{\gamma\,d^{2}}{\pi^{2} K_{11}} = \dfrac{(0.1)(5\times10^{-6})^{2}}{\pi^{2}(10\times10^{-12})}
$$

$$
\tau_{off} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 25\times10^{-12}}{9.87 \times 10\times10^{-12}} = \dfrac{2.5\times10^{-12}}{9.87\times10^{-11}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_{off} \approx 25.3 \text{ ms}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚ÄĒ LCD vs. Competing Displays

| Property | **LCD (TN)** | **LED (7-seg)** | **OLED** | **Nixie Tube** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating voltage | 3 ‚Äď 5 V | 1.8 ‚Äď 3 V | 3 ‚Äď 6 V | 150 ‚Äď 200 V |
| Power consumption | **Very low** | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Viewing angle | Poor (TN) / Wide (IPS) | Very wide | Wide | Wide |
| Lifetime | 50,000 h | 100,000 h | 30,000 h | 20,000 h |
| Contrast ratio | 1000 : 1 | 100 : 1 | **‚ąě : 1** | 100 : 1 |
| Need backlight | Yes (transmissive) | No (emissive) | No (emissive) | No (emissive) |
| Response time | 5 ‚Äď 25 ms | $< 1\,\mu\text{s}$ | $< 1\text{ ms}$ | $< 1\text{ ms}$ |
| Suitability for instrumentation | **Excellent** | Excellent | Good | Legacy only |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Layer Stack-Up of a TN-LCD Cell

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the *functional architecture* of a single LCD pixel, showing the optical and electrical signal flow through each layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[BL Unpolarized Backlight Source] --> B[UPPER POLARIZER 0 deg]
    B --> C[Upper ITO Electrode with Alignment Grooves]
    C --> D[Nematic Liquid Crystal Layer 90 deg Twist Thickness d]
    D --> E[Lower ITO Electrode with Alignment Grooves]
    E --> F[LOWER POLARIZER ANALYZER 90 deg]
    F --> G{Driving Voltage V Applied}
    G -- V LESS THAN Vth --> H[LC Twist Preserved Polarization Rotates 90 deg Light Passes BRIGHT PIXEL]
    G -- V GREATER THAN Vth --> I[LC Molecules Align with Field No Rotation Light Blocked by Analyzer DARK PIXEL]
    H --> J[Observer Eye]
    I --> J
    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#d6d8db,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Multiplexed 7-Segment LCD Driving Architecture (Static & 1/4 Duty)

In a typical 4-digit alphanumeric LCD (e.g., used in multimeters), all backplanes BP1‚ÄďBP4 are common to every digit, and the segment lines (a‚Äďg, dp) are shared. Time-division multiplexing is essential.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[Microcontroller or LCD Driver IC HT1621 PCF8576] --> SEG[Segment Lines seg a seg b seg c seg d seg e seg f seg g seg dp]
    SEG --> MUX[Analog Switch Matrix MUX]
    MUX --> D1[Digit 1 BP1]
    MUX --> D2[Digit 2 BP2]
    MUX --> D3[Digit 3 BP3]
    MUX --> D4[Digit 4 BP4]
    D1 --> LCD[Liquid Crystal Cell 4 x 8 Matrix 32 Pixels]
    D2 --> LCD
    D3 --> LCD
    D4 --> LCD
    LCD --> OBS[Display 4 Alphanumeric Characters]
    style MCU fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style MUX fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333
    style LCD fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333
    style OBS fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 State-Transition Flowchart of an LCD Pixel

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF_STATE: V equals 0
    OFF_STATE --> TRANSITION: V applied less than Vth
    TRANSITION --> OFF_STATE: V returns to 0
    TRANSITION --> ON_STATE: V exceeds Vth
    ON_STATE --> TRANSITION: V drops below Vth
    ON_STATE --> OFF_STATE: V removed and relaxation completes in tau off
    note right of ON_STATE
        Bright to Dark transition
        Time equals tau on
    end note
    note right of OFF_STATE
        Dark to Bright transition
        Time equals tau off
    end note
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of the LC Sandwich (Text Block Diagram)

```
   ‚ĒĆ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ēź  ‚Üź Air gap (viewing side)
   ‚Ēā  UPPER POLARIZER  (0¬į polarization) ‚Ēā   thickness ‚Čą 200 őľm
   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ē§
   ‚Ēā  GLASS SUBSTRATE  (SiO‚āā)            ‚Ēā   thickness ‚Čą 700 őľm
   ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ‚Ē§
   ‚Ēā  ITO ELECTRODE  (‚Čą 100 √Ö)           ‚Ēā   R_s ‚Čą 50 ő©/‚Ė°
   ‚Ēā  POLYIMIDE ALIGNMENT LAYER (rubbed) ‚Ēā   pre-tilt ‚Čą 2¬į
   ‚Ēú‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚ēź‚Ē§
   ‚ēĎ  NEMATIC LC  (e.g., 5CB, E7)        ‚ēĎ   d = 3‚Äď10 őľm
   ‚ēĎ  Helix pitch p ‚Čą 4d (90¬į twist)     ‚ēĎ   birefringence őĒn
```
