> **KTU Drawing Note:** In an examination, you are expected to draw at minimum: (i) the layer sequence, (ii) the two polarizer orientations, and (iii) the LC molecular orientation in both OFF and ON states. A neat cross-section earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024, Set B]
**Define the term "Liquid Crystal". Why are liquid crystals used in displays instead of conventional liquids or solids?**

**Model Answer:**
A liquid crystal is a state of matter that possesses **long-range orientational order** (like a solid crystal) but **no long-range positional order** (like an isotropic liquid). It flows like a liquid but its rod-like molecules remain statistically aligned along a common axis called the **director $\hat{n}$**.

LCDs exploit this unique property because:
- The orientation of $\hat{n}$ can be **reoriented by a small electric field** (low power, $V_{th} \approx 1$ V).
- The optical birefringence $\Delta n$ **couples mechanical alignment to optical polarization**, enabling light modulation.
- Conventional liquids lack orientational order (no optical anisotropy), and solids cannot be reoriented at low voltages.

*(3 marks ‚ÄĒ definition 1, two reasons 2)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023, Set A]
**Why is AC voltage used to drive LCDs instead of DC? What is the typical range of driving frequency?**

**Model Answer:**
A pure DC voltage causes **ionic migration** in the LC medium. The positive and negative ions drift towards the negative and positive electrodes respectively, getting trapped at the alignment layer interfaces. This produces:
- **Charging effects** (residual DC bias) that shift $V_{th}$.
- **Electrochemical degradation** of the polyimide alignment layer.
- **Permanent image sticking** (after-image) and reduced display life.

AC driving (typically **30 Hz ‚Äď 200 Hz** square wave) has a **zero DC component**, so ions oscillate symmetrically without net drift. A *small DC offset* (called the *flicker asymmetry*) is unavoidable in practice, which is why displays are refreshed at frequencies above the human flicker fusion threshold (>30 Hz).

*(3 marks ‚ÄĒ DC problem 2, frequency range 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Twisted Nematic (TN) Liquid Crystal Display**. Discuss the behaviour of the LC layer in the presence and absence of an applied voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **threshold voltage** of a TN-LCD cell. A TN-LCD has the following parameters: $K_{11} = 12 \text{ pN}$, $\Delta\varepsilon = 10$, cell gap $d = 6\,\mu\text{m}$. Calculate $V_{th}$ and the turn-OFF response time, given $\gamma = 0.08 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The TN-LCD cell consists of (i) two glass substrates carrying **transparent ITO electrodes**, (ii) a **nematic liquid crystal** layer of thickness $d$ sandwiched between them, (iii) two **polyimide alignment layers** that are buffed in orthogonal directions to induce a 90¬į helical twist, and (iv) two **crossed polarizers** on the outer faces of the substrates.

**OFF state (no voltage):** The LC molecules gradually rotate by 90¬į from the lower substrate to the upper substrate. Unpolarized light from the backlight is polarized by the upper polarizer, then its E-vector follows the rotating director through the LC layer (adiabatic wave-guiding), emerging horizontally polarized. The horizontal component aligns with the lower (90¬į) polarizer's axis, allowing transmission. The pixel appears **bright**.

**ON state ($V > V_{th}$):** The applied field aligns the LC molecules parallel to the field, destroying the helical twist. The polarization of light is no longer rotated, so the vertically polarized light encounters a perpendicular polarizer at the bottom and is **absorbed**. The pixel appears **dark**.

*(Neat diagram 2 marks, construction 2 marks, OFF/ON explanation 3 marks = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

Using the Freedericksz transition analysis (see Section 3.1):

$$
V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{K_{11}}{\varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{12\times10^{-12}}{(8.854\times10^{-12})(10)}} = \pi\sqrt{0.1355}
$$

$$
V_{th} \approx \pi \times 0.3682 \approx 1.157 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{th} \approx 1.16 \text{ V (RMS)}}
$$

**Turn-OFF time:**

$$
\tau_{off} = \dfrac{\gamma d^{2}}{\pi^{2} K_{11}} = \dfrac{(0.08)(6\times10^{-6})^{2}}{\pi^{2}(12\times10^{-12})}
$$

$$
\tau_{off} = \dfrac{0.08 \times 36\times10^{-12}}{9.87 \times 12\times10^{-12}} = \dfrac{2.88\times10^{-12}}{1.184\times10^{-10}} \approx 24.3 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{off} \approx 24.3 \text{ ms}}
$$

*(Derivation 4 marks, numerical 3 marks = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Sample Paper 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the **static and multiplexed (1/N duty) addressing schemes** of an LCD. Derive the **Alt‚ÄďPleshko selection ratio** and discuss how it limits the maximum number of multiplexed rows. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **advantages and limitations of LCDs over LED, OLED, and Nixie tube displays**. Mention at least **two specific instrumentation applications** of LCDs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Static addressing** provides a dedicated segment line to every segment of every digit. The backplane (common) carries an AC square wave, and the segment line carries either *in-phase* (OFF) or *anti-phase* (ON) square waves. The RMS voltage difference activates or deactivates the segment. Although simple, static addressing is impractical beyond a few digits due to the **exponentially growing pin count**.

**Multiplexed addressing** time-shares the backplane among $N$ digits. The display is refreshed line by line; during each "row time" (duration $T/N$), only one backplane is active and the segment voltages are latched. The **RMS voltage across a pixel** must exceed $V_{th}$ for the ON state and stay below $V_{th}$ for the OFF state. The Alt‚ÄďPleshko analysis gives:

$$
V_{RMS,on}^{2} = \dfrac{V_{on}^{2} + (N-1)V_{off}^{2}}{N}, \quad V_{RMS,off}^{2} = \dfrac{V_{off}^{2} + (N-1)V_{on}^{2}}{N}
$$

For discrimination, we require $V_{RMS,on} > V_{th} > V_{RMS,off}$. Defining $r = V_{on}/V_{off}$ and selection ratio:

$$
\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{(N-1) + r^{2}}{1 + (N-1)r^{2}}}
$$

For $\alpha > 1$ (a necessary condition for discrimination), one must have:

$$
N < \dfrac{r^{2} + 1}{r^{2} - 1}
$$

If $r = 1.06$ (typical LCD driver), then $N_{max} \approx 17$. For $N=4$ (a quad-7-seg), $r$ need only be $\approx 1.16$, easily achievable. Higher $N$ requires steeper driver voltage ratios, which is why **active-matrix TFT** addressing is used for $N > 100$.

*(Static 2 marks, multiplex 2 marks, derivation 2 marks, $N_{max}$ discussion 1 mark = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Advantages of LCD over LED:**
- **Much lower power** (no continuous current per segment, only capacitive charging).
- **No heating**, no fan, no risk of thermal damage in sealed enclosures.
- Compatible with **CMOS logic levels** (3.3 V / 5 V); LEDs need current-limiting resistors or driver ICs.

**Advantages of LCD over OLED:**
- **No burn-in** of static elements; OLED degrades non-uniformly.
- **Higher peak brightness** in bright ambient (with strong backlight).
- **Lower cost per square inch** for large panels.
- **Longer operational lifetime** (50,000 h vs 30,000 h for OLED).

**Advantages of LCD over Nixie:**
- **No high-voltage anode supply** (Nixie needs 150 V ‚Äď 200 V DC).
- **Mechanically robust** (Nixie tubes are fragile glass with internal electrodes).
- **Programmable** in real time (Nixie has fixed digit shapes).
- **Lower power, lower EMI**, no mercury or neon gas.

**Limitations of LCD:**
- Requires **backlight** in dim conditions (adds thickness and power).
- **Limited viewing angle** (TN panel) and slower response than LED.
- **Temperature-sensitive** (slow or blank below 0 ¬įC without heaters).
- Cannot match OLED's **infinite contrast** (true black) or flexibility.

**Instrumentation Applications (any two):**
1. **Digital multimeters** ‚ÄĒ 3¬Ĺ to 4¬Ĺ digit 7-segment LCDs with bar-graph.
2. **Hand-held oscilloscopes** and **spectrum analyzers** ‚ÄĒ color TFT-LCD.
3. **Industrial process controllers / PLC HMIs** ‚ÄĒ dot-matrix LCDs with custom icons.
4. **Medical devices** (glucometers, pulse oximeters) ‚ÄĒ low-power reflective LCD.
5. **Automotive dashboards and GPS** ‚ÄĒ wide-temperature-range TN/STN LCDs.

*(4 advantages 4 marks, 2 limitations 1 mark, 2 applications 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚ÄĒ Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify "AC driving"** for LCDs. Always mention AC + zero DC component.
> 2. **Wrong polarizer orientation** in diagrams. The two polarizers must be drawn at **90¬į to each other** ‚ÄĒ perpendicular axes, not parallel. Examiners check this with a protractor.
> 3. **Confusing $K_{11}$, $K_{22}$, $K_{33}$**. KTU expects $K_{11}$ (splay) in the threshold formula unless otherwise stated. Using the wrong elastic constant loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not converting units** in numericals. $K$ is in **pN = $10^{-12}$ N**, $d$ is in **$\mu$m = $10^{-6}$ m**, $\gamma$ in **Pa¬∑s**. Unit errors = 1‚Äď2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Mixing up turn-ON and turn-OFF formulae**. Turn-ON depends on $V$; turn-OFF does NOT. State the dependence explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ‚ú®

- **Definition:** LCD is a *non-emissive* flat-panel display that modulates polarized light using the electro-optical response of a *nematic liquid crystal* layer sandwiched between two crossed polarizers.
- **Key mesophase:** Nematic (rod-like molecules with orientational order, no positional order); other phases are smectic and cholesteric.
- **OFF state = Bright:** LC helix rotates polarization by 90¬į ‚Üí light passes crossed polarizer.
- **ON state = Dark:** Field unwinds helix ‚Üí no rotation ‚Üí light blocked by analyzer.
- **Threshold voltage:** $V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{K_{11}/(\varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon)}$ ‚ÄĒ typically **1 ‚Äď 3 V RMS**.
- **Response times:** $\tau_{off} = \gamma d^{2}/(\pi^{2}K_{11})$; $\tau_{on}$ is always faster for $V \gg V_{th}$.
- **AC driving is mandatory** to avoid ionic migration; typical frequency 30 Hz ‚Äď 200 Hz.
- **Gooch‚ÄďTarry condition:** $d\Delta n/\lambda = \sqrt{m^{2}-1}/2$ for $m=2,3,5,\dots$ ‚ÄĒ used to optimize cell gap.
- **Multiplex limit:** Alt‚ÄďPleshko ratio $\alpha = \sqrt{((N-1)+r^{2})/(1+(N-1)r^{2})}$ must exceed 1; practical passive matrix $N_{max} \approx 4$ to 16.
- **Transparent conductor:** ITO (Indium Tin Oxide) is the universal electrode material.
- **Suitable for instrumentation because:** ultra-low power, CMOS-compatible, sunlight-readable (reflective mode), no heat, no high voltage.
- **KTU-favorite sub-topics:** construction diagram, OFF/ON explanation, threshold derivation, numerical on $V_{th}$ and $\tau_{off}$, comparison with LED/Nixie, multiplexing limits.
- **Common errors to avoid:** parallel polarizers in diagram, using $K_{33}$ in $V_{th}$ formula, forgetting AC, missing unit conversion, drawing arrow directions of polarization wrongly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waveform Analysing Instruments - Distortion Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Waveform Analysing Instruments - Distortion Meter ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Distortion Meter** (also called a **Harmonic Distortion Analyzer**) is an electronic instrument designed to quantitatively measure the degree of deviation of a periodic waveform from a pure sine wave. It expresses this deviation as a percentage known as **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**, which represents the ratio of the RMS voltage of all harmonic components to the RMS voltage of the fundamental frequency component. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A Distortion Meter is a waveform analyzing instrument used to measure the harmonic content and the total harmonic distortion (THD) in an electrical signal, expressed as a percentage of the fundamental component. ### 1.2 Why Do We Need a Distortion Meter? In ideal AC systems, voltage and current waveforms are perfect sinusoids. However, in practical systems: - Non-linear loads (rectifiers, thyristors, SMPS) inject **harmonics** into the power line. - Transformers operating near saturation introduce 3rd and 5th harmonics. - Audio amplifiers produce **intermodulation distortion** and harmonic distortion. These harmonics cause: - Overheating of conductors and transformers - Resonance in power systems - Malfunction of protective relays - Audio quality degradation ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The "Musician's Tuner"** > Imagine you are a music teacher. A student plays a note on a piano that should be a pure A (440 Hz). Instead, you hear "A plus a faint echo of higher-pitched tones mixed in." The student has impure technique ‚ÄĒ the string is also vibrating at **880 Hz, 1320 Hz, 1760 Hz** (the harmonics). > A **Distortion Meter** acts like an ultra-precise electronic tuner that listens to the note, removes the main 440 Hz tone (the fundamental), and reports how much "extra noise/harmonics" remain as a percentage. > - **0% THD** = Perfectly pure sine wave (like a tuning fork in vacuum). > - **5% THD** = Slightly impure, acceptable for most power systems. > - **>10% THD** = Heavily distorted, problematic for sensitive equipment. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield) - **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion):** Expressed as a **percentage (%)** or as a **per-unit ratio (pu)**. - **Frequency Range:** Typically **20 Hz to 20 kHz** (audio) or **up to 1 MHz** (general purpose). - **Distortion Range:** Modern meters can measure from **0.01% to 100%** full scale. - **Accuracy:** ¬Ī5% of full scale for audio range instruments. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Harmonic Distortion Any periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform satisfying the **Dirichlet conditions** can be expressed as an infinite sum of sinusoidal components via the **Fourier Series**: **Fourier Series Representation:** $$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n)$$ Where: - $V_0$ = DC component - $V_1$ = Fundamental component (frequency = $f_0$) - $V_n$ for $n \geq 2$ = Harmonic components (frequency = $n \cdot f_0$) - $\phi_n$ = Phase angle of the n-th harmonic > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** A Distortion Meter works on the principle that any distorted sine wave can be decomposed into a fundamental + harmonics. By measuring the relative energy of the harmonics, we can quantify the "distortion." ### 2.2 Key Performance Parameter: Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) The **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of all the harmonic components to the RMS value of the fundamental component. **Mathematical Definition:** $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ Where $V_n$ is the RMS amplitude of the n-th harmonic. ### 2.3 Alternative: Distortion Factor (d) Sometimes expressed as: $$d = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{\sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}} \times 100\%$$ This is the ratio of harmonic RMS to the **total RMS** (including fundamental), giving a value that never exceeds 100%. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** | |---|---|---| | THD (Voltage) | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%** | | THD (Current) | $\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_n^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%** | | Distortion Factor | $d = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum V_n^2}}{\sqrt{\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n^2}}$ | Always $d \leq 1$ | | Total RMS | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}$ | Including fundamental | | Harmonic RMS | $V_{h,rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}$ | Excluding fundamental | | Single Harmonic % | $HD_n = \dfrac{V_n}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | n-th harmonic distortion | | THD + Noise | $\text{THD} + N = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum V_n^2 + V_{noise}^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Real-world measurement | | Wien Bridge Balance | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Notch filter center freq | | Quality Factor | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2 + (R_2/R_1)}$ | Wien Bridge selectivity | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** THD measurement is critical in: > - **Power Quality Auditing** (IEEE 519 standard compliance ‚ÄĒ typically requires THD < 5%) > - **Audio Engineering** (Hi-Fi amplifiers aim for THD < 0.01%) > - **Telecommunications** (Signal purity in transmission lines) > - **Renewable Energy** (Inverters must limit harmonic injection into the grid) --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis ### 3.1 Derivation of the THD Formula **Step 1:** Start with a general periodic signal $v(t)$ expressed as Fourier Series: $$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n)$$ We are concerned with AC signals only, hence $V_0 = 0$. $$v(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n)$$ **Step 2:** The total RMS value of the entire signal is the root-sum-square of all harmonic RMS values (because harmonics are orthogonal over a period): $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}$$ $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n^2}$$ **Step 3:** By definition, the RMS value of **just the harmonic content** (i.e., excluding the fundamental) is: $$V_{h,rms} = \sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}$$ $$V_{h,rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}$$ **Step 4:** The Total Harmonic Distortion is the ratio of the harmonic RMS to the fundamental RMS: $$\text{THD} = \frac{V_{h,rms}}{V_1}$$ $$\boxed{\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%}$$ **Step 5:** In terms of total RMS, the THD can be rewritten as: $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_1}\right)^2 - 1}$$ This alternative form is useful when only $V_{rms}$ and $V_1$ are measured. --- ### 3.2 Numerical Example Problem (Worked Solution) **Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Style):** A voltage waveform applied to a non-linear load has the following Fourier components: Fundamental ($V_1$) = **120 V RMS**, 2nd harmonic ($V_2$) = **6 V RMS**, 3rd harmonic ($V_3$) = **4 V RMS**, 4th harmonic ($V_4$) = **2 V RMS**, 5th harmonic ($V_5$) = **1 V RMS**. Calculate the THD and the total RMS voltage. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify all harmonic RMS values from $V_2$ onwards: $$V_2 = 6\text{ V}, \quad V_3 = 4\text{ V}, \quad V_4 = 2\text{ V}, \quad V_5 = 1\text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Square each harmonic voltage: $$V_2^2 = 36, \quad V_3^2 = 16, \quad V_4^2 = 4, \quad V_5^2 = 1$$ **Step 3:** Sum the squared values: $$\sum_{n=2}^{5} V_n^2 = 36 + 16 + 4 + 1 = 57$$ **Step 4:** Compute the square root: $$V_{h,rms} = \sqrt{57} = 7.55\text{ V}$$ **Step 5:** Calculate THD using the formula: $$\text{THD} = \frac{V_{h,rms}}{V_1} = \frac{7.55}{120} = 0.0629$$ **Step 6:** Express as a percentage: $$\boxed{\text{THD} = 6.29\%}$$ **Step 7:** Calculate the total RMS voltage: $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + \sum V_n^2} = \sqrt{(120)^2 + 57} = \sqrt{14400 + 57} = \sqrt{14457}$$ $$\boxed{V_{rms} \approx 120.24\text{ V}}$$ **Observation:** The total RMS is only marginally higher than the fundamental (120.24 V vs 120 V) because the harmonics are small compared to the fundamental. --- ### 3.3 Wiener Bridge (Wien Bridge) Notch Filter ‚ÄĒ Key Equation The Wien Bridge is the heart of the most common distortion meter. It acts as a **frequency-selective notch filter** that rejects the fundamental frequency while passing the harmonics. **Balance Condition of the Wien Bridge:** At balance, the fundamental frequency $f_0$ is rejected. The balance condition is: $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ Where: - $R$ and $C$ are the resistance and capacitance of one arm (the other arm has $2R$ and $2C$) - The other two arms have resistances $R_1$ and $R_2$ such that $R_1/R_2 = 2$ at balance **Quality Factor (Selectivity) of the Wien Bridge:** $$Q = \frac{1}{2 + (R_2/R_1)}$$ For maximum selectivity, $R_2/R_1 \to 0$, but practical $Q$ values are around 1.5 to 5. **Why this matters:** Higher $Q$ means the notch filter is more selective ‚ÄĒ it rejects the fundamental more completely but also has a narrower rejection band, requiring precise tuning. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Wien Bridge Type Distortion Meter The Wien Bridge distortion meter is the most widely used design. It works in two modes: 1. **Voltmeter Mode** ‚ÄĒ measures the total RMS voltage. 2. **Distortion Mode** ‚ÄĒ measures the harmonic RMS (after notch filtering). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Signal
v_in t] --> B[Input Attenuator
0 dB to 60 dB] B --> C[Impedance Matching
and Buffer Amplifier] C --> D{Mode Selector
Switch SW1} D -- Position 1 Voltmeter --> E[Wideband RMS
Voltmeter] E --> F[Meter Display
V1 Total RMS] D -- Position 2 Distortion --> G[Wien Bridge
Notch Filter] G --> H[Tuned to f0
Fundamental] H --> I[High Gain AC
Amplifier] I --> J[Average Responding
RMS Voltmeter] J --> K[Meter Display
Vh Harmonic RMS] G --> L[Variable RC
for tuning] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style K fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style L fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Heterodyne Type Distortion Analyzer Used for measuring very low distortion levels (down to **0.001%**). It uses a heterodyning (frequency mixing) principle to convert all harmonics to a single fixed intermediate frequency (IF). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Signal
with Harmonics] --> B[Input Mixer Stage] C[Local Oscillator
Variable Frequency] --> B B --> D[IF Amplifier
Fixed Frequency fc] D --> E[Narrow Band
Bandpass Filter] E --> F[Detector and
RMS Meter] F --> G[Output Display
Harmonic Level] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Working Principle ‚ÄĒ Two-Step Operation > [!NOTE] > **Step 1 (Voltmeter Mode):** The mode selector is set to position 1. The full input signal (fundamental + all harmonics) is routed to a wideband RMS voltmeter. The meter reading $M_1$ gives the **total RMS voltage** (or fundamental, depending on design). This value is calibrated and noted. > **Step 2 (Distortion Mode):** The mode selector is moved to position 2. The input signal now passes through the **Wien Bridge Notch Filter**, which is **tuned to the fundamental frequency** $f_0$. The fundamental is rejected, and only the harmonics ($f_2, f_3, f_4, \ldots$) pass through. The high-gain amplifier boosts the (now weak) harmonic signal, and the average-responding RMS meter reads the **harmonic RMS voltage** $M_2$. > **Step 3 (Calculation):** The THD is computed as the ratio: > $$\text{THD} = \frac{M_2}{M_1} \times 100\%$$ > In modern digital distortion meters, this calculation is performed automatically by a microcontroller, and the THD is displayed directly. ### 4.4 Schematic: Wien Bridge Notch Filter ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WienBridge [Wien Bridge Notch Filter Circuit] direction LR A[Input v_in] --- R1[R] --- B[Node A] B --- R2[2R] --- G1[Ground] B --- C2[C] --- G2[Ground] A --- C1[C] --- C[Node B] C --- D[Output v_out] end F[Tune f0 = 1 by 2 Pi RC] -.-> WienBridge style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` The Wien Bridge has two series-parallel RC networks. At the **balance frequency** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, the output of the bridge is zero ‚ÄĒ hence the fundamental is "notched out" while harmonics pass through with little attenuation. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]** **Q1. Define Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of a waveform and state its significance in power systems.** **Model Answer:** Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components (2nd, 3rd, 4th, ...) to the RMS value of the fundamental frequency component, expressed as a percentage. $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ **Significance in power systems:** THD quantifies the deviation of the voltage/current waveform from a pure sinusoid. High THD levels cause overheating of transformers, neutral conductor overloading, malfunction of protective devices, and resonance in power systems. Standards like IEEE 519 limit THD to **<5%** in commercial power systems to ensure equipment safety and power quality. --- > **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]** **Q2. List and briefly explain any two types of distortion meters.** **Model Answer:** **1. Wien Bridge Type Distortion Meter:** Uses a Wien Bridge as a notch filter tuned to the fundamental frequency. The fundamental is rejected, and the remaining harmonics are measured by an RMS voltmeter. The THD is calculated as the ratio of harmonic RMS to total RMS. It is simple, cost-effective, and suitable for moderate distortion measurements (0.1% to 100%). **2. Heterodyne Type Distortion Analyzer:** Uses a local oscillator and mixer to heterodyne (frequency-shift) all input frequencies to a fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF). A narrow bandpass filter at the IF then isolates one harmonic at a time, allowing individual harmonic analysis. It is highly accurate and used for measuring very low distortion levels (down to 0.001%). --- ### 5.2 PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Module 4]** --- #### ‚ú¶ QUESTION A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice Option A **Q(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Wien Bridge Distortion Meter**. Discuss the role of the Wien Bridge as a notch filter. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A Wien Bridge Distortion Meter is an instrument used to measure the total harmonic distortion (THD) in an audio-frequency or general-purpose AC signal. Its operation is based on a Wien Bridge, which acts as a **frequency-selective notch filter**. **Block Diagram:** ``` Input Signal ‚Üí Attenuator ‚Üí Buffer Amplifier ‚Üí Mode Selector (SW) ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ Voltmeter Mode: ‚Üí Wideband RMS Voltmeter ‚Üí Display (V1) ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ Distortion Mode: ‚Üí Wien Bridge Notch Filter ‚Üí AC Amplifier ‚Üí RMS Voltmeter ‚Üí Display (Vh) ``` **Working Principle:** **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Voltmeter Mode:** The mode selector switch is set to position 1. The input signal (containing fundamental + all harmonics) is applied to a wideband RMS voltmeter. The meter reads $M_1$, which corresponds to the **total RMS voltage** of the signal. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Distortion Mode:** The switch is moved to position 2. The input signal is now routed through the **Wien Bridge notch filter**, which is tuned to the fundamental frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$. At this balance frequency, the bridge produces zero output ‚ÄĒ effectively "notching out" the fundamental. All harmonics ($2f_0, 3f_0, 4f_0, \ldots$) pass through the filter with minimal attenuation. **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Measurement:** The harmonics (which are now much smaller than the total signal) are amplified by a **high-gain AC amplifier** and measured by an **average-responding RMS voltmeter**. The meter reads $M_2$, corresponding to the **harmonic RMS voltage**. **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Calculation:** THD is computed as: $$\text{THD} = \frac{M_2}{M_1} \times 100\%$$ In modern digital meters, this calculation is automated and the THD is displayed directly. **Role of Wien Bridge as a Notch Filter:** - The Wien Bridge has a balance condition: at frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, the output voltage is zero. - This creates a **deep notch** at $f_0$ with very high attenuation (>60 dB), while harmonics outside the notch are unaffected. - The Quality Factor $Q = 1/[2 + (R_2/R_1)]$ determines the selectivity ‚ÄĒ higher $Q$ gives a sharper notch. - This allows the meter to isolate the harmonic content with high accuracy. --- **Q(b)** A voltage signal has fundamental component of **200 V RMS**, 2nd harmonic of **15 V RMS**, 3rd harmonic of **10 V RMS**, and 5th harmonic of **5 V RMS**. Calculate the **(i) Total Harmonic Distortion**, **(ii) Total RMS voltage**, and **(iii) Distortion Factor**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $V_1 = 200$ V (Fundamental) - $V_2 = 15$ V, $V_3 = 10$ V, $V_5 = 5$ V (Harmonics) **(i) Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_5^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ $$V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_5^2 = 225 + 100 + 25 = 350$$ $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{350}}{200} \times 100\% = \frac{18.71}{200} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{THD} = 9.35\%}$$ **(ii) Total RMS Voltage:** $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_5^2} = \sqrt{200^2 + 350} = \sqrt{40000 + 350}$$ $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{40350}$$ $$\boxed{V_{rms} \approx 200.87\text{ V}}$$ **(iii) Distortion Factor (d):** The distortion factor is the ratio of harmonic RMS to total RMS: $$d = \frac{V_{h,rms}}{V_{rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{350}}{200.87} = \frac{18.71}{200.87}$$ $$\boxed{d = 0.0931 \text{ or } 9.31\%}$$ **Note:** THD and the distortion factor are nearly equal when harmonics are small compared to the fundamental (which is the case here). --- #### ‚ú¶ QUESTION B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice Option B **Q(a)** Compare **Wien Bridge type** and **Heterodyne type** distortion meters. Mention at least **five points of comparison**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | **S.No** | **Parameter** | **Wien Bridge Type** | **Heterodyne Type** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Principle** | Notch filter rejects fundamental; remaining harmonics measured | Frequency mixing with local oscillator; converts all signals to a fixed IF | | 2 | **Filter Used** | Wien Bridge (RC notch network) | Mixer + Local Oscillator + Narrow Bandpass Filter | | 3 | **Distortion Range** | 0.1% to 100% (moderate range) | 0.001% to 100% (wide range, very low) | | 4 | **Accuracy** | Moderate (¬Ī5% of full scale) | Very High (¬Ī0.5% of full scale) | | 5 | **Frequency Range** | 20 Hz to 20 kHz (audio) | 20 Hz to 1 MHz (wider bandwidth) | | 6 | **Cost & Complexity** | Simple, low cost | Complex, expensive | | 7 | **Application** | General audio, power systems | Laboratory, precision testing, R&D | | 8 | **Tuning** | Manual tuning of RC network | Automatic or precise electronic tuning | | 9 | **Output** | Total THD (single value) | Individual harmonic + THD | | 10 | **Selectivity** | Limited by Q of Wien Bridge (~1.5 to 5) | Very high, set by IF filter | **Conclusion:** Wien Bridge meters are economical and suitable for routine measurements, while Heterodyne meters are preferred when very low distortion levels and individual harmonic analysis are required. --- **Q(b)** Draw the **circuit diagram of a Wien Bridge** used as a notch filter. Derive the expression for its **balance (resonant) frequency** and explain why this frequency is "notched out." **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Wien Bridge Circuit:** The Wien Bridge consists of four arms: - Arm 1: Series RC combination ($R$ and $C$ in series) - Arm 2: Parallel RC combination ($R$ and $C$ in parallel) - Arms 3 and 4: Pure resistors ($R_1$ and $R_2$) **Circuit Diagram (Mermaid):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input v_in] --- B[Node 1] B --- R1[R Series] --- C1[Node 2] C1 --- C2[C Series] --- G1[Ground] B --- R2[R Parallel] --- C3[Node 3] C3 --- C4[C Parallel] --- G2[Ground] Node2 --- D[Output v_out] Node2 --- R3[R1] --- E[Node 4] E --- R4[R2] --- G3[Ground] A --- F[Across Source] F --- G[Source] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style R4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Derivation of Balance Frequency:** Let $Z_1$ = Series RC impedance, and $Z_2$ = Parallel RC impedance. $$Z_1 = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}$$ $$Z_2 = \frac{R \cdot (1/j\omega C)}{R + (1/j\omega C)} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$ **Bridge Balance Condition:** At balance, the potential at the detector node is zero. This occurs when: $$\frac{Z_1}{Z_2} = \frac{R_1}{R_2}$$ Substituting $Z_1$ and $Z_2$: $$\frac{(1 + j\omega RC)^2}{j\omega RC} = \frac{R_1}{R_2}$$ Separating real and imaginary parts, and setting the imaginary part to zero for balance: $$\omega_0 RC = 1$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ **Why $f_0$ is "notched out":** At frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, the two RC arms (series and parallel) produce **equal magnitude but 180¬į phase-shifted voltages**. When these are summed at the output node, they **cancel out completely**, giving zero output. This creates a deep notch at $f_0$ ‚ÄĒ the bridge "rejects" this frequency entirely. - Frequencies **below** $f_0$ (the harmonics $2f_0, 3f_0, \ldots$ are above, but for low-pass applications the opposite applies) experience low attenuation. - The bridge acts as a **band-reject (notch) filter** with center frequency $f_0$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing THD with Distortion Factor:** THD uses **fundamental** as denominator; Distortion Factor uses **total RMS** as denominator. Many students interchange these. *(‚ąí2 marks)* > 2. **Forgetting to square harmonics:** The THD formula requires $V_n^2$ (sum of squares) under a square root. Some students simply add $V_2 + V_3 + ...$ which is **mathematically wrong**. *(‚ąí2 marks)* > 3. **Missing the 100% conversion:** THD is a dimensionless ratio, but it must be expressed as a **percentage**. Forgetting the "√ó 100" is a frequent error. *(‚ąí1 mark)* > 4. **Wien Bridge formula confusion:** The balance condition is $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, not $1/(2\pi \sqrt{LC})$ (that's for an LC tank circuit). *(‚ąí2 marks)* > 5. **Not labeling block diagrams:** In KTU exams, every block in the diagram must be **labeled with its function** (e.g., "Notch Filter," "RMS Voltmeter"). Unlabeled diagrams get partial credit only. *(‚ąí1 to ‚ąí2 marks)* > 6. **Mixing up Voltmeter Mode and Distortion Mode:** The two-step process must be clearly explained; examiners look for explicit mention of "Mode Selector Switch." --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > ūüďĆ **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **Definition:** A Distortion Meter measures the **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** ‚ÄĒ the ratio of harmonic RMS content to the fundamental RMS, expressed in **%**. - **Core Formula (MUST memorize):** $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ - **Fourier Foundation:** Any periodic signal = Fundamental + Sum of Harmonics. This is the bedrock of all distortion analysis. - **Two Common Types:** 1. **Wien Bridge Type** ‚ÄĒ uses Wien Bridge as notch filter; for moderate distortion (0.1% to 100%). 2. **Heterodyne Type** ‚ÄĒ uses mixer + local oscillator + IF filter; for very low distortion (down to 0.001%). - **Two-Step Measurement Procedure:** 1. Voltmeter Mode ‚Üí measure total RMS ($V_1$ or $V_{rms}$). 2. Distortion Mode ‚Üí Wien Bridge rejects fundamental ‚Üí measure harmonic RMS ($V_h$). 3. Compute $\text{THD} = V_h / V_1 \times 100\%$. - **Wien Bridge Balance:** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ ‚ÄĒ the frequency that is "notched out." - **Quality Factor:** $Q = 1/[2 + (R_2/R_1)]$ ‚ÄĒ higher $Q$ ‚Üí sharper notch. - **Distortion Factor** (alternative form): Uses total RMS as denominator; always less than 100%. - **Typical THD Limits:** - Power systems: **<5%** (IEEE 519) - Hi-Fi audio: **<0.01%** - Communication signals: **<2%** - **Key Components of a Wien Bridge Distortion Meter:** Attenuator, Buffer Amplifier, Mode Selector Switch, Wien Bridge Notch Filter, High-Gain AC Amplifier, Average-Responding RMS Voltmeter, Meter Display. - **Why We Care:** Harmonics cause overheating, resonance, equipment malfunction, and audio degradation. THD quantifies these problems. ---

topics.title: Spectrum Analyser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spectrum Analyser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Spectrum Analyser** is a calibrated electronic test and measurement instrument designed to display the **amplitude (power or voltage)** of an input signal as a function of **frequency** within its specified operating range. It is, in essence, a *frequency-domain* counterpart to the oscilloscope, which operates in the *time-domain*.

The horizontal (X) axis represents **frequency (Hz)**, and the vertical (Y) axis represents the **magnitude of the signal component at that frequency**, normally expressed in **dBm (decibels relative to 1 milliwatt)** across a characteristic 50 ő© reference impedance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A spectrum analyser is a frequency-selective, superheterodyne receiver that sweeps through a band of frequencies and displays the signal power at each frequency component, allowing engineers to visualize the spectral content of complex waveforms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a noisy market and you want to know **what pitches of sound** are present, and **how loud** each pitch is. Your eyes are like an **oscilloscope** (they see the total sound waveform in time). Your **ears**, however, automatically decompose the sound into individual pitches ‚ÄĒ that is exactly what a spectrum analyser does to an **electrical signal**.

Another way to think: a **prism** splits white light into its individual color components (frequencies). A **spectrum analyser** is the *electrical prism* ‚ÄĒ it splits a complex electrical waveform into its constituent sinusoidal frequency components and shows their relative strengths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Any periodic, non-sinusoidal signal can be represented (via Fourier Series) as a sum of sinusoids at different frequencies. The spectrum analyser lets you *see* those components directly, even when they cannot be observed in the time domain.

### 1.3 Salient Features Highlighted in KTU Board Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlights ‚ÄĒ Spectrum Analyser**
> - Operates in the **frequency domain**, unlike the oscilloscope.
> - Measures **power spectral density (PSD)** of signals.
> - Modern spectrum analysers combine **swept-tuned (analog)** and **FFT (digital)** techniques.
> - Standard reference impedance is **50 ő©** (some specialised systems use 75 ő©).
> - Displays the **positive frequency axis only** (one-sided spectrum, 0 Hz to f_max).
> - Resolution is governed by the **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)** filter.

### 1.4 Primary Applications (Why Engineers Care)

- **Telecommunications:** Measuring carrier frequencies, sidebands, harmonics, and intermodulation products in transmitters and receivers.
- **EMI / EMC compliance testing:** Detecting spurious emissions in electronic products.
- **Radar and RF engineering:** Characterising pulse spectra.
- **Audio and acoustic engineering:** Harmonic distortion analysis.
- **Oscillator design:** Measuring phase noise and frequency stability.
- **General laboratory use:** Verifying the spectral purity of signal generators and oscillators.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Spectrum Analysers

Spectrum analysers fall into two broad architectural families:

**(A) Swept-Tuned (Superheterodyne) Spectrum Analyser**
- Fully analog signal path up to the video detector.
- A local oscillator (LO) is swept linearly in frequency, and the resulting IF signal is filtered and detected.
- Best suited for **wide frequency spans** and **high-frequency (RF / microwave)** measurements.

**(B) FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) Spectrum Analyser**
- Digitises the input signal with an ADC and computes the Discrete Fourier Transform using the FFT algorithm.
- Best suited for **narrow-band**, **baseband**, and **low-frequency** signals with high resolution and real-time capability.
- Modern hybrid instruments can perform **both** swept and FFT analysis.

### 2.2 Functional Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Swept-Tuned Spectrum Analyser

A swept-tuned spectrum analyser contains the following functional blocks in cascade:

1. **RF Input Connector & Input Attenuator**
   - A calibrated, switchable attenuator (typically 0 to 70 dB in 10 dB steps) protects the mixer and sets the reference level.
2. **Low-Pass / Band-Pass Preselector Filter**
   - Rejects image frequencies and out-of-band interferers before mixing.
3. **Mixer (with Local Oscillator ‚ÄĒ LO)**
   - A non-linear device that multiplies the RF input with the swept LO to produce sum and difference frequencies.
   - The **intermediate frequency (IF)** is fixed (commonly 10.7 MHz, 21.4 MHz, or in modern instruments several hundred MHz for image rejection).
4. **IF Band-Pass Filter (Resolution Filter)**
   - The bandwidth of this filter sets the **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)**.
   - Common shapes: Gaussian, synchronously-tuned multi-pole, or digital.
5. **Logarithmic Amplifier / Linear IF Amplifier**
   - Provides gain and (optionally) logarithmic compression so signals spanning >80 dB can be displayed on one screen.
6. **Envelope / Video Detector**
   - Rectifies and smooths the IF signal to recover its envelope (amplitude).
7. **Video Filter (low-pass)**
   - Smooths the detected signal to reduce noise variance on the trace.
   - Sets the **Video Bandwidth (VBW)**.
8. **Display (CRT / LCD)**
   - Plots detected amplitude vs. tuned frequency on calibrated graticule.

### 2.3 Operating Principle ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step

- The local oscillator is swept linearly from $f_{LO,\,min}$ to $f_{LO,\,max}$.
- For each LO frequency, the mixer output contains a component at $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$.
- When the LO passes through $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ (or $f_{RF} - f_{IF}$), the IF filter passes a signal whose amplitude is proportional to the input signal's amplitude at $f_{RF}$.
- The detector's output is synchronised with the LO sweep, producing a **trace** of amplitude versus frequency.

### 2.4 Key Performance Specifications (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favourite Specifications** ‚ÄĒ Be prepared to define, state the typical range, and explain the trade-off for each of the following:

- **Frequency Range:** The minimum to maximum frequency the analyser can tune (e.g., 9 kHz ‚Äď 26.5 GHz).
- **Frequency Span:** The width of the frequency window displayed on screen: $\text{Span} = f_{stop} - f_{start}$.
- **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW):** The bandwidth of the IF filter. Determines the ability to resolve two closely spaced signals.
- **Video Bandwidth (VBW):** The post-detection low-pass filter bandwidth. Affects displayed noise level.
- **Sweep Time:** Time taken to sweep across the selected span.
- **Sensitivity (Displayed Average Noise Level, DANL):** The lowest signal power the analyser can detect, expressed in dBm. Related to RBW and noise figure.
- **Phase Noise:** The short-term frequency stability of the internal LO, displayed as noise skirts around CW signals.
- **Spurious Responses:** Image, IF feedthrough, intermodulation products.

### 2.5 The RBW‚ÄďSweep Time‚ÄďSpan Relationship

There is a fundamental trade-off governed by the IF filter shape. For a synchronously-tuned, approximately Gaussian filter:

$$Sweep\ Time\ (ST) \approx \frac{k \cdot Span}{(RBW)^2}$$

where $k$ is a constant depending on filter type and shape factor (typically $k = 2$ to $3$ for Gaussian filters, $k = 10$ to $20$ for rectangular filters).

For digital / FFT analysers, the relationship is different because the entire span is acquired in parallel:

$$Sweep\ Time_{FFT} \approx \frac{N}{f_s} = \frac{1}{\Delta f}$$

where $N$ is the FFT size, $f_s$ is the sampling rate, and $\Delta f$ is the FFT bin spacing.

### 2.6 Sensitivity and Noise Floor

The displayed average noise level is given by:

$$DANL\ (dBm) = -174\ dBm/Hz + 10\log_{10}(RBW) + NF$$

where:
- $-174\ dBm/Hz$ is the thermal noise floor at 290 K (i.e., $kT$ at room temperature).
- $NF$ is the noise figure of the analyser in dB.
- $RBW$ is in Hz (linear).

> **KTU Insight:** Halving the RBW lowers the noise floor by **3 dB**, improving sensitivity, but lengthens sweep time.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$ | Heterodyne mixing produces fixed IF |
| 2 | $f_{image} = f_{RF} \pm 2 f_{IF}$ | Image frequency rejected by preselector |
| 3 | $Span = f_{stop} - f_{start}$ | Width of frequency display |
| 4 | $DANL = -174 + 10\log_{10}(RBW) + NF$ | Sensitivity in dBm |
| 5 | $Sweep\ Time \approx k \cdot Span / (RBW)^2$ | Trade-off: narrow RBW ‚Üí slow sweep |
| 6 | $\Delta f_{FFT} = f_s / N$ | FFT bin resolution |
| 7 | $P_{dBm} = 10\log_{10}(P_{mW})$ | Power in dBm |
| 8 | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{P \cdot R}$ with $R = 50\ \Omega$ | Convert between power and voltage display |
| 9 | $6\ dB\ \text{down\ rule}$ | RBW definition: 3 dB or 6 dB bandwidth convention |
| 10 | $Shape\ Factor = \frac{BW_{60\,dB}}{BW_{3\,dB}}$ | IF filter selectivity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** The standard reference impedance for spectrum analyser measurements is **50 ő©**. Any dBm reading assumes this termination.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wireless product design:** Spectrum analysers certify that transmitters meet spectral mask requirements (e.g., 3GPP, FCC, ETSI).
- **EMC pre-compliance testing:** Identifies spurious emissions that could fail certification.
- **Radar/EW systems:** Characterise wideband chirp signals and pulse compression waveforms.
- **Audio engineering:** High-end audio DACs and amplifiers are characterised for harmonic and intermodulation distortion.
- **Education and research:** Fourier theory is visualised in the lab directly with a spectrum analyser.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ Heterodyne Frequency Translation

**Given:** An RF input signal at $f_{RF}$ is mixed with a local oscillator signal at $f_{LO}$.

**To prove:** The mixer output contains a component at $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Mixer as a Multiplier:**
A non-linear device (idealised as a multiplier) multiplies the two input signals.

$$
v_{mixer}(t) = v_{RF}(t) \cdot v_{LO}(t)
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Express the Input Signals:**
Let $v_{RF}(t) = A_{RF}\cos(2\pi f_{RF} t)$ and $v_{LO}(t) = A_{LO}\cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Multiply Using the Trigonometric Product-to-Sum Identity:**

$$
\cos\alpha \cdot \cos\beta = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[\cos(\alpha-\beta) + \cos(\alpha+\beta)\right]
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Substitute:**

$$
v_{mixer}(t) = A_{RF}A_{LO}\cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\left[\cos\big(2\pi(f_{RF}-f_{LO})t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi(f_{RF}+f_{LO})t\big)\right]
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Take Absolute Value of the Difference Term:**
The IF band-pass filter selects the lower sideband at the difference frequency.

$$
f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Note that for the spectrum analyser, the LO is swept linearly:**
A voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) driven by the sawtooth sweep ramp generates:

$$
f_{LO}(t) = f_{center} + \gamma \cdot t
$$

where $\gamma$ is the sweep rate in Hz/s. As $f_{LO}$ changes, $f_{IF}$ is satisfied in turn for each $f_{RF}$ present in the input spectrum, and the IF filter passes these sequentially.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Sensitivity (DANL) from Thermal Noise

**Given:** The input is terminated in 50 ő© at room temperature ($T_0 = 290$ K).

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Thermal Noise Power Spectral Density:**

$$
N_0 = k_B T_0
$$

where $k_B = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is Boltzmann's constant.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Evaluate in dBm/Hz:**

$$
10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{k_B T_0}{1\ mW}\right) = 10\log_{10}(4.00 \times 10^{-21}) \approx -174\ dBm/Hz
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Noise Power in the RBW Filter:**
The IF filter integrates the noise over its bandwidth:

$$
N = k_B T_0 \cdot RBW
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Convert to dBm:**

$$
N_{dBm} = -174\ dBm/Hz + 10\log_{10}(RBW\ \text{in\ Hz})
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Add the Analyser's Noise Figure $NF$:**
The internal electronics contribute additional noise, characterised by $NF$ in dB.

$$
DANL = -174 + 10\log_{10}(RBW) + NF
$$

**Numerical Example:**
For $RBW = 10\ \text{kHz}$ and $NF = 20\ \text{dB}$:

$$
DANL = -174 + 10\log_{10}(10{,}000) + 20 = -174 + 40 + 20 = -114\ dBm
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The analyser can detect a CW signal as low as **‚Äď114 dBm** (about **0.04 ¬ĶV** across 50 ő©) when its RBW is set to 10 kHz.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚ÄĒ Resolution of Two Closely Spaced Tones

**Question:** Can two CW tones separated by $\Delta f$ be resolved on the screen?

**Criterion (Rayleigh-like for spectrum analysers):** Two equal-amplitude tones are said to be resolved when the dip between their peaks is at least **3 dB** (or in stricter definitions, **6 dB**).

**Step 1:** For a Gaussian IF filter of 3-dB bandwidth $RBW$, the response at frequency offset $\Delta f$ from the centre is:

$$
H(\Delta f) = e^{-\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\sigma}\right)^2 \ln 2}
$$

where $\sigma = RBW / 2$.

**Step 2:** The minimum resolvable separation is approximately:

$$
\Delta f_{min} \approx RBW
$$

**Step 3:** For a rectangular IF filter (synchronously-tuned n-pole), the separation must be **wider** to resolve two equal tones, leading to the empirical rule:

$$
\Delta f_{min} \approx k \cdot RBW, \quad k = 1.5\ \text{to}\ 2.0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Resolution does **not** equal RBW exactly. The shape factor of the IF filter (e.g., 60 dB / 3 dB ratio) determines how close two unequal tones can be before one is masked by the other's filter skirt.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Swept-Tuned vs. FFT Spectrum Analyser

| Parameter | Swept-Tuned (Superheterodyne) | FFT-Based |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Path** | Analog up to detector | ADC at front end, then digital |
| **Frequency Range** | Up to mm-wave (50+ GHz with external mixers) | Limited by ADC bandwidth (typically ‚Č§ 6 GHz direct) |
| **Span** | Very wide, real-time impossible | Limited by ADC rate; real-time capable |
| **Resolution (RBW)** | Set by analog IF filter | Set by FFT bin width $f_s/N$ |
| **Sweep Time** | Slow for narrow RBW (Span/RBW¬≤) | Independent of RBW; fixed by $N$ |
| **Sensitivity** | Good; can be improved with pre-amp | Limited by ADC noise and aperture |
| **Real-time capability** | No (it is a sequential swept receiver) | Yes (if capture memory is sufficient) |
| **Best for** | RF / microwave, EMI, spurs | Baseband, audio, vibration, narrowband |
| **Phase noise** | Limited by internal LO | Very low (no LO needed) |
| **Cost & complexity** | High at mm-wave | Lower for low-frequency applications |

### 3.5 Numerical Solved Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A spectrum analyser with $NF = 15$ dB is set with $RBW = 1$ kHz. The reference level is set to 0 dBm. Find (a) the displayed noise floor and (b) the sweep time if the span is 100 kHz and the filter constant $k = 2.5$.

**Solution:**

**(a) DANL Calculation:**

$$
DANL = -174 + 10\log_{10}(1000) + 15 = -174 + 30 + 15 = -129\ dBm
$$

**(b) Sweep Time:**

$$
Sweep\ Time = \frac{k \cdot Span}{(RBW)^2} = \frac{2.5 \times 100{,}000}{(1{,}000)^2} = \frac{250{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.25\ s
$$

> **Final Answer:** $DANL = -129$ dBm; $Sweep\ Time = 250$ ms.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Swept-Tuned (Superheterodyne) Spectrum Analyser

The Mermaid diagram below maps the functional architecture and signal flow of a classical swept-tuned spectrum analyser, including control paths from the sweep generator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RFIn[RF Input Connector 50 ohm] --> ATTN[Input Attenuator 0 to 70 dB]
    ATTN --> PRE[Preselector Low pass Filter]
    PRE --> MIX[Mixer]
    LO[Voltage Controlled Local Oscillator Swept] --> MIX
    MIX --> IFFIL[IF Band pass Filter Resolution Filter RBW]
    IFFIL --> LOGAMP[Logarithmic IF Amplifier]
    LOGAMP --> DET[Envelope Detector]
    DET --> VIDFIL[Video Low pass Filter VBW]
    VIDFIL --> DISP[Display CRT or LCD]
    SWGEN[Sawtooth Sweep Generator] -.drives.-> LO
    SWGEN -.horizontal axis.-> DISP
    DET -.vertical axis.-> DISP
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of an FFT Spectrum Analyser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Signal] --> AA[Anti aliasing Low pass Filter]
    AA --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> WIN[Window Function Multiplier]
    WIN --> FFT[FFT Processor]
    FFT --> MAG[Magnitude Calculator]
    MAG --> VIDFIL[Video Filter]
    VIDFIL --> DISP[Display Log or Linear Scale]
    CLOCK[Sample Clock f sub s] -.timing.-> ADC
    CLOCK -.timing.-> FFT
```

### 4.3 Display Conventions ‚ÄĒ What the Screen Shows

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SCREEN[Spectrum Analyser Display]
    SCREEN --> XAXIS[Horizontal Axis Frequency Log or Linear]
    SCREEN --> YAXIS[Vertical Axis Amplitude dBm or dBuV]
    SCREEN --> GRAT[Calibrated Graticule 10 dB per division typical]
    SCREEN --> MARK[Marker Readout Frequency and Level]
    SCREEN --> PEAK[Peak Search Function]
```

### 4.4 Signal-Flow Summary (Hybrid View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time Domain Signal v of t] --> B[Fourier Decomposition]
    B --> C[Frequency Domain Representation V of f]
    C --> D[Plot Amplitude vs Frequency]
    D --> E[Identification of Fundamentals Harmonics Spurs and Noise]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Hint for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw the block diagram, mark the **three signal-flow paths** explicitly:
> 1. The main **RF ‚Üí IF ‚Üí Video ‚Üí Display** path.
> 2. The **Sweep Generator ‚Üí LO** control path.
> 3. The **Sweep Generator ‚Üí Display X-axis** synchronisation path.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Model]**
*Define a spectrum analyser. State any three of its important specifications.*

**Model Answer:**

A spectrum analyser is an electronic instrument that displays the **amplitude of signal components as a function of frequency**, providing a frequency-domain view of an electrical signal.

Three important specifications are:

1. **Frequency Range** ‚ÄĒ the minimum to maximum frequency the instrument can measure.
2. **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)** ‚ÄĒ the smallest frequency difference the analyser can distinguish.
3. **Displayed Average Noise Level (DANL)** ‚ÄĒ a measure of sensitivity, indicating the smallest detectable signal.

*(Other acceptable specifications: Span, Sweep Time, Phase Noise, Spurious Responses, Video Bandwidth.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the role of the Resolution Bandwidth (RBW) filter in a spectrum analyser? How does it affect sweep time?*

**Model Answer:**

The **RBW filter** is an IF band-pass filter whose bandwidth determines the smallest frequency difference between two signals that the analyser can resolve as separate. A narrower RBW gives better frequency resolution and lowers the noise floor by **3 dB per halving**, improving sensitivity.

However, a narrower RBW also **increases the sweep time** because the IF filter takes longer to settle. The relationship is:

$$
Sweep\ Time \propto \frac{Span}{(RBW)^2}
$$

Thus, **halving RBW quadruples the sweep time**.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is typically divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives below.

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **swept-tuned superheterodyne spectrum analyser**. Label all blocks. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the following specifications with their significance: **(i) Resolution Bandwidth, (ii) Video Bandwidth, (iii) Spurious Responses, (iv) Phase Noise**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Block Diagram & Working (7 marks):**

```
[RF IN] ‚Üí [Attenuator] ‚Üí [Preselector LPF] ‚Üí [Mixer] ‚Üź [Swept LO]
            ‚Üí [IF BPF (RBW)] ‚Üí [Log IF Amp] ‚Üí [Detector] ‚Üí [Video LPF (VBW)] ‚Üí [Display]
            ‚ÜĎ                                                                              
        [Sweep Generator ‚Üí drives LO & Display X-axis]
```

**Step-by-step working:**

1. The RF input first passes through a **calibrated attenuator** (0‚Äď70 dB) to protect the mixer and set the reference level.
2. A **preselector low-pass filter** rejects image frequencies and out-of-band signals.
3. The **mixer** multiplies the RF input with the swept **local oscillator** signal. The output contains sum and difference frequencies; the difference is fixed at the **intermediate frequency (IF)**, typically 10.7 MHz, 21.4 MHz, or higher.
4. The **IF band-pass filter** (the resolution filter) selects only frequencies near $f_{IF}$. Its bandwidth defines the **RBW**.
5. A **logarithmic IF amplifier** compresses the wide dynamic range (often >80 dB) so the display can accommodate both small and large signals.
6. An **envelope detector** rectifies the IF signal to recover its amplitude.
7. A **video low-pass filter** smooths the detected envelope to reduce displayed noise (sets the **VBW**).
8. The detected signal drives the **Y-axis (vertical)** of the display, while the **sweep generator** drives the **X-axis (horizontal)** synchronously with the LO frequency.
9. As the LO sweeps linearly, the analyser sequentially measures the input amplitude at every frequency in the span, producing the **frequency-domain display**.

**(b) Specifications (7 marks):**

**(i) Resolution Bandwidth (RBW):** The 3-dB bandwidth of the IF filter. Determines the analyser's ability to resolve two closely spaced signals. Smaller RBW ‚Üí better resolution and lower noise floor but longer sweep time.

**(ii) Video Bandwidth (VBW):** The bandwidth of the post-detection low-pass filter. Smoothing the displayed trace reduces noise variance, making weak signals more visible. Smaller VBW ‚Üí smoother trace but slower sweep.

**(iii) Spurious Responses:** Unwanted signals displayed by the analyser that are **not present** in the actual input. These include **image responses, IF feedthrough, and intermodulation products** generated in the mixer. Specification is given in dBc (decibels below carrier).

**(iv) Phase Noise:** Short-term random frequency fluctuations of the internal LO, appearing as noise "skirts" around CW signals on the display. Specification is in **dBc/Hz at a given offset** (e.g., ‚Äď100 dBc/Hz at 10 kHz offset). Low phase noise is critical for measuring weak signals near a strong carrier.

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Model ‚Äď Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **swept-tuned** and **FFT-based** spectrum analysers. Mention the relative merits of each in at least six points. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A spectrum analyser has a noise figure of **18 dB** and an RBW of **3 kHz**. Calculate its displayed average noise level (DANL). If the RBW is reduced to **300 Hz**, what is the new noise floor? Comment on the implication for sweep time. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Swept-Tuned | FFT-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Heterodyne sweeping | Digital sampling + FFT |
| Frequency range | Up to mm-wave (with external mixers) | Limited by ADC bandwidth |
| Best suited for | RF, microwave, EMI | Baseband, audio, vibration |
| Real-time analysis | No (sequential) | Yes (parallel FFT) |
| Sweep time dependence | Span / RBW¬≤ | Independent of RBW (set by $N/f_s$) |
| Sensitivity to weak signals | Excellent (with preamp) | Limited by ADC noise |
| Phase noise performance | Dominated by internal LO | Very low (no LO) |
| Cost at high frequencies | Very high | Low‚Äďmoderate |

**(b) Noise Floor Calculation (7 marks):**

**Case 1: RBW = 3 kHz**

$$
DANL_1 = -174 + 10\log_{10}(3{,}000) + 18
$$

$$
= -174 + 10 \times 3.477 + 18 = -174 + 34.77 + 18
$$

$$
\boxed{DANL_1 = -121.23\ dBm}
$$

**Case 2: RBW = 300 Hz**

$$
DANL_2 = -174 + 10\log_{10}(300) + 18
$$

$$
= -174 + 10 \times 2.477 + 18 = -174 + 24.77 + 18
$$

$$
\boxed{DANL_2 = -131.23\ dBm}
$$

**Improvement in Sensitivity:**

$$
\Delta DANL = -131.23 - (-121.23) = -10\ dB
$$

> **Comment:** Halving the RBW ten times (from 3 kHz to 300 Hz) lowered the noise floor by **10 dB**, confirming the **3 dB per halving rule**. However, the sweep time has increased by a factor of:

$$
\frac{Span / (300)^2}{Span / (3000)^2} = \left(\frac{3000}{300}\right)^2 = 100
$$

> **Implication:** The analyser now requires **100 times longer** to complete one sweep, illustrating the classic **sensitivity-vs-speed** trade-off in spectrum analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units mistake:** When computing $10\log_{10}(RBW)$, students often forget that RBW must be in **Hz**, not kHz. Plugging RBW = 3 (instead of 3000) is the most common mark-losing error. **Always convert to Hz before computing.**
> 2. **Confusing RBW and VBW:** RBW sets frequency resolution; VBW only smooths the displayed trace. Reducing VBW does *not* change the analyser's true sensitivity, only the visual noise.
> 3. **Sweep-time formula misuse:** $Sweep\ Time = k \cdot Span / (RBW)^2$ applies only to **swept-tuned** analysers, not FFT types. Examiners deduct marks if applied to FFT problems.
> 4. **Forgetting the noise figure:** Many students compute only $-174 + 10\log_{10}(RBW)$ and ignore the analyser NF, losing 1‚Äď2 marks.
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows:** KTU valuators expect clearly labelled arrows showing signal flow direction. A diagram without directional arrows or with unlabelled blocks loses up to 2 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Spectrum Analyser**

- ‚úÖ Spectrum analyser = **frequency-domain** instrument; oscilloscope = time-domain.
- ‚úÖ Standard reference impedance is **50 ő©**; dBm assumes this.
- ‚úÖ Two main types: **Swept-Tuned (superheterodyne)** and **FFT-based**.
- ‚úÖ Swept-Tuned block flow: **RF IN ‚Üí Attenuator ‚Üí Preselector ‚Üí Mixer ‚Üí IF Filter (RBW) ‚Üí Log Amp ‚Üí Detector ‚Üí Video Filter (VBW) ‚Üí Display**.
- ‚úÖ Heterodyne relation: $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$.
- ‚úÖ Image frequency: $f_{image} = f_{RF} \pm 2f_{IF}$ (rejected by preselector).
- ‚úÖ $DANL = -174 + 10\log_{10}(RBW) + NF$ (dBm).
- ‚úÖ Sweep time for swept analyser: $Sweep\ Time = k \cdot Span / (RBW)^2$.
- ‚úÖ FFT bin width: $\Delta f = f_s / N$.
- ‚úÖ Halving RBW ‚Üí **3 dB lower noise floor** but **4√ó longer sweep time**.
- ‚úÖ Lowering VBW ‚Üí smoother trace but slower sweep (does **not** change true sensitivity).
- ‚úÖ Shape factor = $BW_{60\,dB} / BW_{3\,dB}$ measures IF filter selectivity.
- ‚úÖ Key specs to remember: Frequency Range, Span, RBW, VBW, Sweep Time, DANL, Phase Noise, Spurious Responses.
- ‚úÖ Two equal tones are resolved when their separation $\geq$ RBW (or $1.5 \times$ RBW for non-Gaussian filters).
- ‚úÖ Phase noise is specified in **dBc/Hz at offset** ‚ÄĒ critical for measuring weak signals near a strong carrier.
- ‚úÖ **Real-time analysis** is possible with FFT but **not** with classic swept analysers.
- ‚úÖ $k_B T_0$ at 290 K = $-174$ dBm/Hz ‚ÄĒ fundamental noise floor reference.
- ‚úÖ Convert dBm to voltage via $V_{rms} = \sqrt{P \cdot R}$ with $R = 50\ \Omega$ (e.g., 0 dBm = 224 mV across 50 ő©).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Spectrum Analyser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT403 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Digital Spectrum Analyser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Digital Spectrum Analyser (DSA)** is an electronic test and measurement instrument that converts a time-domain input signal into its constituent frequency components using digital signal processing techniques, primarily the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** algorithm, and displays the resulting **amplitude versus frequency** spectrum on a digital display. It is the digital successor to the traditional analog (swept-tuned) spectrum analyser and provides superior accuracy, stability, flexibility, and post-processing capabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A Digital Spectrum Analyser is essentially a dedicated, real-time digitiser followed by a high-speed computational engine (DSP/FPGA) that computes the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), usually via the FFT algorithm, on sampled data windows of the input signal to produce a real-time frequency-domain representation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **prism** splitting white light (which contains all colours mixed together) into its individual rainbow components. Each colour represents a different frequency.

- The **white light** = your complex input signal (sum of many sine waves)
- The **prism** = the FFT processor inside the DSA
- The **rainbow spread out** = the frequency spectrum displayed on screen
- The **intensity of each colour band** = the amplitude of that frequency component

Now, instead of doing this for light, a Digital Spectrum Analyser does it for **electrical signals** ‚ÄĒ radio waves, audio, vibration signals, communication carriers, etc. ‚ÄĒ by mathematically separating them into their frequency "colours" using the Fourier Transform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Digital"?** The analyser samples the input signal with an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), stores it in memory, and then performs all the frequency analysis numerically ‚ÄĒ unlike its analog cousin which used sweeping bandpass filters.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Maximum Frequency (f_max)** of a digital spectrum analyser is governed by the **Nyquist‚ÄďShannon Sampling Theorem**:

$$f_{max} = \frac{f_s}{2}$$

where $f_s$ is the ADC sampling frequency. This is a hard physical limit ‚ÄĒ **no signal beyond $f_s/2$ can be correctly analysed**.

- Standard units used:
  - **Amplitude**: dBm, dBV, dB¬ĶV, or Volts (linear)
  - **Frequency**: Hz, kHz, MHz, GHz
  - **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)**: Hz
  - **Sweep Time**: seconds
  - **Span**: Hz (full frequency window being viewed)
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block-Level Breakdown

A Digital Spectrum Analyser is built from several tightly integrated functional blocks. Each block performs a specific digital-domain operation:

1. **Input Attenuator** ‚Äď Protects the ADC from large input signals and adjusts the dynamic range so the signal optimally fills the ADC's input range. Typical attenuation steps: 0 dB, 10 dB, 20 dB, ..., 70 dB.
2. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter** ‚Äď A critical analogue filter placed *before* the ADC. It removes all frequency components above $f_s/2$ to prevent **aliasing** (false frequencies appearing in the output).
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** ‚Äď Samples the filtered input at $f_s$ samples/second, producing a discrete digital bitstream. Resolution typically 12‚Äď16 bits.
4. **Memory / Acquisition Buffer** ‚Äď Stores a block (frame) of $N$ samples for processing.
5. **Windowing Function** ‚Äď Multiplies the time-domain data by a window (Hann, Hamming, Blackman, Kaiser) to reduce **spectral leakage** caused by finite-length FFT.
6. **FFT Processor (DSP / FPGA)** ‚Äď Computes the Discrete Fourier Transform using the Cooley‚ÄďTukey FFT algorithm. Converts $N$ time-domain samples into $N/2$ complex frequency bins.
7. **Magnitude Computation Block** ‚Äď Takes the complex FFT output and computes the magnitude: $\vert X[k] \vert = \sqrt{Re^2 + Im^2}$.
8. **Logarithmic Conversion (dB)** ‚Äď Converts the linear magnitude to dB scale for display: $dB = 20 \log_{10}(\vert X[k] \vert)$.
9. **Display / Video Processing** ‚Äď Renders the spectrum trace (Amplitude vs Frequency) on the LCD/graphical display. May also do averaging, peak hold, trace math.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Block

- **Why Anti-Aliasing Filter?** Because once aliasing occurs, you cannot separate the real signal from the aliased one later ‚ÄĒ the damage is irreversible. Hence the anti-alias filter is placed *before* the ADC.
- **Why Windowing?** The FFT assumes the signal is periodic within the captured window. Real signals are not. A rectangular window (no tapering) causes **spectral leakage** ‚ÄĒ energy from one frequency bin "leaks" into neighbours. Tapering windows like Hann or Hamming reduce leakage at the cost of slightly wider main lobe.
- **Why dB scale?** Spectrums span enormous dynamic ranges (small signals buried next to large carriers). The logarithmic dB scale compresses this range into a viewable trace.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU examiner can ask on this topic. **Memorise these.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Boundary / Units |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Nyquist Frequency Limit | $f_{max} = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | $f_s$ = sampling rate | $f_{max}$ in Hz |
| 2 | Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = \dfrac{f_s}{N}$ | $N$ = number of FFT points | Hz per bin |
| 3 | Equivalent Time Resolution | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_{acq}}$ | $T_{acq}$ = acquisition window time | Hz |
| 4 | FFT Computational Cost | $N \log_2 N$ (complex mults) | For $N$-point radix-2 FFT | Floating-point ops |
| 5 | Number of Useful Bins | $N/2$ (real input) | Nyquist symmetry | Integer |
| 6 | Linear Amplitude from FFT | $A = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert X[k] \vert}{N}$ | $X[k]$ = FFT bin value (rectangular window) | Volts/Units |
| 7 | dB Conversion (Power) | $P_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)$ | $P_{ref}$ = 1 mW (dBm) or 1 W (dBW) | dB |
| 8 | dB Conversion (Voltage) | $V_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V}{V_{ref}}\right)$ | $V_{ref}$ = 1 V (dBV) or 1 ¬ĶV (dB¬ĶV) | dB |
| 9 | Spectral Leakage Reduction | Hann window: 3 dB main-lobe width = $1.5 \cdot \Delta f$ | Trade-off: wider lobe | Hz |
| 10 | SNR of ADC | $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB | $N$ = ADC bit resolution | dB |
| 11 | ENOB | $ENOB = \dfrac{SNR - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Effective number of bits | Bits |
| 12 | Record Length | $T_{rec} = \dfrac{N}{f_s}$ | Time captured per FFT | Seconds |
| 13 | Span of Analyser | $Span = f_{stop} - f_{start}$ | Frequency window viewed | Hz |
| 14 | RBW Definition | $RBW \approx \dfrac{f_s}{N}$ | Resolution bandwidth | Hz |

> **Note:** All tables above have been written using the `\vert` latex operator instead of the vertical pipe `|` character to prevent markdown table breakage. The KTU Formula Sheet is your exam-day lifeline.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Digital Spectrum Analysers are indispensable in:

- **Telecommunications** ‚ÄĒ verifying carrier frequencies, harmonic distortion, channel power, and EMC compliance.
- **RF Design & Test** ‚ÄĒ measuring filter roll-off, mixer spurious products, oscillator phase noise.
- **Audio Engineering** ‚ÄĒ identifying feedback frequencies, room modes, equalisation.
- **Vibration & Mechanical Diagnostics** ‚ÄĒ analysing rotating machinery, bearings, gearboxes via accelerometers.
- **EMC/EMI Compliance Testing** ‚ÄĒ pre-compliance swept scans of radiated/conducted emissions.
- **Radar & Electronic Warfare** ‚ÄĒ intercepting and classifying unknown RF emissions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Sampling Rate, Record Length, and Frequency Resolution

This is the most fundamental derivation a KTU examiner will test. We derive why $\Delta f = f_s / N$.

**Starting Principle:** The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of an $N$-point sequence is defined as:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Time Quantisation.**  
The samples are taken at uniform time intervals. The sampling period is:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Total Acquisition Time (Record Length).**  
For $N$ samples, the total time captured is:

$$T_{rec} = N \cdot T_s = \frac{N}{f_s}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Frequency Spacing of DFT Bins.**  
The DFT produces $N$ output bins, distributed uniformly from $0$ to $f_s$ (or $-f_s/2$ to $+f_s/2$ in two-sided view). The spacing between adjacent bins is:

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Equivalently, Using Time.**  
Substituting $f_s = 1/T_s$:

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{N \cdot T_s} = \frac{1}{T_{rec}}$$

This is the **fundamental relationship**: the frequency resolution of a digital spectrum analyser is the reciprocal of the total acquisition time window.

> **Conclusion:** To get finer frequency resolution, you must capture a **longer time record**, *or* use a *larger FFT size* at the same sampling rate. You cannot get arbitrarily fine resolution at high sweep rates.

### 3.2 Derivation: ADC Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNor) and Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Quantisation Noise Power.**  
For an ideal $N$-bit ADC, the theoretical quantisation noise is uniformly distributed across the Nyquist band ($0$ to $f_s/2$). The RMS quantisation noise voltage is:

$$v_{q,rms} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N \cdot 2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N+1}\sqrt{3}}$$

where $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$ is one LSB size, and $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale input range.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Full-Scale Sinusoidal Signal Power.**  
For a sine wave of peak amplitude $V_{FS}/2$ (so it just fills the ADC range), the RMS amplitude is:

$$v_{sig,rms} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ SNR Computation.**  
$$SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{v_{sig,rms}}{v_{q,rms}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{FS}/(2\sqrt{2})}{V_{FS}/(2^{N+1}\sqrt{3})}\right)$$

$$SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2^{N+1}\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(2^{N} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Simplification Using $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.0206$.**

$$SNR = N \cdot 6.0206 + 20 \log_{10}(\sqrt{1.5}) = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{SNR_{ADC} = 6.02 \cdot N + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Effective Number of Bits (ENOB).** Rearranging the above for $N$:

$$ENOB = \frac{SNR_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02}$$

This tells you the "real" usable bits of an ADC after non-idealities like clock jitter, harmonic distortion, and noise are considered.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example ‚ÄĒ Full Solution

**Problem (Typical KTU 14-mark type):** A digital spectrum analyser uses an ADC with $f_s = 100$ MHz and computes a 1024-point FFT. Calculate (a) the maximum analysable frequency, (b) the frequency resolution, (c) the record length, and (d) the number of frequency bins displayed.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Maximum Frequency (Nyquist):**

$$f_{max} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{2} = 50 \text{ MHz}$$

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Frequency Resolution:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{1024} \approx 97{,}656 \text{ Hz} \approx 97.66 \text{ kHz}$$

**Part (c) ‚ÄĒ Record Length:**

$$T_{rec} = \frac{N}{f_s} = \frac{1024}{100 \times 10^6} = 10.24 \text{ ¬Ķs}$$

**Part (d) ‚ÄĒ Number of Bins Displayed (Real-Input Case):** For real input signals, DFT symmetry means only the first $N/2$ bins contain unique information (positive frequencies up to $f_s/2$):

$$N_{bins} = \frac{1024}{2} = 512 \text{ bins}$$

> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Note that the resolution bandwidth (RBW) in a digital analyser is *not* set by analog filters as in swept analysers ‚ÄĒ it is **inherently** $\Delta f = f_s/N$ and is *tied* to the FFT size and sample rate. To get a finer RBW, the analyser can either lower $f_s$ (sacrificing span) or use a longer FFT (more memory and time).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Digital Spectrum Analyser

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete signal flow inside a Digital Spectrum Analyser, from RF/analog input to the digital display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp[RF INPUT] --> att[INPUT ATTENUATOR 0 to 70 dB]
    att --> aaf[ANTI-ALIASING LOW PASS FILTER]
    aaf --> lna[PREAMPLIFIER LNA]
    lna --> adc[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER ADC]
    adc --> buf[ACQUISITION BUFFER MEMORY N SAMPLES]
    buf --> win[WINDOWING FUNCTION HANN HAMMING]
    win --> fft[FFT PROCESSOR DSP FPGA N log2 N]
    fft --> mag[MAGNITUDE COMPUTATION SQRT Re2 plus Im2]
    mag --> log[LOGARITHMIC CONVERTER dB]
    log --> vid[VIDEO PROCESSOR AVERAGE PEAK HOLD]
    vid --> dsp[GRAPHICAL DISPLAY LCD]

    clk[TIMEBASE AND CLOCK GENERATOR] -.controls.-> adc
    clk -.controls.-> fft
    ctl[FRONT PANEL KEYBOARD] -.configures.-> att
    ctl -.configures.-> clk
    ctl -.configures.-> win
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture ‚ÄĒ Acquisition, Processing, and Display Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[STAGE 1 ANALOG FRONT END]
        A1[RF INPUT BNC] --> A2[STEP ATTENUATOR]
        A2 --> A3[PRESELECTOR FILTER]
```
