```mermaid
        A4 --> A5[ANTI ALIAS LPF]
    end

    subgraph B[STAGE 2 DIGITISATION]

    subgraph C[STAGE 3 DIGITAL PROCESSING]
        B3 --> C1[WINDOW MULTIPLIER]

    end

    subgraph D[STAGE 4 PRESENTATION]
        C4 --> D1[TRACE BUFFER]

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are purely **alphanumeric** (no reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph` used as standalone IDs).
- All labels are inside double quotes and contain **no markdown formatting** (no `**bold**`, no `*italic*`, no HTML tables).
- Reserved words like `STAGE` are used only inside double-quoted labels, not as bare node IDs.

### 4.4 Signal Flow Description (For KTU Diagram-Marking Questions)

For a 7-mark diagram question, the expected KTU board answer should include:

1. The signal enters the **input attenuator** to control amplitude.
2. It passes through a **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** to satisfy the Nyquist criterion.
3. The **ADC** samples the waveform at rate $f_s$.
4. A block of $N$ samples is stored in **memory**.
5. The samples are multiplied by a **windowing function** to suppress leakage.
6. The **FFT processor** computes the spectrum.
7. The **magnitude** and **dB** are calculated.
8. The processed data is rendered on a **graphical display**.

A labelled diagram showing all eight stages typically scores 5‚Äď6 marks. A short paragraph explaining the function of each block earns the remaining 1‚Äď2 marks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions are short-answer, definition-type questions. The answers given here are KTU board-style model answers ‚ÄĒ concise, keyword-rich, and exactly 3-mark length.

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]**  
**Define a Digital Spectrum Analyser. List any two advantages it has over a conventional analog spectrum analyser.**

**Model Answer:**  
A Digital Spectrum Analyser (DSA) is an instrument that uses digital signal processing ‚ÄĒ primarily the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) ‚ÄĒ to convert a sampled time-domain signal into its frequency-domain representation and display amplitude versus frequency.

**Advantages over analog (swept-tuned) analysers:**

1. It can capture and display **transient and non-repetitive** signals (real-time analysis) because it processes an entire block of samples simultaneously.
2. It offers much higher **amplitude accuracy and stability** because frequency reference is derived from a digital clock/crystal, eliminating analog sweep errors.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for the two advantages ‚ÄĒ total 3 marks.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]**  
**What is the Nyquist frequency? Why is an anti-aliasing filter necessary before the ADC in a digital spectrum analyser?**

**Model Answer:**  
The **Nyquist frequency** is half the sampling rate: $f_N = f_s/2$. It is the highest frequency component that can be unambiguously reconstructed from samples taken at rate $f_s$.

An **anti-aliasing filter** (a low-pass filter with cutoff at $f_s/2$) is necessary *before* the ADC because any input frequency content above $f_s/2$ would otherwise "fold back" into the lower frequency range as **alias frequencies**, distorting the displayed spectrum. Once aliasing occurs in the digital domain, it cannot be removed by post-processing.

*(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for the necessity of the filter ‚ÄĒ total 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 scheme, Part B questions of modules carry 14 marks with an internal choice. Each sub-part is 7 marks. Two alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below.

#### ‚ĚĖ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]

**(a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a digital spectrum analyser. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The block diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The functional description is:

1. **Input Attenuator** ‚ÄĒ Adjusts the signal level to fit the ADC's input range; provides overload protection.
2. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter** ‚ÄĒ A sharp-cutoff analogue filter at $f_s/2$ to prevent aliasing.
3. **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA)** ‚ÄĒ Boosts weak signals above the ADC's noise floor.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** ‚ÄĒ Samples the input at rate $f_s$ and produces a digital bitstream of $N$ samples.
5. **Acquisition Memory** ‚ÄĒ Stores the $N$ samples as a block ready for FFT.
6. **Windowing Function** ‚ÄĒ Multiplies the time samples by a window (Hann, Hamming) to minimise spectral leakage.
7. **FFT Processor (DSP/FPGA)** ‚ÄĒ Computes the FFT of the windowed samples; produces complex frequency-domain data.
8. **Magnitude / dB Conversion** ‚ÄĒ Calculates $\vert X[k] \vert$ and $20 \log_{10}(\vert X[k] \vert)$.
9. **Video Processor & Display** ‚ÄĒ Performs averaging, peak hold, and renders the final amplitude-versus-frequency trace on the LCD.

*(3 marks for labelled diagram + 4 marks for explanations of each block = 7 marks.)*

**(b) A digital spectrum analyser uses a 2048-point FFT at a sampling rate of 50 MHz. Compute (i) frequency resolution, (ii) maximum analysable frequency, (iii) record length, and (iv) the RBW if the Hann window is applied. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $N = 2048$, $f_s = 50$ MHz.

**(i) Frequency Resolution:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{2048} = 24{,}414.06 \text{ Hz} \approx 24.41 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Maximum Analysable Frequency (Nyquist):**

$$f_{max} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{2} = 25 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iii) Record Length:**

$$T_{rec} = \frac{N}{f_s} = \frac{2048}{50 \times 10^6} = 40.96 \text{ ¬Ķs}$$

**(iv) RBW with Hann Window:**  
For a Hann window, the equivalent noise bandwidth is approximately $1.5 \times$ the bin width:

$$RBW_{Hann} \approx 1.5 \cdot \Delta f = 1.5 \times 24{,}414.06 = 36{,}621.1 \text{ Hz} \approx 36.62 \text{ kHz}$$

*(2 marks each for parts (i)‚Äď(iii), 1 mark for (iv) = 7 marks.)*

---

#### ‚ĚĖ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) based digital spectrum analyser. Discuss the role of the windowing function. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In an **FFT-based digital spectrum analyser**, the input signal is first conditioned (attenuated and filtered), then sampled by an ADC at $f_s$, and a block of $N$ samples is taken. These samples are multiplied by a **window function** (Hann, Hamming, Blackman, etc.) to suppress spectral leakage. The windowed samples are then passed to the **FFT processor**, which computes the Discrete Fourier Transform using the Cooley‚ÄďTukey algorithm in $O(N \log_2 N)$ complex multiplications ‚ÄĒ vastly faster than the $O(N^2)$ of a direct DFT.

The FFT output gives $N$ complex values $X[k]$ representing the amplitude and phase of each frequency bin. The analyser then computes $\vert X[k] \vert$ and converts to dB for display. Each bin corresponds to a frequency $f_k = k \cdot \Delta f$, where $\Delta f = f_s/N$.

**Role of the Windowing Function:**  
A rectangular window (no tapering) treats the captured $N$-sample block as one period of a perfectly periodic signal. Any discontinuity at the edges causes **spectral leakage**, where the energy of a single frequency "smears" across multiple bins. Tapering windows smooth the edges, reducing leakage at the cost of a slightly wider main lobe. Hann and Hamming windows are the most common compromise in spectrum analysis.

*(3 marks diagram + 2 marks working explanation + 2 marks windowing role = 7 marks.)*

**(b) Derive the relationship between frequency resolution, sampling frequency, and FFT size. Why is this relationship fundamental in digital spectrum analysis? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key result is:

$$\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{1}{T_{rec}}}$$

**Why this relationship is fundamental:**

1. It defines the **smallest frequency difference** that the analyser can distinguish between two close spectral lines.
2. It shows that to improve resolution, the analyser must **increase $N$** (larger FFT) or **decrease $f_s$** (narrower span) ‚ÄĒ both have trade-offs.
3. It establishes that the analyser has a **finite record time** ‚ÄĒ to see very fine frequency detail, you need to wait and capture a longer waveform. This makes the digital analyser less suitable than the analog (swept) analyser for very wide spans with fine resolution.
4. It dictates the **memory, processing power, and sweep time** required ‚ÄĒ $N$ points of storage and $N \log_2 N$ operations per FFT.

*(3 marks for derivation + 4 marks for the engineering significance = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the $N/2$ rule** ‚ÄĒ Students often quote the total number of FFT bins ($N$) instead of the useful $N/2$ for a real-input signal. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Confusing Record Length with Sweep Time** ‚ÄĒ These are *not* the same. Record length $T_{rec}$ is the time captured per FFT. Sweep time is how long the analyser takes to update the entire display. In a real-time DSA, sweep time $\geq$ record time.
> 3. **Mis-stating the Hann RBW** ‚ÄĒ A common error is to say Hann window RBW is the same as the bin width. The correct equivalent noise bandwidth is $1.5 \times \Delta f$, not $\Delta f$.
> 4. **Mixing up dB scales** ‚ÄĒ Use $10 \log$ for power ratios, $20 \log$ for voltage ratios. Examiners actively look for this.
> 5. **No anti-aliasing discussion** ‚ÄĒ In block diagram questions, students frequently skip the anti-aliasing filter. KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists it as a key block ‚ÄĒ missing it costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **DSA = Sampler + FFT Engine + Display**. It is the digital counterpart of the analog swept-tuned analyser.
- [x] **Nyquist limit:** $f_{max} = f_s/2$. Anti-aliasing filter is *mandatory* before the ADC.
- [x] **Frequency resolution:** $\Delta f = f_s / N = 1 / T_{rec}$. Finer resolution ‚áí longer record or larger FFT.
- [x] **Number of bins (real input):** $N/2$ unique positive-frequency bins.
- [x] **Record length:** $T_{rec} = N / f_s$. For a 1024-point FFT at 100 MHz, $T_{rec} = 10.24$ ¬Ķs.
- [x] **Windowing** reduces spectral leakage; Hann and Hamming are the most common. Trade-off: wider main lobe.
- [x] **dB scale:** Use $10 \log$ for power, $20 \log$ for voltage.
- [x] **ADC SNR:** $6.02 N + 1.76$ dB for an $N$-bit ideal ADC. Real ADCs have lower SNR ‚ÄĒ leading to ENOB $< N$.
- [x] **RBW in a digital analyser = bin width** (modified by the window's equivalent noise bandwidth).
- [x] **Advantages:** Real-time capture of transients, better amplitude accuracy, flexible post-processing (averaging, math).
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Limited frequency span (because of Nyquist), high-speed ADC cost, memory-intensive for large FFTs.
- [x] **Applications:** Telecom carrier verification, EMC compliance, audio/ vibration analysis, radar and EW.
- [x] **KTU buzzwords to use in answers:** Nyquist criterion, spectral leakage, frequency bin, RBW, span, real-time bandwidth, FFT, anti-aliasing filter, windowing, dynamic range.
- [x] **Memorise the four key formulas** (Nyquist, Resolution, Record Length, ADC SNR) ‚ÄĒ every KTU question in this module tests at least one of them.
- [x] **Always draw a block diagram** for any descriptive question. KTU awards 3‚Äď5 marks for a correctly labelled diagram alone.
